The statue of the Virgin Mary in front of the church apparently made headlines in local news a few years ago for shedding tears.

Very nice looking cathedral, however there was loads of scaffolding around it and it deterred from it's true beauty. We didnt go in and I'm not sure with the work going on if you actually could. It's a majestic looming small cathedral

This cathedral is under construction but we attended a Sunday mass, that was in English. The interior is okay but not as grandiose as European cathedrals. The outside has scaffolding all around so not sure what it looks like. It's in a very central part of town so hard to miss.

It was a pity that the cathedral was closed for renovation but the outside was a great look in itself and the outside space really shows off the architecture and sets it off well. Like to go and see inside one say when it reopens.
Visited in early February and currently there is no access to the interior as restoration work is currently being carried out.  Nice enough exterior and square with historic post office opposite.
Too sad coming all the way here to find it under construction. But it’s still worth for a nice picture. Just a cross the street there is the beautiful post office.

Because the church is not open to the public, tourists are not allowed to enter, so we have no idea what's inside. We only got a glimpse from the outside! It's next to the Central Post Office, so we took a look around!
It was near the post office. It was under construction, so I could only view it from the outside, but it was a rare and beautiful sight, something you don't see in Japan.

Difficult to give a full review here as the cathedral is undergoing extensive renovations and is closed to the public. However, from the outside it's clear to see this is a very beautiful building and Im sure when completed will be well worth visiting to see the inside.
If you are near the city, stop for a photo! You can't go inside, but the image is impressive!

Made an exciting walk from my hotel, crossing the roads in Saigon traffic in order to get a peak at Saigon Notre Dame Cathedral, only to be disappointed as it is currently undergoing restoration. Unfortunately could not see inside and much of the outside was obscured by scaffolding. I visited in March, but I believe the restoration works are still on-going.
The Notre Dame Cathedral was under construction, so unfortunately we were only able to view from the outside.

It is located right in front of the Central Post Office. It seems you cannot enter because it is currently under construction. It would be better if there were trees nearby, like in the old photos posted around. Taking a photo from the front with the statue of the Virgin Mary and the church in the background does turn out well, but someone—perhaps a local—keeps interfering.
The church facade is nice but the building is currently under construction so you cannot get close or get great photos.
The church is currently being renovated, so we couldn't visit it. You can simply take a photo from the outside.

I came here before the renovations and it was beautiful. I came again two years ago and it was closed for renovations. The last time I came, the renovations weren't finished yet. If you come, be careful of pickpockets; there are a lot of tourists, so thieves tend to target those areas specifically.
The magnificent church was under renovation and we couldn't go inside; scaffolding had been erected on both sides and the back. It was a truly impressive church.

Look, in reality, it’s just another church. It should be noted we were unable to have a look inside. It’s currently got scaffold down either side atm, which is unsightly, but necessary. It’s worth including though as part of a city tour.
The Cathedral looks good from outside. As restoration work is in progress, visitors are not allowed inside. A few good clicks from outside is all A tourist gets these days.
Under Renovations. Closed. But still a good surround contrast architecture. Most other tourist attractions are around the corner so good location to stay.

Building undergo g renovation at the moment. Expected completion is in year 2022. Can see the building externally. Still a good spot for some nice picture with the building or a statue in the front of the church.
The church was closed when we arrived, so we only saw the outside. Next to the post office. Nice when you are in the neighborhood, but nothing very special.
The interior was closed at 11 am, full of disappointed tourists. Beautiful building, very heavy traffic nearby.

you can only look the front of the Cathedral. it is not possible to enter due to reconstruction works, and also the outer sides are packed for renovation. Located just beside the nice Central Post Office, it is still worth a short look and a picture.
I visited between December 19-22, 2019. The church was closed inside due to renovations, so I had to take photos from the outside. There were still some good spots to take pictures.

While we were able to appreciate the spectacular gothic architecture on the outside, we were unable to venture inside. We were informed quite aggressively by a woman at the main gate that we could not enter unless we were attending Mass, due to the renovations taking place. As neither my husband or I are Catholic, we were forced to simply enjoy the exterior of the church. I hope on our next visit we have more luck.
Ok if walking by from the War Remnants Museum. Not sure when them or one of the beautiful nearby parks but you can't go inside at least as of November 2019.

Nice but nothing really special, the most impressive about it being the fact that all building materials were shipped all the way from France. In the evening jolly families sit around with their kids and picnic that was nice. Pigeons here look cool too, not just the gray type.
Can only view front of building from outside the compound, sides blocked by construction panels. Not worth the trip
Visiting Saigon Notre Dame Cathedral but seems like it is under renovation.  Was pretty disappointed. But well, at least got to catch a glimpse of it.

Username: Fynbos Guest Farm and Animal Sanctuary
Comment: We decided to visit the church on the 3rd visit to HCMC. It was disappointing. It was under renovation so no access and no photo opportunity except for the front. The boards on the outside walls were really fascinating. Literally a 5 minute visit.
Unfortunately the building was covered when we were there. It was a beautiful day. A bit disappointed, but I am sure that it will be restored to its former glory.
Nice to visit briefly, but otherwise a church like many in Europe. The old post office is located next to the Cathedral, so the trip is well worth it if you have some time.

Right across from the Central Post Office, there was a cathedral standing prominently on the roadside—it was obvious to anyone that I was Notre Dame Cathedral—but unfortunately, it was under construction at the time... so I just passed by...

It's a really pretty building but it's currently closed access and covered by scaffolding and boardings for building work. It looks really good from the courtyard in front and better from the helicopter building elevated nearby, but would be better when the works completed hence lower score. Free to look at
Unfortunately it was covered in scaffolding when I went. However the parts I could see were stunning and I’ll be back again to see it in all its glory.

We walked all the way to visit this place but not allowed to enter into the cathedral. Many tourists were turned down from the entrance, no reason was given. Only they said they were closed to tourists. We took photos outside the cathedral only. A bit disappointed.
This site is being restored at the moment so covered in scaffolding. Will need to come back another time.

The cathedral of Vietnam, built modeled after Notre-Dame in Paris, has been under renovation for quite some time and cannot be visited. When it is finished, it will certainly be worth the effort, but currently it is covered in scaffolding and unfortunately, it is impossible to take a nice photo of it. So if you came here for this: Tough luck! However, on the same square stands the post office, which is open to visitors and still functions as a post office! Go post your postcards and letters here! They are guaranteed to be home within 8 days. There is even more to eat and see around here, so your trip will not be in vain.

I went to see the Notre dame Cathedral but it is still being renovated. I had read about it on trip advisor many months ago that it was under renovation and it is still ongoing. You only see the outside what ever is not covered. Later on we saw people being let in Well Vietnamese and tried to get in and lady refused any tourist with no explanation WHY???

The famous historical landmark, Saigon Notre Dame Cathedral, is located in front of the equally famous Central Post Office. I was staying at the InterContinental Saigon, and my room offered a view overlooking the cathedral, so I always passed by it when going out, and it was always crowded with tourists taking pictures. However, it is currently undergoing renovations, and scaffolding has been erected around the outside of the church, making it difficult to take pictures.
The cathedral shares the square with the city's historic post office. Both buildings are must-sees for tourists.

Was here over 20 years ago when the cathedral was not open to the public so we were disappointed again as the cathedral is under renovation and will not open until 2023 according to one fellow we spoke to. Around the cathedral there are many photos of the area from 10-50 years ago, some very interesting to look at.
Our stop in our city tour, as the church were in midst of restoration and was closed to the public visit.  We manage to capture some nice picture of the church surrounding.
Unfortunately, the church is currently a construction site, so it cannot be visited at the moment and can only be photographed from the outside. It is located directly opposite the post office.

The church is currently undergoing repairs and maintenance. Located in a central position, opposite the Independence Palace and near a park, it's a popular spot for tourists and couples taking wedding photos.

Went there in 2017 and they were closed for renovations and when we went back this year they were still busy. Beautiful building and look forward to seeing it when the work is completed some time in the future.

On the way from the airport to the hotel, the taxi driver stopped for me, so I took a few photos. The gate was closed due to construction, so I could only take a picture from the front, but it's a very beautiful and wonderful building. There weren't many people in the early morning, but there were so many pigeons that they wouldn't move even when I got close, making it difficult to take pictures. There was a lot of droppings, so you should watch your step.

It was built by the French towards the end of the nineteenth century and is characterized by two sixty-meter-high turrets on its façade. It stands in a square surrounded by large flowerbeds, next to the much more interesting post office. Unfortunately, during our stay in the city, it was undergoing restoration, so we had to limit ourselves to a quick stop in front of the front facade; the gates were closed, so our opinion is necessarily influenced by the little we were able to see. Our guide told us that religious services are held there not only in Vietnamese, but also, on Sundays, in English.
French colonial heritage can be seen in the church of Notre Dame, built entirely with materials imported from France. It was not possible to visit it due to its ongoing restoration.

The 19th-century church impresses with its harmony and simplicity. Unfortunately, we didn't have the opportunity to see the interior. Luckily, Saigon wasn't bombed, so this gem, in the shape of a cross with two symmetrical spires, survived.
Under renovation, not much to see. Would be impressive otherwise would be interesting. Right next to the Post Office. So 2 birds with one stone.

When we arrived we couldn't see it because it was closed, and it had blue paint all over almost the entire roof, a shame, because apparently, from what we can see, it looks very good, I hope they don't take long to finish the restoration or conservation and that it can be visited as soon as possible.

We came but unfortunately the cathedral was closed. Not sure what they are doing or for how long but there was a lot of scaffolding on the outside obscuring the view and there was no entry inside. Shame really!

From outside you can appreciated that is the first renovation in 200 years, There is a walls of scaffolding that not permit you to see the external walls. It was closed the access to the interior. Bad luck.
We went here in November 2019. It was undergoing renovation and covered in scaffolding. What a pity!
It was under renovation, so I couldn't go inside, and scaffolding had been erected around the exterior. The central post office is next door. I recommend visiting both together.
I expected a bit more from the cathedral... besides, we couldn't go inside because it's under construction... you have to see it for its history, but little else.

Currently under restoration, we were only able to admire the exterior. A small church in the center of Ho Chi Minh City, with a square in front dominated by a statue of the Virgin Mary. Nothing to miss.
Unfortunately, at the moment it can only be viewed from the outside, it is fenced in and not open to visitors.

I would have loved to visit it on many occasions when I've visited this city, but unfortunately, only a portion of the exterior can be seen, as it's been wrapped up and cordoned off for restoration. I imagine the interior is very beautiful. The exterior, which is visible, is still worth seeing.
Just like Paris at the present time, you can not go inside , bit more difficult to take photos, still its worth a visit

Nice to look at from the outside. It's made from stone imported from Marseille. Under renovation inside. Opposite is the post office so definately go in there for a look. McDonald's there too If you need a cold drink and facilities.

Not much like the real Notre Dame in Paris. Under renovation at present so can’t see inside and quite a bit of scaffolding around the outside  A photo opportunity and amazing to think all the resources came through Marseille all that time ago

Unfortunately it was under construction when we visited so we could not explore the inside. But it was a beautiful structure and wonderful photo opportunity as usual. It was busy when we visited and there were many street sellers which approached us for buying their goods - beware and please don't be afraid to say no as majority of them lie to get their stuff sold

Located in the city center, near the Central Post Office (about a 1-minute walk away), the Basilica of Our Lady is currently under renovation with scaffolding behind it. Therefore, it's not ideal for taking photos and isn't particularly spectacular. However, it's worth a visit if you're passing by, and admission is free at certain times.

It was undergoing extensive renovations. Nevertheless, it's a must-see spot for Ho Chi Minh City tourists, so I couldn't miss it... You can take beautiful photos from the square in front, and the post office, another tourist attraction, is right next door, so it's definitely worth a visit.
It is near the post office. An average cathedral building. Lot of tourists are came here for taking photo. there is nothing more. only good for taking some photo
I am reviewing this as a tourist attraction fans not as a church. There’s nothing much to see and nothing special about it.

When I visited, the renovations had already begun, a process that would have lasted for three years (?). Although most of the exterior was still visible, its unique, understated beauty had been diminished. You could indeed see the shadow of Notre Dame, but it lacked that awe-inspiring sense of holiness.

I went to see it because it's a tourist spot, but unfortunately, it was under construction, so I couldn't see the whole thing or go inside. Despite that, many people were visiting and taking pictures.
The Saigon Notre Dame cathedral is under construction so you want get to see much. Unable to get in.
The cathedral was closed for restoration so couldn't see inside but had some good night time photos from outside.
The Saigon Notre Dame cathedral is under construction presently with walls around the cathedral, hopefully your able to find your way through the entrance!

It can be placed next to the Central Post Office, but it's currently under renovation and inaccessible, and the photos don't turn out very well. Pigeon keepers along the road release pigeons to circle the square, which is quite a sight.
Currently inaccessible due to construction. The cathedral is also scaffolded on the sides and back. It's quite beautiful from the front, but its size is not comparable to cathedrals in Europe.
Beautiful building but cannot currently go inside. Have a look though if you are near the post office building as it’s right next door.

True to its name, the Pink Cathedral was pink and pretty. It was difficult to take photos because there were so many people. The lights were on at night, so it looked beautiful even then. It is located in the city center, so it is easily accessible.

Its another old building in the town with good history attached to it. Its still under construction and its been quite long, main reason as told is that people making it don't demand charity and if people are contributing themselves then only they take it.  If your near District 1 then do spend sometime around for pics. This is just next to oldest post office of Vietnam and both are good spots for few pics.
It's a very noticeable building. Unfortunately, it was under construction, and we couldn't even go inside. We could only take pictures from the outside. Even so, there were a lot of people there.

Unfortunately, the Notre Dame Cathedral of Saigon, located opposite the main post office, is currently being renovated, so there is not much to see and it is not possible to take good photos using the scaffolding.

Located in a prime spot in Saigon, the cathedral is worth a visit for its 19th-century colonial architecture. However, aside from that, it's a closed monument under renovation, so it's not particularly interesting.
We went to see the Notre Dame Cathedral and it was closed and renovations was being conducted on the day we were there, as it is straight across from the old post office.
It’s a shame this is currently under renovation, but as the post office is across the road it’s not a big issue.

To be fair, it's historical and and has a story to tell. But unless you are attehding mass (Sunday only) you can't go in and admire what it has to offer. The are also renovating it so perhaps that's the reason. We took our photos ans moved on.
Unfortunately it's under renovation with lots of scaffolding and fencing. Should look great when that's removed.

Not a lot really, the cathedral is currently closed for renovations so access unavailable at the moment. The outside is 80% covered in construction scaffolding as you can see from one of the pictures.  It may be worth postponing your visit until such a time as renovations are complete at this famous landmark!

Notte Dame is a church (obviously much smaller than the original) built by French colonists in the second half of the 19th century. It's currently under renovation (the sides are practically covered by structures for the work) and, while it's not a gem, it's worth stopping by for a quick photograph.

Saigon Notre Dame Cathedral is located in the heart of Saigon's bustling District 1, not far from the Saigon Post Office and the Reunification Palace. It is currently undergoing extensive renovations, which began in August 2017 and are expected to last until June 2020. Although Saigon Notre Dame Cathedral is currently closed to tourists, a service is held every Sunday at 9:30 am in Vietnamese and English. This allows you to admire the cathedral's impressive architecture from the surrounding area.

The area around Notre Dame Cathedral has been fenced off for repairs for a long time, but it's still not finished. Many tourists come to visit but are disappointed because they can't see the building constructed by the French in Vietnam. Hopefully, the repair and restoration work will be expedited so that Notre Dame Cathedral can be restored to its original beauty.
It's basically just a place to take pictures and be done with it. A short stay is fine. It's a good spot for souvenir photos. It might be worth visiting once.
They're renovating it, so they're not letting you in. The outside, as you can imagine, isn't exactly perfect either, with the rafters and the fence. The Main Post Office is on the right, so go there.
Closed for a refurb and scaffolding outside when we were there. Close to the Post Office so wasn't a wasted trip but dissapointing that we couldn't even see inside.
We were really looking forward to seeing a mini version of the Paris Notre Damme when we got to the centre of Saigon. Unfortunately, much of the building was under wraps and the church was closed.
When we visited it was closed so really only the outside. Fine to combine with the post office but only 30 mins for both.
Access is currently completely restricted due to renovation work. It appears it will not open anytime soon. It is located near major tourist attractions.

We only saw this church from the bus because it was under renovation, and I wasn't able to take a single photo. It's a shame. From the outside, I've been told, it's more beautiful than from the inside.
It was the first church that truly moved me when I visited in 1997. That's why I wanted to show it to my children, but unfortunately, it was under renovation and we couldn't go inside.
During our visit in early August, access to the cathedral was impossible due to renovations. The main post office is right across the street, so your trip won't be wasted.
Next to the Central Post Office, the cathedral is another point of interest, but it's closed this summer for renovations. We eagerly await its reopening!
Unfortunately this cathedral was under construction when we visited so we could not visit inside. Once you visit the Notre Dame in Paris you will disappointed by this one.
Inaccessible due to renovations. Covered in scaffolding. Nevertheless, beautiful to photograph from the outside.

The church is well kept and at the moment of our visit, undergoing renovations in the back part. Unlike his "sister" in Paris, it's ornaments are simple as it is not gothic style but more of a colonial one. You can visit the inside outside of the service times. ( closed to visitors during mass) The little sqare in front of it allows you to get some good pictures. Lots of cafes and restorants in the area . There may be some local vendors offering fruit at very high prices, watch out! ...

It is interesting to glance at as you walk by, but you can’t see inside because of renovations. There are definitely much more interstate things near to see, including the post office across the street.

if visiting check if you're allowed inside the cathedral and if any kind of renovations are in progress. all we did is to take some photographs from the ouside and go to the post office which is across the road to buy some memorabilia.

The cathedral is beautiful and truly looks like Paris, if not for the palm trees surrounding it. :) The guide, a rather rude person, didn't mention that the cathedral was undergoing restoration, so we were only able to admire the exterior. Based on the information we received, restoration materials have already been ordered: at the very least, the roof tiles, some bricks, and glass will be replaced. It's worth a visit, but only when you're sure the restoration is complete.
It's is a pretty building to see if you are nearby, but nothing spectacular and you couldn't actual enter the cathedral
Unfortunately closed when we got there, could only see it partially from the outside. Quite a powerful church, probably worth visiting when they finish the renovation.
You can see many tourists taking photos in front of Notre Dame Cathedral, located in front of the Central Post Office. It is a must-see spot for sightseeing in downtown Ho Chi Minh City.
It was covered in scaffolding during our stay in Saigon. The church also has two towers at the front, but is otherwise not comparable to Notre Dame.
Also designed by Eiffel. Only viewed from the outside, it was covered in scaffolding. One of the highlights during our cycling tour.

I thought the front was beautiful, exactly the architectural style I liked. However, it was under renovation when I visited in March 2018, and it was still under renovation when I went back at the end of October. It was never open to the public, and the sides were surrounded by iron railings, making it impossible to get a perfect full view. I don't know if it's been repaired now.
Unable to view inside unless you are here on a Sunday to go to mass. The building is also under restoration apparently going to take a long time to complete. Building is nice from outside
A magnificent cathedral, but it was undergoing restoration, so we couldn't visit it. I would love to see it again when it's finished.
Disappointing closed for repair at the moment. However you can still visit the post office opposite to get your daily dose of tourist bucklist building ticks off the check list.

Located in the heart of Ho Chi Minh City, it's a good starting point for sightseeing. Even if the taxi driver doesn't speak English, you can get there by pointing at a guidebook! Just be careful as there's a lot of traffic.
A nice church but currently being renovated so the whole back is covered in blue tarpaulins and scaffolding.  I didn’t go inside, I’m unsure if you can or if there is a fee?!

The cathedral is under repair so was unable to go inside. The architecture looks great from what we could see so if travelling to Saigon just be aware there is a lot of scaffolding around the building so you can only see the front.
I only got to see the statue of Mary from the outside. Believers were singing what sounded like hymns in front of the statue. I'd like to visit once the renovations are finished.
OK from the front, but currently under renovations. Worth a look after it's finished, post office across the road

It is located in the same area as the Central Post Office. When I visited, it was under renovation so I couldn't look inside, but it is a place I often stop by when passing through District 1 in the city. I only took photos of the exterior.
It was covered in scaffolding at the time, so I could only see it from the outside. Beautiful building with a large statue of Mary in front of it on the square.

Nice-looking church but closed due to restoration work. Got some good photos, but beware of people selling stuff to tourist. Some are good people really trying to make a living, some are scammers (shoe-repair guy walking around wanting to fix tourists’ shoes and then charge them 500000++ VND. But yeah this scam is common in other countries as well).
I went in the evening after the rain. The weather was very nice, but unfortunately the building was under renovation so I couldn't see the inside. But it was a good place nonetheless.
We only managed to take photo at the garden as it is under renovation. Still an iconic place to visit nonetheless.
We were very disappointed because we got a taxi there and then realised it was all under renovation and couldn't see much anyway.

This is a beautifully designed french building that still functions as a church in the heart of Saigon city. It is within walking distance of district 1. You can walk inside which is nice and free of charge. The beauty of it is that it's right next to the central post office another impressive building and very close to the war museum and reunification palace so it's not a significant detour. I would recommend people visit it for 10 minutes as a part of a walking tour of the city featuring notre dam cathedral, post office, reunification palace, war museum, ben thanh markets and bitexico financial tower. Go to the central post office after getting a nice quick picture.

This cathedral was beautiful! Unfortunately for us though, they don’t allow people to actually go in... This means you can look from the outside and take a photo and then leave! :)  I would say that if you’re in the area, it’s worth stopping by, if you need to get in a car to get here, pass on the opportunity. I’ve seen much more beautiful cathedrals all around the world.

On Sunday, May 12th, at 8am, many people were gathering for Mass. As expected, tourists were not allowed in. Some people who were mistaken for Caucasian tourists were stopped at the entrance, but they met a local acquaintance and went in together. As I was looking at the church from the outside, a man on a motorbike started explaining things in Japanese. I wanted to hear more, so I thought it might be a good idea to join a proper guided tour to learn about its history.

Judging by the efficiency of the Vietnamese, it's here to stay. It doesn't look particularly impressive. Not in terms of size...or anything... The famous post office...which, other than its fame, doesn't impress with anything special. Perhaps only the stuffiness and the abundance of souvenir shops. That's where you'll find the stars. I don't think I'm missing out on much.

Not sure why I rushed to visit this. We have churches bigger and older and far more embellished and I think they're just mehh compared to Spain, England, Scotland...so I guess my lack of Vietnam history came into play here and my ignorance got the better of me. Saw it, walked past it.

Early May 2019 - There appears to be a lot of construction or renovation at this site. Both sides of the cathedral are covered with scaffolding. Although you may want to visit the garden in front or the amazing post office that is adjacent. Still a lot of tourist strolling around here.
Tourist spot, for photo taking only. Nothing spectacular. Can walk to nearby shopping though. Close to intercontinental Hotel.
If you're staying in the Dong Khoi area or a nearby hotel, you can see the exterior on foot without joining a tour.

It was a bit disappointing that it was under construction, but it was a truly magnificent building. It seemed that general tourists weren't allowed inside, as the woman guarding the gate was turning tourists away with a rather stern expression.
Some repairs are taking place, so we couldn't get in, a total shame because it looks like a very nice church from the outside
It was only possible to see it from the outside, so there are few comments. It's right in front of the post office, and it's possible to do both in one go.
Looks good but at present closed to public so only outside views slightly spoilt by scaffolding but as post office next door worth a visit
Wasn’t able to go inside, due to renovations. This is the second time I’ve been there in 2 years and it’s not complete.

The most part of the exterior was covered in scaffolding which was a major disappointment. The queue to get in was lengthy and at fear of sounding critical there wasn’t much to see. A pleasant building from the outside from what we could see.
Cathedral was under renovation reason why of low rating. Post Office and other tourist highlights are nearby.
This pretty but small cathedral is currently under renovations so we were unable to go inside which was a bit disappointing.

We got close enough to look inside on the first day of our visit, but by the time we came back, there was construction/repairs going on, and barricades all around the site, keeping tourists out.  Built in early 1860's by French colonists.  Nice for a church buff, and reminds us of the French influence in Vietnam culture. Not quite the same for us as seeing the original in Paris, but it is an interesting site.  Hope you enjoy it.....  Mahalo
Unfortunately you cannot even take s good photo on the outside without including reconstruction/renovation material. It is closed for quite a while

The cathedral is undergoing restoration (as of April 2019. The main thing is that it's not restored like... well, you get the idea (topical)). The building is covered in scaffolding, and entry is closed.
Sadly it is closed for renovations at present, however the post office nearby is worth a visit - pop in to buy a stamp or souvenir or just to check it out
European building (Paris). Currently under construction and inaccessible. The photos aren't very good.
This is built by French powers when they had occupied Saigon, similar to Paris notre dame. You can see the French architecture in Saigon so much.
Pretty from the outside but inaccessible inside. Worth checking out if you're passing by! There's scaffolding.
Large renovation works going on on this historic building. Odd realising it that every stone was shipped out if France....

Make sure you see at least one Notre Dame, as this one is also covered in scaffolding (humor). It was built by the French, then brought in and reassembled here piece by piece. It evokes absolutely no emotion. It's hard to miss, as it's right on the way from the post office to the War Remnants Museum. What can I say, just take a photo and move on.
Unfortunately Notre Dame Cathedral was closed for restoration while we were in HCMC, so we were only able to view the exterior. Maybe someday we’ll be back.
It was a shame we couldn't go inside because it was under construction. But even just seeing the exterior was worth it. It's next to the central post office, so there were a lot of tourists.
Very pretty from the outside, but unfortunately the cathedral is closed for renovations. It's impossible to visit.
Currently (and for some time) the cathedral is closed because roof repair work is underway.

Saigon Notre Dame Cathedral is located in a very busy place. It surrounded by more of those Saigon roads where you just have pick a moment in time and go. Just keep walking!  We didn’t see the Cathedral at its best hence my rating being lower than it probably would have been as it was surrounded by hoardings because of work that was in progress.  The small garden to the front was being tended by gardeners and was in immaculate condition.  That said the Cathedral in a good location and well worth a visit.  Standing with your back to the front looking at the buildings opposite you can just pick out the building (18 Gia Long Street) where the iconic photograph was taken of the last helicopter evacuating refugees.

I couldn't go inside because it was under renovation, so I didn't get a good look, but the atmosphere in the square in front of the cathedral was nice in its own way. It is fun to watch the old man writing calligraphy.

The cathedral is nice and is good to see it BUT they just started its renovation in April 2019 so there is scaffolding all around the cathedral so dont count with any excellent pictures in near future. But once you are going to see the Central Post Office you can see this one too, its just opposite the street.

We we're disappointed as the cathedral was closed for renovations. The disappointing bit was we weren't aware that it was closed even the hotel shuttle bus did not mention that. Could not get a good view of the building as it was covered in areas. Was so looking forward to check it out as it is one of the top things to do in HCMC.

While exploring the city, I found myself in the square where the Saigon Notre Dame Cathedral stands.... It's actually a bit similar to the one in Paris.. However, it's smaller.. At the time I was there (March 2019), it was partially renovated externally.. Well, it's "nice.". It's an interesting feeling to see something familiar from Europe.. There are many souvenir sellers on the square in front of the Cathedral.. However, you can't go inside.... which is a pity..
I only saw it from the outside. I passed by it after visiting the post office. It looked like the kind of church you'd find in a region influenced by Europe or European culture.

This is Notre Dame de Saigon, a functioning 19th-century Catholic church located in the center of Ho Chi Minh City on the Place de la Commune. The cathedral was built in 1880 according to the designs of architect J. Bourad. All construction materials were imported from France. The outer wall of the cathedral is made of Marseille red brick, which has not faded and looks much as it did in the 19th century. The thickness of the walls is approximately 65 cm, providing sound and thermal insulation. The full height of the cathedral, including the crosses, is 60.5 meters. The stained glass windows and columns were delivered ready for erection. Construction lasted 2.5 years, and a solemn consecration ceremony took place on Easter 1880. In 1895, two bell towers with six bells were added. Each corresponds to a specific note, the note "F" is missing. On weekdays, two of them ring (at 5:00 AM and 5:30 PM), and on weekends, all three ring at the same time. The six bells ring together only on Christmas Day! In 1959, a 4-meter-tall white marble statue of the Virgin Mary, made in Rome, was erected. She holds a small globe (a symbol of the spread of Christianity) and steps on a snake (the victory of good over evil). At the statue's foot is a prayer box for parishioners. In 2005, it was announced that the Virgin Mary was streaming myrrh. Eyewitnesses allegedly saw a tear trickling down the stone face of the Virgin Mary. Thousands of people began flocking here to witness this miracle. The Vatican has not confirmed the "streaming myrrh" but has recognized the church as a basilica. At the time of our visit, Notre Dame de Saigon was undergoing a major renovation, which will last until 2020.

This best part of seeing this area WAS THE WALK from the war museum to reunification center TO Notre Dame and seeing all the local shops, etc.  The BOOK STREET closely was fun to go through. SO many other things to see.

France established Saigon as the representative city of Indochina, constructing buildings in the city center almost as if transplanting their own culture. Construction of this cathedral also began in 1877 using materials imported from France and was completed on Easter Sunday in 1880. Two bell towers were added in 1895, and it remains an iconic structure that stands in its place to this day. Following South Vietnam's independence, a statue of the Virgin Mary was erected in front of the cathedral in 1959, and it came to be known as "Notre Dame." With the establishment of the Diocese of Vietnam in 1960, it became the "Saigon Cathedral," and subsequently, in 1962, it was named the "Saigon Notre Dame Cathedral" as an archbishop's cathedral. Although the cathedral, which has remained in its place even after the unification of Vietnam, is currently surrounded by barriers for renovations, the square containing the statue of the Virgin Mary serves as a resting place for citizens. Furthermore, the Central Post Office is located right next door, and most of Ho Chi Minh City's major facilities are close enough to walk to.

It overlooks Paris Square and perfectly represents the French presence in that area: the façade has two bell towers over 50 meters tall which, before the city's face changed, could be seen from kilometers away; now the new, very tall buildings obscure it. It was designed by a French architect, the cornerstone was laid in 1877 by a French clergyman, and even the bricks were imported from Marseille (ah... typical grandeur!!). It was inaugurated three years later and its six bells weigh just under 30 tons. There's no denying it: it's worth a look inside and noticing how it differs from the Parisian Notre Dame in terms of its ceiling. The side niches feature the Stations of the Cross and statues of various patron saints.
