The building might be huge, but it is less then 200 years of age. Still, it is currently nearly fully wrapped. And this will take substantial time for ongoing renovations. It is by no way a cathedral comparable to European of French cathedrals, but a mere bad copy. Not worth visiting.
While it looks quite interesting it is covered in scaffolding and so you cannot access it. Even then it does not look like it would be much more than average.
With the scaffolding for the work, it is impossible to access the heart of the cathedral. Too bad, not sure I'll be able to go back there one day.
What a pitty… the cathedral is under construction (remodeling I guess), so you can view nothing!!!! It was disappointing and sad 😒😒😒

The Notre Dame Cathedral, which is one of the main attractions in HCMC, is unfortunately currently a large construction site. The church is closed off from the outside and you can't visit the inside either. It was supposed to be finished by 2023, but I've heard it's been delayed until 2027. It's right next to the post office, so you can still stop by.
Sadly you cannot go in as its under repair. And you cant even get a good photo outside. What is more annoying is that it doesn't even look like anyone is working on it.

Don't make a wasted trip, you won't see anything. Let's try again next year. It's all under construction; you can't see anything around there, just scaffolding. The staff said it won't be until the end of next year. I'm a little sad. ~Ah ...
The mass held inside is very boring and annoying as you can hardly understand a single word. Brand New sound engineering is needed in this otherwise picture perfect church.

This is Ho Chi Minh City's landmark and offers the most Instgram-able photo for many visitors. Unfortunately the city's main cathedral is under renovation with its facade completely covered. Better come later.
I was looking forward to a visit since my last, a couple years ago, it was closed for renovation. Same situation in 2020, yet I did hear a choir rehearsing inside the cathedral.

The exterior is clad in scaffold and nets and it is impossible to ignore . Who wants a photo of that clutter ? You can walk through the gates but not into the building. For the foreseeable future this is no longer a tourst attraction. However, the location is great, very central, facing the independance palace about 200 metres away . Lots of very expensive restaurants close by and a shady little park.
The church looks like it could be amazing, but unfortunately it is being totally refurbished. Almost the entire structure is covered by scaffolding.

What a shame. The cathedral is undergoing extensive remedial works and is closed to the public. The building looks impressive from the outside and I am sure the inside must be something special, but it will have to wait until we visit Ho Chi Minh again.
Selected times you can visit as undergoing refurbishment so we didn't get to see inside. Think it's open from 5lm for hour or so.
A visit is impossible due to the construction work; you can only go around it and stroll around the beautiful post office building right next door.

I was disapointed that I didn't get to see inside as it was having repairs done due to water damage to the roof. Having been to Notre Dame in France was looking forward to the comparison. The photos that surround the building of what it looks like inside were very intriguing. Oh well maybe another time.

The Cathedral was first on my list of places to visit in HCMC. Very disappointed to find it was under renovation and visitors were not allowed to even peep in. Crossed the street to go to the Post Office...too many vendors harassing you to purchase their wares.
We’re able to walk around but not go in. Lots of vendors on the square. Short walk to post office and city hall.

It's a shame it's currently closed for renovation. Otherwise, from the outside, the cathedral is very pretty. Just before Christmas, it's a pity we couldn't go inside... There's a lovely nativity scene in front, which adds to its charm.
At the moment is closed for restauration We only have the chance to see from outside But you have the post office on the right side and is nice to visit
In my idea saigon notredom catderal nothing have special point you can check better place in the city
The church they pompously call a cathedral is little more than a neighborhood parish in Spain. A very discreet French cultural legacy. One can do without it.
Building is expressive from outside too bad it’s under construction and is closed for a good while. Maybe a year.
I don't like churches, especially not in Southeast Asia. But I'm sure you'll pass by here on your visit to Saigon.
we went to see it but it was being renovated at the time so could not go in due to the work and could not see much on the outside
Beautiful building but you can not get inside while renovation is going on ...a bit disappointing. Worth driving past but if you are on a tight schedule then probably miss and see something else

It's under renovation and you can't go in, can only stand outside and take pictures. The side of it is covered with tarp and scaffolding. Not really worth the trip, ok if you have spare time and want to walk around the city.
Lovely building but currently clothed in scaffolding and not open to public. Disappointed. We had walked there in blistering heat and hoped for respite and calm. A shame.

Check the opening hours as visiting hours are very limited. Just opposite the central post office. Nothing compared to the ND churches, atleast from the outside. In case you havent got enough time dont bother, you wont be missing much.

Well, I guess I cannot really say much about Saigon Notre Dame Cathedral since it is, unfortunately, still under massive renovation.  We walked by and snapped a couple of pictures from the outside and that's it.  You cannot miss it since it's right next to the Saigon Central Post Office (another place of interest).
The front is still viable but the rest of the building is covered in scaffolding. The opening times don't match up to the advertised time, whether that's due to works I'm not sure
We arrived in the evening, The church was not lighted well. The garden in front had some light, with people sitting on the grass. It was next to post office. A fast food restaurant and a cafe nearby.

There are several churches in Ho Chi Minh City that are symbols of the colonial era, but this most famous one is currently closed for renovations and cannot be entered. Dong Khoi Street, which leads from here to the Saigon River, is one of the city's fashionable streets.
Went here with lots of expectations. But unfortunately the church was closed for renovation.  A must visit in Saigon.
It's just another run-of-the-mill cathedral; it doesn't have any particular charm or picturesque appeal. Hanoi's cathedral is much more striking and beautiful than this one.
We wanted to visit Notre Dame in Saigon, but due to renovation work it was closed, and we could only view the exterior partially.
Unfortunately you cannot go inside this cathedral at the moment as it is closed for renovation work. You can get a good photograph of the building from the square outside the front of the building.
This disappointed me a lot. Not only was it closed when I visited but it was a lot smaller than expected. Near Vincom Center so after passing through, you can check out the mall and some ac.

Entry inside is not possible as it is still under renovation. There isn't much to see, as taking photos with the statue of the Virgin Mary in front is pretty much all there is to see. The Central Post Office is right next door, so the route is convenient.
You cannot enter the church because the roof is under repair. You can often see believers praying in front of the church. There is nothing particular to do.

Unfortunately, we can't really judge Notre Dame Cathedral as it's currently undergoing restoration or something similar. In any case, we couldn't go inside, and it was covered in scaffolding on three sides. The facade is quite nice to look at, but nothing particularly special.
Sadly it was under renovation. Outside was blocked by scaffolding and barriers. Will be amazing when it's completed.
Cathedral is clad in scaffolding and you are unable to see inside. Come back in several years and it might be worth a visit.

When I visited its sides were covered with scaffolding.  As it is simply a french catholic church that is much smaller than that in Paris I would advise that ones time might be spent better elsewhere. Without going in it is a 10 - 15 minute photo shoot to get pictures that you can find all over the internet.  Just not much history or significance to this site/building.  Note: It is right across the street from the Post Office
It's a shame not to be able to access even a secure area. The cathedral was completely barricaded due to renovation work.

It is under renovations at the moment so all you see is the front of it as it has scaffolding around the rest. It is a lovely building and you can take pictures from the front. Not sure how long it will be like this- no one seemed to know.
Don't waste time for those who know the original and only a disappointment, an imitation better to have an aperitif on the skyscrapers

I am not sure how we missed that there were renovations going on so all we got to see was the exterior. It was disappointing not to be inside. Under those circumstances, our visit was over in five minutes!

As of Feb2019 this place is not worth visiting, it will be a complete waste of your time. The whole building is completely surrounded by scaffolding and safety netting you can hardly see a thing, and the rate that the Vietnamese work at would imagine it to be on going for the next 12 months. The Post Office is virtually next door but in my opinion go and see for the sake of being able to say you have seen the post office.
Not worth viewing currently as undergoing Renovations and covered in tarpaulins and scaffolding Close to the palace and 10 mins walk to the war museum though

You can enter the Catholic church from the French colonial era unless there's a special event going on inside. However, it doesn't seem to have any World Heritage designation. There are many Western-style buildings remaining in Ho Chi Minh City.
Closed for restoration. Will certainly take a while longer as there is little activity. Beautiful surroundings and open space.
This was ok but could not go in as it is being renovated until 2022. It would of been nice to go inside and see if any difference from the one in France, having visited there several times

Even thou they highly advertise, not until you reach there you know it is under constructions. We were there in Dec 2018. The entire church is scaffolding and trap covered . Hope they would finish it soon.
Covered over with tarpalling and surronded with scaffolding for on going maintenance so nothing to see today.
Visiting this Cathedral was on our itinerary - but its really not worth including it (tour operators please note) as its being renovated and not open for the next 14 months.
was closed off and the back part was all scaffolded in, the front was okay for a picture but people kept away, so stop if you are walking nearby, otherwise forget going there.

It's located next to the central post office. Because it's under construction, we couldn't go inside, and could only admire the exterior. Churches are definitely more enjoyable when you can see the inside. I hope the construction is finished so we can see the interior.
I didn't really find anything interesting about the church. Perhaps it's because we've seen too many church's recently or could have been the weather - so hot and humid, we couldn't be bothered.

As of December 23, 2018, it is under construction. Believers are allowed inside. You can take pictures from the outside. The Ho Chi Minh Central Post Office is right across the street, so you can visit that as well.
Just passed by and had some clicks on the way to Saigon river. Many tourists were here as it was December 19... all the preparations of xmas and new year on its way.

It's just a place to take photos. It's one of the few tourist attractions and is located right next to the post office, so there are a lot of tourists. However, aside from taking pictures, it isn't particularly impressive. It would be good to visit Book Street right next door as well.
We were unlucky; it was under renovation. The storefront, however, is very pretty. It's opposite the central parking area.

I visited again many years later, and the church was under renovation, so I couldn't see its full beauty. However, tourists still flocked to visit, and unfortunately, I still couldn't go inside. It was a bit of a shame.

As noted by others, the Notre Dame Cathedral is undergoing renovations. I was not allowed to enter when I was there on a weekday. This being the case, I was not impressed when just looking at a building sandwiched by scaffolding under a gray sky. I've been to Europe though.
There are certainly more fascinating cathedrals. It was closed. The post office opposite is nice, though. Lots of traffic.

The cathedral, of little interest, is closed for restoration. However, the central post office, much more interesting, is right next door, and nearby there's a small street where you can buy all sorts of books, new and used.
It is a place you will pass by anyway, even if you do not go out of your way to visit it. Since tourist spots are clustered nearby, I think it would be good to combine them into a single trip.
Excited to see the views of the church there unfortunately it is under renovation. U guys might take the statue outside. its worth it
When I went, it was under construction or something, so I couldn't go inside and only managed to get photos of the exterior... Even though I couldn't go inside, the exterior was beautiful.

The cathedral was closed for renovations, but even granting that (I've seen interior photos online: fairly bland, especially if you've already seen the original in Paris, as I have), the structure itself is nothing special. Overall, its rating mirrors that of the city, which honestly seems vastly overrated. If you're in Ho Chi Minh City, go see it, but don't expect too much.
Unfortunately the church was under repair and cleaning so access was limited you can view only from the outside. But still a cute miniature of the Paris church.
Really wanted to see the church from inside unfortunately its under construction and you cant go in.. ask if its now before you go there..

It's simply not worth seeing. When we went, it was covered in scaffolding. It has nothing to do with the real Notre Dame. I was disappointed, and it's given far more hype than it deserves. If I were passing by, I'd take a look, but I wouldn't make a point of going there.
Went to visit only to find out that it is under renovations for a significantly long period of time. Outside viewing only available.

You can not visit inside at present unless you attend mass.  There is much scaffolding so not attractive for photography. However it is an interesting area to visit and a short walk from the hotel we stayed in.  Observing the traffic and negotiating crossing the roads is all part of the HCMC experience. It did not bother me, but you do need to be hyper alert and find the spaces.

The cathedral is currently being restored. There is not much to see. It’s not nearly as impressive as its sister in Paris. I wouldn’t recommend a special trip to find it, but you really can’t miss it because we passed it several times just walking around the city.

For now the Cathedral is covered by a large wall around it with lots of support to prevent things falling down. And this has been so for the last 2 years. If HCMC wants to please visitors, they should start fixing those remarkable objects, not cover it up and let it simmer away. I know, large part of the city are in disarray because of the metro construction. Let it not be a reason to postpone restoration of the Cathedral as such old buildings will be very difficult to restore once structural damage occurs by neglecting maintenance.
Most of the Cathedral has been boarded up for repairs.  Not woth the taxi trip as you cannot see a great deal

I went to see the stained glass, but it was under construction and completely inaccessible. I wouldn't recommend walking there just for this church right now. However, there's a post office right next door, so if you visit both, they make a nice photo spot.

It was under construction and we couldn't go inside, so this review is based on the exterior. Nothing particularly noteworthy. The architecture is different from the rest of the city, but since the city itself is so diverse in terms of styles, it's not really surprising... At least from the outside, it's not worth going to see it.

Notre Dame is covered in scaffolding. In the middle of Saigon, a remnant of the French era. On the square, a beautiful view with the old post office on the right and the church directly in front of you. Traffic rushes chaotically around you. You cross at risk of your own life. A magnificent scene in this metropolis of allure.

Quite dispensable, and besides, it was under construction, surrounded by scaffolding. Compared to any other European cathedral, it has nothing unique about it. If you happen to be passing by, you might glance at it and take a photo; I doubt anyone goes there specifically to see it.
We were there in August and stumbled upon it while strolling around; we were going to visit the post office, which is lovely. But frankly, the cathedral isn't worth it. It's small.

This is a ripoff of a masterpiece in Paris. There was nothing entertaining about it and it is constantly under construction. You can look for a bit but then hoards of guides and people selling things swarm you so you end up sprinting away as fast as possible.
Very beautiful from the outside, but you cant go inside and the building is covered in scaffolding at this time of year. Worth a look if you are in the area, but don’t go out of your way.
The church is under renovation...it's impossible to visit and the scaffolding ruins the photo...we did appreciate the period photos at the foot of the gates.
Currently under construction with the majority of the cathedral hidden. However, it’s not unlike any others I’ve seen.

Yes, this cathedral, built between 1877 and 1883, is actually quite beautiful, but somehow it's not all that overwhelming. We wouldn't make a special trip to see it again, but if it's on the way, we'd certainly take a quick look. Well, as Europeans, we've probably seen similar things before.
Even in this communist country, the Catholic Church is showing narrow-mindedness by refusing access to this building.

Interesting French copy of the Norte Dam building. However on our visit it was closed for repair inside and outside. I would have like to have seen more as this city is heavily influenced by the French.
Wouldn't recommend this place to see. There were renovation works happening when we visited. We got a photo and that was it.
Currently, the church is under construction and you don’t see much from the outside. Just come back anytime later...
A very beautiful cathedral from the outside; unfortunately, it was closed to the public to see the inside. It's located right next to the old post office.
Went there on a Sunday morning. It’s undergoing restorative work. People manning the cathedral gate was very rude when they turned tourists away from the premise...
Closed and hidden behind scaffolding. No access available which for a place of worship appears contradictory. Nothing special in exterior design. Saved by the post office next door.

Went to visit on 1st June and there is a fence all the way around and scaffolding etc around the site. This makes taking photos challenging. visiting is possibly still worth it but you wont get the look you would expect.

Except for the fact that it's Catholic in a country that's 95% Buddhist, its only real appeal lies in its distant resemblance to its namesake in Paris, due to the side towers. But they're not even remotely similar, as these towers end in points. It's one of the city's few attractions, and you'd obviously have to fill a half-day city tour to see it. It's as skippable as the Post Office across the street.

Visited when I first got into Saigon. Beautiful cathedral on the outside, couldn't go in as it's under construction. Also had a lot of scaffolding and fences around. Ok to visit as long as you don't want to go in.

You can take a few nice pictures out front. Closed to public for construction. There is a nice statue to take a photo with. Also right across the street from the post office. Two spots in one stop. Lots of cabs available outside post office
Maybe couldn’t enter because of construction or mass. I wasn’t sure which they were telling us. Looked ok from outside but I’ve seen a lot of churches at this point in my travels.
Nice enough church undergoing renovations, but doesn't look any more special than thousands of Catholic churches and cathedrals around the world.
Largely covered in scaffolding and not open to visitors in next few months. Nice from outside, bell ringing is lovely and adjacent post office is worth a look.

The uninteresting brick building was surrounded by blue fences because it was under construction. Furthermore, the original stained-glass windows of Charyres were destroyed during the war. The only noteworthy thing was hearing the eight o'clock bells as we returned from a tiring day of shopping.

Our first visit to Saigon a few years ago allowed us to visit this lovely cathedral located in the very center of the city, a building constructed using bricks imported from Toulouse. This year, unfortunately, visits were impossible due to the construction work and scaffolding completely surrounding the building.
We walked around this cathedral as it is currently closed or was anyway on the day we were there. It's impressive and very french. It's worth a visit particularly if it's open
Renovation work prevents all visits; there's not much to see from the outside. It's right next to the post office, which is worth a visit.

You can see the church from the outside only as it’s being refurbished. We had trekked to see the church , but it was a waste of time as it’s covers in scaffolding at the moment and we could not see anything at all .

We visited yesterday. There's a huge amount of scaffolding on one side of the cathedral which was a shame as you could not go in. They have also put gates up to stop you going in with big signs which spoil the view slightly. I'm sure it is lovely without the construction - just be aware at the moment!
It's a nice church to see. It's being restored this year so you can't go in. It's next to the post office. If you are in the neighbourhood it's nice to see. I would not go there just to see it.
I wish I could have gone inside, but the exterior was under construction and the door was closed. Maybe it was because I visited during the holidays.

They are renovating the church (January 2018), so there wasn't much to see. Moreover, we weren't allowed inside at the time; no idea if this is always the case or not. It is located opposite the post office, so you can combine the two.

It's just a cathedral and nothing special comparing with European counterparts. Perhaps if you're visiting from an Asian country or a place where Christian religions are less prominent it would be worth a visit.

The Cathedral is under major renovation, covered in scaffolding, and is inaccessible to tourists at this time and for some time to come. Go to the old Post Office across the street in lieu of your cathedral visit.
You can walk around the outside of this cathedral but not get too close as it is surrounded by scaffolding and temporary walls. The inside is closed and you cannot currently visit it.
It is covered in scaffolding, and that makes it less attractive. Saigon in the center is excellent for walking, by the way.
It didn't look pretty because there was construction going on right away. I don't know how long it will last, but construction structures were visible from the front, side, and back. It was a shame.

It seems to have been under construction for a long time, and the information from previous visitors was no different from what I had read. However, the lack of information from the travel agency regarding the completion date and other details left me with a rather unhelpful impression, making it an unpleasant experience and a bit disappointing.

Apparently it is restored every 20 years and is currently covered in scaffolding so didn’t see it at its best, but still a lovely building and worth a wonder past. Didn’t go in as couldn’t find an entrance open.
Miniature version of the original building. Building is currently undergoing restoration, so closer visitations are restricted.
As of the end of December... it is literally under construction. You can't even go inside~ It's right in front of the post office, so let's take a look around together.
We were rather disappointed that we could not enter. Neither the hotel nor our Uber driver mentioned this. Please call ahead before you visit.

Anyone who's ever stood in front of Notre Dame will be truly disappointed here. Compared to the original, it's tiny and nowhere near as imposing. What really surprised me was the condition of the building. With the environmental and air pollution in Vietnam, everything is usually damaged and blackened. Not so with this recently restored building (it was still completely covered in scaffolding on one side). Is it worth seeing? You practically walk right past it in the city center, but I wouldn't make a special trip there.
Can't visit the interior since its still under renovation. Hope to see it during the next visit. Took photos from outside.

Went on a Sunday. Couldn’t even go inside. No idea how it works - if we are only able to go in during mass, or what..? No instructions also. Everyone stayed outside took picture within 5 mins proceeded to post office.

Just across the road from the Central Post Office, so we popped over for quick photos outside. Do be careful of touts who offer to take pictures for you and then attempt to charge. A selfie stick would be best (though I'd be cautious of people snatching your phone).

The inside is not open during renovations and the outside is not spectacular. It's near the War Remnants museum, so a short walk. Beware of rickshaw drivers in this area, the prices are not what they state and they are aggressive.
Small, not a beauty and actually closed because of renovations. But some groups got the permission to go in. Could not get an answer, why.

I went at the end of November, and unfortunately, the exterior was under maintenance (or perhaps the interior was also under maintenance?). The entire church was fenced off, so I could only take pictures from a distance. It looked very old, probably quite old. I suggest checking the maintenance schedule before you go, but since the Central Post Office is right across the street, visiting the Notre-Dame Basilica on the way is fine.
Just another building. Not worth gping out of the way to see. Prettier buildings around including the hotels.

Don't make the trip to the cathedral right now, as it's under construction. Between the hoardings and scaffolding, it's impossible to get a clear view of the exterior. It's also impossible to get inside.
Such a shame that only the exterior could be seen and even parts of that were shrouded in scaffolding. I was amused by the sign at the front door that I have posted.
We were looking forward to visiting Notre Dame but it was closed for renovation! No mention of that when we booked the trip by the operator - very disappointing!!

After having been told by everyone to go and see the Saigon Notre Dame I did. It is pretty cool to see, but I wouldn't walk too far out of your way to see it. If you want to see amazing architecture, it's got to be the real Notre Dame
The only special thing about this cathedral is that it's located in Vietnam. Otherwise, we in Europe are used to something different.
Closed during our stay in Ho Chi Minh. Not much to see unless you particularly like churches. Centrally located.
The church is nice – nothing more. You can easily take a look at it in passing and don't need to make a conscious effort to visit it.
It was closed because of renovation. Could only see the outside. But for sure after they are finished it will be worth a visit. Just beside the historic post office.

Nearly 100 years of French rule have produced a poor copy of Notre Dame in Paris. This does not do France credit. Frankly...it's a truly ugly construction, and even the bricks used for the facade are of poor quality. It's a shame because it's located right in the city center and doesn't look good in the urban context.

Since there are set entry times, it is difficult to view the interior. Unlike European churches or cathedrals, do not expect ornate stained glass or magnificent sculptures. It is simply a place to get a sense of the exterior. If you have seen photos, you do not necessarily need to visit. Because entry is difficult unless you arrive at the right time, viewing it from a passing car is sufficient.
As a tourist, you can only view the cathedral from the outside. Because while it's being renovated (for another two years), entry for tourists is prohibited. Very sad.

The outside still looks to be a major tourist attraction as you will find many tourist taking photos of the building. Unfortunately it is closed for renovations so we were unable to go inside. Don't go out of your way to find it if our not close by until it is open to the public again.

Nice building but as yet I have not been able to get inside. They have recently put a barrier around the side of the building on the post office side which tends to obscure the view further however, it shouldn't spoil your visit.
Not amazing at all. Stop by if you're passing by, it won't cost you anything. A little disappointing. Looks really nice.

Like many of Saigon's historic buildings it is in a state of some decrepitude- and while it appears to be closed for renovations I assume - this wasn't clear and no works were taking place. Worth a look as you wander by but not the most interesting building church.
It is surrounded by fences due to construction work. Unfortunately, it was closed, so we couldn't get in. The reason is unclear, and there are no times posted outside the closed gate. A shame.
At present it is under refurbishment so exterior is only visible. But post office is worth a visit next door.
It can be seen while visiting the central post office; it's very close. I couldn't enter it because of renovations.
It's a famous tourist spot, but it was under renovation. I'll look forward to visiting it next time. There's a post office next door, so I'll head over there.

Being used to European churches and cathedrals, this one is nothing special at all. Worth a quick look from the outside and inside but not a place where you will spend more than 10-15 minutes. The cathedral is nicely situated at a larger square and close to the post office (which is also worth a short stop).
In my opinion, a visit to this place is not at all essential. Unfortunately, I didn't find it particularly charming.
There's nothing special here, just the building and a small garden in front of it, which locals often use for photography, especially newlyweds.

The cathedral is closed for re furnishings- how long and when it will open again is unsure.  Out side the cathedral is a true copy of the one in Paris. But sometimes in the 1900 they put the top on each tower an that changed the building quite a lot. The stones to the cathedral is made and transported all the way from Paris.

I originally planned to visit the post office as well, but when I arrived, I found that it was under renovation. Friends who are planning to visit should check the opening hours and whether there is any construction going on beforehand to avoid making a wasted trip.

I expected a larger building, and was disappointed with the building. I would describe it as a mini Notre Dame, most disappointing, it was constructed of new materials. No comparison to the Notre Dame, not in the same league. The building is OK, next to central Post Office so see both at same time. The feeling of awe at the original Notre Dame is not present at this place.

I went to see it because I think it's one of Ho Chi Minh City's most iconic buildings, but I couldn't go inside (it seemed to be under construction), so all I could do was take pictures. It's a shame. My opinion might change if I could see the inside...
Disappointing that access is not available due to construction work and hoardings around building limiting access.
The walls were covered in various graffiti, which was a bit of a letdown. I couldn't go inside.

We couldn't go inside the cathedral so ur pictures are all of the outside. Nothing special to me. Would have liked to go inside and tour the cathedral but it wasn't an option. U could pass by on car or bus and wouldn't miss out
It's cordoned off for construction, and you can only take a photo from the statue. It's a shame, because it looks beautiful.

It was announced in English last Sunday to please come and celebrate St. Ignatius of Loyola's day on Monday, but because it was in Vietnamese, non-Vietnamese were not allowed in....I am wondering why it was announced nor why was it not made clear that the mass would only be in Vietnamese and only Vietnamese would be allowed to enter? I will talk to the very nice priest about it next weekend. He speaks fluent English and is very good.

Overall... an impressive building, but entry is prohibited. The place was under renovation. Again, this is one of those sites next to each other that you pass on a city tour. I enjoyed walking around it. The building itself is nice... the least interesting thing to see is it (what is Europe here?)
Cant go in, government are restoring it. Don't know when they will re open but not really worth the walk just for the outside
If your goal for the day is to visit the cathedral, you're out of luck. It's closed for renovations. You can only see the facade.

Unfortunately the Saigon Notre Dame Cathedral was closed. Not sure if there are renovating? The building is amazing from the outside, shame we couldn't access the inside.  It's straight across the road from the post office.

Went to the cathedral today but apparently due to loud Chinese tourists found out that entry is restricted during services so was unable to get in. Good picture opportunities outside. Tourist police are very helpful in helping tourist get across the street.

......was there question my wife was asked as she went through the gate separating the tourists from those wanting to pray.  Really? If you are passing pop in, but the outside is much nicer than the inside, especially applicable to the woman who asked that question.

This old church built in the French style is nothing like the one in Paris. The inside is dark and drab. There is nothing striking inside. If you have visited churches throughout Europe, you will be disappointed.

It's a strange place. I tried to get in twice, but it was either the service or something else. The opening hours are short and inconvenient. In short, it's not the most welcoming place, and it's not geared toward tourists.
Not much to see, not much to do. Its an averagly sized church surrounded by busy streets. Go next door for the far more interesting and functional post office.
Large brick built edifice with limited decoration to which access is limited to entrance area. Missable.
Nothing amazing as title says. Seen better churches in other parts of the world. Wouldnt be in any rush to see it again.

Quite a spectacular building opposite the Saigon Post Office. We could go just inside the main door but not fully inside which was a big disappointment. An opportunity to take a few photo's outside but that was it unfortunately.
A bland building both inside and outside. Not able to go past the rear of the seating, . Very few items of beauty to be seen. In my opinion not worth a visit.

We have visited there part of our arranged city tour and unfortunately it was Sunday and we couldn't enter to the "Norte Dame Cathedral" because Sunday there is prayer session for catholic people so if you are catholic only you can enter to the cathedral. I think this place you must visit on week day not on a week end. Architecture of the cathedral 18th century French architecture as far as I learned.
As a catholic, they wouldn't allow us in during holy week as we were tourists. Too discriminatory! Go, take a picture and leave.  Try it, you might like it!
Not very interesting since you can't really visit the inside. It's a church like many we have back home.

We sought this building out after reading about on trip advisor. It was on our walk to the museum. We saw it from the street and were it impressed enough to bother exploring further or take a photo. I wouldn't go back and I would only recommend to walk by our drive by in a taxi as you go to Something else.

We went 2 times to the kathedral for vissit, we liked the mis but it was closet on sunday. The day After we get back and you guess, closed.... So it was not an amazing building on the outside and the inside, we don't no!

The building has its charm, but visitors are confined to the entrance. The guards protecting the monument are the most aggressive we encountered in Vietnam... bare female shoulders are strictly forbidden. One can understand the reasoning, but there really is a way to make it clear. A shame.

My husband and I visited on March.5th which was a Sunday. We went there as part of a self guided walking tour in the Lonely Planet guide book.  We took some photos from the outside and was looking forward to seeing the inside. It was very crowded outside the cathedral and when we got to the entrance we were turned away with a simple no and wave of a hand.  At first we thought it was because they were doing some crowd control, only letting in a certain amount of people at a time but we then realised that they were only letting people in who were there for mass/to pray.  It was disappointing that we couldn't go in. We could only see a little bit of the inside through the railings. There were no times printed in the guidebook for what times mass is on and there was no schedule for mass at the actual cathedral itself.  If we had walked there especially, not as part of an overall walk I would have been even more disappointed. As others have said, not really worth the time. Perhaps visitors have more luck during weekdays.
Really not worth seeing. The spot is extremely crowded and full of wedding photographers. The cathedral inside looks like a regular European church.
This cathedral is truly very ugly, so it's absolutely not worth visiting. Moreover, when we went, it was even closed! But seeing it from the outside, you'll be convinced that it's not worth visiting.
The church is beautiful from the outside, but what a disappointment when you go inside... brown is the dominant color, and there's no decoration to draw the eye. Ultimately, it's rather sad to see.

This is a magnificent building with a significant history. You may enter by the west doors but your visit is limited to about 5 meters in front of the doors meaning you can not even enter the nave of the church. The altar can be viewed from 100+ metres. What a disappointment - a waste of time to visit! I make these remarks as a keen enthusiast of church architecture. It is shameful that the church does not allow visitors when it has such a magnificent building.

It says something about the presence of the French, but not much more. You are not allowed to go much further than the portal to take a few photos. Fortunately, you can visit the nearby post office at the same time. It is much better to tour the city's Chinese quarter.

After visiting many beautiful churches throughout Europe I had expected this one to be of similar grandeur. But it's not. Just a shadow of the great churches in other countries. I'm sure it has significance, but I hardly feel it deserves as much popularity as it gets. This a not a "must see destination" in Saigon. The alleys and streets are much more interesting than this average church. Walk by it while you explore the city centre and that's all you need.
A bastion built outside of time and history, as void of expression as its interior is bare and cold. A colonial power transplant that hasn't taken root, and perhaps shouldn't have taken root.

There are not many sights to see in Saigon, thus you are compelled to pass through the Notre Dame Cathedral. It is quite a fault to compare the cathedral in Paris and this one. Do not go unless you just love churches.
Not impresive, not beutifull, inside is really nothing to see. Bun its just at thw side of the post office, so if you have extra time, you can walk around
After the gaudy and Gaudi (if you get my meaning) delights of Spain and South America, this is a decidedly dowdy cathedral.
Nice to see if you're there to see the post office anyway - not something you need to spend a lot of time on.

Why would you visit a French building in Vietnam? We booked the excursion in combination with the presidential palace, the post office, and the museum. But the Cu Chi tunnels are more impressive, should you want to choose.
Nice building but not too much you can really see or do. Walked in and out in about 5 minutes. Nice part of town

It's got a great name taken from the Paris cathedral but that's where it ends. It's a small church in reality with no great external features or architecture. The opening times are a bit of a joke as it seems to be shut mostly. It's disappointing, don't make it a special journey but you can include it in your walk as the post office is opposite and you can carry on to the war museum and the palace.
This is an attraction I could have easily skipped. The Cathedral was not really that great compared to Cathedrals in other areas of the world.

Back in the day you'd glimpse the two towers at various points from the Mekong delta as you sailed into Saigon. Today it's dwarfed by the bustling, burgeoning high risen city. That's diminished the site and the sight. Shame really but that's modern development for you. Once there there's space to move around it, manicured space with way too many tourists feeling they have to pay a visit; hawkers, hustlers and herds abound. Inside its quiet, plain and very fourth division when compared to the glory and pageantry of the numerous and colourfully packed temples and pagoda's of HCMC. It has a faded beauty and simplicity and a short but significant history. But you are left feeling 'so what' and that's a shame but with a minority 7% stake in a country where over 80% don't believe in a god it's hardly surprising.
Looks a beautiful building from the outside but unfortunately when we visited it was closed was real!y disappointed as wanted to look around the inside .

More talk. An old crumbling building in the center of a small square, I don't know what it is that excites people. We didn't go inside, I don't even know if you can. On the right is the post office building, though it's geared towards tourists. There are a million different shops there, you can find cool souvenirs. We bought some old coins from the colonial era as a souvenir, and some T-shirts, as usual. We didn't buy any pictures of the church itself, we took pictures nearby and that's enough. Across the street is a McDonald's and a Dunkin' Donuts, where you can eat inexpensively. If we hadn't absolutely had to be nearby, we wouldn't have even thought about coming here. Generally speaking, Saigon is only worth visiting if you're passing through or on a business trip; there's absolutely nothing to do there with a family.

But it was all locked up with fences and we couldn't get in. It is in a good location and a lovely building but can't comment on the inside. There was no information about opening times either. It looked properly shut?!
Lovely to look at but you can't go inside which is a pity. This beautiful building is rich in history and architecture just a pity we couldn't get in
The exterior looks quite nice, but that is about it. Inside, you won't find anything beyond a fence. Let me put it nicely: It is a functional church. Nothing more.
I may be biased as I have been to Notre Dam in Paris so this is somewhat underwhelming compared. Even if you have visited any cathederals you are not going to get too excited by what you see here.

After passing the test to prove our Catholicism, we were allowed into a disappointingly bare and architecturally dull church. Visited during the Christmas season, we couldn't get close to the nativity scene; they demanded an admission fee. The only thing that was present was a large TV mounted on each column of the church. You can easily skip the visit.
We could not get in, but we peeked in the doors and what we saw in a church. Nothing like St Patrick's Cathedral for example. Not sure if it's a necessary thing to do.
Couldn't do much as everything inside was blocked off. Maybe because it was during the Christmas period. Literally spent 5 minutes.

Can't compare to the one in Paris, "by a long shot". Much less interesting, inside and outside. No admittance after 4 pm. Across the street from the Post Office, so two tourist attractions in one place.

A religious organization in a communist country that has survived wars, Revolution, and regime changes is something to admire regardless of beliefs. As a structure, the church is pretty but common to so many other Catholic Churches built more that a 100 years ago scattered across the globe.
It's definitely a nice building but not a must see. However, it's close to the "cluster" of other attractions so worth a visit when you're nearby.

Closed, completely shut. A house of worship closing its doors. Unbelievable, where is this going? A plain, ordinary church with one nice detail. Couples in love are writing their names on the wall with markers and then taking selfies. The church also seems to be in need of maintenance. You can just walk around it.
If you decided to visit the central post office then you will have more photo opportunities across the road at the cathedral. Nice piece of history and architecture

Without trying to sound arrogant or disrespectful, this is not a patch on the original in Paris. That said, when doing a walking tour of the main attractions in Saigon, you will inevitably walk past this anyway.  I can't say much more but I guess if you want to see some beautiful churches, go to Europe - South East Asia is more about the temples.
The cathedral is nothing special, similar to the one in Paris. Probably a rest of French imperialism, but nothing typical for sure
Hmhm...what can I say...as I am coming from Europe, where you can find doses of breathtaking cathedrals there is nothing to write home about in this case

From an historical point of view the presence of this Victorian French building is of interest but it is not a thing of beauty. It was a 20 minute visit. It is clearly on the tourist trail but I am not sure why (even to me, a Roman Catholic).

It turned out the church could only be viewed for a limited time due to a wedding and was subsequently closed, even though the opening hours sign indicated that you should be able to enter. Eventually, I was able to take a look, and it was very disappointing. A rather bare church, and that is putting it mildly.

I came here as it was on a list of things to see. To be honest I was disappointed. The decor isn't particularly good and you couldn't see the whole Cathedral when I was there as it was roped off  By all means go in if you're in the area. But I wouldn't recommend specifically going there

You will probably pass by this walking if you venture into the streets. The area around the cathedral is more interesting. A lot of people gather there, and is near the post office which is worth seeing. As someone said, it is surprising that this cathedral built by the French is not grander. I found watching the motorbikes navigate the streets nearby more interesting.
If you're european or have been to europe, this cathedral will seem as a regular church in any town. Nothing special.
This is directly opposite the post office so we called in. It's okay, nothing compared to cathedrals in other cities
It's not really of any architectural interest, but it's right in front of the post office, so you can see it in passing.
Seen it, walked past. Didn't really think too much of it tbh. It's pretty but it's nothing too out of this world exciting
Not really a big church fan, but this ranks as one of the most boring. Fine architecture, from the outside it's impressive, inside its unadorned and plain.

Really nice building that gives the impression of French history. But inside only 1/5 of the church is accessible and it was not particularly impressive. A lot of traffic around the church. Close to the post office.

Don't pay for a tour to go here. Just walk around the city center and stop for a quick visit here and at the post office just across the street. We spend approximately 5 minutes on both. If you been to some of the major churches etc in Europe this is nothing..
Here is a church that immediately stands out because it is nicely situated and looks nice from the outside. Inside it is somewhat dark.

I went to see it because it's across from the central post office. The church is only open to the public at set times. There were stained glass windows and such, but I don't think it's worth making a special trip for.
Like any church in Europe. Quite dilapidated but apparently still in use, services in English on Sundays
Don't even try to get inside! Don't ruin your experience. Why do you think they only let you in during certain hours? It's to protect you from disappointment.
No write-up on its history whatsoever. It's a good photo spot tho. Must beware of donut 'seller', looks scam-ish.

As a Belgian, I have visited hundreds of churches in the past. This was just one of many. Not impressive at all. Every city or decent municipality in our country has a church that certainly does not fall short.
Ok from the outside couldn't get in. It's on a busy intersection so if photos is what you're looking for be prepared to wait and snap quickly.
This church doesn't really belong here, so incredibly French and closed as well; that shouldn't be the case for a Catholic church.
Definitely something familiar in a place of differences. Right next door to the post office and a small almost missable McDonalds. A place where plenty of visitors are keen to take pictures.

From what we read it was one of the most important sights in Saigon. We went there and it was closed (we went a bit earlier than noon on a Saturday). From outside its not a beautiful cathedral, it's made of bricks and all the latetal walls are full of graffiti and white writings which I did not like at all.  Hopefully inside God has something better in store for us.
The provincial church is a total disappointment; there's nothing to see. Don't waste your time going inside if you're going to the area; just seeing it from the outside is more than enough.
Although the cathedral was open to public, rather disappointing that it was nothing to see and no lights inside.
For a European tourist, this building won't be anything special. Nothing special. Moreover, if you want to go inside, check the opening hours beforehand, as the cathedral closes mid-day.
Nothing really special, quite central, nice square to the front and buildings around about. Went to see it because it was local to my hotel otheriwse would not have bothered.

It's a really annoying place during the day—hot and nothing to see. At night, it's decent for taking photos because of the night view, but I don't know why anyone goes there during the day. It's just a place to take a few pictures.

It's impossible to visit anything other than the entrance. The square is pretty, but the cathedral itself is less impressive compared to the other jewels of France :) Nevertheless, it's always funny to see it here.
Well then... a relatively small church compared to the original. Admittedly, it's quite amusing in this setting, but nothing more. Anyone interested should definitely check out the original ;)

walked all my way to see 'Saigon Notre Dame Cathedral' and was utterly disappointed. Maybe coming from Europe, i have seen enough of these architecture to get wowed.  Its nothing special, just a french style church in Ho Chi Minh. Its next to independence palace so if you are there, why not (tick it).

Not worth a special trip to see. It was closed when I got there. Mainly there were couples talking their wedding photos. It's across from the old post office so if you're going you can see both at the same time.

We went here on a tour, but were not allowed inside. Hmmm. We've been to the 'real' Notre Dame, so this was it's poor cousin. Loads of people taking pictures. Lots of tourist vehicles to dodge. I suppose you have to go here whilst in HCM - but allow 15-30mins.
Just a place. Worth seeing but just an average 'copy' of the real Cathedral. Not much to see there. Don't know if you can get in inside. When I came it was closed.

First, I went to see the night view (around 7:00 PM on Saturday). It was beautifully lit up. I heard that the bricks were brought in from France. On another day (before 4:00 PM on Monday), I made it in time for opening hours and went inside. When I went inside, the passageway was closed, so I could only look from the back. Just as I was relieved to be able to go in, an older woman who seemed to be an employee came over to complain. With a face full of displeasure, she told me to take off my hat. (Her tone wasn't a warning, but more like she was venting her frustrations.) I apologized sincerely and took off my hat. However, that wasn't the end of her complaints. She gestured and complained that I had just walked past her. I hadn't ignored her when she called out to me. It seemed she was unhappy that I had already passed her by the time she thought to call out to me. I think it was only natural that I was reprimanded for violating the church's rule that women are not allowed to wear hats (some churches don't have this rule, so it would be nice if they wrote it down). However, I was quite annoyed when I was reprimanded for walking past that woman quickly. A group of Koreans and a group of Japanese tourists who came in after me were also scolded for wearing hats. For some reason, two women wearing black caps (I don't know which country they were from) were not reprimanded. The way they were reprimanded (scolded) was quite surprising, so please be sure to take off your hats before entering. I read that the stained glass inside is beautiful, but perhaps due to the weather or the reprimand I received, I didn't feel that way. Unfortunately, I didn't feel any grandeur or sacredness either, and the exterior was much more beautiful.
You can visit the beautiful view with the statue of Mary in front. It's perfect for taking photos. However, when I visited, the cathedral was closed. Apparently, it's only open on certain days.
Crowded with tourist, and unobstructed photo opportunities is limited.  Watch out for the local taxi approaching you.
Really a disappointing experience as most of it is blocked off so really just a few photo opportunities and that's about it.

I was expecting more. You were not allowed to go past the lobby unless ypu were praying or attending the service. My understanding is the church eas built for the French Occupiers. I would skip it, unless churches are really your thing. I was in and out in 5 minutes. The court yard had more beauty than the inside of the church.
The building is closed, so you have to make do with the exterior. The history surrounding it is interesting. The building itself is not.
Maybe it was under renovation when we were there today since we see fences all around it. Just took some pictures and decided to move on.
Sadly the name wrote a cheque that the building couldn't cash.  Its an interesting and different building, but its not great inside and surrounded by beggars and hawkers
Touted as Notre Dame I saw nothing even reminiscent of Paris. Beggars at the door and lots of tour groups.
An ordinary church; the exterior is truly unremarkable, and the interior isn't worth the visit either. Keep walking.
Nothing special and you need to cover yourself up. The outside was boring and inside was nothing interesting to see.

Oh well! What can I say? The outside is worth the photos we took, but inside? Who's seen it??? We went three times and each time it closed at different times!!! The security guard on our third and final attempt was incredibly rude!!! I was 10 minutes from closing time (on Sunday, a church closes at 10:30: it closes because they've boarded everything up!!!) and he absolutely wouldn't let us in. Tip: There's so much more to see in Saigon, you can even skip the cathedral!

Following a failed visit due to the opening hours, when I actually got to enter this cathedral it was very disappointing. There is only a very small area to visit with the vast majority of the building being reserved for prayers (during alloted service times). They do offer a variety of regular services, including in English.  All in, not one to visit unless you are going to partake in a service.
You can't go inside, and it's a replica of Notre Dame Cathedral in France. It's worth taking pictures of from the outside, but unfortunately, you can't go in.
It's not even comparable to European cathedrals. The building itself—especially the facade—is more interesting than the interior.
There are so many more beautiful places. In my opinion, it's not really worth going there, apart from the fact that it was built by the French?
You only get to go 5 meters inside. Not particularly exciting. If you are really into cathedrals go here but otherwise you'll find better use of your time by visiting almost any cathedral in Europe.

I'm giving it a rating of 2 because I couldn't go inside and there was broken glass, etc. It's a shame because the exterior was nice. I found out later that you can go inside. Maybe I just had bad timing.

I have seen a lot of churches all over Europe and even outside, but compared to that the exterior is a nice copy of the Paris church but the Saigon equivalent had a very sober (dull) interior. It was a one minute visit for me.
A nice church for Asians but not compared to some of the churches in Germany, United Kingdom, France and Italy.

Upon turning up to see Notre Dame's Asian counterpart shortly after lunch, we discovered that the place has the rather strange opening time of 15:00. As a result, we were forced to wait out an hour or so in a nearby bistro to prepare for the opening.  When we eventually got to the inside of the cathedral, we didn't think it was particularly worth the wait. Essentially, visitors are allowed to stand in a small channel at the end of the hall and take a photo down the main room. That is it.  At least the entry is free, but even the inside is not particularly impressive - especially when one compares it inevitable to the original Notre Dame in Paris. People are allowed to actually go in, but only to pray. I fail to see why the rest of us cannot appreciate a historic site, and a culturally fascinating one at that, just because of a minority cult's misplaced belief.

The church is nice, but we have hundreds of them in Europe that are more beautiful. The floor, however, is very beautiful. The best part is the square in front of the church, where one future couple after another takes photos. Great for people-watching.
Walked past this building twice without realising it was indeed the cathedral we were seeking. Smaller than its EU counterparts, but pretty, esp. when viewed from the opera house side.

Looks huge but is actually very small inside. Not all that much to see. The only time we saw a 'charity' person was outside who you had no choice but to pass on entry. My partner took pity on him not having any arms and gave him 20AUD but I reminded him that probably 95% went to the government!
You can go in the cathedral, but just only the front entrance. Not as spectacular as the one in Paris! But you can take some nice pictures from the outside.
The cathedral is located towards the shopping mall and post office and is nice to have a place in the photo album.

Yes, another church. For those who have seen churches around the world, there's not much to be excited about except for the fact that this is a church in Vietnam, and it doesn't have a unique name. We only saw the building from the outside because it was closed.
Nothing like the one in Paris, I don't remember exactly if it's only open in the morning or only in the afternoon, but what is certain is that it's not open all day.
well, I was there at night but certainly did not experience anything special.... so maybe if during the day and with a guide the experience could be different

Expected more after it being touted by hotels, on tours etc. It's a good looking but small church. Walked round the outside. Took a photo. 5 minutes later. Cool. Not so impressive for those from European countries I'd expect.

I didn't find the cathedral particularly remarkable; from the outside it looks like a 'normal' church/cathedral and the inside is no different. There is a guard standing there randomly approaching women about what they are wearing and whether they are allowed inside or not. If you have to travel a long distance, it is not worth the effort to visit. If you are in the area, it wouldn't hurt to go there.
I understand it's a historical building. But nothing to get exited over. Only worth visiting if convenient to do so.

Intriguing from the outside & known as a landmark, but unfortunately not worth it. A very plain church in terms of Windows, religious artefacts & internal architecture. Nothing special. Sadly I would not hurry back.

This is Notre Dame Cathedral, located right next to the Central Post Office. However, entry is blocked, and crossing requires jaywalking, which is extremely dangerous. Even if you manage to cross, the interior is closed, so unless you are particularly interested, taking a single photo from the outside should suffice.

There's really nothing much to see here. Some mosaics are nice, but you can't walk in and it's actually quite simple and plain in decor. Consider this a quick 2 min stop on the way over to the post office across the street.

Nothing much to see actually, the Notre Dame itself looks nice and fresh to Asian but not for the Westerners as the whole building is an French style architecture. Just take a photo or selfie in front of the Notre Dame and you may proceed to Central post office next
The only remarkable thing about this place is that it is still standing after the war.  It is surprising to see a Cathedral of this type in Asia but other than that it isn't of much interest.

While I appreciate that this is a replica of Notre Dame in Paris, it only had a very small visiting area and it was not possible to walk closer to the alter - only if you wished to pray. The opening times were limited to week days only

The church looks nice from the outside, but the interior is surprisingly dark and dimly lit, and not very spacious. There are few windows, and the stained glass is small and few in number, which is why it feels dark. The Chuo Ward Post Office is immediately to the left after leaving the church.

If you've been to any other cathedrals, this one is pretty plain. It looks like a fine place for prayer, but isn't distinguished as a destination to visit.  There is some beautiful stained glass work.

While Americans marvel at the old cathedral built by the French, we weren't particularly impressed. It's certainly interesting in its depths to find a cathedral in Asia modeled on European designs, but overall, it didn't exactly blow me away. There are far more beautiful and exciting buildings in Ho Chi Minh City!

This was a bit disappointing and we were glad we stumbled across it rather than making a special trip. You can only view from the outside and it's nothing like its name suggests. I was expecting a miniature version of its name sake but it's just another pretty church

While strolling past, it's certainly worth a visit, but it's not worth a special tour. It certainly doesn't compare to Notre Dame in Paris. We walked there in the evening, expecting it to be beautifully illuminated, but it wasn't. If you're looking at it, it's mostly during the day.
There was nothing grand or impressive about it.. If you are passing by, you could just stop and take a few pics..

I have seen many beautiful cathedrals and unfortunately this one is not quite like the great Notre Dame of Paris. It was not open on the day we walked there, so I'm glad we found our own way around Saigon and did not pay for a tour. But if you're in the area and it's open have a look inside because we couldn't.
We couldn't get in to see it, not sure when it is open. Part of our Cyclo Tour which was great! I would recommend a visit using this form of transport.

In terms of worldwide churches, this one isn't too special. Not knowing too much about any historical significance, I'm basing this off aesthetic alone and I've seen better churches in the streets of Sydney. A big ugly banner on the front of the church didnt help either.  Note: dont let 'tour guide' motorcyclists take you here. It's a ploy to amp up there hours to rip you off. Not worth it.
Very bland. The exterior is still worth seeing. The interior is completely uninteresting. The security staff were very rude and impertinent.

Colonization went hand in hand with Catholic missions: the army and the holy water sprinkler have always been a good match for destroying "native civilizations." Everywhere they went, they left us monuments of mediocrity, like that pretentious brick cathedral in Toulouse (as if making bricks in Vietnam were impossible) with its bland interior. Personally, it makes me uneasy to think of the unbearable vanity of our country, which claimed to bring Truth and Civilization. But is it really over?
The building itself wasn't anything special... But attending a mass in Vietnamese, now that was something else! And the newlyweds' photo shoot was something else too!
Beautiful area but the church is better from outside. Nothing special, anyway, for westerners. Too many tourists...to see what?
This church is not very interesting. Pass by it, take a picture and move on. The postal office is at the same square. If you are in the area go see them both.

I know it's been there for ages and had history. But we would have got the same experience out of it if we had driven around it once in a car. For me if its not open don't stop off there not much to see, read or do. We just walk straight over to the post office which is just 15 m across the road. Lots of Vietnamese get wedding photos at Norte Dame you'll probably see that.

It's hard to even call this a landmark. Vietnam, and Saigon in particular, is so unique and interesting, both in its distinctness and in its dissimilarity to Europe, that seeing this shameful imitation of European cathedrals was a complete disappointment.

Notre Dame was described as a must-see and is certainly something exotic for visitors from Asia, who are confronted with a multitude of temples here. For Europeans, it's just a church, and a very simple one at that... I found it very uninteresting; after two minutes we were back outside and went straight across the street to the post office.
A very nice building and well worth getting some snaps in front of. Nothing happening inside, and not the most decorated of insides. Well worth driving to and looking at but don't bother going in.

the public for some reason are only allowed in at certain times of the day and when closed several uniformed personel are obvious at the gates. We went after 3pm to find that you can only enter at the back of the building and once inside you can not walk any further in than about the first 3 or 4 yards as the place is blanked off. Nothing really inside worth looking at. The outside of the building is better.
We wanted to have a look around here but arrived just a little too late as it seems to close at 11am for a lunch break. The outside looks nice but there are more stunning things out there

First time we walked here it was closed (apparently between local siesta time of 11-2). I was decidedly underwhelmed (as I have been fortunate to have visited cathedrals in both France& Italy). My husband on the other hand said wow when he walked in, impressed with the height of the ceiling. Seriously if I'd missed visiting I wouldn't mind.

Neither from the outside (a reddish brick building) nor from the inside does this "cathedral" offer anything worth seeing – it is a simple church from the French colonial period and, for a Catholic church, surprisingly unadorned inside. While it perfectly fulfills its purpose, it is by no means interesting.

The cathedral may look impressive from the exterior but once you walk inside you will be sorely disappointed. The interior is bland with no decoration and you are not able to walk around.  The highlight will be if you happen to see some of the locals posing for their wedding photographs outside.  It may take you longer to cross the road from the central post office or the square than you spend here.  Disappointing
Neither beautiful nor impressive. Nothing to say about it, it's a church that's neither very old nor particularly special.
This brick building is reminiscent of the churches of the Southwest. It is certainly more vibrant today than French churches.

We couldn't go inside. There are plenty of cathedrals like this. Even though it harks back to French times, it's boring... There are prettier ones in Europe. Even Wawel Cathedral is more interesting...
Unless you are a church fanatic nothing special or interesting to be seen here. Could pop in for mass ifso inclined, in English some datys
The only interesting thing is the exterior...if you have little time in Saigon...you're better off just wandering the streets or visiting a museum.

There are plenty of such churches around the world, but there are so few monuments in Saigon that even a church like this could be considered an attraction here. You can see it, but only occasionally.

Not really worth the time. It it no way lives up to the name Notre Dame, so if you have seen its Parisian counterpart prepare to be underwhelmed - both from the outside and in. Not really worth the time to seek out and visit, but if you are passing (which you are likely to given its location) it is worth a 2 minute glance.
Nice enough to look at from the outside. Inside is just a standard church with nothing that makes it stand out.
Very little to see - inside plain though large. Can't walk past the first 15 feet of entering. Not worth a special visit but is next to the equally boring post office.

Quite nice exterior with a lot of red brick. However the interior is only open for very limited times during the day, so best to check before hand if you want to see inside. We did not know , so missed seeing inside..

Part of the Saigon tourist route. It might be of interest because there are few Catholic churches in Vietnam, but it's not particularly special... and since it's usually closed, I don't know if the interior offers anything interesting.
I dont see the great resemblance. I was somewhat dissapointed, even though it is a nice looking church.
Boring and uninteresting....most was inaccessible but dark, hot and lifeless. Definately not a must see in Saigon

The outlook architecture is very nice as well as inside. However that's about it. We are only allowed to view the inside and not allowed to go into the praying area. The visit can finish within 5minutes or less if you just want to take pictures.

If you have no expectations whatsoever, it's fine. But only because you decided to stop by after visiting the post office. The church is nothing special, nothing like European churches. I wouldn't spend more than a few minutes there.

When we visited Notre Dame it was closed so we could only see this attraction from the outside. A beautiful building but not much more than that. Go for a walk but don't plan your whole day around this attraction

We visited the Cathedral. The view from the outside shows somw similarity to the Paris model from which it was copied -- but missing some of the details. The interior was ordinary and not worth your time.
The opening hours are very short, and even if you manage to get in, you can't go all the way inside, so there's not much to enjoy. It's probably best to just look at it from the outside.
It this is the best Ho Chi Minh City has to offer, that is so sad. Calling it Notre Dame is insulting to the one in Paris.
Nothing interesting inside, they only opened a small path for visitors, which was disappointing. The only nice thing about this church is the exterior architecture - beautiful.
The church has nothing special. It's quite bare and contains no interesting works of art. It's worth a look only if you're in the area.

I've given it a poor overall rating, but it's not something that should be evaluated. This is the Church of Our Lady, a sacred place. The building materials were imported from France, and it was built in 1883. I hope it will be cherished.
A beautiful landmark that needs a major restoration inside out. Hope the country leaders do something for this church.

The two or three times we went it was closed. It's not lit up at night. A shame, we missed out. I'd still recommend seeing it from the outside and enjoying the magnificent Post Office building next door.
Beautiful church, but in the middle of a very busy square - a lot of traffic and noise. the Cathedral was not open to the public when I was there. Lots of interesting people watching nearby.
If you're not passing by, it's not worth the trouble of visiting it; it's just an ordinary church like the thousands you can see in Europe.

It doesn't look anything like the original. They only let you in through a narrow passage by the door, and I think there are always crowds of people there. Everyone's pushing and shoving, trying to take photos, but everything is gray, there's no beauty there. But that's just my opinion...
The main attraction of this cathedral is that it's located in Ho Chi Minh City. In Europe, it would be just one among many.
Not interesting at all for people from Europe. Ho Chi Minh has not much to offer in terms of architecture. This cathedral is not special or interesting either.
A church with beautiful exterior architecture. I wasn't able to see the interior because its opening hours are 5:00-11:00 and 15:00-17:30; you need to know this before planning your visit.
Standard architecture for such buildings. The façade and interior are very simple. It's located next to the post office, so you can take a photo.

If you happen to be passing by, take a look at it from the outside. It's pretty, but I wasn't impressed (which is what I expected from churches in Asia). There's nothing special inside. However, there's a very good ice cream shop nearby!
If your'e not religious then this is a bit of a bore. You can't really explore inside, and it looks like any other cathedral from the outside.

More of a curiosity, as it's located in Vietnam, built by the French during the colonial era. The facade is vaguely reminiscent of Notre Dame de Paris, but beyond the name, there's nothing else. The interior is bare and rather unremarkable, except for some beautiful stained glass windows to the left of the entrance.
As far as churches go this one bottoms out. Take a pass at the Cathedral and walk across the street to the Post Office...a much better attraction.

Very rarely open. Even if advertised as going to be. Looks like the Christians have nothing to offer the world here. Shame as if open all day could provide so much in a country who know so little about Christianity.
As we traveled to Vietnam in this December, we have visited many intresting places and this church didn't attract me. But it was near the central post office and our tour plan included it.
A nice catedral but not much to see inside and an amazing amount of traffic outside. Impressive, floating like a n island in the middle of the street.
Having seen many beautiful cathedrals in Europe and elsewhere and other ornate religious buildings in HCMC, this cathedral has little to offer.

Although this is another location featured in most/all travel guides to HCMC, I feel the same about this as I do of the Opera House: it's not worth the touristy attention it receives. It looks and feels out of place in Saigon, and its history and significance is simply that of a reference to Vietnam's colonial past.  Interesting factoid: the bricks were brought over from Toulouse, France... and the slightly lighter bricks at the top of the towers were sourced locally after the building was damaged during the war.  - locals feed the pigeons which flock here during the day; so beware: you may be pooped upon... - lots of wedding shoots take place here, so it's a great people-watching location, but not many benches... (Dunkin' Donuts nearby though!)
I would not suggest to make a special trip for this one. It is however opposite the PO and near the palace and remnants museum, so if about, have a quick look, because that is all it takes.
One is allowed into the building just far enough to get to the army of donation boxes, but not into the body of the cathedral. Not worth the effort: just look at the architecture externally.
Nice but a church like in every country,  Near the postoffice. Do it with a map en walk Don't pay a tour for this
If you've been to Europe, you won't be impressed by this (except that it is in a communist/socialist country).

Why in the world would you visit a mediocre church while in Vietnam? There are so many more unique places to visit. You can see a church like this, in every city, in America. Apparently, it's only uniqueness is the fact that it is a Cathedral in Vietnam.
We are far from Gothic cathedrals. The exterior is original, but the interior is unremarkable. We were surprised by the number of worshippers attending mass.
Just a church as er know it- Nothing special. But still I Think it is one of These places that you need to see while in Saigon.

The Notre Dame Cathedral is modelled on the Paris one but actually does not show that much resemblance. All the tour operators include a visit to the cathedral in their package, so the place is usually packed. The inside of the cathedral is very bare and you are only allowed to go into the back part of the cathedral. The cathedral is located in the middle of a big square (with lots of traffic!) and is the backdrop of many wedding photos.

The church is a nice fiscal point but it was not impressive or even large. I'm not really sure why it's a landmark. Until I saw it on trip advisor I thought it was artificial. It is nice. It's next to park and a post office but that's all there is to it.

I was not overly impressed by this cathedral. There is not a lot of info on the Cathedral itself and if you have traveled through Europe and seen Cathedrals there then you will want to skip this.
