We did a simple walk through because there isn't much to see and not a lot of it is open to the public. Very simple inside compared to all the beautiful marble and stained glass you see in Europe. I would skip this one unless you are at the Central Post office and you wan to pop in as it is across the street.

Unlike its namesake in Paris this cathedral is closed from 11-3 daily. Even when it is open you cannot access any part of it other than the immediate entrance - signs inside indicate that the body of the church is for worship not tourist access (which is fair enough) and there are wooden barriers just behind the rearmost pews to prevent access. Worth a look if you are going that way (while it is open) but not worth a specific trip.
Not lit at all at night and closed. Not even worth taking a picture of, but if you're passing by, it's nice to have seen this building at least.

This 100% French church is very well preserved; young couples getting married often have their photos taken in front of it. It's still used as a place of worship, so you go inside and just stay at the back of the church.

It's not even close to being the prettiest part of Saigon. The cathedral is ridiculous compared to the real Notre Dame, but the area is nice. Right across the street are some gardens, and to one side is the post office, which is a very pretty building.
Looks ordinary, nothing too impressive. Post office next to the cathedral is even more worth visiting.
None of the glamour that you would expect of a cathedral bearing this fabulous name. Needs a few bob spent on it.
A church worth a visit while passing through. Nothing special. Closed until 1:30 PM, and once inside, you can only stay in the back area behind bars. Sad.

I wish that people would stop trying to glorify very average buildings to impress the tourists. It's a basic cathedral, with a basic interior. Nice to walk past, but as a tourist attraction, give me strength!

Not really sure why this is on all the tour itineries ...much more impressive from the outside than in . You can't go into the main nave ( unless praying ) very dark and undecorative ....no stained glass to talk about . Saw what looked like a much more interesting church during a wander around plus a couple of super little and very welcoming Hindu temples
As you can only look outside the church, it's not that good. Since it's close to many other attractions it's worth a quick stop.
Viewed as part of a walking tour. Underwhelming on its own but a nice inclusion as part of a tour. Close to central post office.

This church is huge and always full of people. Easy to reach but not so interesying , especially for Europeans. ...i mean, we know that colonialism existed and this is a big residue, but i would prefer to have saved my time for something "more vietnamese" :-(
While it's acceptable from the outside, it's bare inside. There's no point even entering. The unique thing that makes it worth visiting is that many newlyweds come here for their photoshoots.
Seem many cathedrals over the years and have to say it's a pretty standard cathedral compare to others but still very pretty.

Interesting architecture and a nod to Saigon's past but aside from that, the inside that you wouldn't bat an eye-lid at in Italy or France. If you are at the post office and the cathedral is open, pop in, if not, don't bother going out of your way.
We visited the place during the restricted visiting hours, but you can only get in the first 5 meters. Not really worth it.
Cant say that this is must-see if you are from Europe. I went on a Sunday so the church was closed. The outisde is nice to take pictures but I think that's all you can get from it.
Looks impressive from the outside but boring and plain inside. It just serves as a reminder of religious servitude.
Nice design but locked up. Unable to enter was disappointing.  Nothing much but to take photos outside.
It seems like you have to come and visit, but don't get your hopes up; there's not much to it. You can go in and see, but they close a large part to tourists.

We had to return after three during our tour as the Cathedral was closed. When we arrived back we found it still closed as it was being used for a wedding. Can you believe it? Instead of letting a horde of sweaty tourists in they used it for a wedding! Only joking. We got to see it in use and while a nice building not overly exciting. We had already driven past it a number of times and had seen it from all angles with minimal enthusiasm so if you are short on time look at other stuff first.
Just a big buliding, not very pretty, have a look if you want to see the post office is next door. Pickpockets are around
Interesting but not impressive . Access was limited, and there was not a great deal to see. I would not make a social trip to see it.

The church was completely closed; you could only take pictures from the outside. There were some pigeons in front of it. It was quite boring. Luckily, it was next to another attraction, the Central Post Office, otherwise it would have been incredibly dull.

The most disappointing attraction in Ho Chi Minh City. It looks modest and very European, as it was built by the French. Ho Chi Minh City generally looks like a mix of European and Asian styles, and the temple is like a little piece of Europe here. I didn't go inside, as the exterior wasn't impressive. The location is in the very center of the city, within easy reach of other attractions.

Went to look around but the Catherdal is only open between 1500-1600 which is a little bit of a let down. There were hundreds of people outside getting photos so it would have been nice to have a look around inside.

It is special to see a copy of Notre Dame in Ho Chi Minh City. The square and the surroundings are beautiful. The post office is a "must see," but the cathedral is not. It is a legacy from the French period. Sit comfortably on the terrace next to the post office and watch the hustle and bustle. It is more fun to mingle with the students, tourists, and locals in the park.
The name "cathedral" would suggest a larger building. Rather simple, it dates from the time when France was still in charge.
An ordinary catholic church in the middle of Saigon. Parts of the church is closed and you can not reach the beautiful paintings and sculptures.
I do not understand why this cathederal is in vietnam. I think its a classic church. It can not be compared by notre dams at european countries especially at paris.

The temples are much more beautiful than this church. But because the post office is located nearby, as a tourist you get two curiosities to tick off as visited in one trip! We didn't specifically go looking for it; we happened to be in the area during our scavenger hunt in HCMC.

The exterior is as nice as it gets. The interior is dark, dank and run down. Visitors are not allowed to move into the pew and altar area - but there wouldn't be much to see anyways. Stick to the pagodas ;)
It's a big brick building with a couple of spires. Compared to the Post Office opposite it has nothing much to add to life.
Very difficult to visit as it is often shut to visitors for local services. The exterior is interesting but on a busy road.

Apart from being a Catholic church in a Buddhist country, this church doesn't have much architectural or religious appeal. However, a visit is almost mandatory, as it's located opposite a very nice train station.

There are some similarities, but they're not quite the same. Paris is way cooler, way cooler. I don't even think there's any point in including it in my tour itinerary. We drove by, checked it out, stopped for five minutes at most, took a photo, and then moved on.

Nice to see when you walk past but not worth to go inside in my opinion, maybe because we have so many beautiful churches/cathedrals in Europe this didnt impress at all. Maybe people from other countries/regions this is different

This place closes for visitors at 11 am. But it will only take you a couple of seconds as 95 percent is cordoned off and you only get to visit the foyer ! A couple of nice windows but If you are I a rush don't bother.
Nothing exceptional to see, not even the stones imported from France. And so many people! Move on to something else quickly!

Our title says it all .. we have never been able to have a look inside .. the cathedral always seems to be shut with no information for travellers. It looks lovely from the outside - would love to see the inside.

It is one of the main sites of the city, but not very interesting, and you are not even allowed to walk around inside the church. If you are European and has seen churches around Europe, don't be bothered visiting this one.

I did modify my plans to be able to enter the cathedral but regret it biterly... There's nothing to see worth waitting. You are allowed only 5 meters into the building in a crowded environment. This is easily falling in the tourist trap category.
Any random church in Europe is more beautiful. The interior is bare and the stained-glass windows are no longer there.
Okay, it is a church from the French era, on a square near the post office; not very exciting from the outside, and certainly not on the inside.

Check their opening hours if you wish to go inside. I think they're only open to visitors/non-worshippers from 9 to 11AM Monday thru Saturday, and you're only permitted into a partitioned portion at the rear of the cathedral. Nothing to see really, especially if compared to any of the outstanding cathedrals throughout Europe.
In Vietnam it is certainly a rare thing... but perhaps we have so many of them that obviously it didn't leave us enchanted.
Church was barricaded up when we visited. The architecture is French however, so is lots of Vietnam. If you want to see a replica of another cathedral go and see this otherwise don't bother.
We made a special effort to find our way to the cathedral only to find it closed - with no sign advsingwhen it is open. So - if you want to go her then check ahead!

We only had two days in Saigon, so were very rushed for time. When we got to Notre Dame on Friday 3rd April 2015, it was around 11:30am and it was CLOSED until 3pm. This - on Good Friday - the holiest day of the Catholic calendar??? I was horrified! In my travel experience, churches and cathedrals are freely accessible during the day. It was deeply disappointing to encounter locked doors at a Catholic church on such a holy holiday. Pretty disgraceful in my books!!!! The outside of the building was pretty though - sorry we did not get to spend any time inside.

This is not often open, so it's worth a brief glance as you visit the Post Office across the street. It's beautiful and definitely European in a city which is definitely Asian. What impressed me most was that nearly everyone respected the request to stay off the grass in front!

You'll see this anyway if you go to the post office. Unfortunately they let giant tourist coaches park in front of it so you can't take the view in. Also it was shut when were there so couldn't go inside. Not sure what the opening hours are if any.
If you happen to pass by, stop for a quick stop. The outside is certainly more pleasant than the inside. It only takes a few minutes.

Part of the standard Saigon sightseeing itinerary, it's worth a visit. Weddings take place on Saturdays and Sundays. The photo marathon of the bride and groom is more impressive than the cathedral itself. Western wedding attire seems to be in vogue at the moment; we were more than fascinated. Back to the church: As with the main post office across the street, it's worth seeing, but not essential.

Of course beautiful architecture, but not sure why people love it so much. The Mother Mary is quite magnificent. I have a had time understanding what makes this a landmark in Vietnam... I guess because of the history. So many people love the Notre Dame Cathedral. It is huge for tourists, locals, photographers, models, weddings and such. I don't really understand why or why people are so attracted to it. Is it because so many people go to it and people like being around other people? I'm not sure. I love Vietnam and Saigon, but there is absolutely no reason to recommend this to anyone unless they are interested in architecture, France, Catholicism/Christianity... or history

There's nothing to see inside, it's poor and modest. From the outside, you can only take a photo for keepsakes. On New Year's Day, there were a lot of people at the service, locals were standing outside, and all the doors were wide open.
Just across the road from the central post office - we went here three times but on each occasion it was shut. A great shame - we would have loved to have seen inside

Dull exterior. Duller interior. A biggish 19th century Catholic church from a minor provincial French town transplanted to downtown Saigon with no concessions whatever to Asian sensibilities. The most interesting thing I could discover about this dull building was that the French imported all the bricks from Marseilles. Skip it and spend the time in the Post Office across the square.
Outside it looks much better than the inside. Actually outside looks very nice, the inside it seems very empty and poor, not interesting to visit.

I'm not religious but I do appreciate design. The French must have lost the plot sometime after having built some of the most inspiring religious buildings in the world. This is a colourless affair (yes it is RC) that looks unloved. Perhaps it's a different affair during a service but at the time of our visit (11 am) there was nothing about it to make the trip enjoyable. As one Tripper said go to the Post Office instead.

The cathedral is only open 9 to 11.Proably a good thing .Very little of interest and beauty inside .Yes it is large but that is about it .Nothing explaining its history and so dull .Have a glance and walk across the road to the delights of the old post office . An interior for the religious only .I have to say it had no spiritual impact on me .

It isn't open for visiting all day - it is a still a church. We visited early in the day and it was closed - popped back when we were close by on the off chance it was open and it was closed because a mass was going on - it was stunning to see the church 'in action'

Well what a disappointment. About the same size as a decent church in the UK, closed half the day and when open you can walk in about 15 feet after which it is closed off except to people praying. Dull from the outside to boot. Nice for the tour operators though, who can park up and visit both this and the Central Post Office across the road which is even more tedious.

I understand there are approx 5 million Catholics in Vietnam. Many anti-communist Catholics migrated south in '54 when Vietnam was divided. Work still needs to be done by the Government to remove the suspicion associated with this faith group 40 years after the country was 'united'. The church is a landmark but not grand when compared with other Cathedrals throughout the world. No propaganda movie and no charge though donations towards the restoration of the church are welcomed. The real sights seem to surround the Church as it's a popular place for wedding photos and a busy school is across the street. I included this site in my self guided walking tour (Parks, Reunification Palace, War Remnants Museum and Central Post Office and in the evening, Ben Thanh Market).

Probably uglier than any European cathedral you've ever had the chance to visit. Interesting to know how the Catholic religion served as an excuse for the colonization of the country, but you don't need to visit it for that.

go there to see the outside but you are only allowed 20 feet in to the nave and not allowed any further which i thought was unusal as most churches and cathedrals i have been in the only place you cant go is the alter...

Ok, if you are walking around Saigon and you don't have any plan, you can just visit this place - in any case it's a city landmark. But if you are struggling with time, don't waste it. There are nicer things to do in HCM City than visiting this Cathedral (unless you are looking for a place to pray - then it's a different story)

Nice for photos out front but is fenced off inside with a bit if an impolite sign up for tourists saying something like, "this is for praying not looking". Thats fine but don't have it as a main tourist attraction in that case. Having a local guide with you to give a bit of its history is good tho.

This church is next to the magnificent Eifel designed post office and rather boring by comparison. It has no architectural merit and is not suitable as a former capital city cathedral. It is also crumbling before our eyes with plants growing out of the brick work. It was closed every time we went to it, so I assume is only open during services. It is not worth going out of your way for.
Interesting building from the perspective of the influence the French had it in in Vietnam but I would hardly call this a point of interest for tourist.

You're likely to visit whilst walking around the other sites as they are fairly close together.  The building from the outside is a poor copy of the original with dull brickwork instead of beautiful masonry. It didn't entice us to investigate further.
We stopped by to have a look but really, nothing to see here. There are some time restrictions on entry after 4.00pm.

Maybe coming from Europe (UK) i see many of these so not really much of an attraction for me - but if you like Cathedrals go, otherwise dont worry, you wont miss too much...kind of like the post office on the other side of the street - its just a post office.
All very impressive, sitting on an island in a sea of motorbikes by itself. Fun to walk around, but don't hope to find a door that allows you to enter the cathedral...

The outside of the church is nice, as you can see on the pictures posted on this website. The interior of the church remains mostly in the dark. As a visitor you are only allowed in the front porch of the church. Where many Vietnam and foreign visitors (tourists) take pictures from each other. Although several signs ask visitors to be quiet and respectful, no body seems to take care of that. In front of the church do hawkers sell the usual stuff i.e. postcards, water. See the church if you pass it by, otherwise leave it.
The building is beautiful from the outside, but there is little to see inside. Not a sight you necessarily need to visit if you are in Ho Chi Minh City.

We visited this church along with the nearby central post office. I had high expectations because it's a church from the French colonial era, but it was a disappointment... It seems to be a popular wedding photo spot, as we saw several couples there. The exterior is beautiful, so I would recommend it if you want to admire the exterior and the surrounding scenery.
It's enough to see it from the outside......... For Westerners it's just a blasphemous reproduction of the original..
We only saw the outside as part of a city tour. It's attractive but not exciting if you come from Europe! It's next to the post office so worth a look as you pass by but don't go specially.
Reminds me more to a North German/Baltic Sea red brick achitecture rather then Notre Dame in Paris. Nothing to write home about

Take note of the opening hours. We first arrived when it was closed and only took pictures of the exterior facade, which was beautiful. We thought we would be able to explore interiors of the cathedral so we decided to come back a few hours later but there is nothing to see inside and we cannot proceed beyond 10metres of the entrance (presumably so that people can pray in peace without hoards of tourists). So just taking photos from the outside when you're passing by on your way to other attractions is sufficient

Just like many other small cathedrals in many smaller cities around the world - no, this one is not as interesting in architecture as most others - rather boring we thought  And beware - limiter opening times
Notre dame cathedral Stuck in the middle of the main road, nice but not special. Post office across the road worth popping into for a quick look.

The exterior is beautiful, and couples in wedding dresses often take commemorative photos around the church. The interior is relatively simple, and it's best to keep in mind that it's not like the lavishly decorated churches you might find in Europe.

We had expected a nicer church, at least inside. During visiting hours, you can only enter through the rear left entrance and immediately exit through the right; the main part of the church is inaccessible. It's also quite crowded during visiting hours; the church is certainly more interesting to Asians than to Europeans.
Slightly interesting on the outside, but really boring on the inside. Walked in, stayed about 5 seconds, no photos, walked out.  Not like Paris at all.
Looks nice from the outside, but the inside is kinda disappointing. Really nothing special. Limited opening times. Well... thats it basicly. Some nice cafes on the opposite.

I love a cathedral, and this is almost as magnificent as the ones I have seen in France. Unfortunately the inside has very little public access so you can only walk in and peer into the building.  Shame.
Colonial. A must-see. There's very little to see in Saigon. No artistic value, no sacred awe.

This cathedral is a curious sight in the middle of Ho Chi Minh City; it's in poor condition and almost always closed. You can get a good photo of the cathedral from the post office and take the opportunity to visit the post office as well.

The title says it all. You can walk in the entry and view the church from there from close to the entry doors. Nothing special and not worth a special trip. I really preferred interacting with the people trying to find out more about their lives that stops like this.

You can see it as passing by on a motorbike or in a taxi, no need to stop and hang around. The most fascinating thing about the Cathedral in Saigon is the couples taking posed wedding photos in rented white dresses and tuxedos.
Just opposite the post office. But you cannot get inside....otherwise its a small Cathedral.... Part of the circle ... Opera House....PO and Cathedral.. within 3 mins walk of each other

I wasnt impressed with this, Yes it is a beautiful old build building BUT Ive been in lots of ordinary churches grander than this, plus Ive been to Notre Dame Cathedral in Paris - now that's magnificent. After you have seen the Cathedral pop across the road to the New York Cheesecake shop, fantastic food, great prices.

It's right across from the post office. As a monument, it's not very important; as I say in the title, the church at my school is better, but of course, it's not in Saigon and it's in a Christian country.
You can't escape it anyway; it's opposite the main post office. Avoid times of services, you wouldn't even get onto the steps. There's a lot of devotion.
When we got to the Cathedral it was in the morning and wasnt going to be open for another hour. Unfortunately we didnt have the time to return to see inside of the church.
The name promised much more. Furthermore, some parts of it are poorly preserved. This glimpse of European architecture in an Eastern context certainly stands out.
Fortunately, the post office is nearby, because the cathedral is not worth getting out of the car or bus for. The exterior is acceptable, but inside the church is very austere.

Went there but was somewhat disappointed. Plan not more than 15 minutes here to 30 minutes. Have seen better cathedrals in the past better than this.  Was lucky though to see a newly wed couple taking pictures here, so wasn't that bad after all.
The day we went to see it, it was closed. Did not look that impressive from the outside. Of course after seeing the Notre Dame in Paris everything else that resembles it is a cheap copy.
The church is rather ugly, and I don't know how they can call it Notre Dame when any resemblance is purely coincidental. You could save yourself some time by visiting something else.

Indeed, there's nothing original about this cathedral; it's as French and classic as they come for this type of place. So if you're expecting to see Notre Dame (like the one in Paris), forget it. If you want to explore a bit of the history of French Catholic places of worship in Vietnam... go for it.

Not that impressive...seen more ornate cathedrals back in North America. Just drop by if it is part of one's walking tour of the district and go in to cool down. If there is a line-up, skip going inside and just take an exterior photo. There is a gate to restrict visitors to the back area/entrance of the cathedral but just open the gate and take a seat at one of the pews. The cathedral is right across the street from the post office.....just watch out for traffic and all the tour buses when you cross!!
Looks lovely from the outside but the inside is very plain and you are only allowed very limited access i.e. a small fenced off area at the back.
I love Cathedrals, but this one had nothing really nice to see in it. Overcrowd and hot. I spent probably 5 minutes in it. Thank goodness it was included in the tour I was taking.
It is located in the French Quarter. Built in the late 19th century. Very sparsely decorated and with very few stained glass windows. Inside is a statue of the Madonna who is said to be weeping.

were the words our guide used before we arrived. That statement could not have been further from the truth - very simple, very unimpressive - and a barrier stopped you even going into the cathedral itself - all you could vist was the rear behind the pews

It wasn't opened when we turned up, and we weren't inspired to hang around or come back to go inside. We probably spent a few minutes here. It looks just like in to the photos, it's really busy outside and not very enthralling. If you can't find anything else to do then maybe take a look, but it's not a must see. If you want to see this type of building, go to Europe.

Impressive building on the outside but very strange opening hours. Very disappointed when we entered to be greeted with iron railing, preventing us from visiting the body of the church. Having been welcome in wats and places of worship in all other places I was disappointed that this church chose to block people out.

The cathedral is only open for one hour in the afternoon (from 3 pm to 4 pm). Anyone arriving even a minute late will be rudely turned away by an unfriendly woman. Not recommended for the Catholic community in Vietnam!

From reviews in travel guides and books, I'd gotten an impression of grandeur. Nothing special, really, but that's just my personal opinion. It's right next to the Central Post Office. You can visit both in a short time. Be aware that the Cathedral gates close inexorably at 4:00 PM.

We arrived at the cathedral around 11:00 am. We took some exterior photos. Then we found out that you can only enter the church from 3:00-4:00pm. ( from the crossing guards stationed across the street). We came back at 3:00 pm. Only to be disappointed. It's very old inside,...not really kept up. It's really not very impressive. The beautiful Post Office is across the street, so we got to see both in the same trip.
Note Dame in Saigon is not much more interesting than a random cathedral in a French provincial town. Take a picture from the outside and let the church be a symbol of French colonialism.
A not very impressive church. You are not allowed to sit in the pews. Nothing like the real Notre Dame. Fine as part of a tour though,
Closed, not sure if it's ever possible to see the inside. You'll probably bump into the cathedral at least once while you are in town, so take a photo and move on...

As with all christian churches so far in Asia, this will be a disappointment if you come from Europe. Quite large by Asian standards, but still far from being a "cathedral", it has nothing special about it. Only to be visited if you are a devout christian and there for the spirituality. On the other hand given the other rather limited options of attractions in Ho Chi Minh city, if you need to see something might as well look at this, it is centrally located.
The inside is laws to visitors, the outside is nothing special and has little of the splendour of the actual Notre Dame. Take a photo of you're walking past, but don't go out of your way to see it
This is a relatively new cathedral, European style. not very impressive, if you come from the western part of the world. There are better attractions in Saigon.

A temple made for photography. They let you inside, but it's not crowded, and there's nothing to see. It's better to go to a remote temple in a non-tourist area; it's much more interesting and you'll feel the energy. This is just a tourist attraction.
...which was a shame. We only wanted to see inside. I got a little upset and went across the road to the big old french style post office instead. So there.

...the thing with the NDC in HCMC. As a tourist you are fooled that the NDC is a historical attraction but there is actually nothing interesting to see. Admittedly a very beautiful cathedral but the limited accessibility (most of it blocked off when it is not a mass) and the lack of "something out of the ordinary" made me rather have an ice cream at Häagen-Dazs about 50 m from the NDC.

Looked promising from the outside but nothing to see once you get inside! You file in through one door, peer through railings at interior and then walk out again! Only let in past railings if you want to pray.
Maybe it's because we know the real Notre Dame, but it seemed insignificant to me. In Vietnam, I prefer the local architecture.
Not impressive at all if you have seen the real. Inside plain and what might have been there did not survive the end of the Vietnam war. The park in front of the Cathedral is however cared for.

Also known as St. Mary's Church, it's a Neo-Gothic building constructed in the late 19th century. There's a statue of the Virgin Mary in the square in front of it. Although it's a landmark building in Ho Chi Minh City, there are restrictions on entry times, and even if you go inside, you're only allowed in for a few meters, so taking a commemorative photo from the outside is sufficient.

Coming from Europe this church is most likely not going to impress you. It is not very big, the interior is pretty bland it is full of tourists since it is included in all city tours and since it is pretty new it also lacks any atmosphere that old churches often have.  The only slightly interesting thing to note is that the a lot of steel was used to stabilise the building. We were in and out in 5 minutes.
All locked up and we could not see inside lots of wedding parties getting photos taken before they get married outside weird

The Cathedral is beside the Post Office. Basically, there is nothing much to do or see here unless you are interested in watching local couples having their wedding photos taken. Maybe worth a quick photo shot but thats about it.... It will take you no more than 10 minutes at this destination.

This was the second stop on the $7 tour we did, and thank goodness there was no entry charge because all you can do is walk in to the back section of the church and then straight back out. The outside of the building is as good as the inside - and as it is not an old building, a lot of the charm you may automatically think is there is missing.

We arrived at Notre Dame at 11:30 to discover it is closed between 11-3 each day. So we stayed around the general area for a few hours, had lunch etc and then returned at 3pm - only to wonder why we bothered. Not really anything here worth seeing.
Very unimpressive, maybe the name is too big? Just a dull, dirty cathedral - Can't think of much more to say about it!
You can only linger in the entrance of this church so not much to see. It was modeled after the one in Paris but inside is a big disappointment, very bare - no statues, little stained glass.
We had missed the opening hours twice, so when we finally got in and found that you are only admitted into just inside the entrance, we were very disappointed.

We went on a Sunday and found the doors closed. A church closed on a Sunday! We were intrigued to see the inside but had to be content with walking around it and admiring the Toulouse brickwork. Luckily, there were weddings taking place so we could see the bride and groom having their pictures taken in front of and around the sides of the church. That was the only reason to be there at the time.
A typical colonial affair, ok for a 10 minute visit but not worth going out of the way for.

Saigon/HCM city does not have that many genuine attractions and this certainly is not one of them. Boring, ugly, red brick building, barely wortht driving past, certainly not worth slowing down, never mind stopping to go and have a look. Go shopping instead.

In December 2011, my boyfriend and I were in Ho Chi Minh City, and that's why we visited Notre Dame Cathedral. From the front, it looks really beautiful and is nice for taking a few souvenir photos – but inside, it's a very understated cathedral where we only stayed for a few minutes (a quick tour, so to speak). Conclusion: outside -> wow, inside -> meh :S :S :S
This is not a must see at all if you have been to the original in Paris, this one is really run down and not spectacular at all.
Walk past it, snap a pic and move on. Nothing else to really see...

It was a pretty cathedral to see, however it was nothing special. We stumbled upon it because of its convenient location within HCMC, however I was not impressed. The inside is plain, and contains HD TV's every pew for better views of the alter during mass (kind of ruins the mystique). I wouldn't go out of your way to see this.
go to paris to see the real thing !! it was a bricked building that had the same structure as the real thing but with no statues or gargoyles ...

Compared to other large churches it is not very impressive. It is large and the stonework is good however it lacks the decorations and paintings of even small churches in Europe. It is a site for wedding photos so expect to see at least 4 or 5 couples having their photo taken around the Cathedral. In Vietnam Wedding photos are done months before the wedding!
An impressive enough building from outside but not open to public which was somewhat dissappointing

In most of Europe this would barely be considered a local church, let alone a cathedral. It is built completely out of brick and not very impressive. It sits on the same square as the old post office so can be seen quite quickly if you choose to visit.

someone needs to tell the public, just in case they're making a long trip to visit this Cathedral.
It has been shuttered off for years and years as it is being refurbished. no chance of going inside.
with high walls around it and most of it covered in scaffolding. if that's your thing, come in and enjoy it.

If you enjoy having your daily time with God interrupted by a balding, greying, geriatric's grandstanding for his YouTube channel including but not limited to: talking about pointless things on his 20 minutes homily, ensuring everything that could be sung was with his voice leading and drowning out the choir, only allowing the 2 other priests to speak 3 times total, and wasting an hour and a half of my time with God.

RACISTS. We tried to enter the cathedral. Some Asian-looking guys entered right in front of us. We tried to enter and we were told we couldnt. We told them we wanted to go to the mass (which was true), but they said no and started to laugh. We were clearly left out because we weren't Asian-looking. We feel we have been discriminated. As many of the other reviews have stated the cathedral is covered in scaffolding as it is under restoration.

We arrived at 17.10 to attend the mass. Mass times are clearly stated on the noticeboard outside the gate. On weekdays there is a mass at 5.30 and 17.30.

The lady at the gate would not let us in. At first she said there wasn't a mass and the church was under restoration. We showed her the noticeboard and she then said only Vietnamese people could attend the mass.

We were told to go to the office to complain if we were unhappy. We spoke to someone  in the building across the street and had the same story.

This is absurd! As Christians, and Catholics, surely we should be able to attend mass and not be turned away.

If the church is unsafe because of the work that is being undertaken then it is unsafe for everyone and there shouldn't be services taking place.  If its safe enough for Vietnamese Catholics then surely it is safe enough for everyone.

To be prevented from attending mass is just unbelievable and quite upsetting.

I understand that the church is undergoing renovation, but there's an extreme rude/racist lady at the gate, who only allows Vietnamese people to attend mass and not an outsider. She had no valid reason as to why, I as a Baptised catholic was not allowed to attend mass at 5:30PM on a Wednesday.

The church committee needs to manage this better.

I don't dispute the beauty of the place, its historical value, and its spiritual importance. But we were denied access to the building, despite three attempts in three days. We were turned away without any real, understandable explanations or explicit signs, either from the guards, the worshippers who were there, or the information available online. In short, a disappointing experience that wasn't softened by the view of the cathedral's exterior, currently under renovation; here too, a little information would have been welcome.

To summarize: Catholics passing through Saigon, don't get carried away with the idea of ​​visiting or praying in this haven of spirituality; it's potentially closed, or you'll be denied entry. On the positive side, they're restoring the cathedral in Saigon at a time when in France they're letting Catholic buildings crumble. However, you remain standing there with your faith and your questions. Unbelieving French-speaking tourist, no stress, you still have the post office, the opera and the market of the cock-a-doodle-doo temple, Vuitton, Hermes and company nearby.
The cathedral is unfortunately covered in scaffolding :( You can only see the side view.
This place is perfect for observing the weeds that have grown due to the extremely long construction period, lol.
It was clear it would be busy there. But the sheer number of people all around was awful. And the church was 90% covered up for renovations. I could skip it.

I'm sure the cathedral is impressive, but it's currently under complete restoration, so it's closed and covered in building materials. We'll have to wait until they repair it, and it doesn't look like it'll be done quickly, the renovation is major, so there's no point in going there for now.
The Notre Dame Cathedral in Saigon is under renovation, but it's beautifully decorated for Christmas, attracting a large crowd.

Ugh, yeah, crap.

The whole place is a complete construction site and you can't go in.

That was a bust!
It was under construction both two years ago and today (August 2025), so I couldn't go inside.

I arrived in shorts and understandably refused entry. On my second attempt, wearing long clothes, I said I only wanted to pray, but was repeatedly turned away, while the locals went in one after the other…

How long are they going to keep doing renovations...? I thought it would have been finished ages ago, but the front and the area around the spire are completely covered up, and I couldn't even get in. There's absolutely no consideration for visitors or tourists at this Roman Catholic church. Perhaps it's not necessary for the locals, and its importance as a tourist spot is low.
I just went, 07/01/2026. Everything is under construction, you can't see anything.

Unfortunately, interior visits are not currently possible. A large part of the cathedral is covered in scaffolding. I wonder when visits will be possible again, as plants are already growing on the scaffolding, indicating this situation has been ongoing for quite some time.

Very regrettable.
Looking at the scaffolding, it's been under renovation for at least a year, but there's too many weeds around to believe there's any work going on!!!
Currently under construction, so it's not worth going there at the moment.

No visits allowed! No visits allowed! No visits allowed!

This is important, so I'm saying it three times.

It's under repair. You can go for a walk across the street.
It was a bit disappointing. I came here in 2019, and while I was standing there taking pictures, the parishioners wouldn't let me in.
It is under renovation and you can't see anything.

Unfortunately, the entire tower was covered in scaffolding due to construction, ruining the view.
The guidebook said you could enter outside of mass hours, but it seemed that it was only open during mass hours due to the construction.

After the disappointment at the Reunification Palace, we were hoping for a beautiful church here. Unfortunately, that wasn't the case either; you can hardly see anything through the scaffolding. Another disappointment.

When are they going to finish repairing it? lol

Still unreachable for me!!!

I'm back again and you can't get inside even though it's the Holy Year... you can get a plenary indulgence... but not here because they won't let you in, not even for 2 minutes for a SHORT prayer!!! I don't have time to come at a different time because I'm just passing through. They treat the church like a museum... although it's easier to get into a museum... a sad sight for the church.
It must be magnificent without the scaffolding. And inside it's surely very beautiful, but currently closed.
They've been renovating it for years. You can't see it from the outside and they don't allow foreigners in. Bad experience.

During the renovation work, it's impossible to enter and it's covered in plaster.
Don't waste your time. You'll find it like this: completely under construction, and it's going to be a long process. If you want to see it, use Google or go to Paris.
During the week it is only open for 1 hour from 5.30pm to 6.30pm and is under restoration so its beauty cannot be appreciated.
This is what it looks like now.

On the evening of January 28, 2025 (29 AL), around 8:30-9:00 PM, two men in white shirts stood in front of the gate preventing some people from entering to visit without explaining the reason. Meanwhile, many people and groups were entering and exiting normally without needing permission.

I would like to ask what conditions and requirements must be met to be allowed inside the church to visit and meet God?

December 14, 2024. It is currently under construction.

You can only view it from the outside.
The church is closed. There is construction work and scaffolding covers large parts of the church.

I was unable to go inside because it was under construction when I visited.

During the day, you only need to view the exterior.

It stands out even more under the night lighting.
It's a shame the Cathedral is closed for renovations.

Whoever covered the church completely with lights has wasted money and effort, only to see the light from the lamps and not the windows or the beautiful details of the church. It's a real shame. I hope someone responsible will read my feedback and reconsider the decorations so that the lighting is just right, allowing the beauty of Notre Dame Cathedral to be revealed. Thank you.

We went on Christmas Day but weren't let in. The guard told us people were celebrating Mass now. When we said we were there for that purpose (it was 10 minutes until Mass), after looking us up and down, he said it lasted an hour, not 5 minutes...
Wow! Mass lasts an hour, what a novelty... We said we knew, but he probably didn't believe us and wouldn't let us in.
It's a sad thing for a Catholic to be excluded from celebrating the birth of Christ.

Completely enclosed and fenced off. Not worth the trip. The fact that the scaffolding, about 10 to 12 meters high, is covered in greenery certainly hasn't been there since the beginning of the year. Saigon generally doesn't do much for tourism. A thousand times better to go to Da Nang or somewhere similar.
No interest at present, the cathedral is covered in scaffolding.
Façade under construction in October 2025. Major works, building hidden by scaffolding.
You can't get in, the guards don't speak English and the treatment is rude.

We pay the church construction contribution every year. But I've never been able to visit the church because I can't find a place to park. The area around the church is chaotic with vendors. It's terrible.

Notre-Dame Basilica in Paris was rebuilt in just four years after it burned down. What about yours? It's the same name, but it's going to take forever. I wonder how many more years it will take to rebuild your scaffolding.

We came here to see it, and the entire main facade is under construction, so you can't see ANYTHING. Understandable.

What I don't like is that when we tried to go in, they turned us away because "they don't want tourists inside." Wonderful, isn't it?
We were there in early April and the facade was covered in scaffolding, so we couldn't go inside. Disappointing… I doubt the renovation will be finished soon.

Under construction, full of scaffolding. We couldn't park the motorbike for free in the square because there were parking attendants.
As of today, not recommended. The only option is to walk along Book Street, which is next door and is a pleasant stroll.

I wonder when I'll be able to see the cathedral, haha.

A strange place. I wanted to go to the fair, but I wasn't allowed in. Apparently, you have to be Vietnamese to get in. That's very odd.

Avoid this Lotte store, everyone :) The tickets are cheap, but they'll trick you into buying a Minion popcorn and drink combo without even asking your preference. They'll say there's a discount when you buy the combo??? Five people, five tickets cost 325k, but the total bill came out to 900k?????? That combo is actually 575k. They really know how to rip off customers :) I'll never go back.

My first time going to see it, and I have no intention of returning.

Checking the showtimes on my phone was very difficult because the interface wasn't mobile-friendly. Booking tickets in advance was impossible due to errors; the screen kept jumping around, and the same thing happened with both morning and afternoon tickets. When I arrived at the ticket counter, all I got was the message, "Today's screenings are sold out because the schedule changes daily." They even added, "Now only Lotte is showing Song Lang." Terrible! So I used Lotte's Wi-Fi to watch it and booked tickets at CGV the very next day, ruining my plans for that day.

I think in this modern age, with trends changing so quickly, Lotte should improve its service instead of giving customers such a terrible experience.

On the first day of Tet (Lunar New Year), I went to see the 10:20 PM screening of the movie "Trạng Nguyên". It was a bit cool when I entered the theater, but after sitting for a while, it became very hot and stuffy. It was as if they were afraid of wasting electricity, so they turned off all the air conditioning and didn't let anyone cool down. We had already paid more than usual, and we paid properly for the movie, so is this acceptable? It was my first time at a Lotte cinema, and this was definitely not a pleasant experience. The theater was small and the seats weren't comfortable, and they were still irresponsible in serving customers on the first day of Tet.

Bottled water costs 35k, the price is different with or without a receipt. The service is complicated, slow, and illogical. The seats are old and not as comfortable as at CGV. Ticket prices are higher than CGV. CGV is recommended.

I often choose the cinema near my house to watch movies.
I don't know about other branches, but today when I went there, I couldn't buy the 85k popcorn and drink set and had to buy it through the app, which was very inconvenient and time-consuming, whereas not long ago I could still buy the 85k set directly.
I hope that cinemas in particular and the system in general prioritize customer experience over chasing revenue like this.

Today I'm exposing Lotte because I'm so pissed off. This morning, my friends and I went to see Minions, and the cashier was awful. She charged us almost 1 million VND even though there were only 4 of us, and two of us didn't take any food. The movie tickets were 65k VND, plus the food, bringing the total to 235k VND. If we didn't take popcorn and drinks, it would be 130k VND, plus the two who did, that's 470k VND. The total should only be 600k VND, but she charged us for everything. Then she only gave us one portion of popcorn and one drink, even though we ordered two portions. And then she made us pay an extra 170k VND! What the hell??? We paid for the full combo, and she didn't give us our food? Since we'd been looking forward to this day, I gritted my teeth and paid her. In total, we paid 770k, and even with my student ID, she didn't reduce the price at all. Meanwhile, at CGV, you only need 200k for a popcorn and drink, and you even get a discount with a student ID. I recommend CGV if you're planning a movie! 🐸
I have many memories associated with passing through this place.

At 9:10 PM on March 5, 2021, I bought two tickets to see the movie "The Godfather" at Lotte Cinema Cong Hoa. After checking my tickets, there was no one to guide me to my seat. When I got to my seat, someone else was already sitting there. When I asked, I was told that their seat was also occupied, and they complained and were then directed to my seat. When I went to ask again, the staff member led me to another seat, which I found very annoying because the movie was already playing and I was being led around the theater. When I finally sat down in the seat the staff member had assigned me, someone came and claimed it was their seat. I told them to call the staff member back because I had been assigned that seat. When the staff member came back, they moved me to my seat again. I disagreed. I stated that I either get my seat back or I wouldn't watch the movie. The staff member kept saying, "Let me arrange it," and I requested to speak to the manager. What angered me most was the manager's unprofessional handling of the situation. From their uniform to their manner of speaking, the manager gave me options:
"Can you watch the movie tomorrow? I'll reschedule your ticket for tomorrow." (What a ridiculous question!) If I were watching it tomorrow, I wouldn't have needed to buy a ticket today to see the first showing of the new movie.

"I'll let you watch it at a later showing or give you a refund." The most infuriating thing was the refund, with the attitude of "if you can't watch it, take your money back." Do I have time to book tickets just to go see a movie and then get a refund?

Terrible.

The staff cheated customers. Once they made me buy popcorn and drinks as part of a combo, saying the theater didn't sell them separately. Another time, I bought a ticket using the U22 member discount, but was still charged the adult price when paying; I didn't notice at the time. I'll definitely never watch a movie here again.

From the order pickers to the security guards, the work is terrible. One Grab delivery order, just a single tube of toothpaste, took half an hour to process. The next person, if they forgot something, would say "Oh, I forgot," get offended, make me wait, and then I have to cancel the order, wasting an hour of my time and kicking them all out.

I don't know if the security guard here was hired just for show or something. I asked him to help move my car and he had a grumpy look on his face, and was careless when bringing the car out for the customer. He even loosened the car window and caused a serious collision with another car while moving it, and instead of apologizing, he casually walked away like a thug... I hope Lotte will review this... (Male security guard, about 1.60m tall, dark skin, a bit thin. I met him at the parking lot on January 11, 2020, at 7 PM)
Unfortunately, the basilica is closed for restoration and is completely covered in scaffolding.

The service staff were incredibly slow. There were only two minutes left until the movie started, and they still wouldn't sell tickets? The cleaning staff would yell at anyone they wanted. What kind of attitude is that?
We couldn't see anything, it was under renovation and all covered up, so we went around and headed to the post office.

Withdrawal process is flawed due to system errors. They require an appointment slip a few days or a month in advance. They deduct money but don't release it – how unscrupulous! They hold onto customer funds for a whole month without resolving the issue. They have many staff members but their work is incompetent. I'm boycotting this bank and will switch to another one.

On May 19th, around 7 AM, I went to pick up my tickets at the online ticket counter. The staff member had a very rude attitude towards me. She didn't even look at us once when we arrived, showing a very unpleasant attitude. After a while, she looked up and saw the three of us standing in line together, and told the next customer not to stand next to us. Is it possible that all Lotte staff are like this?

It would be better if the food stalls in front of the gate were removed. The vendors brazenly occupy the space. Those renting the place are allowed to sit, otherwise they get kicked out. They'll probably get their land deeds reissued to them.

Lotte Cong Hoa
I can't understand how many people are standing in line waiting, with only one cash register working. Buying tickets requires queuing, and even buying popcorn and drinks requires standing in line with ticket buyers!!! Then there are those clueless customers, standing around choosing seats, thinking, changing their minds for ages!

This kind of business practice is ridiculous; no wonder their revenue is so low that people flock to Galaxy and CGV!
Every time I watch a movie, I get annoyed by having to queue for popcorn and drinks with ticket buyers. The service is getting worse and worse!

Normally, there's nothing special here, I just stopped by to eat at Dokki.
The parking is cramped, making it extremely difficult to park, and it's outdoors, which is very annoying!
The parking attendant was incredibly rude!
I parked my motorbike for about 3 hours while I went to eat. When I came out, I didn't know how much it cost, so I took a 5,000 VND note to the parking attendant. When I asked how much it was, because I was worried it wasn't enough, the security guard, who looked to be around 40, spoke very rudely! "Give me the change, why are you asking so many questions?" He spoke in such an unbelievably rude way. I can't believe that an older security guard would talk to someone older than me like that!
Coming to this church means not receiving the blessing that the Bible teaches is the blessing of eternal life. This is because the New Covenant Passover is not observed here.
When I went in the evening, I wasn't allowed in for some reason.
Due to renovations and on Sundays, there are no opening hours.

Vietinbank, Ba Ria branch. My ATM card is malfunctioning; it can deposit money but not transfer it. I've been to the bank twice and called about 10 times, but it's been over half a month and they still haven't resolved the issue. The staff keep making excuses. The worst bank in Vietnam.
Not allowed to visit ={

The facade has been covered up for construction for some time now; nobody warned us, and people in groups and with guides kept going despite not being able to see anything. Very bad of the guides who knew perfectly well about it.

Terrible service. The late-night screening ended at almost 1 AM, and when I went down to the parking lot, I was told I couldn't pick up my car until 9 AM the next day. This put customers in a situation where they couldn't get home late at night. It was a terrible experience. This will be the last time I watch a movie at this theater.

First, the working methods should be reformed. Checking age information before selling tickets is necessary. Customers should be asked to provide identification, not just buy tickets and then demand to see it upon verification. If they have it, that's fine, but this is a trip for leisure, not work, so why should everyone remember to bring their ID? If they don't have it, they should be polite and say, "Please leave, your two friends can come in," adding, "I bought the ticket and it's non-refundable." I agree that it was my fault for not bringing my ID, but the ticket seller is also partly to blame for not requesting it at the time of purchase, putting the customer in a difficult situation where they not only missed the movie but also lost money.

The restaurant was closed. I called customer service and they said it was open and directed me to the first floor, but I couldn't find it. Even the security guard didn't know. It was a terrible experience; I was hungry and had to wander around the city center. If it's no longer open, they should put a "closed" sign so customers don't need to look for it anymore. Thank you.

The staff worked without any clear procedures or order. It was very chaotic. My friend and I waited in line for 30 minutes to buy tickets. Then, at the counter, the staff made us wait even longer. Suddenly, they switched to issuing tickets to customers who had booked online. So my friend and I had to wait another 30 minutes while the staff processed our online bookings. What's the point of queuing then? Terrible!

The ticket seller didn't clearly explain the popcorn and drink combo options, the popcorn and drink vendor was chatting amongst themselves without paying attention to customers' questions, telling them to go somewhere else, the security guard gave 5k and waited a while before giving back 1k change, clearly showing his attitude: "If it's good and you don't give me change, I'll curse you." Those who work without integrity will learn their lesson.
Unfortunately, it's been scaffolded for a long time.

February 25, 2017: Helmet Lost - Security Guard's Terrible Behavior.

My friend and I went to eat and watch a movie at Lotte Cinema on the 4th floor. We parked our motorbike in the parking lot in front of the main entrance of the supermarket. Both of us carefully secured our helmets in the trunk before going inside. Around 3:40 PM, we returned to find one helmet missing. It was appalling that only a part of the helmet strap remained when we opened the trunk. It was easy to see that someone had used scissors to brazenly cut the strap. Although it wasn't a valuable item, it was a gift, and my friend cherished it. We had no choice but to leave. When we retrieved our motorbike, we reported the incident to the security department.

From what I observed, the parking lot isn't very large, and there are usually only about 3-4 people, including the ticket checker. It's highly unlikely that someone would bring scissors in and cut the strap to steal a supermarket customer's helmet like that. And one of the two people, after hearing the report, said, "If you have valuable helmets, just bring them out and give them to the security guard. We saw someone come to your car to take your helmet, and now you're saying they'll beat us up?" I've never seen security guards like this. Their responsibility is to protect customers' property, yet they're afraid of being beaten up. What's the point of having 3-4 people guarding the car?

I've always been satisfied with Lotte's service and have been a member for a long time. But today I'm very disappointed with this incident. What we wanted to say was to report it so that the security department could be more vigilant and detect thieves. But hearing about his attitude while bearing the responsibility of security is truly disheartening. I hope Lotte sees this post to ensure customer peace of mind.
Unfortunately, it's currently being renovated, but it opens at 3 a.m.
The cleaning lady was wiping the waiting area right when people were buying tickets, and she was rude to customers buying tickets. On a normal day, they charge 12,000 VND for parking a cup of tea.

I bought something and they said there were no bags to put it in, so I had to carry it home. Why is the customer service so disorganized? When I went to buy something and asked which section was for heavy items like spoons and chopsticks to save time, the staff inside had a grumpy face and were dismissive. Very poor service... I also criticize the staff member in the frozen food section.

Customers need to check the product code on their phone when buying products for relatives, and need their relatives to see the product beforehand.

Regarding the ban on filming and photography, there should be signs prohibiting the use of devices at each booth so everyone is aware.
The attitude of the security guards should be professional.
Every customer contributes to the overall existence of the sales system.
Disappointed with Lotte.

They've been occupying the sidewalks and roads for too long.
Almost 10 years and nothing has been done.
What used to be the most beautiful place has now become the ugliest in the city.

Overall, I'm very dissatisfied with Lotte Republic. The air conditioning is too hot, the parking area is disorganized, and the selection of products is limited. The spaces feel cramped and confined. I feel disappointed.
It wasn't worth it, it's currently being renovated.

The cashier's attitude was unpleasant. Even though we bought a lot, they insisted we put our purchases in a bag, which was quite expensive. I asked for a bag, but they absolutely refused. I understand it's about protecting the environment, but I only asked for one bag that was just enough, not extra, and the staff's attitude was terrible.

The first and last time I went was finding the cinema, which was very time-consuming. When buying tickets, the ticket seller was vague and unclear. We bought a ticket and a combo of one popcorn and one drink, not one popcorn in a plastic cup. When we took out the plastic cup, she just said it was a free gift but didn't point to what it was. Other sellers even explained the cost of each item separately. This seller didn't explain clearly at all. They need to review their customer service and communication methods.

LOTTE MART (PICO PLAZA)
The parking attendants are incredibly rude. They don't give change back for overcharging, they just silently take the car without giving it back to the customer. This has happened to me several times already, and I hate it. It's not much, but I don't like it. Even worse, my car was stuck in traffic, and when I asked them to move it, they wouldn't, and even said unpleasant things. I really hate that kind of staff.

Comment:

The cathedral is currently under repair. I had just gone to attend Mass but was stopped at the entrance. The reason given was that tourists are not allowed to enter during the repair period.

However, they didn't stop any white or black people; they only held back Asians who had cameras around their necks. Among those who went in were some Korean kids I had seen on the flight from Korea to Vietnam. I asked why they were letting tourists in, but they pretended not to hear me.

I just got unlucky. As expected, it is a problem wherever you give armbands to people who aren't prepared.

That isn't the real issue.

The problem is the extremely rude attitude of the woman blocking the tourists. First off, "Go out!" Even when I asked for the reason, she just said "Go out!" unconditionally. I was shocked by the fact that Vietnam, a place I always considered first for vacations, could make me feel this unpleasant. I have occasionally seen ill-mannered Vietnamese people frowning at tourists during my travels, but being ignored like this made me feel terrible. The positive impression I had built up of Vietnam until now was completely shattered in an instant.

The cathedral is currently under repair. Now that I return to Korea, I will tell everyone about what happened today. And the story will definitely end like this: 'Never' go to Vietnam.
We went to see it and unfortunately it's under construction with scaffolding on both main facades
It is under renovation, closed and fully covered.

The screening room was too cold, so cold that the water bottle inside felt like it had just been put in the refrigerator when I went outside. I asked the staff to increase the temperature because it was too cold, but they didn't. This was my first time at Lotte Cong Hoa cinema, and it might be my last. Although it's near my new house, I'll probably never go back; CGV offers better service.

The construction has been ongoing for over a year now without any changes, detracting from the church's beauty, the surrounding landscape, and seriously encroaching on the road. It's unclear when the repairs will be completed and the area restored to its original state.

The website is not mobile-friendly and constantly crashes. Phone calls are also unreachable (at the Cộng Hòa cinema). A large company in this era of massive online shopping investing in such a poor customer service system is a failure.
The movie theater was great, but I'm giving it 1 star because the security guard at the supermarket was terrible (angry, rude, and extremely loud).
Absolutely nothing to see, there's scaffolding everywhere and you can't even go inside. Apparently, the work has been at a standstill for months and months.

I bought my ticket, was "old enough" to get in, but wasn't allowed because I was "too short, too young-looking" 😏😏, and they wouldn't let me "change my ticket" or "get a refund" either, what the hell 😏😏…
The parking situation is very chaotic, especially for those visiting for the first time; they close after midnight and won't let you retrieve your vehicle.
I tried to buy tickets on 123 Movies, but when I went to the store, they said I had to go there to pick them up. I already bought them, I didn't book them!
The staff had a bad attitude; when we were four and bought only two drinks, the staff seemed annoyed and contemptuous of us. 4:15 PM, July 11th
I still can't understand the way churches in Vietnam are managed; churches in Europe don't bother visitors like that. Maybe they need to reconsider their behavior!
Bad luck. When we arrived to visit, it was completely covered in scaffolding. It seems to be undergoing a thorough cleaning.

Closed.

It's currently under construction, which is a shame.
Clicking on anything doesn't bring up what I'm looking for.

Here we have one of those traditional attractions that set trends and become famous, but which, in my opinion, is not worth wasting a single minute of the trip even taking a picture of. It's a French colonial building in a state of obvious disrepair... To put it another way... It's a desperate attempt to copy the famous Parisian cathedral, but made entirely of brick, with broken tiles and gaps in the roof due to neglect, lacking any architectural distinction... not even remotely resembling the original... A fiasco resulting from the empire's haste, no doubt... an insult to intelligence and aesthetic sense... not to mention matters of religious sensitivity... A perfectly dispensable visit... were it not for the fact that it's located right in the heart of that enormous city, making it almost impossible to pass by without seeing it, even if only out of the corner of your eye... The surrounding park-plaza is another matter, reasonably landscaped, with the minor drawback of usually being clogged with frenetic traffic... Visiting this area at night can be relaxing for those staying in nearby hotels...
Apart from the fact that the building materials come from Toulouse, there's nothing to report except for the visiting hours.

First of all, it is only open for 1 hour/day (between 3 & 4). Second of all, you are only allowed to visit the first 10 feet or so. There is a barrier & gate blocking entry but the sign says that you are allowed to go in to pray. Being a CHRISTIAN, I decided to go in, to sit & to pray. But the Vietnamese caretaker, who was holding the door open (I thought she was holding it open for me), GRABBED my arm and PUSHED me back and pointed to the sign about praying. I said yes, I am here to pray. Another woman let me in, but being physically grabbed & pushed had ruined the visit. And in a church of all places. This behavior is appalling. I am a caucasian Westerner who has been barred from entering other religions' houses of worship elsewhere in the Middle East/Asia where the assumption was made that I was Christian, which is fine - that's their prerogative. But I was shocked that someone attempted to do the same for my own religion. I am so sorry that we made a second trip back to the church to see the (poorly maintained) stained glass windows as I was victim & witness to un-Christianlike behavior that I will not soon forget.
You don't get to view anything as they block you from going anywhere. Not worth doing. It's free so you may still want to look but it really is a waste of your time

The cathedral itself was nice looking (from the outside). I was let in to the cathedral with another friend but the other girl that we traveled with was not let in. (not sure what the standard was). Not only that, the gatekeeper grabbed her arm and threw her out when there was a confusion in whether she can enter. We ended up not visiting the Cathedral since our friend was not allowed in. There was a service going on and some tourists were allowed in but some were not. It was not the most pleasant experience.

In my simple Italian, I always think it's wrong to deny access to a place of historical significance. It's more tolerable to impose rules of conduct, such as prohibiting photography, prohibiting entry in beach attire, and even prohibiting speaking, but not denying entry. Moreover, the Cathedral was practically empty, so the excuse of restricting access to members of the faithful for space reasons wouldn't even be justified. My guide didn't explain the reason for this, but he did say that many Europeans are annoyed at having to justify themselves as Christians and practicing Christians to enter. In any case, I didn't like it.

Beautified Version:
Po Nagar Temple is a truly beautiful ancient temple nestled in Nha Trang. I may not know all the details of its history, but the moment I stepped in, it reminded me of Angkor Wat in Cambodia — a place rich in soul, history, and quiet grandeur. The temple is remarkably well preserved, and its atmosphere feels both sacred and grounding.

The entrance fee is 30,000 VND, which I find very reasonable. It goes toward maintaining the site and supporting the staff who take care of this historical treasure. Po Nagar Cham Towers – A Sacred Echo of the Lost Kingdom of Champa 🏯✨

Standing proudly on a hill overlooking the Cai River and the Nha Trang coastline, Po Nagar Cham Towers are among the most iconic and well-preserved Cham monuments in Vietnam. Built between the 7th–12th centuries, they were dedicated to Yan Po Nagar, the Cham Mother Goddess who created the earth, rice, and trees.

✅ What You’ll See:

Four remaining brick towers out of the original eight, each with distinct Cham architectural styles.

The main Po Nagar tower (23m tall), housing a black stone statue of the goddess, worshipped even today.

Intricate sandstone carvings of apsaras (celestial dancers) and Hindu deities.

A panoramic view of Cai River, fishing villages, and Nha Trang city—stunning at sunrise or sunset.

✅ Cultural Experience:

You’ll see Cham women performing traditional dances on certain days.

Local Vietnamese Buddhists still worship here, now calling the goddess Thiên Y A Na—showing how history blended faiths over time.

It’s a living heritage site, not just a ruin, so you’ll feel a sense of quiet reverence.

✅ Practical Info:

Entrance Fee: ~30,000 VND (~RM6)

Best Time: Early morning (cooler, fewer crowds) or late afternoon (soft golden light).

Dress modestly—it’s still an active place of worship.

---

🌟 Verdict:
Po Nagar isn’t just a tourist spot—it’s a spiritual monument that whispers the story of the Champa Kingdom, their rise, fall, and legacy. Standing inside the ancient tower, smelling the incense, you’ll feel like you’re touching a fragment of a lost civilization.

If you’re visiting Nha Trang, this is a must-see cultural and historical gem—a perfect contrast to the city’s beaches.

Ponagar Tower – A Symbol of Cham Culture in Nha Trang

Ponagar Tower is one of the most prominent historical and cultural relics of the Cham people, reflecting the flourishing Cham Pa art and architecture from the 7th to the 12th centuries. Located on the Cù Lao hill (about 10–12 meters high), by the Cai River in Vinh Phuoc Ward, Nha Trang City, Khanh Hoa Province, the site is just about 2 kilometers north of downtown Nha Trang.

⸻

Opening Hours & Ticket Price
• Opening hours: 6:00 AM – 5:30 PM daily
• During Tet (Lunar New Year): Open until 9:00 PM to accommodate visitors

Entrance fee:
• Adults: 30,000 VND/person
• Children: 15,000 VND/person

⸻

Overview & Historical Significance

Ponagar Tower was constructed between the 8th and 13th centuries and is considered a masterpiece of Cham architecture. The name “Ponagar” means “The Mother of the Country” in the Cham language, referring to Goddess Po Ina Nagar, revered as the deity who created the Earth, plants, and taught people agriculture and weaving.

⸻

Unique Architecture

The Ponagar complex consists of three main levels:
1. The Lower Level:
This level originally had a gate and a flight of stairs leading to the middle terrace, but only the foundations remain today.
2. The Middle Level (Mandapa):
A rectangular hall where pilgrims used to rest and prepare offerings. The hall includes 4 rows of pillars: 12 smaller outer octagonal ones and 10 large inner columns.
3. The Upper Level:
This is the sanctuary area with the main towers. The largest tower is about 23 meters high and dedicated to the goddess Ponagar. These towers were built from red bricks without any adhesive, showcasing the advanced construction techniques of the Cham people.

⸻

Ponagar Festival

Held annually from the 20th to the 23rd of the 3rd lunar month, the Ponagar Festival features traditional rituals and cultural performances such as Cham dances, spiritual singing, and offering ceremonies. It attracts thousands of visitors and devotees each year, honoring the Goddess Po Ina Nagar.

⸻

Visiting Tips
• Dress code: Wear modest and respectful clothing suitable for religious sites.
• Best time to visit: Early morning or late afternoon to avoid the midday heat.
• Respect the site: Do not touch ancient relics, litter, or cause damage to the structure.

⸻

Po Nagar Tower is an ancient Cham temple complex near modern-day Nha Trang, Vietnam, built before 781 CE. It was dedicated to Yan Po Nagar, the goddess of the land, associated with the Hindu deities Bhagavati and Hariti, and revered in Vietnamese culture as Thiên Y Thánh Mẫu.

Key Facts:

Historical Significance: Part of the medieval Champa Kingdom, the site reflects Hindu and Buddhist influences.
Architecture: Made of red bricks using advanced masonry techniques. Originally had 10 towers; 4 remain today, with the North Tower standing as the tallest (23m).
Religious Importance: A place of worship for Cham people and Vietnamese devotees, focusing on fertility and protection rituals.
Tourist Attraction: Located along the Cai River, it offers stunning views and a glimpse into Vietnam’s cultural heritage.

The site is open year-round and features a small museum showcasing Cham artifacts.

The architecture is a fine example of Cham artistry, with intricate carvings and impressive brickwork.

The temple provides a breathtaking view of the surrounding city and the river, making it a great spot for photos. Besides the stunning views, visitors can often witness traditional Cham dance performances and ceremonies, adding to the cultural experience. The temple grounds are well-maintained, offering a peaceful atmosphere for exploration and reflection. Be mindful that it can get quite crowded, especially on weekends, so visiting during off-peak times might enhance your experience.
