The Ponagar Tower is a landmark of Hindu architecture.

It's the collective name for a cluster of Champa towers located on a hilltop at the mouth of the Cai River.

The towers were built to worship the goddess Po Ina Nagar – the creator of the Earth, trees, and rice, who protects people from rain, wind, storms, and floods, bringing bountiful harvests and peaceful lives.

There are many tea stalls around, and one of the stalls on the tower itself offers a view of the entire Cai River estuary.

A beautiful temple to see. The colors of the red brick stand out against the bright sky. Various terracotta works adorn the various areas. There is an ancient tradition of terracotta manufacturing, very interesting. At intervals (which I didn't understand, however), you can watch a small demonstration of traditional dances. Admission 30,000 dong
You will also find a mini museum with the history of the various restorations and some ancient artifacts.

The Ponagar Tower is one of the most famous historical sites in Nha Trang, steeped in ancient Champa culture and well worth a visit. From the moment you enter the site, you'll feel the solemn, dignified atmosphere and rich traditional character. The towers, built with original red bricks and intricately patterned, create a beauty that is both vibrant and mysterious, reflecting the Champa culture.

The surrounding area is well-maintained, spacious, with plenty of greenery and wide pathways – perfect for strolling, learning about history, or taking photos of the scenery. The guides here are also very enthusiastic in explaining each structure and sculpture, helping me understand more deeply the religious and artistic significance of the site.

What I liked most was the harmonious blend of ancient architecture and peaceful natural landscape, making the visit not only an educational experience but also a relaxing one. This is a great place for both older adults who want to learn about history and tourists who want to explore local culture.

The Cham Po Nagar Towers in Nha Trang are a place where I literally felt antiquity in my skin. As you climb the steps, the bustle of the city leaves behind, and above, the air is different. The red baked bricks, warm in the sun, smell of time and wind. The towers stand on a hill above the Cai River, offering a beautiful view that evokes silence.

This is a Cham temple complex, built between the 8th and 13th centuries, and it's still alive, not a museum. People actually pray here, make offerings, and offer prayers—and you find yourself within the culture, not just next to it. The main story of this place is connected with the goddess Po Nagar, patroness of the earth, fertility, and maternal power, whom the Cham revered as the creator of the world. I loved how spirituality and architecture merge in one space—the austere power of stone and the gentle energy of prayer.

A striking fact: the bricks in the towers are so tightly fitted that the joints are almost invisible, and there's still debate about the exact binder the artisans used. It's dim and cool inside, which only heightens the sense of mystery. The grounds are rich in detail—carvings, statues, altar areas—where you want to take your time and examine everything carefully. There are plenty of tourists, but the place still retains its power—it's as if the towers aren't trying to impress, they're simply standing there and doing their job.

For me, Po Nagar isn't just a Nha Trang landmark, but a place where you can remember for a moment that the world is much older than our plans and worries.

This place is very different from the other temples we've visited so far in Vietnam. Its architecture and atmosphere are more reminiscent of the Khmer temples of Cambodia. It's still an active site, where religious practices are very much alive.

It's important to note that shoulders must be covered and skirts or shorts are not permitted. However, sarongs are available to borrow on-site so visitors can dress appropriately.

The deity is magnificent, and it's impressive to realize that this place has over 800 years of history. The view of the city is also very beautiful.

A truly wonderful place to visit, both culturally and spiritually significant.

The Ponagar Tower (also known as the Nha Trang Ponagar Tower) is one of the places that left the deepest impression on me when visiting this coastal city. It wasn't because of its grandeur like modern structures, but rather its ancient, peaceful atmosphere and rich Cham culture that made me feel like I had stepped into a completely different space from the bustling life of Tran Phu beach.

On my first visit, I was surprised that the site is located right in the city center, only about 2-3km from Dam Market, easily accessible by motorbike or taxi. The entrance fee is only 30,000 VND, incredibly cheap considering its historical value. Climbing the stone steps, the faint scent of incense mingled with the breeze from the Cai River, calming the soul. The complex consists of four main towers remaining (out of the original 7-8), with the main tower, 28m high, being the most impressive. The tightly fitted red fired bricks, without mortar, and the exquisite carvings on the walls—images of Shiva and Apsara dancers—have retained their mystical beauty after centuries. I stood silently for a long time before the main tower, imagining the goddess Ponagar—the protector of land and agriculture for the ancient Cham people.

The atmosphere here is very sacred; many locals and tourists come to offer incense and pray. I saw a group of Cham people performing a ritual, the gentle sound of gongs and drums echoing—that moment made me clearly feel the cultural exchange between Vietnam and Cham. If you visit during the Katê festival (usually in the 7th lunar month), the atmosphere will be much more lively with shadow puppetry and traditional activities.

However, the area is rather small; a visit only takes about 45-60 minutes. Some corners are slightly commercialized with souvenirs, but it's not too significant. I recommend going early in the morning to avoid the sun and crowds, and taking photos with the ancient tower and the emerald green Cai River in the background.

In short, Ponagar Tower is not only a historical site but also a place that helps us understand more about a part of the cultural soul of Central Vietnam. If you come to Nha Trang just to swim, take some time to visit – you will find your trip more meaningful and profound. A memorable experience, 9/10 in my opinion!

⭐ Po Nagar Tower

Po Nagar Tower is an ancient Cham temple complex located on Cu Lao Hill, right at the mouth of the Cai River in Nha Trang. It is one of the important Cham relics and is still relatively well-preserved.

🔸 Unique Cham Brick Architecture

The towers are built with bricks without mortar; the construction technique remains a mystery to this day. Every brick joint, the reliefs of deities, and the stylized patterns all demonstrate the masterful craftsmanship of the Cham people.

🔸 Worship of the Goddess Po Nagar

The center of the complex is the main tower (Kalan) dedicated to the Goddess Po Nagar – the “Mother of the Land,” the goddess who protects the land, crops, and the Cham people. Inside the tower are Linga-Yoni statues and sandstone statues of the goddess.

🔸 Sacred Space – Overlooking the City

Standing on the hill, you can see the panoramic view of Nha Trang, the Cai River, and the Xom Bong Bridge. The atmosphere is both sacred and ancient.

🔸 Ancient Monument

The tower, built between the 8th and 13th centuries, has undergone various architectural periods and is an important testament to the Champa civilization.

💰 30k/person

⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ Ponagar Tower – A Sacred and Historical Landmark in the Heart of Nha Trang

During my trip to Nha Trang, I had the opportunity to visit Ponagar Tower and truly felt that it's not just a tourist attraction but also a very special cultural and spiritual space. From the moment I entered the site, the first impression was one of solemnity, tranquility, and complete separation from the bustling life outside.

Ponagar Tower was built a long time ago, bearing the strong imprint of ancient Cham culture. The tower's brick architecture impressed me greatly, as the bricks are fitted together so tightly that the mortar joints are barely visible. Despite hundreds of years, the structure still stands strong, creating a feeling of both antiquity and sacredness. The carvings and reliefs on the tower, though not elaborate, are very soulful, clearly reflecting the beliefs and spiritual life of the people of the past.

The interior of the tower complex is quite spacious, offering views of the Cai River and a part of Nha Trang city from above, with a cool and pleasant breeze. I saw many tourists visiting and taking photos, but everyone maintained a respectful attitude and remained quiet. Local people mainly come here to pray for peace, health, and good fortune, creating a very unique atmosphere that combines tourism and spirituality.

What I appreciate most is that the site is well-maintained, with clearly marked pathways and informational signs to help visitors understand the history and significance of the Ponagar Tower. The staff and caretakers are quite friendly, readily reminding everyone to maintain order and dress appropriately.

From a tourist perspective, the Ponagar Tower is a very easy destination to incorporate into a Nha Trang city tour. The visiting time isn't too long, about 45 minutes to an hour is enough to explore, take photos, and experience the atmosphere. This place is suitable for everyone: families, groups of friends, senior citizens, and even foreign tourists.

What I liked most about visiting the Po Nagar Cham Towers was the peaceful and tranquil atmosphere, a stark contrast to the hustle and bustle of Nha Trang beach. Whether you are religious or not, this place offers a very memorable experience.

👉 Overall rating: 5/5

🏛 Review: Po Nagar Cham Towers
Overall impression:
This is one of Nha Trang's main landmarks and a place with a strong energy. If you want to experience ancient history (the towers were built between the 7th and 12th centuries) and see something different from Buddhist temples, this is definitely worth a visit. This Hindu temple complex, dedicated to the goddess Po Nagar, is still active—people still come here to pray.
👍 What I liked:
* Architecture: It's striking how the ancient Cham built from red brick without using visible mortar. The towers look very textured and vibrant, especially against the blue sky.
* Views: The complex is located on a hill, offering a stunning panorama of the Cai River, bridges, and the town itself with its fishing boats. A great photo spot!
* Atmosphere: Despite the crowds of tourists, the towers themselves feel sacred. The smell of incense is in the dim light and cool.
* Cultural program: If you're lucky, you might catch traditional Cham dances performed on the lower platform. 👎 Nuances (what to be prepared for):
* Crowds of tourists: This is a very popular place. Busloads of Chinese and Korean tourists arrive here. Someone is constantly getting in the picture.
* Heat: The area is open, with little shade. Walking around at midday can be difficult.
* Size: The complex itself is quite compact; you can walk around it in 20-30 minutes if you don't wait in line at the shrines.
💡 Tips before visiting:
* Dress code: This is a religious site. Shoulders and knees must be covered if you want to enter the towers. If you're wearing shorts or a T-shirt, you'll be given free gray robes (gowns) at the entrance, but they can be hot.
* Visiting time: It's best to arrive early in the morning (before the opening at 6:00-7:00 AM) or closer to sunset to avoid the crowds and the scorching sun. * Footwear: Shoes must be removed when entering the towers, so flip-flops or sandals are recommended.
Bottom Line:
This is a must-see place in Nha Trang, at least once. Beautiful, historic, and inexpensive. Ideally combined with a walk across the bridge or a visit to the mud baths, which are located in the same direction.

These towers, once dedicated to Shiva, whisper tales of the migration of faith in the wind. The Cham people inscribed the order of the universe and the majesty of the gods upon these structures; every brick embodies reverence for eternal order. Today, amidst the swirling incense smoke, the devout figures of Buddhists can still be faintly discerned. The forms of faith have shifted and transformed throughout history, but humanity's yearning for transcendent existence, like the ever-burning incense before the towers, remains constant. This makes us reflect: have modern people, in the frenzy of rationality, lost this humility and inquisitiveness towards the mysteries of life?

The exquisite reliefs on the towers have been blurred by wind and rain, much like the modern mind worn down by fast-food culture and fragmented information. The ancients dedicated their lives to crafting a tower, imbuing it with their spirit and faith; yet we, in the ever-changing torrent of digital technology, struggle to leave any trace worthy of the test of time. We possess unprecedented efficiency and convenience, but seem to have lost the focus and steadfastness of dedicating one's life to an eternal cause.

The flames of history have long since died out, and the ambitions of dynasties have become ruins for people to mourn. Standing here, the narrative of "progress" seems so pale. Are we truly more "advanced" than those ancients who measured life under the stars and found solace for their souls before the gods? Their world may have been small, but its order of meaning was as solid as a rock; our world stretches infinitely, yet a sense of disorientation echoes deep within our spirits.

The wind whistles through the gaps in the ruined tower, like the sigh of history. What the Po Nagar Tower ultimately reveals to us may not be religious dogma, but rather humanity's relentless pursuit of the meaning of existence throughout time. True wisdom lies not in how much we conquer outward, but in the inner order we build within. Before these ancient ruins, the hustle and bustle of modern life gradually fades away, leaving behind an eternal question about how to settle our own lives.

Introduction to Tháp Bà Ponagar Cham Towers

📍 Location

Located on a hill about 2 kilometers north of the city center of Nha Trang, a coastal city in central Vietnam, overlooking the beautiful bay and the Cái River.

🏛️ Historical Background

Tháp Bà Ponagar Towers are one of the most important religious buildings from the Champa Kingdom period, built between the 8th and 13th centuries AD. At that time, Champa was a Hindu maritime kingdom that ruled the south-central coastal region of Vietnam, engaging in frequent trade with India, China, and other parts of Southeast Asia.

🙏 Significance of Religion

The towers are dedicated to the goddess Yan Po Nagar, one of the most revered deities in Cham mother goddess worship, and a symbol of land and abundance. With the introduction of Buddhism and folk beliefs, she gradually became Vietnameseized and is now worshipped by many Vietnamese as the "Holy Mother of Goddess."

🧱 Architectural Features

The entire site comprises four main towers, constructed of red brick without cement or mortar, showcasing the advanced architectural skills of the ancient Cham people.

Among them:

The main tower, approximately 23 meters high, is dedicated to the goddess Thien Y. Originally, it housed a black stone statue; a replica is preserved, while the original is now in a museum.

The tower is adorned with numerous Hindu-style reliefs and deities, such as Shiva and his image seated on a sacred bull.

🌸 Cultural Fusion and Celebrations

Today, Po Nagar Towers is not only a sacred site for the descendants of the Cham people but also revered by the local Vietnamese. Every year in the third month of the lunar calendar (around April), a grand **"Po Nagar Goddess Festival"** is held, featuring music, dance, rituals, and traditional costume performances—an event of profound cultural and religious significance.

🧭 Tourist Highlights

The elevated location offers panoramic views of Nha Trang city and the bay, making it ideal for photography.

Cham women in traditional clothing offer bouquets and incense, showcasing a living testament to their faith.

There is also a small museum introducing Cham history and culture, suitable for those seeking to gain a deeper understanding of this ancient civilization.

The complex has three levels, with the most prominent being the group of four towers, the main tower being approximately 23 meters high, dedicated to the Goddess Thien Y Ana. Smaller towers are dedicated to other Hindu deities such as Shiva and Ganesha.

The Ponagar Tower in Nha Trang is one of the most representative architectural and religious structures of the Cham people in Vietnam, located on Cu Lao Hill, near the mouth of the Cai River, in Vinh Phuoc Ward, Nha Trang City, Khanh Hoa Province. The entrance fee is 30,000 VND per person. Built between the 8th and 13th centuries during the flourishing period of Hinduism in the Champa Kingdom, this complex is dedicated to Thien Y Thanh Mau – the goddess considered the mother of the land, who taught the people farming, weaving, and pottery. The Ponagar Tower complex consists of three levels: the lower level, formerly a gate tower, now only has its foundation and steps remaining; the middle level is the Mandapa – where devotees prepare offerings; and the upper level has four main towers, the most prominent being the tower dedicated to the goddess, approximately 23-25 ​​meters high, built of sturdy fired bricks, decorated with intricate patterns and reliefs depicting gods, mythical creatures, and fairies. Beyond its exceptional architectural and artistic value, the Ponagar Tower is also a significant religious center for the Cham and Vietnamese communities in Central Vietnam. Every year, from the 20th to the 23rd of the third lunar month, the Ponagar Tower Festival takes place here, featuring many unique cultural activities that attract numerous pilgrims and visitors. With its long history and profound spiritual significance, the Ponagar Tower is not only a source of pride for the people of Nha Trang but also an attractive destination for both domestic and international tourists.

Po Nagar Temple complex, located near Nha Trang, is maintained by a small Hindu minority in Vietnam. It was originally built in about 781and dedicated to Yan Po Nagar, a goddess connected to the Hindu goddess Bhagavati. A gold statue of the goddess was stolen by the Khmer and replaced by King Indravarman I with a stone statue. After many years, the Vietnamese reoccupied the area and took control of the temple. Made of brick, the outside of the temple has been worn away by pollution and heavy rains that hit the region during monsoon season. The area also is surrounded by formal gardens with walkways for meditation.

As a family, we're always on the hunt for spots that aren't just pretty but offer a bit of learning too, and the Po Nagar Towers absolutely knocked our socks off. Stepping onto the site, we weren't just admiring the gorgeous old architecture; we could really feel the strong historical footprint of the ancient Champa Kingdom. Taking the kids around the complex, pointing out the ancient bricks and the bas-reliefs that have stood for centuries, was a much cooler and livelier history lesson than any textbook could offer. The atmosphere here is quite spiritual but still super approachable, making it easy to talk to the kids about the unique culture, beliefs, and building artistry of a once-powerful kingdom. It was a brilliant bonding experience—getting the whole crew to appreciate and learn more about a rich and proud slice of Vietnam's cultural heritage. Po Nagar Towers is genuinely a memorable historical outing for all ages.

♵In my travels, I always visit the places of the Force of those regions, or countries, where I am. I do this for my "recharge" because working with people always expends a lot of mental energy, and places like this have a powerful energy that can quickly recharge your "battery"⚡ 🇻🇳In Nha Trang, one such place is the Cham Towers of Ponagar, located on Mount Cu Lao, overlooking the sea, the Kai River and the city, it is a complex of temples erected in honor of Buddhist and Hindu gods, over 1000 years old! The legacy of the powerful state of Champa (or Champa), which existed in the 2nd to 19th centuries A.D., before Vietnamese emperors invaded its territory. 🛕Ponagar Temple has been built for five centuries (7th-12th), it is a shrine of shrines and a piece of eternity in the heart of Nha Trang, these ancient structures inspire strong feelings of admiration, awe, respect🙏 🔅Ponagar Temple Complex - three-level. On the first level there are columns, an altar and a stone staircase. On the second level, a platform where pilgrims could rest and prepare to perform rituals. On the third level are towers. 🛕Each tower has its own features:  🔅Central tower (12th century). The smallest tower erected in honor of Shambhu, the god of fertility. It is he who is usually asked for children by barren people. The altar in this tower is decorated in silver.  🔅South Tower (12th century). It is dedicated to the god Shiva, who is one of the top three deities of Hinduism and represents both male and female. That is why the Lingham sculpture, a symbol of these beginnings, stands in the tower.  🔅Northwest Tower (10th century). It was erected in honor of the god of wisdom Ganesha. On the inside, it is decorated with many inscriptions in ancient Cham languages.  🔅North Tower (11th century). The tallest tower. The temple of the mother goddess Po Nagar, especially the goddess revered by the ancient Chams, is believed to have passed on farming and handicraft skills to the human race. The height of the sanctuary is 23 meters. Inside is a statue of Po Nagar. The facade of the entrance to the tower is decorated with an ornament depicting Shiva, a saddling buffalo.  🪷Believers most often pray to the goddess for the well-being of their loved ones. But it is also the case that these prayers are not about specific destinies, but about the entire population of Nha Trang and its surroundings. You can find this at religious festivals. Like at the Thap Ba festival. 🌸There are also daily performances on the grounds of the temple, such as folk Cham dances performed by beautiful girls, to the sound of national instruments. And the whole area of the temple complex is simply drowned in flowers that exude their aromas, create a charming atmosphere around😇  🔆And of course, by tradition, I left my Mandala here as a tribute to this sacred place, at the statue of God Ganesha🙏

Ponagar Tower (Tháp Bà Ponagar) was built between the 7th and 12th centuries AD. It is a representative Hindu architectural style, somewhat reminiscent of Angkor Wat, but much smaller in scale. Cham Towers of Po Nagar in Nha Trang. Traveling around Vietnam, one of my stops was in the city of Nha Trang, formerly a fishing village, which later became a favorite destination for tourists. Since this was not my first time in Vietnam, according to tradition, one of my obligatory visits is the Cham towers. Getting there is not difficult by public transport (local buses); for those who like to walk, you can also walk there, examining the surroundings and the life of the Vietnamese. You can purchase your entrance ticket at the entrance gate; you definitely won’t be able to pass it by. Next you find yourself on the territory of the temple complex. Immediately after entering the complex, large pillars rise. These are the columns of the former ritual hall. Here sacrifices were prepared and ritual dances were performed. Next we go up the stairs to the towers. The towers are the most ancient relics of Nha Trang. These towers were built between the 8th and 12th centuries, during the existence of the kingdom of Champa or Champa. The temple complex is built on a hill on the banks of the Kai River, which flows into the South China Sea. It was in this place, according to the legend of the Champa people, that the mother goddess Po Nagar emerged from the foam of the sea onto the shore. As soon as her foot touched these lands, rice began to grow here. Mother Goddess taught the Chams to grow and process rice. And she herself moved into a luxurious palace and acquired a harem of 97 husbands. Yes, yes, then in the principality of Champa there was a matriarchy, and only women could afford a harem. But there is another interpretation of the origin of this goddess. According to Hindu mythology, Po Nagar or Parvati is the wife of the god Shiva. Parvati is the mother of the elephant god Ganesha and the god of war Skanda. Therefore, on the territory of the temple complex there are #temples of this entire family: Parvati herself (Po Nagar), the Shiva temple, the Ganesha temple and the Skanda temple.  In general, the temple complex previously consisted of 6 temple towers. But only 4 have survived to this day. But they came to us in excellent condition.  The mystery of making bricks for the construction of these towers has not yet been solved. After all, they are more than 1000 years old, and these bricks did not get wet, did not become covered with mold and moss, and almost did not collapse. The thickness of the brick walls of the towers is more than 1 meter!

The four Cham temples of the Po Nager complex date from the early period of Cham architecture and date from around or after the year 781. The temple complex is dedicated to the goddess Po Nager. The main sanctuary is 25 meters high. Above the main entrance to the main sanctuary is a depiction of the Indian goddess Durga. In the central sanctuary, at the entrance to the main cella, there is a Nandi belonging to the god Shiva. However, this statue is no longer in its original position. Of the four temples, three are in a row. Remains of sculptures made from bricks can still be seen on the temples. The statue of a Garuda can still be seen on the fourth, free-standing temple. In addition to the brick architectural decorations, stone sculptures can also be seen in the upper area of the main sanctuary.

Po Nagar Cham Tower is a Cham temple tower founded sometime before 781 C.E. and located in the medieval principality of Kauthara, near modern Nha Trang in Vietnam. Visitors can enter the temples and see the elaborate buddhas and shrines and if not appropriately dressed there are smocks you can use. It was quiet the afternoon we visited as the coronavirus is having an effect on tourism. There is a showroom with a lot of photographs and some costumes. This would be an ideal place for information boards about the history of the place and its current use and status in Vietnam. Well worth a visit and then a walk along the river.

My friends and I took a taxi. We arrived in the morning, and there weren't as many people as we'd feared. They gave us robes, and we could go into the temples. There was a drummer and dancer show going on.

Then I went to Po Nagar Cham Towers, a complex of brick towers built between the 7th and 12th centuries AD, belonging to the Cham culture. Originally, there were eight towers, of which only four remain, but the most impressive is the one on the north side, which is approximately 28 meters long. Its exterior retains its pyramidal shape, and inside, a sanctuary houses Buddhist relics and ceremonial centers, where it's common to see locals entering to pray. I forgot to mention that the entrance fee is 20,000 VND.

I don't know any history, I was just going for the ride. Overall, I was pleasantly impressed by this place; now I need to read up on history. The churches turned out to be active, and the interior was intriguing, with its ancient scents.

We were very pleased with our visit to the towers. The guide told us about their history and the traditions associated with them while still on the bus. It's interesting to learn about the history of the country you're visiting.
Beautiful and ancient, I know more about the local culture and history, you go up there and you can see the surrounding sea, so wonderful.

We got there quickly on bus number 4. I highly recommend it as a sightseeing tour—it's a beautiful place. You're given protective clothing to enter the temple, and the scent of incense is strong inside. I noticed that not only there, but even at a regular fruit stand, people light it and leave offerings of flowers, fruits, and drinks to attract good luck and wealth.
A rather curious place, it conveys the atmosphere and spirit of ancient India, the entrance is not expensive and it is worth it, inside the temples there are characteristic statues of Hindu deities
Good service, friendly staff, and cool natural scenery, suitable for everyone to visit.

The temple was really beautiful and nice. Great for photo-taking. Had to pay a small entrance fee to get in, but definitely worth it. Although the area was small, it was still really nice with a great view at the top. The place can be a bit crowded at times. There’s souvenir shops and a bar to buy drinks. You can even enter the temple with proper attire that’s given. Apparently there are dancers who will perform but unfortunately I did not get to watch the performance. Still, a trip that’s worth going. Also, there’s a lot of walking and steps to get to the temple
A truly remarkable place. I'd love to visit again. Beautiful views for memorable photos. Arrive around 7 or 8 p.m., when the crowds aren't too crowded.
Definitely go—the excitement is guaranteed, and you'll get some great photos to remember it by. It's best to go early, right before it opens, as there are fewer people and you won't be in the shot.
An interesting place; we visited in the late afternoon. There are beautiful views of the river and bridge. You can enter the towers; special robes are provided.

Nice history and a nice view from the top of the tower. Good place to take some photos and see a bit of the city on the road. Most restored, this temple is a good introduction to Cham architecture. The dancers are charming.
This is a historical building locates very near to Nha Trang. It was back to the Hindu period. The building is very beautiful

It is a Hindu temple not far from the city center. It is my first time visiting a Hindu temple, and it is unique. There are also many places where you can take pretty photos. The admission fee is about 1,000 won for adults.

Yes these are well worth a look as are fascinating and just have a great feeling about them when one is there. The entrance fee is low at 20,000 dong so that everyone can afford to enter there. You get a souvenir plastic ticket but this is kept by the machine at the entrance to use again! There is a small coffee and cold drinks bar there which again is very reasonably priced - try the local coffee with condensed milk! Have a good look around at the bonsai and inside the towers. There could have been explanation boards in English that would have been good for tourists. There is a stall selling souvenirs that does not want to trade and didn’t seem friendly so would avoid if I were you. Overall a great place to visit and would highly recommend. Enjoy.
Entrance fee is 20,000 dong. There are souvenir and food shops on the premises. The area itself is not large, a visit takes about an hour. The towers are beautiful.

We visited here in the morning and it was very busy but well worth a visit. Lots of steps so not for everyone. Lots to see and temple attire was available if you want to go inside. We walked across the bridge from here into town, not the best of ideas in 30 degrees but gave us an insight to local living.
The place is very colorful and interesting. The temples are impressive. It's best to visit on your own and early in the morning; locals come to pray and there are no tourists.

It's hard to believe the towers were built in the 7th and 8th centuries; they're so well preserved. It's a pleasure to stroll around the grounds, watch dancing, visit the small museum, watch mat weaving, look down on the city from above, and take great photos.
Enjoyed the towers. Not spectacular but well worth a visit. People still worship there so need to be respectful.

Po Nagar Cham Towers, gives a very good idea of building many years ago, without having to go to Angkor Wat. Downside was, it was very very crowded, had no chance to take a pic without at least 20 people on it. Pity, but understandable.

There's something sacred and peaceful about this place. The Ponagar Tower is hundreds of years old but remains undamaged. The architecture is still beautiful and very sturdy. If you visit during the festival season, there are many unique cultural aspects worth seeing.

We were there in January 2020. There really were a lot of Chinese people. It's hard to take a good photo. Admission was 20,000. There was a performance and drumming. It was beautiful, but there was nothing to do there for more than 40 minutes.

It's a beautiful place. It's good that they've started restoring it. I think it's a must-see. There's another temple nearby that's rarely visited by tourists. The locals recommended it. I'll post a photo.

Great. A great spot for photos with plenty to see. Unique historical, architectural, and religious facilities in an area with large Buddhist and Catholic populations. Personally, I was very satisfied. Naturally, it is crowded. Entry may be restricted for sleeveless tops and shorts. Worth historical sight to visit. One of the best photo spots. Reasonable admission fee. Spent 1 hour, including chilling out on the top looking out the city.
The place is beautiful and historic. I love nature and old architecture; everyone should visit.
Needs to be seen, very cheap entrance fee, it is rather busy at all times, the surrounding shops are very expensive by comparison.
I recommend going there by bus, it's very interesting and beautiful there, I love Buddhas and Asia))) Magnificent Vietnam)
Hello! We visited this complex twice. It's a very picturesque place. There are plenty of great photo spots and beautiful panoramas. The temple complex is a must-see!

It's best to visit in the morning, as there are many tourists in the afternoon. A taxi from the European quarter cost 80,000-100,000 dong each way. An interesting place for history and architecture buffs.

Not a bad place to visit, I recommend walking there rather than taking a tour. It's only an hour's leisurely walk from Lotus. The views along the way and once there are stunning. Chinese people with selfie sticks are everywhere, shouting and pushing each other.

We traveled on our own, on bus number 4. We showed the conductor where we were going on the map, and he showed us where to get off, although it was difficult to get through. We could already see from the window that it was time for us to go. It was a very beautiful place. Yes, there were a lot of people, but that didn't stop us from taking amazing photos!

You can go there yourself, city bus #4 went there from our hotel, the fare was 9 thousand dong, we were there in cloudy weather, but I recommend going there closer to sunset - the sight is beautiful, ancient towers, I think they are from the 12th century, we were there during our last stay in Vietnam, back in 2014, but little has changed...
It's better to go to this attraction early in the morning or late in the evening, because there are CROWDS of Chinese and other tourists there.

I went in the evening of December 30th, 2019. We bought entrance tickets for 22,000 VND per person, prayed and made wishes, then walked around. There were a lot of people, and more kept coming, but it was beautiful.

One of Nha Trang's most popular attractions! Four towers remain standing to this day. Anyone interested in exploring ancient Cham structures, dating back to the time when Nha Trang was known as Kawthara, is welcome! The complex is open from 6 a.m. to 6 p.m. Entrance is 20k. Sunscreen and water are recommended.

It was interesting to see one of the old Hindu temples in Vietnam. There were Shivalingas on the towers and Champa dance every hour. Beautiful view from the top. Local people visit and offer prayers. Lord Shiva and the Goddess Devi, The Mother Earth deity was decorated very beautifully.. Peaceful place.
Very nice place, but really hot too. Entrance fee is only 22,000 VND per person which is about $1.  There are stalls inside and outside, selling bags and clothings.

Very impressive towers or columns with statutes....have a rich historical of the Cham people. The view from the top is photogenic. Well worth spending the time walking around and admired the beautiful layout of these historical towers. Lots of space for everyone, even though it was busy.
A beautiful, historic place. There's an entrance fee. It's hot, so I recommend bringing an umbrella. From here, it's a short walk to a seafood restaurant popular with the locals.
There is a lot of history at this attraction so it's worth going there and reading the information boards in the museum and walking around to get a feel of what it was like.

The Champa Kingdom had a pretty long run in Vietnam spread across a few centuries and this is the best preserved relic from that era.Though it is in active use but the place is more of a Tourist attraction for Chinese & Russian tourists by the bus loads.The views are amazing and they also have a Cham dance performance every hour.

Nha Trang is a resort city so there isn't much to see, but this was one of the few places worth visiting. The atmosphere didn't quite fit in with Vietnam, which was refreshing. I think the entrance fee is 22,000 VND for adults. You can walk there from Nha Trang Station, but I recommend just taking a Grab.

This is a complex of several temples. You don't need to buy a tour; you can read everything online (Google is a good option). The grounds are not large—you can walk around them in an hour or an hour and a half. IMPORTANT! When entering the temples, you must remove your shoes and cover your arms and legs. If you're wearing shorts or revealing clothing, there are hangers with hoodies—you can borrow them to cover up. The towers are located right in Nha Trang; you can get there by bus or taxi. Within the city, the whole thing will cost around 300 rubles, in our money. Entry is 220,000 dong, which is 660 rubles. The place itself is beautiful and ancient, but there are a lot of Chinese tourists who shy away from it...sometimes it's annoying—they're everywhere. There are some great photo opportunities. I definitely recommend visiting.

It opens at 6 a.m. and is a must-see attraction in Nha Trang. The red brick tower is very distinctive, and there are many Hindu gods on the tower. It's a very unique building, and there are a lot of tourists. There are also people dancing!
A fantastic spot, and a must-visit when traveling to Nha Trang, very old and historic; from here you can observe a part of the city.
A must-visit spot when in Nha Trang, it's amazing, very old-fashioned, and from here you can also see a part of the city.
If you are a history buff and love temples then you need to go. Granted it will be packed with tourists it is worth a visit. How often do you get to see an 8th century temple?
Do not miss this temple, It’s busy but still worth the visit. Vietnamese people line up to pray for the goddess Ponagar.

Interesting site, can be very crowded st some times, but after waiting some 15 minutes, it was much more quiet (maybe due to some large bus groups leaving?). Definitely worth a visit and the fair entrance fee (to date 22k D per person).
The many pagodas in Vietnam may have a similar look, but this place is completely different and super beautiful! And old! I think it's worth a visit

The Po Nagar towers reminded me of the temples of Angkor. The same intricate patterns, a sense of mystery. Of course, most of what you can see is a reconstruction. In the local showroom, you can see what buildings were there before the reconstruction. The area is small, but there are many interesting places, including a traditional dance. Admission is 22 kd.
I highly recommend this place; it's cozy and atmospheric. I went with a friend. There's plenty of room for photos. We got there by motorbike. Entrance is 22,000 dong.
A very interesting site. need to learn more when visiting the Cham Museum in Da Nang. The small display room gives only some limited information.
Visiting the towers felt like we were touching the roots of Vietnam. The unique architecture, miraculously preserved to this day, is a must-see. We were thrilled!

We stopped here during a sightseeing tour. There were quite a few Chinese people there :) apparently, you can't go anywhere without them. The location is very beautiful for photos, and 40 minutes was enough for us to explore. You can drive there yourself and buy a ticket at the entrance.

It isn't large, but if you go to Nha Trang, I highly recommend stopping by to take a look, even if just for a moment. The view is nice, and there is even a traditional dance performance, which is great.

For a nominal entry fee we were able to spend about 1 hour wandering around the attraction admiring the architecture, venue and bonus views back across to Nha Trang.  It was very busy despite being early in the day but there was plenty of shade and a nice escape from the heat.  We saw sellers and people begging being actively moved away from the entrance and not inside, so it was possible to spend the time without being hassled. Reasonably priced cold drinks were also available inside.  There were many bus loads of tourists taking selfies from all sorts of vantage points so you need to be prepared to wait your turn for the good pictures.  Tourists were also being approached by official survey takers asking about the venue and experience with well founded questions so it’s obvious the people in charge are interested in making it a worthwhile experience for all.
It’s a beautiful place to check out but it was quite crowded when I was there so you may have to wait awhile to get a good picture
It's a historical site, and you can take a bus there yourself, which is exactly what we did. It was a great way to experience history and take some great photos.
Very nice. Everything is great from people scenery to food. It was a wonderful trip. Will definitely come back again.

It's very beautiful and soulful! The view of the city is absolutely stunning! You can buy incense and burn it in the temples. Chinese tourists are brought in by bus. There's an entrance fee, and parking is also charged.

This place ideal to take photos. Need 22,000VND for the entrance.At the entrance,there are many hawkers selling fruits. You may need to bargain for the price of you want to buy. In the place, there are also souvenir stalls.Visitor need to wear decent clothing to enter the tower. There are robes to be wore at the side of the tower. There is also a mini showroom displaying the photos of the towers. Can be listed in a must-go place in Nha Trang.
This is a beautiful temple, well worth the visit if you like seeing stuff like this. An affordable thing to do at VND 22,000
Nha Trang is so beautiful, everyone! Let's travel all over Vietnam! #vietnammylove #NhaTrangMYLOVE

I visited for the second time, and I want to go back again. The atmosphere is charged with positivity, despite the noisy crowds (everyone knows who I mean). On the first visit, we didn't see the girls performing, but this time, as we were getting ready to leave, we stumbled upon a girls' show.

really a highlight of my trip. have to go early to take good picture to avoid crowd tourist. Take your time, walk and enjoy everything including the tree and plans. worth paying 22,000 entrance fee. Hope that they can provide more history information in english.
Temple complex was built 8th and 11th centuries by the Cham people who once ruled the Central Plain of Vietnam. Entrance fees is 22k vnd.

Temples of another civilization and culture than the European one. You don't understand them but you never stop admiring them for their uniqueness..The sanctuary area has a park for a walk and a souvenir shop. Nice experience

It was a worthwhile tourist attraction in Nha Trang. I heard it used to be a Hindu temple of the Champa Kingdom, but most of it was destroyed, and now only about two buildings remain. It is a great spot for taking photos, so there are many tourists, especially Chinese tourists... It seems difficult to take pictures without people in them. It isn't very high, but the view looking down from the top is refreshing and nice. The entrance fee is 22,000 VND, and it seems that children over about 100cm tall have to pay as well. It is one of the places you must visit if you go to Nha Trang.
A place where you can see some traditional vietnamees dancers, for more informations refer to a guide

The towers are included in the tour operator's sightseeing tour. But why pay $16 per person when you can get there yourself? Take bus number 4, stopping at the Galina Hotel. The conductor will charge you $7 for the ride and help you get off at the right spot. Entry is $44 per person. The tower itself resembles Cambodian ones. There are dancing shows for tourists on the grounds.

Go as early as you can. We visited about 2-3pm and you could barely move there was that ma y Chinese tourists coming straight off busses into the place. Almost ruined our experience but the place is still amazing and it's definitely worth a visit
A two thousand year old temple, right next to the river. Interesting, beautiful and only a short taxi ride from downtown.
Small temple however the place is folded by Chinese with no manners and ettitqutes. Totally ruins your experience.

We visited the Po Nagar Towers Museum Complex in Nha Trang, located behind the bridge over the Cai River. The towers (only four remain) were part of a temple complex built between the 7th and 12th centuries. This is a spiritual site where Buddhist ceremonies and rituals are held. Each of the four towers is dedicated to a specific deity. The tallest tower is 28 meters. The towers are located on a mountain overlooking the city.
Fabulous place full of culture, a must though doesn't take long to visit. It certainly is worth the time

We visited as part of a sightseeing tour from Anex. There are a lot of people here. You can walk around the whole place in 30 minutes. The views of the city are beautiful. If you don't have a hat, cover your shoulders, and keep your pants below your knees covered, you can go inside with your shoes off. There's a small museum dedicated to the site, displaying cultural artifacts. There are also columns left over from the meditation hall.

My family and I visited the Ponagar Tower. It's a magnificent structure, situated in a beautiful location on 2/4 Street in Nha Trang city. Looking down from the top steps at the city and the river flowing into the sea, the view was breathtaking. It's an ideal place to explore the architecture of the Cham people, with its ancient and unique towers. However, there were so many Chinese tourists that it was difficult to find space for photos. Hopefully, on our next visit, my family and I will be able to take more beautiful pictures to create lasting memories.

Perched on a hill not far from the beach, this ancient temple has a certain charm. Firstly, its elevated position offers a beautiful view of the sea and the city. Secondly, the landscaping is superb, with lovely flora and, above all, cleanliness. For US$1, it's definitely worth a visit.
Many tourist especially from China going up and down this place. Very interesting and beautiful and sunny too. Can view nha trang city from above. There's live cultural performance too.

The place is simply wonderful. You can go inside the towers, but you need to cover your knees and shoulders. If you are not wearing appropriate clothing, you can rent one on site. There is a beautiful view of the river. They show miniature dance performances.

Located on a hill next to the Nha Trang River, this Hindu temple was built by the Champa Kingdom. Most of the temple was destroyed by attacks from the Javanese army in 774 and 784. Although it was rebuilt around the 10th century, a fire reportedly obliterated most of the ruins. Inside the central tower, one of the three towers, is a statue of the goddess Po Nagar, created in the mid-11th century. "Po Nagar" is said to mean "Goddess with Ten Arms." As it is a crowded tourist spot, it is recommended to visit early in the morning.
Very interesting construction and especially how the building got constructed without cement or any natural glues. Just fit the stone perfectly into each other. Very energetic place, very positive.

Beautiful ancient structures. We were lucky, we arrived early in the morning and managed to see the Towers without the crowds of Chinese. They appear after 9 a.m. in wild crowds... If you want to take beautiful photos, come just before opening. There are so many beautiful views there.
These buildings are very impressive, it takes your breath away how old they are and the fact that they are still in operation.
It's quite an interesting place; it's best to just go there without a tour or time limit, so you can stroll around peacefully and enjoy a beautiful sunset.

A beautiful place, worth a visit. Getting there is quick and inexpensive by taxi or bus. During peak season, there are a lot of Chinese people, just like everywhere else. Admission is 22,000 dong, and it's open all day. It's worth taking the time to visit this site and also the Catholic church and the rock beach. Everything is close by and can be done at once.

A wonderful, atmospheric place. The towers are very ancient and beautiful. We took a taxi from the Lotus Temple; there are fewer people there in the evening before closing. For those sensitive to energy, this hill offers a very strong sense of joy, bliss, and peace. A must-see! You have to experience it. You can offer fruit and flowers to the temple.

We visited this place with the wonderful guide Dmitry Selivanov. It was fascinating to listen to his story about the history and modern-day Ponagar Cham Towers: why they weren't designated a World Heritage Site, what places of power can be visited there, and the structures and sculptures on display. The place is iconic for the city. And, of course, it makes a big difference whether you visit on your own, having read online reviews, or with someone who will introduce you to all the towers' mysteries and even the mistakes of local reconstructionists. In any case, whether you decide to go on your own or with a guide, this place will not leave you indifferent. You can wander around and explore for hours.

The colors look great even in quick shots. Ah... the husbands must be exhausted. I struck a ton of poses too. They seem to go there to take photos, not to sightsee the temple. Wow... there are so many Chinese people. As expected of the mainland; they are everywhere. But you shouldn't be walking around like this in Vietnam or other hot countries. If you're a couple, go; if you're a family, just go somewhere fun. You could probably just pass by in a taxi and think, "Oh, this is it," and just take a look.
This is a good place to visit in Nha Trang when you need sightseeing rather than relaxation. Be cautious as it is always crowded. Although it is not spacious, there are many things to see.
An interesting place where the buildings and walls inside have preserved a piece of history. You don't need to buy a tour; you can take bus number 4, but be sure to protect yourself from the sun.

Po Nagar Cham Tower is a Hindu temple located nearby modernized city center, Nha Trang in Vietnam. Its complex was built between 8th and 11th centuries by the Cham people who once ruled the central plain of Vietnam. Po Nagar Cham Towers is also one of the most famous cultural and historical relics in Nha Trang.

This landmark is a must-see. If you're staying in the European quarter, you can get there by Grabr for about 40-50,000 dong. The towers themselves are very beautiful, and their architecture is stunning!
It is a place worth visiting at least once. Although Nha Trang doesn't have much in terms of sightseeing, this place is worth a visit. It takes good photos, and the red tower is quite fascinating.

If you like looking at history of how people in the past build things this is one place you best not miss. It’s a very colourful place they have used red stone and when it’s dry it has a mat look at it they had been raining it takes on the more glossy look. A great place to take the children they will enjoy it has playing hide and seek also a great opportunity to get your camera out and take some good photos. Plenty of opportunity around the place. The place itself is built up a small hill overlooking the river and Nha Trang, where are you can get some great shots on the camera there. Is a wonderful atmosphere there as well when you’re wondering around certain carpus of the place surround you. If you are asking if it’s worth a visit easy answer to that, yes. Just one thing first thing in the morning can be very busy with all the Torres going there especially the Chinese in the afternoon later on is far better,,, you may also get a good chance of a good sunset.

For a solo visit, it's great! Entry is 22,000 dong, easy to get to, lots of beautiful Instagrammable nooks, plenty of space to explore. Lots of tourists? Well, yeah, like everywhere else. Arrive early for a quieter time. Or come later.

We visited ourselves, taking bus #4 from the city center. Tickets were 20 dong. We watched a dance and music show, touched some antiques, and met some Chinese people, chatted, and took some photos together. We loved it all!

Visit the place at almost 4pm. Probably because of low season, not much tourist & i can still enjoy & wander around. The area was bit small but the tower was in good maintenance (& look good on photo). Plus the time it's just right for sunset.
Interestingly, we got caught in a tropical downpour, thanks to the staff for saving us!!! Interesting music and dance program.

A divine place, literally! At the top of Cu Lao Mountain, from where you can see all of Nha Trang, stand the Po Nagar Towers. Locals consider them a powerful spiritual place, so Buddhist ceremonies and a whole host of rituals are held there. We reached the towers without any problem: not far from our hotel (Crown Nguyen Hotel) is the final stop for bus number 4, which goes directly to this place (the fare is 9 dong, about 30 ours). Rituals are held inside the towers, and statues of goddesses are located there. You can't enter with bare knees, shoulders, or shoes. But especially for people like me 😁, there's a cloakroom on site where you can borrow robes to enter. It's a truly enchanting and quiet place, if only the Chinese weren't so noisy—there are tons of them there!
It's very beautiful, you can touch a piece of antiquity, there are beautiful statues and an altar to the goddess Ponagar, go there first!!!
This landmark is a must-see. It has its own atmosphere. It's best to go early in the morning to avoid crowds.

I was here on a sightseeing tour. I loved it; it was impressive. The tribes that once inhabited this area, where matriarchy reigned, erected these structures. They're majestic, truly magnificent. There were a lot of people, but it didn't bother me. Considering the temple's age, the masonry material (the bricks are held together) is astonishing. It's still holding the main structure together, thousands of years old (!) after all. We only discovered cement relatively recently... Some of the masonry was replaced by modern workers; you can tell by the color of these areas; they're a different shade. To enter the temple, women must cover their arms up to the elbows and legs up to the knees. At the entrance to the main temple, they provide robes if you don't have suitable clothing.
Beautifully restored since my last visit 10 years ago. Very busy though with many tourists visiting this site. Well worth the entry fee though.

Very impressive historical site to see fascinating structures which are 1200+ years old. Very spiritual place even with bus loads of cruise ship passengers visiting same time as us. Good place to take a break from the beach for an hour or so and soak up some history.

We found that there are not many cultural places to visit in Nha Trang or at least weren't obvious to us. So we went to Ponaga Towers and Long Son Pagoda and we loved both. Ponaga towers are only a short taxi ride from the centre. Worth the visit! And there is a nice little market outside as well.

We visited in the late afternoon when most visitors had gone as it can get really busy. An amazing, well renovated temple to visit and only a short walk across the bridge from the busy beach front road. With good views from the top.

We went to the Cham Towers on our second day of vacation, on our own, without guides. It's a beautiful place, but it's unrealistic in the heat, and there are a lot of Chinese people there. But the photos turned out beautiful.

This is the most magnificent Cham tower complex I've ever seen in Vietnam. It's great for taking photos, has a place to light incense, and a solemn atmosphere. The scenery is beautiful and impressive. The entrance fee is 22,000 VND per person. It closes at 6 PM.
I think this is the only attraction worth visiting in Nha Trang. And you can do it yourself.
This is a sacred place left behind by our ancestors, and it's quite interesting; standing here and looking down, you can see a beautiful view of Nha Trang city.

It's a place of great historical value, built between the 7th and 15th centuries AD. There's a small museum documenting its appearance before restoration. Tank tops and shorts are acceptable, but you'll need to wear a free robe to enter the temple. It closes at 6 PM, so it's recommended to arrive around 4 or 5 PM for a small dance performance; the sunset view is absolutely stunning and will surely make you want to take many photos.
We got to this site ourselves on bus number 4, which was very quick. It's best to go there early in the morning. Then you can enjoy the beauty in peace and quiet.

We came to see these towers without too many good expectations, assuming that its a tourist trap in a resort.  What we found instead were really magnificent pieces of ancient architecture. They are masterfully restored - everything original was kept, and the repaired brickwork merges very well with the original. Additionally the whole area is well arranged, with a small park and a restaurant which serves ice cream and cold beer with a view on the tower and the panorama of the city.  There are a lot of tourists there, so either you are ok with that, or should go very early in the morning.  Nonetheless, its a place that would be worth seeing even if it would be 2 hours drive away - and its in the edge of the city instead. Go and see it, you wont regret it.
Short taxi ride from town, we visited in the afternoon when the weather was not so hot. Nice temple, they had a dance show. Good experience.

We visited the Cham Towers during a sightseeing tour of Nha Trang. It's a very unusual and beautiful place. There's a lovely garden with exotic plants on the grounds. We also visited Buddhist temples. I highly recommend it!
This website about the nearly extinct Cham civilization is very interesting. It's a shame there aren't more explanations and historical maps.

Of course, there are a lot of Chinese people, but what would you do without them? But the place is great, you can take lots of photos ❤️ We went down to the river and visited the temples. It's a very green area with lots of flowers 🥰
Very well maintained, we even attended the dance performance, no need to wear trousers, they lend a blouse to enter the temple!

You absolutely must visit this place when traveling to Nha Trang, and remember to tip the Cham ethnic dance troupe. They are preserving these dances. It only costs about 40,000 VND for a taxi from the city center.
A small historical site with 4 towers still standing. Very cheap to go and see and very easy to go around. Well worth a visit. Cost was 20,000 dong, less than a £1.

I agree with many who have visited: these towers are more interesting early in the morning or in the evening. There are indeed a lot of Chinese people, but if you really want to, you can always take a photo. You might ask why the temples are stuffy, but it's because they lack the ventilation system we're accustomed to. Every people has their own traditions and their own religion. And it's truly a miracle that after so many centuries, the temples are still standing. For those who are tolerant of foreign and even alien religions, I recommend visiting these towers and other temples!

A wonderful, beautiful Hindu place! Visit either early in the morning or in the evening. It's very hot... To avoid the heat spoiling your spiritual enlightenment, do three things right: bring water, cover your head from the sun, and, as I mentioned above, choose the right time.

It's a very beautiful place with a huge number of visitors. The crowds of Chinese people, scurrying around chaotically, taking photos of every bush, stopping in the middle of the road, slowing down traffic, spoiled the impression a little, but it's definitely worth seeing at least from the outside, without going into the temples!
Beautiful temple. Worth a visit. Go in the morning when it’s not too hot or crowded. Cloaks are provided for free to enter the sanctuary.

It located near Nha Trang,and it is a must visit place if you come to Nha Trang.the temple dedicated to Yan Po Nagar,the goddess of the country.It consists of three levels, the highest of which encompasses two rows of tower.the place not that big,and rather crowded,you can see some believers worship here.there is a small museum inside,and some stalls selling souvenirs,light food and drinks.you can watch Champa traditional dancing here at specific time.since it located at the hill,it has a good view of Nha Trang city,even you are not believers,still can feel the charming of this Cham temple.
Beautiful but full of Chinese and Russians, but it only costs 20,000 VND per person and you can walk there in 40 minutes or take a taxi for 2 euros.

We arrived during the harvest festival; there were a lot of people, all bearing offerings. Someone even slept in the garden with a suitcase under his arm. But we loved the atmosphere and the view! Although we didn't take any photos.

The towers are truly worth visiting at least once. It's like stepping into another civilization. The relatively small tower was packed with tourists, but not crowded. I highly recommend not taking a sightseeing tour to such places. It's a shame to waste time visiting shops designed for Russian tourists and then rushing through such beautiful places. It's better to take a taxi or a bus and spend as much time as you want!

I strolled around the towers for an hour and a half before closing. Their color in the setting sun was simply magical. I found many interesting things: a small one-room museum, shelves of ceramics, and a short musical performance. There was also a spontaneous attraction: a very beautiful shooting angle from a pile of boulders. There was a line of people climbing, photographing, and simply observing. The place itself by the towers was so pleasant that I didn't want to leave. The towers are still functioning temples. They are very impressive, although small inside. The surrounding park is picturesque, as is typical in Asia. The view of the river from the top of the hill is poetic. If you have a little time and don't rush, you can get comfortable and soak in the atmosphere. The historical details are well covered online, and once you're there, it's easy to soak in the beauty and antiquity.

An amazing site to visit if you like location/country history and only a very small entrance fee. It's a religious site, so respect is required when entering the religious monuments. It can easily be done in an hour. There is a cafe on site (although a little pricey).

Love this place. Having places to sit with shade and wind flowing though from the beach at the very hot weather. Great views to the city from the top and great spots to take pictures to the city, bridge, and ocean. Strongly recommended this place if you are in town.
Really excellent. Do take off your shoes and go inside the temples. Because especially the biggest one has Some impressive sights
We took the bus there ourselves, walked around a bit, it's very beautiful and majestic... and there are also markets nearby where you can eat seafood, cheaper than in Nha Trang itself.

It feels a bit new in Nha Trang (like Cambodia??), which is nice haha. It's good for looking around, taking photos, and sightseeing, and the view is great once you go up~ It's also the place where I saw the most Koreans while walking around downtown Nha Trang~~ The entrance fee is 22,000 VND.

There and back on a city bus. We arrived early in the morning to avoid the heat and the crowds. The conductor asked us where we were going, probably to avoid missing our stop. We were dressed appropriately for the place. The place is interesting, somewhat reminiscent of Angkor in Siem Reap. Girls are dancing. There's a small park where you can sit on a bench in the shade, and a nearly tame crow pestered us. There's a small building with an exhibition of photographs of Po Nagar before its restoration.

Good morning!! We've been going to Vietnam for as long as we've been, and we'll keep going to the Cham Towers. They're a unique, unforgettable sight! The colorful dances of professional dancers and music complement the scenery of a thousand-year-old civilization. The towers are built of solid brick and have burned down several times. The interior is stunningly elegant! Everyone is welcome! New features include turnstiles and a move to the ticket office. Natalia Trusova

We organized this excursion ourselves. We left the hotel, caught a bike taxi, and off we went. The towers are a MUST see, as they're unlike anything else! The photos turn out great! And nearby, you can see the "non-touristy" part of Vietnam. It's very crowded and a bit hot, so it's best to arrive in the morning for those who are afraid of the sun. You must be covered to enter the tower-temple.

We were here on a tour at 4 PM. There were crowds of Chinese people, but it wasn't too hot. The place is atmospheric and beautiful, and it's worth a visit, but you should go early in the morning before it opens. Then you'll have a chance to explore everything at your leisure, take your time, and take photos of the towers, not the Chinese people.

We stopped by after returning from a day trip somewhere up north of the city. Its magnificent and one of a kind in a much smaller scale than My Son close by Hoi An. though we already went to My Son, its still worth stopping by for a look. There's also a dance show - depicting how people used to gather water, etc in the old days which is nice.
The place is very beautiful. We got there ourselves on bus #4 (ticket 9,000 dong per person). Entrance to the Po Nagar Towers is 22,000 dong.

We visited this place during a city tour. It's a great place, the first temples we saw in Nha Trang. You can rent a robe (free) if you're wearing shorts or bare shoulders and enter the temple. But there are a ton of Chinese people here, as there are throughout Nha Trang. I highly recommend it!

We visited last week me and my friends is palace was lovely looking good and we feel glad see this palace isn't too old art made by humans hand inside palace was so clean and fresh have a so many kind temple and so many interesting and wonderful art so we recommend when u are in nah trang you differently visited This palace

We took a tour of these towers with Pegasus. I want to digress and warn those visiting Nha Trang for the first time with this company: don't take the tour guide from your hotel guide. It's a scam for a shopping tour with exorbitant prices. After five hours of exhausting presentations on medicines, latex pillows, tea and coffee, and jewelry, we were finally taken to this cultural site. I think if we had arrived there ourselves in the morning, instead of in the thick of it, the experience would have been much more impressive. But otherwise... It was a truly captivating sight. At the foot of the towers, girls dance to traditional live music. The whole place exudes antiquity, grandeur, and... the Chinese. They're like locusts, just everywhere. I don't have a single photo where there isn't at least one Chinese person in the frame. They're everywhere: pushing, yelling, smoking (which is unthinkable for us, because our people would never allow themselves to smoke near an active shrine). This screaming and smoking mass spoils the whole impression. You could go inside the towers, light a candle, think, etc. But... You'll definitely get pushed, barked at, etc. by those glorious people from the Middle Kingdom. Bottom line: you have to go there yourself, preferably in the morning before the heat. Then it's not so stuffy and the Chinese are still asleep.

The towers are impressive. We took a guided tour of them. We didn't spend much time in the complex itself. We strolled around the grounds, and were allowed to enter some of the buildings. As far as I remember, there's a mini-museum of sorts inside. I remember being told to take off your shoes upon entering.
An interesting complex, there are places to see and listen, there are always lots of people, but it's worth it! The only downside is that it's very hot!
I really enjoy visiting ancient sites; they are very special, possessing a unique characteristic that makes them unforgettable to me.
classic, monumental, magnificent scenery of the Cham people. The customs, temples, costumes, ancient worship objects and are quite different.
Beautiful😍 Crowds of Chinese, but we were somehow lucky, there were only a few people who arrived, we managed to take some photos) but after 15 minutes, friends from the Middle Kingdom arrived

Nowhere near the magnificence of Angkor Wat, Cambodia or Ayutthaya, Thailand but still worth the visit.  Of course, with all these sorts of things, there is an entrance fee. A ticket can be purchased from the booth for 22,000 Dong (£0.73) per head.  Although on the way out we noticed a gap which people were just walking through and going up!  The place reminded me of Ayutthaya in Thailand which is a whole city of ruined temples much older and much more interesting than this.  It was still nice to go and see!
Hindu temple of Cham people, when they were still Hindus. Well protected and maintained heritage site. Nice architecture. Reach before 11 for less crowd
If you've never seen ancient temples before, you'll love it. The place is atmospheric, but it's best to visit at off-peak times to avoid the huge crowds. I really enjoyed the place itself.

A magnificent temple that will leave no one indifferent, especially architecture buffs. Picturesque and beautiful from any angle. Go in the morning to fully explore and take photos, otherwise, the entire experience can be ruined by the gigantic crowds of screaming, dehumanized Chinese.

The place is beautiful and the gardens are very well maintained. We were able to see a dance performance in the gardens, which was lovely. Wear shoes that are easy to slip on and off to enter the temples without too much trouble.
A trip to the Po Nagar Cham Towers is included in the free Nha Trang sightseeing tour. I highly recommend this tour. It's very interesting.

I heard that this place has a 1,300-year history, and it was a historic site that was simple yet showed that history. It was too hot during the day so I didn't even think about going, but I started exploring around 5 PM. The sight of Po Nagar bathed in the sunset light was impressive, and since it is located on a high hill, it was the best to enjoy the cool breeze while taking in the view of the sea below!!!
Definitely worth seeing, and since it's on a promontory there's also a beautiful view of Nha Trang and the river. We saw a wonderful music and singing performance there, with costumes.

The Panagar Towers in Nha Trang are a must-see. These towers are over 1,000 years old. They are very well preserved, and you can walk around the temple grounds and touch the ancient walls. You can go inside, but photography is prohibited. If you have exposed knees and shoulders, you'll be provided with a veil. You can buy souvenirs on the grounds, and you can even catch a concert, as I did. The towers are usually included in a sightseeing tour offered by hotel guides, but you can also explore independently.
Very cool place to visit and it’s a must see with the dancing and song sing praying every where and it’s a large area with lots of singing
Worth to visit especially after 3pm because the sun is not too strong and able to take more nice photos.

It was truly thrilling to touch such ancient walls; as a history buff, I was impressed. You can spend maybe an hour there; there are so many Chinese people wandering around, it's hard to take good photos. But I highly recommend visiting.

My husband and I visited Nha Trang for the first time in May 2019. The Po Nagar Towers were on our must-see list, as they're located within the city limits and preserve centuries of history. There's an entrance fee, but it's inexpensive—just 22,000 dong per person. The first thing we felt upon entering the park was an incredible energy, a sense of antiquity. Ancient structures surrounded by tropical greenery and... the sounds of Chinese people all around...! That last detail was perhaps the only thing that prevented us from truly enjoying the complex! You can enter the towers and even make wishes. However, to enter the main tower, it's important to cover your shoulders and knees. If your clothing doesn't meet the requirements, you can use the clothing provided within the park, completely free of charge. And there's plenty to see beyond the towers. It's a truly interesting and unusual place.

We came here on a weekend, and it was very crowded. It's hard to take good photos, but it's easy and inexpensive to get here by bus from the center. Admission is low, and the views are beautiful. Souvenirs are expensive.
A cozy and quiet place, everything is very beautiful, worth a visit. The main thing is that the dress code must be specific, shoulders covered, otherwise they won't let you in.
They put on a performance. Local dances. You can buy coconuts and lots of souvenirs at the entrance. There's a beautiful view of the river and the Champa Hotel.

Besides swimming, there's quite a bit to see in Nha Trang (churches, Long Son). But in my opinion, the most interesting thing is this over 1200-year-old Hindu temple of the ancient Vietnamese Cham people! The complex isn't too large and is manageable even in the usual heat. What I found most fascinating was that the bricks were laid seamlessly using a technique unknown to us today and therefore impossible to reconstruct. Considering the age of the structure, it's truly remarkable.
The towers warm you with the warmth of Vietnam and make your wishes come true if you believe and wish hard enough. This is a place of worship and veneration for the Vietnamese.
Gotta check this place out when in Nha Trang! But be prepared for heaps of tourists!!! entry fee is cheap.

It was very hot when I was in Nha Trang. But I still decided to go during the day. It's a great Vietnamese attraction. I consider it a must-see on any trip to Nha Trang. The only downside is the sheer crowds of Chinese, wild Chinese...
This place is amazing. I love the mix of restored stuctures with the original stuff. Easy to get to.
You can see the ruins of the Champa Kingdom. Until the 18th century, the region south of the central area of ​​Vietnam was ruled by the Champa Kingdom. The sunlight is intense.

I highly recommend visiting this place. No one is indifferent. The entrance fee is 22,000 dong per person. It's open until 6:00 PM, but it's best to arrive before 5:00 PM. Besides the Cham temples, there's a nice garden and park with rocks and sculptures. The restrooms are clean and spacious under the stairs. There are excellent panoramic views of the city. You must enter the temples barefoot and with your shoulders and knees covered. They provide free robes, which are hung on hangers, and there's a mirror and benches. Everything is very well-organized and well-kept. I really enjoyed it.

An interesting tourist attraction, an ancient architectural complex, looks very impressive and beautiful from the outside. Inside, there's very little to see, just a table with a Buddha statue on it. You can borrow a free robe at the entrance, as it's not customary to wear outerwear with knees uncovered. It gets crowded during the day, so it's best to visit early in the morning or 1.5 hours before closing. Tickets cost 22,000 dong. There's a souvenir shop, but there's not much to buy. Behind the main tower, there's a small building displaying photographs of this architectural complex at various times.

I went to the Ponagar Towers on a recommendation. The place is impressive, and you can feel the spirit of antiquity. There's a wonderful view of the city from there. But there was a downside: the large crowds from "China." I recommend visiting.

We arrived here without a guide, on our own. The place is truly captivating, a sea of ​​impressions! The best part is that everything is free, even the temple is open; if you're wearing shorts or a short skirt, you can borrow a free robe. After these towers, I was eager to visit India, as the buildings are very ancient and beautiful.
This is a must see if you are in Nha Trang. It is right in town and takes about 30 minutes to see everything. Very beautiful and great ruins.

The temple complex consists of the main temple dedicated to Ponagar (which we couldn't enter because it was open only at 12:00 PM due to preparation of offerings) and additional temples. In the back are shelters and areas for locals, who gather there with their families to eat and pray. It felt like a marketplace, but there were a lot of tourists. I didn't sense any history or atmosphere. There are two souvenir stalls and a restroom. To enter the temple, please wear clothing that covers your shoulders and knees; you can borrow some for free (gray robes) to the right of the main temple.

Shoes are not allowed inside the shrine. Incense and candle offerings are also prohibited due to the smoke. The view is great, so it's recommended to come here as early as possible to get a feel for the town. It's a large temple, so there are plenty of casual, affordable restaurants in the surrounding area.
Was very delighted to witnessed a brief VN traditional cultural dance apart from having a close view and many snap shots of the remarkable historical structure.
When visiting Nha Trang, you absolutely must come here; it's a place that gives you a very real and complete sense of the historical life of the city!
It's definitely worth going and seeing. We were lucky and even witnessed folk dancing. An ancient ethnic culture and people who still live on and impress with their beauty and attention to detail.

Nha Trang city is always hot, and extremely hot during day-time. Po Nagar tower is photogenic, but if you visit before 5pm, you may want to hide in a shadow. My suggestion is visiting after 5pm, especially during sunset. It's located on the way to Hot springs so you can combine with the swimming and spa treatment in Thap Ba hot springs.
