Very interesting. You can get there by city bus number 4. There's an entrance fee, but it's not expensive. The towers themselves are slowly being restored. You can go inside. It's very interesting. There's a small museum where you can learn a little about the structure (though you'll need to know English or Vietnamese). I recommend everyone visit.
This is my first time seeing a functioning Hindu Cham temple. I've only seen ruins or abandoned stupas in Vietnam before. I'm interested, but it's more of an amateur or specialist's experience.
Ancient Asian architecture. A religious site. It's best to book a guide, but you can also wander among the Buddhist buildings on your own.

An unusual place that combines astonishing beauty with a certain mystery. You can simply stroll here and admire the beauty, or you can enter the temple and experience something that will take your heart by storm. And it doesn't matter whether you're Buddhist, Christian, or atheist. And be sure to watch the Vietnamese girls dance with bowls on their heads.

It's a beautiful place, I really liked it. The only downside is that it's very small. A very leisurely stroll took about half an hour to forty minutes. Entrance is 22,000 DNG per person. We got there from Nha Trang (the Louisiana bar area) by taxi for 45,000 DNG, and took a bus back for 16,000 DNG for two.

The grounds are spacious and airy, overlooking the sea. It's an ancient site, but we're grateful to the locals for their contributions in preserving it so that everyone can learn more about Champa culture.
Thoroughly enjoyed the view, the history and the ambience. The music and dancers were an additional joy.

This is a practicing temple with many worshipers still lighting incense and praying, and so a quiet place, which as a tourist we have been allowed to invade, something lost on some tourists, it was lovely.

It feels even more mysterious because actual locals use it! It is very hot, but the course is very short, so it is worth visiting at least once!! There aren't really any dress codes except for the main temple~ They even provide rentals, so you don't need to worry!

The Cham Bani people in the Ramawan Festival: If the Kate festival is considered the biggest festival of the Cham Brahman community, then the Ramawan festival is the biggest festival of the Cham Bani community. This year, the festival takes place from May 6th to June 3rd, 2019. Before preparing for the Ramawan festival, the Cham Bani people go to the Ghur (cemetery) to perform the tomb sweeping ceremony in each clan and each palei (village), marking the first day to begin the Ramawan festival. The second day is the ancestral worship ceremony. The third day is a fasting and prayer ceremony at the shrine dedicated to the Po Acar priests.
A must see in the region, an example of preservation and multiculturalism. A little bit crowded (go in the early morning, it's hot there!)
An interesting place, and it's great that it's well maintained. We visited it ourselves, taking a bus. There were a lot of Chinese tourists.

A place where history breathes... That's how I'd describe the Po Nagar Cham Towers. I was fortunate to visit this place and sense the aroma of history. A visit to this place is part of a sightseeing tour of Nha Trang. The Po Nagar Tower is the largest of the four towers in this architectural complex. It gives it its name. The Po Nagar Towers complex was built during the reign of the Cham King Hanrivarman in the early 9th century. This complex consists of two separate areas. The first has a row of pillars and a brick staircase leading to the site of the four towers. Their walls are built of bricks laid closely together, using almost no binders. The exterior is decorated with bas-reliefs, columns, and facets. During our stay, Vietnamese girls performed traditional dances. The visit left a very lasting impression.

A magnificent place. The temples are well-preserved, expertly restored, and meticulously maintained. The park is wooded and shady; if it's not too crowded, it's a haven of peace offering a stunning view of the fishing port. There's a scooter parking area. There's an entrance fee, but it's very modest.

I really liked it. It's beautiful and colorful, there are a lot of tourists, but it's not too much of a nuisance. There are even some spots where there are no tourists at all. A great place to take some great photos.

If you're in Nha Trang, be sure to visit this place. Even with the large number of tourists, it's very cozy and offers some great views. It's very easy to get there—bus #4, which runs through the entire city, stops nearby.

The Po Nagar Cham Towers boast a unique architectural style of the Cham people. They are very close to the city, about a 15-minute taxi ride from Nha Trang. If you're in Nha Trang, you should definitely visit this place.
We rocked up about 30 minutes before sunset/closing. It was busy but not overly crowded. The towers have been restored so in places look rather new. Good views of the harbour.
This place is worth to visit, nice structure and not quite far from city center. Should come in early morning when it is quite. I was lucky when coming here before big groups
A wonderful place. The energy in the temples is strong. A very beautiful garden, views of the city and the Kai River. A 10-minute taxi ride from Lotus costs 70,000 d. A must-see.
Interesting! I want to visit again! Vietnam is a very ancient country. I'll recommend it to my friends! It's easy to get there by bus number 4.

A unique monument to the country's pre-Vietnamese history. The Cham people who built it still exist today, including in Vietnam. I read online that the temple complex was built from the 7th to the 12th centuries, so each of the surviving towers has its own distinct architectural style. The scale of the restoration work carried out during the monument's restoration is impressive, clearly visible in the amount of modern brickwork. The towers are still used as temples today. They are located on a mountaintop, offering a beautiful view. There are plenty of photo opportunities. The grounds are well-maintained, with plenty of greenery. The entrance to the central, tallest tower is adorned with a sculpture of the goddess Po Nagar. Numerous images of her can be found throughout the complex, especially among the souvenirs sold there. The downside is the crowds of tourists from well-known countries, mostly busy taking endless selfies. There is an entrance fee. We didn't have a guided tour; we wandered around and explored everything for quite a while. On the one hand, this is good, but on the other hand, I would like to know more about this place.

Location is just within the city. It has quite a charm. The temple is very nice and the surroundings are clean and inviting. There's also a cultural dance but seems like that the performers are not too happy performing.
Beautiful views, I recommend going further to the therapeutic mud, clothes to cover the knees and shoulders are provided for free

A must-see place. Since you've come to a new country, you need to see as many sights as possible. The towers are located within the city limits and can be easily reached by bus. Entrance is 22,000 rubles. Lots of tourists (Chinese). I suppose when there are no tourists, there's peace and quiet here. The question is, when are there no tourists? It's a beautiful, captivating historical site. You must enter barefoot and in a special robe (they fit any size).

Hello, let's get straight to the point. There are only four towers: one large and three small ones. The grounds are very compact. Inside the towers are altars left over from the Cham Kingdom. The Cham were absorbed by the Vietnamese; there are about 500,000 of them left. This is the only remaining temple complex of theirs in the world.
It's very interesting, but there are a lot of Chinese people, absolutely unceremonious, it's better to come early in the morning before they're there :)

Went on the day tour and this came with it! Learned soooo much about these towers! What did they use for the brick morter back then? It's a mystery! Very interesting! Used for worship. Great to learn about!
What a lovely, active temple that displays all what’s best of the past and merges into our present. Despite the crowds, it was well worth a visit!

The temple is very close to town but a huge tourist attraction. There are almost always 20 plus busses lined up and parked around the temple. At the temple there are a number of other garden spots to walk around and see plus a small museum. You need to be properly dressed (no short shorts) to go inside - they have clothes you can where at no cost if you need them.

Since we love everything old and historical, we absolutely loved it. The towers are large, and you want to get a taste of antiquity. It's better to go on your own than with a tour. A tour can take anywhere from 40 minutes to an hour, but it's definitely worth it.
A very beautiful place. It's nice to touch the country's 10th-century history. A captivating atmosphere in the center of the metropolis. A fairy tale.
As an architect, it was important for me to visit this landmark - the architecture is different from the rest of the architecture on the coast, it stands apart.

One of Nha Trang's most ancient and important historical monuments. An impressive and interesting structure, it houses a small museum, drumming and traditional Cham dance performances, and offers a beautiful view of the city, as the Cham Towers are located on a small hill. The view is marred by the throngs of Chinese people, who prevent good photos and a leisurely stroll, but what would you do without them? So, ignore them and simply enjoy the ancient sights.

The Po Nagar Cham Towers have been restored, so they look new. I prefer the old-fashioned look of this place. But if you haven't been there yet, you should visit and learn about its history. The towers and the sky look very beautiful. Great for taking pictures.
Definitely a visit, it's educational. The towers themselves are quite ancient, but they look beautiful. Be sure to go there to see what they're all about; the guide will explain the rest.
We visited the Ponagar Towers. It's beautiful, it brings you closer to the Eternal! Vietnamese women in costumes perform dances here.
Collection of towers on a hilltop. Many shrines and a great view over Nha Trang from the top! Dates back to 9th Century but has been restored in recent times.

The view of the towers is stunning, despite the crowds of tourists. The grounds are spacious, with plenty of photo opportunities. The staircase is picturesque. People play drums and dance in traditional costumes right there. There's also a loom where you can watch them make bags and purses.

We took a guided tour. It was very heartwarming and beautiful. Despite the tragic historical events, the monument is in excellent condition. Beautiful scenery, pleasant benches around, but the price tag is exorbitant... And of course, it's a great photo spot.

without meeting all around Chinese people in a Vietnamese famous place, this is wonderful to travel and discover. Be constructed in VIII century, more than 1000 years ago, it was destroyed by time, sun and wind, but the structure of it was still a mystery. They didnt use cement to stick, which is now common. We came on Tet Holiday, where they set some cultural worker to do something like: handicraft, dancing and weaving.

nice piece of history close to town. you can still find tranquility is a few corners of the place if you unfortunately time your trip after all the tour bus holds get there. early morning later evening after sunset probably more relaxing.

We reached the Cham Towers on foot—the journey from the city's tourist center across the island takes about two hours. This Buddhist temple was built in the 8th century and was later restored with public funds and local residents. It's a beautiful place, perched on a hill, with a stunning view of the sea and city. The red-brick towers are hidden behind trees. Like other temples, it has excellent landscaping. There are secluded corners with benches where you can relax and enjoy the view. The temple grounds have clean restrooms and a small museum with ancient stone sculptures. Guests were treated to a performance featuring music and dancing with pots and heads. Admission is very inexpensive: 10,000-20,000 dong.

The place is quiet and has an ancient feel. The towers are very beautiful and are located in a separate area. Some of them have access inside. To enter, you need to wear special clothing, which is available free of charge for both men and women. You can take it off and put it back on when you leave. It's a fascinating place! I highly recommend visiting; you won't regret it.
Entry fee is really minimal n what is surprising is right smack in the tourist area w river n sea you see this hillock with these old towers from another era. Love the performances too within
It is so pretty. It is a bit inconvenient to walk around with a baby because you have to go up stairs, but it is beautiful and cool. It is great for taking photos. There are a lot of tourists.

This landmark is 14 centuries old, but it's quite well preserved. The towers are located on a small hill and are visible from afar. Robe-like coverings are required to enter the temples, as knees and shoulders must be covered. The place is very crowded, but definitely worth a visit. As you approach, locals sell fresh vegetables and fruits, as they do elsewhere in Nha Trang.

My friend and I took a regular minibus to the Cham Towers. The bus ticket was 8,000 dong, plus the entrance fee was 22,000 dong per person. The towers are very beautiful and tall, with several prayer areas. It's very hot inside and smells of herbs and incense. You can wear regular clothing, although they provide a free robe-like garment with Velcro fastenings. Children's robes are also available. We went inside barefoot, and photography is only permitted without a flash. We were very pleased and impressed. We recommend everyone to visit.

A beautiful place. It's sacred. You can feel the energy of the place. The only downside is the Chinese tourists, there are so many of them. The price is only 22,000 rubles. There are taxi drivers at the entrance, which is very convenient.
Worth a visit; free on tours, but individual visits require a fee. Visitors will explore Hindu temples.
It's easy to get there by public transport. The Cham towers are definitely worth seeing; they're a beautiful place.
Visit the Po Nagar Cham Towers to admire the beauty of this Cham tower. It's an ideal and beautiful place to travel.

If you're vacationing in Nha Trang, I think visiting these towers is a must. Firstly, it's very reminiscent of the Cambodian complex, albeit on a smaller scale. Secondly, it's a truly atmospheric place, so you don't need to book a tour; you can just get there yourself. Incidentally, contrary to popular belief that children in Asia are limited to 120 cm, our 10-year-old, who is 142 cm tall, was admitted for free.

Since my purpose for visiting Nha Trang was relaxation, I didn't really go around to any tourist attractions, but I visited this place because it is an old historical site. There aren't many places in the city center that could be called historical sites, so there are only a few people here.

I really liked it! Excellent views of the city. A global layer of history. Visit the showroom to see the restoration story. It's a functioning church—you can only enter in protective clothing, which is provided at the entrance.

Our vacation wasn't without its cloudy days, but we made the most of them. On one of those days, we went to the Po Nagar Towers. A taxi from the city center cost 70,000 dong, and the driver immediately understood our destination when I showed him a picture. Entry is 22,000 dong, free for children. Like everywhere in Nha Trang, it's crowded. At the foot of the temple, there's a cafe, a souvenir shop, and a small market near the ticket office with traditional clothing, although the quality of the fabric leaves much to be desired. There are many ancient buildings, and you'll want to take photos of each one. You can enter the temples, but you must wear clothing that covers your face, or they provide special capes (they even provide capes for children), and everything is clean and ironed. Photography is prohibited inside the temples. The place exudes a sacred aura, despite the crowds of tourists. Overall, an hour is enough to walk around and take photos.

This is a must visit site in Nha Trang and we visited as part of a half-day tour.  It was only rediscovered in the 1930’s and renovated again.  The towers were built by the Cham People between the 7th and 12th centuries. There were many tourists when we visited but still thoroughly enjoyed the visit.
In Nha Trang, where there aren't many famous tourist attractions, this is one of the few historical buildings. It's best to access it by taxi.

We visited here in 2018 December after finding out about it by accident. It is clean and beautifully kept with many organized places to park and many spots to take frame worthy pictures.  It is less crowded if you arrive in the morning with less tourists. It's gives you an opportunity to explore with out being bombarded by tourist buses. The admission fee is really reasonable ($1 USD) and though most people only spend an hour or so, if you take time to enjoy the beauty that surrounds you, you could easily spend 2 or more there.  It's worth a visit. P.S It's also worth just paying the parking fee right next to the temple instead of looking for places several blocks to save a dollar.

Nha Trang is one of Vietnam's most famous beach resorts. It has become the Vietnamese "Benidorm." It's one of the few remaining vestiges of the local ethnicity amidst the frenzy of high-rise buildings and hotels that characterizes the city today. It's not spectacular, but it's a must-see for tourists.

It's beautiful there; you can stroll for half an hour or an hour, admire the views of the city from there, catch a fan-dancing show, and the towers themselves are ancient and awe-inspiring. Of course, there are a lot of people there, but that's to be expected. You can easily get there on your own, without a tour. They're just across the bridge from the center, in the north of Nha Trang.

Po nagar is one of the older temples in the area, and is north of the City Centre in Nha Trang. Dress code applies, but there are coats to wear if you do not have the correct clothes. Some parts of the temple have been renovated/repaired, and this has been done very skillfully for the most part. A must see in Nha Trang. Take a cab or a grab from the hotel if you are on the Southern beaches though.
Built between the 10th and 12th centuries, it crosses the Kai River. The towers are made of red-orange bricks and have unique shapes. Lots of tourists there!

A picturesque place, lots of interesting plants and bonsai. Very beautiful! But there are a lot of Chinese people, blocking everything. Although it's cool to take pictures of the Chinese too. There's Wi-Fi on the benches.
It's atmospheric. It's well-kept. The staff is friendly. You can feel the strong energy of the place. There's even a squirrel living near the towers.

You have to visit this temple if you are in Nha Trang, as it is the main cultural attraction of the town.  Price 20.000VD and you will need to make some steps to go up. You should have your knees and shoulders covered, if not please wear the special attire kindly provided by the temple.  If you are lucky you may witness a beautiful traditional dance.

We stayed at Vinpearl Discovery and paid around 100K VND to get to this place. You pay 40K VND entry for 2 people and it is worth every Dong. Po Nagar is a Cham temple tower founded sometime before 781 C.E. and located in the medieval principality. It is dedicated to Yan Po Nagar, the goddess of the country.

We walked to this site from the main part of the city, an easy 1hr stroll along the beach front. Cheap entry (22 000 vnd pp) and despite the popularity of the historical site there was plenty of room to take photos and casually look around.

The towers are a must-see, even though the temple is over 1,000 years old and still in use. Dress appropriately, including covering your knees and waist. If you're not dressed appropriately, don't worry; the temple staff will offer robes. It's best to go in the morning, as climbing to the observation decks in the blazing sun is a bit of a hassle. However, the view from the observation deck and the majestic white Buddha statue are worth it. You can get there from Line 1 by bus #4; the conductor will tell you where to get off. Admission is 22,000 dong. You can also buy various souvenirs there, at reasonable prices. The only thing is, when you approach the Buddha statue, there's a sly Vietnamese woman on duty offering you incense sticks. Once you've got them, she tries to shake down your money. The solution was simple: we shrugged our shoulders and said there was no money, and the answer was, "Oh well, that's the Vietnamese, gypsy-like business..."

A charming historical site, highly recommended when visiting Nha Trang. Entrance fee is only VND 22,000, and there's a motorbike parking lot nearby. It's a great place for taking photos; I suggest allocating about an hour for it.

Many people come here specifically for the Cham culture. The buildings have stood for hundreds of years, making them quite impressive ancient structures. It's a great place to learn about Cham culture and religion. There are many fruit and sugarcane juice vendors nearby, and the coconut jelly is quite good.
Nice temple, rainy day, not much to see or do. Parking is across the street. Ticket is under $1. Many stairs.
Magistic ! I really wonder how the ancent people can build so magistic pagoda! Nha Trang is so nice every time I come
This is a beautiful spot, well worth it. The temple is well maintained and the area is clean with good views. Nice little museum to give you all the information you need about the temple.

This is a must-see! It's best to go early in the morning—a bus costs 4,000 dong or a taxi costs 50,000 dong. There are few tourists at this time. It's a great time to stroll, admire the scenery, and recharge. The temple complex is perfectly maintained, and Buddhist rituals are held here. Sacrifices were once made here, but now only flowers and fruit are visible. The Cham Towers are a religious structure over 1,000 years old! Those "inappropriately dressed" are given free monastic-style clothing. Of the 10 towers built between the 7th and 12th centuries, only four remain, and the fifth is in ruins. The Vietnamese revere this place and believe that the towers protect Nha Trang from disasters. This place has a special aura.

"A place of power"—that's how you feel in this place. Try visiting it at the end of the day. There are fewer people. It's quieter. You can go without a guide. Be careful what you wear. You need to respect Vietnamese traditions.

Worth a visit during your stay at Nha Trang. Serene buildings with majestic views of the bay. Easy to get to on the bus if confident or taxi can drop you off out front. Not easy if climbing stairs difficult for you and the terrain is uneven in parts. Beautiful though so make the effort if you can.

We passed by on a Sunday and was crazy busy with bus groups- maybe caused by the two cruise ships on the bay- but on the Tuesday when we passed it was not. Stopped and loved walking around. Views are gorgeous. And it is pretty incredible that this has survived for so many years. Well done Vietnam for preserving it so well. Definitely worth visiting, right in the city, overlooking the river.
There are plenty of great photo opportunities. The place is perfect for strolling and is very clean. A nice sight to see if you're already in the city.
A beautiful place with a vibrant energy. It's worth arriving at opening time or after 4 p.m. to calmly enjoy and understand this place.
It's a nice place, but there are a lot of Chinese people there. You can get there on your own. We arrived on a moped, and it was raining, so we managed to take decent photos.

An interesting temple complex, but we didn't get to see everything because we were on a sightseeing tour with Coral Travel and didn't have much time. As soon as we arrived, a flood of Chinese tourists descended on us. There were lines to get into the temple, and we couldn't really take photos, as they have no sense of tact or decorum. We enjoyed the fan dances, and then the drumming began. If you want to enter the temples, you must wear clothing that covers your elbows and knees. There are lockers nearby with robes, including children's sizes. Definitely go there if you have the chance! The architecture is very interesting; I thought it resembled Khmer architecture.
It's worth visiting, but I recommend not overpaying for a tour. It's easy to get there quickly by bus and buy tickets. Then transfer to another bus and visit the Big Buddha.

I really liked this place. There are four towers, and you have to cover your shoulders and knees to enter. To the right of the towers, there are hangers with robes you need to put on. You can enter each tower and make a wish. You have to take off your shoes before entering the tower; the Chinese people are just barging in without taking their shoes off, but that's just the way it is, they're Chinese, they're always intruding. A must-see!
We got to the Po Nagar Towers ourselves by taxi, which is very cheap in Vietnam. We paid the entrance fee, but it takes about 30-50 minutes to see them.

We went on a tour with the tour operator Pegas Touristik, visited a Buddhist temple, made a wish, I think it will come true, behind the temple Vietnamese girls performed with fans to national music, took photos, it was beautiful, I recommend it
Very nice experience. They have free covering clothes for visitors to borrow, so everyone is able to visit the different temples.
A very beautiful place that recalls the architectural relics left to us by the ancient and living religions of Asia.
I really liked the architecture. Lots of visitors... crowded and noisy. Despite that, still recommendable.

It's a very beautiful place; we walked around it from all sides, and you can even pop into the temple. The only downside is the Chinese. They're very noisy and rude, and they behave terribly. The place itself is very clean and unusual; you can sit on the rocks and enjoy the views. A must-see if you're in Vietnam.

The main attraction of visiting Nha Trang is Po Nagar Pagoda, with its mysterious cultural atmosphere. The exact date of its construction is still unknown. It attracts many tourists and is well worth a visit.

I enjoyed viewing these ancient buildings. Beautifully restored with good information available.They have free 'robes' to wear over yoru shorts, and shoulders with the price of the entrance ticket. Scooter parking is plentiful.

We visited here on our own without a guided tour (the tourist groups were running around like crazy, apparently under time pressure). We walked around calmly, looked at everything, and took photos. We really liked it; the clay towers are certainly impressive, and they offer beautiful views of the surrounding area.

The first place I explored independently in Nha Trang was the Cham Towers. The architecture of these structures is quite intriguing. These fascinating towers are unlike other religious buildings in Vietnam. I've heard that the Cham religion is related to Hinduism. The ancient Chams, who lived in Vietnam and established their own state there, erected these religious buildings. After the collapse of the Cham state, the temples remained intact. Currently, it is an active temple where religious services are held. A small number of descendants of the ancient Chams still exist. They have their own culture, writing system, and language. As far as I understand, they worship gods of the Indian pantheon and the lingam as the source of all creation. The Cham religious and cultural heritage, the Po Nagar Towers as they are also called, are being restored and maintained in good condition. This is evident in the skillfully replaced crumbling bricks. The cost of a visit on November 6, 2018, was 22,000 dong. You can get there by buses 4 and 6. To learn at least a little about Vietnam's history, be sure to visit the Po Nagar Temple. But don't rush through the sights. Touch the ancient brickwork. Feel the hoary antiquity of the buildings. Step inside and soak up the vibrations created over millennia by the cult's many devotees. And perhaps your deepest wishes, expressed there, will come true.

Very good. It's not a large place, but it's worth a visit, especially from the hilltop where you can see Nha Trang city – it gives you a sense of panoramic views. It's located within the city limits and is a popular tourist destination in Nha Trang, Vietnam.

We visited the Cham towers (from the indigenous Cham people) during a sightseeing tour of Nha Trang with the RIC. It was very impressive! We heard the story of the origins of the Cham people and their architectural structures. This is the history of the country, so contact with history is always exciting. I imagine this small but great people, who left us, contemporaries, a legacy of such grandiose structures, indestructible over the centuries.
It's a temple. It's a nice visit, great for photos, and has a peaceful atmosphere. There are some lovely little gardens behind it. Entry is about €1.
This place is beautifully preserved. It is well maintained without compromising its character. The toilets are clean and you are not bothered by souvenir vendors. Highly recommended.

We visited the towers on a tour with PEGAS. The tour was called "Kaleidoscope," and the price only included lunch. Overall, it was a good tour; the towers were very beautiful, and the stories behind them were interesting.

A beautifully restored and planted temple, a relic of Vietnam's past! After a long walk through the crowded, noisy city, we could see the sacred site from afar! It feels like a jungle springing from the very modern and bustling city, with this beautiful complex! Entrance cost 22,000 VND. Very pretty, definitely worth seeing!
This is a historical site in Nha Trang - an ancient building well worth seeing with its unique architecture... Everyone should visit here when coming to Nha Trang.

The Panagar Towers date back to the period when the ancient Cham Empire existed in this area. This, mind you, dates back to the 10th-4th centuries. It is still a functioning temple. Please respect local customs: cover your shoulders and knees. This applies to both men and women. Capes are available near the entrance. The goddess is benevolent; ask her for anything. She is especially favorable to requests for family, children, and relationships. You will see other gods near her, each with their own theme (search online to make your requests "to the right address"). Wait for the Vietnamese girls to dance near the pagodas. It is a pleasant and interesting sight. They dance every 30 minutes, with pots on their heads. Fun!

A beautiful place located beyond the bridge to the north. We got there by bike. Entry was 22,000 rubles per person. Everything was wonderful, but these Chinese people filled the place!!! It was impossible to walk; their wild shouting and chatter ruined the harmony and tranquility of the place. You can enter the temple by taking off your shoes and putting on a robe, which was provided free of charge.

I recommend visiting this iconic place in Vietnam. The religion of the places visited by tourists deserves great respect. For centuries, people have come to this place to ask their gods for help and compassion. Only by understanding the religious traditions of a people can one truly understand the people themselves. I really enjoyed it.
Ancient and rich in customs, it's definitely worth a visit, especially the towers arranged and connected to stones over a thousand years old.
A wonderful educational tour. Bring comfortable shoes and water. Ancient buildings, amazing history, and stunning nature.
It's very beautiful, there are few places left where antiquity breathes warmth on you, a unique flavor, and the peace and quiet are inspiring!!! I really liked it!!!

An interesting place. You can enter the temples, but you must wear clothing that covers your shoulders and knees. If you don't have one, they'll kindly provide you with something like a gray robe (free of charge). It's within walking distance for those vacationing in the northern part of the city. Overall, it's very inspiring and definitely worth a visit. You can also burn incense for free, but just don't turn your back to the temple.

What could be better than escaping the hustle and bustle of the city? The Cham Towers offer peace and tranquility, with a beautiful view of Nha Trang from the observation deck. The complex is surrounded by a garden, a fountain, bonsai trees, and a small museum. Entrance to the shrine is permitted, but you must remove your shoes and wear a robe (provided at the entrance) that covers your knees and shoulders. A must-see for anyone interested in history and Angkor Wat-style architecture.

We went to the Towers on a short tour (a sightseeing tour of Nha Trang), organized by Russian guys. We really enjoyed it; we got a glimpse into centuries-old history. It's a place of incredible power. We visited each temple and asked each god for what they are responsible for. Thank you for their hospitality!

Probably one of the (if not the) most interesting historical spot in Nha Trang, this unusual ancient temple from the Cham period, the Po Nagar Cham Towers are the remains of larger complex dated around the 9th Century. The towers are made of red stone bricks and the architecture is very unusual for this area, not following the typical temple architecture that you see everywhere in Vietnam. Unfortunately the place is very crowded, but this does not reduce the interest of the area and won't prevent to find beautiful spots for pictures, especially if you have the patience to walk around the entire complex area from which, by the way, you also have nice views on the Cai river and its fishers boats and life, since the towers are on the top of a small hill. However this is not only a touristic spot but also a real place for cult since you can see the offerings to the statue of Uma in the main chamber. A must-see while in Nha Trang.
I love these buildings, they are so fascinating. I was spending a good 1 to 2 hours here walking around and taking stunning photos.

I visited the Po Nagar Towers on my own. It's very easy: take bus #4 in the tourist area, tell the conductor where you're going, and they'll tell you when to get off. A bus ticket costs 8,000 dong. Once you get there, buy a ticket for 22,000 and go inside. They look stunning. To enter, you must have your knees and shoulders covered. If not, there are free robes available. You take off your shoes at the entrance. The interior is very small and dark, but the atmosphere is amazing. There are only four towers, so you can explore them all. There's a small museum, and there were performances and musicians near the temple. There are a lot of tourists (mostly Asian). You can go behind the museum and there's a surprising silence. You can barely hear the musicians, but there aren't many people there. I sat and watched the squirrels. It's worth a visit. It's a great way to experience Vietnamese culture. It's very easy and not very expensive to do. I also took the bus back. The whole trip cost me 38,000.

You can easily reach these towers on your own, but this is the one place I don't recommend visiting without a guide or a piece of paper with everything written down in detail! You need to know its history; it needs to be told by someone who can describe everything, every tower, every faith, every history, every meaning behind the building! Just walking and looking is probably fine, but then you won't get a picture of the hundreds of years that have passed here... And the endlessly shouting Chinese in huge numbers will completely distract you from contemplating the beauty.

On a previous visit to Vietnam, we visited the museum in Da Nang, which housed beautiful statuary from the Cham period. When we learned that we would be visiting NhaTrang as part of a cruise tour, we looked forward to seeing the Po Nagar Cham Towers. The towers that remain were part of a much larger complex at their height of influence, around the 9th Century. The towers are made of beautiful red stone and the architecture is very special. There is no cement between the stones and they are held together by the design and placement of the stones themselves. Be sure to walk around the entire tower area so you can appreciate how remarkable the architecture is. Go inside the main chamber to see the statue of Uma and the offerings that are still made to her. It was rather crowded when we were there and it was difficult maneuvering among the many visitors to take photos. However, the towers are well worth any inconvenience.
We went with Alexander and learned a lot of interesting historical information. We got a taste of history and took some memorable photos.

Cham Island, with its majestic and sacred ruins, and diving experiences that rival those of Phuket, makes it a perfect destination for a trip with friends. Experience the differences and exchanges between different cultures here.

We went with a tour, and that's a big downside. It's better to go on your own to see everything properly, sit there, and get a feel for the place. It's a beautiful place, with an air of antiquity; the buildings have been restored, but it's not immediately obvious.

An interesting place, with well-maintained and beautiful grounds. Entrance is 20,000 dong. You can stroll around and explore in just half an hour. Nothing out of the ordinary, but perfect for general well-being. A good spot for some beautiful photos.
A touch of Vietnamese history. A beautiful park and towers, an engaging musical program.

Nha Trang doesn't have many special attractions for children other than swimming, so this temple felt even more special. I think it is a wonderful place. Although it is small, there are people praying, and you can even rent clothes for free!

It's very close to Nha Trang city center; we always took a Grab there, which was very convenient. The site isn't very big, much narrower than My Son Khoan, but if you're nearby, it's definitely worth a visit.

I liked everything, there are many different places where you can take great photos and just look at sculptures made of clay and cement. The clay in Vietnam is very durable, it’s noticeable; it’s hard as stone.
We went on our own, or rather, walked. Entrance is 22 rubles per person. I recommend reading up on them before visiting to understand what's going on.
My wife and I visited this sacred place, learned about the country's history, and immersed ourselves in a world of religion and mysticism. It's easy to get there by bus. There's an entrance fee.
We visited it on our own, taking bus 4. I recommend everyone to visit this wonderful attraction.

It's right next to the main road—lots of cars, so be very careful when crossing. There are many trees inside, so it's not too hot. You need to buy a ticket outside first. It gets quite crowded, but this historical site is definitely worth seeing.

The Cham Towers are a small temple complex in Nha Trang, built in the 8th-9th centuries. Of the original 8 temples, only 4 remain. They are surrounded by a small green area where you can take beautiful photos, watch performances by national groups, and buy souvenirs. It's best to get there by taxi, which is relatively inexpensive. The temples are more interesting from the outside than the inside. Entry into the temples is strictly prohibited without covering your shoulders and barefoot. There's a good observation deck overlooking the river.

It's very beautiful, like a little piece of Indonesia. Entry is 22,000 dong. Lots of tourists, parking is prohibited nearby, so we arrived by taxi. Beautiful columns and towers, but we didn't go inside, just walked around and around the grounds.

Went here as part of an organised tour of Nah Trang. From the parking it was a little walk through a small but nice market, selling delicious fresh fruit amongst other things. Wheelchair access was a problem for one of our party. The temple is in great condition both inside and out. Sensible dress is compulsory and shoes cannot be worn inside. They provide grey over garments in various sizes. This place was very busy when we visited. There are a few kiosks on site selling refreshments and souvenirs and seats to rest your weary legs. With its position the place also allows for good views of Nah Trang.
The temple complex is situated on a small hill and is very well preserved. At under €0.70, the entrance fee was also very affordable. Highly recommended! :)
An interesting temple on a hill, dating from a very ancient historical period; we had to walk barefoot. The building is quite remarkable.

It's worth a visit, especially since you can get there by bus. Therefore, a tour will cost you 30,000 rubles: the bus costs 8,000 rubles and the entrance fee is 22,000 rubles. The grounds are picturesque and clean, there's a musical show, and you can catch a prayer offering. Inside the temples, I recommend looking up. Due to the architectural features, the ceiling seems infinitely high. It's a fun visit for all ages.
It's worth seeing in person once. The grounds are clean, there are few tourists in the evening, and you can take photos of everything separately as a souvenir!

There's an entrance fee, but it's 20 VND per person—I don't think it's expensive. The place is atmospheric and interesting. It's best to go there with a guide or bring a guidebook, as it's truly a historical site. Be sure to put on a robe and go inside each temple.

Generally, Vietnam isn't as rich in ancient landmarks and historical artifacts as, for example, neighboring Thailand or Cambodia. Therefore, history buffs will have a hard time finding anything ancient or archaeological. Here's one example of the most ancient remains of civilization in Vietnam. We visited a week before Chinese New Year, and it was very difficult to navigate the crowds of Chinese tourists. Otherwise, it's a very picturesque place.

A wonderful historical site. The garden near the towers is very beautiful; we even saw a local squirrel. :) We were lucky and even got to see a performance near the temple, where there were dancers in national costumes.

You can get there by bus number 4; the fare is 8,000 dong (24 rubles), and there's an entrance fee of 22,000 dong per person. The grounds are beautiful and well-maintained. You can go inside the temple; if your clothing is too revealing, you can borrow a free robe; barefoot is the only option. It makes for beautiful photos.

If there’s only one thing you do in Nha Trang other than the beach it’s visit Po Nagar. Beautifully restored old temples and architecture, in keeping with its old appearance. As with other Cham style architecture it appears very Cambodian. You can go inside some of the towers (most are quite small) so be sure to wear long pants. Great views around the river mouth and looking to the south of Nha Trang. Expect to spend 30mins here. It does get busy so plan accordingly, I’d aim early morning or very later afternoon.

We visited the Cham Towers as part of a sightseeing tour of Nha Trang. We'd already seen Cham Towers in Phan Thiet about seven years ago, but I like places like this. They're beautiful, unusual for European tourists, with different architecture and different customs.

We always love visiting temples and historical sites. This reminded us very much of architecture of Angkor Wat in Cambodia. It's very close to town so I don't see a need to take a tour. You can get a Grab car or taxi and be there in a few minutes. It's well worth the time spent for a visit.

The towers are located near the Cai River, which flows into the sea, and are surrounded by beautiful, ancient towers. On special occasions, there are also girls performing traditional dances. The area is green and clean.

Don't take the paid tour; it's not worth it. Take bus number 4, get on near the Louisiana restaurant, and go. This is a tourist route, with lots of Russians. Just show the conductor a picture or the address, and you'll see where to get off through the window. I'd give it a 5 for the historical factor, but there are way too many Chinese. There's nothing to do there for more than 30 minutes. Entrance is 22,000 rubles per adult, and the bus fare is 8,000 rubles per person.
A very atmospheric place. Beautiful and impressive. Upon arrival, I immediately started reading about the Chams. The place itself is peaceful. Even the crowds of Chinese don't bother me.
A must-visit place when in Nha Trang, you'll find many surprises here, especially the ancient Bodhi tree over 300 years old!

One word. Breathtaking! I couldn't stop taking pictures of it jst so that I could take it home with me. We went late afternoon, around 5+ so we caught sunset there which makes it even more gorgeous. I would definitely recommend going. Oh do note that you can borrow robes if you're interested in going in the temple but not dressed properly.

Just about 3 km from the center of Nha Trang City and the beach, Po Nagar Temple is an interesting religious place of the Cham people since 8th century. Should visit if you are in Nha Trang. Come in the afternoon for a brighter photo.
Learning about the culture is certainly great. The tour guides give a brilliant update on the past traditional stories.
Great temple, very interesting. Beautiful view. Lots of greenery, small shops and stalls. I would visit this temple again.

It feels very exotic to Koreans. I recommend taking your time to look around, spending at least an hour taking turns snapping photos and resting in the shade when it gets hot. There is an entrance fee, but it is not expensive, and unlike other Nha Trang ruins that feel like a rip-off, there is an official ticket booth. However, I suggest visiting during lunchtime when there are relatively fewer Chinese group tourists.

It is truly magnificent and wonderful. I usually don't like looking around and wandering about at places like temples, so I wasn't planning on going, but someone I know highly recommended it, so I went and it was really great!
Doesn’t take too long, very special architecture, loved it. If you want to go inside you need to put on a monk robe that is provided near the entrance and take off your shoes as well.

It's easier to visit this place yourself without a tour. The entrance fee is about $1.50. There are very beautiful views from all sides. If you read the history of the towers before visiting, it will be doubly interesting. We were satisfied, but it's a one-time place.

It was fine for a sightseeing tour, but the heat was killing it))) We flew in late July-early August 2017, peak season) well, it's our own fault. Otherwise, it was very interesting; there were a lot of Chinese and Russians, yes, but there was no getting around it, we resigned ourselves to it and enjoyed it.

I recommend visiting this place either by bike or bus. For first-time visitors, it's definitely worth going and seeing it (not as a standalone destination—it can be combined with Hon Chong Cape; a day alone would be plenty for just the towers). An hour and a half is more than enough for a visit. You can take some photos and walk around.

It felt very sacred. There were certain dress codes; shorts, short sleeves, and dresses were not allowed. The entrance fee was 25,000 VND. There were a lot of Chinese people there. The street in front of the ruins is a seafood street; you can go eat seafood after visiting the ruins.
The only downside is the crowd of noisy Chinese. Although the place is supposed to be quiet and relaxing! It's conducive to peace and tranquility! It's very beautiful!

The Cham Towers are great for taking photos and have a great atmosphere. They're a must-see attraction in Nha Trang. There are vendors selling coconut jelly and sugarcane juice nearby, but they're quite expensive. Coconut jelly is 50,000 VND for two, and coconut juice is around 20,000 VND. You can try them out.

I'm not sure if my interpretation is correct, but it is a building from 1,000 years ago. Well, it has been reconstructed, though. It was magnificent. It is small, so 20 minutes is enough to look around. You can get there from the city center by taxi for about 2,500 won, or by bus; take bus number 4. There is a bus stop near the iPhone store close to the cathedral (since I stayed in the city center, this is the only place I know). Anyway, it is small but incredibly grand!! It gave me a cozy feeling. Photography is prohibited inside, and you must be dressed appropriately to enter. It is extremely hot inside. The scent of incense is incredibly strong. It’s not unbearable, though. Seriously, you must go to Po Nagar!!
Highly recommended. Fantastic place of power) Not far from the centre, come earlier than 5pm, entrance fee is 20 000d don’t worry about dress-code, you can get long robes aside from the temple

I wish we had visited this temple in the evening because it was very hot mid day and from our hotel we could see it lit and night and it looked rather special. It was busy but this did not hinder our experience. Toilets avail. This is an 8th century temple and I haven’t seen anything like it. Definitely worth a stop! We also enjoyed watching the traditional music and dancing.

The Cham ruins are essentially a miniature Angkor Wat. Nha Trang used to belong to Cambodia, but the South Vietnamese conquered it. This place is a must-see for tourists. My mom took many beautiful photos there. The entrance fee is very cheap, and the site is located in the city center. I highly recommend everyone to visit and experience the beauty of Angkor Wat. There are also performances inside, but remember to check the schedule online beforehand, otherwise you might have to wait to see one.
Po Nagar Temple was much more impressive than Long Thorn Temple!! I liked the unique atmosphere of the temples. I took tons of photos~~ Everyone should visit at least once. It's great!
We went there on a tour) it will only impress those who love antiquities... 40 minutes was enough for us to walk around everything, take photos and film roles.

The Po Nagar Cham Towers are a cult Vietnamese structure consisting of four towers, the largest of which is dedicated to the goddess Po Nagar, who, according to legend, taught people crafts and agriculture. The complex is very compact, so a visit will take no more than an hour.

Tháp Bà Pô Nagar is an ancient Cham temple complex located about 2–3 km north of Nha Trang, along the Sông Cái.

If you have time, you can walk to the towers and enjoy the cool sea breeze along the way. The distance is not too far — you only need to cross one bridge. On the way, you can also stop by the Alexandre Yersin Monument, which is located along Trần Phú Street by the beach.

During the tourist season, it’s recommended to travel by motorbike or walk, as it’s usually much faster than going by car. Since cars often get stuck in traffic on the way to the towers, especially since cars are restricted to a single lane.

The entrance fee is 30,000 VND per person.

Hope you have a wonderful experience when visiting this cultural and historical site.

We visited Po Nagar Temple during our trip to Nha Trang, and it was a fascinating glimpse into Cham culture and history. The ancient towers are beautifully preserved, with intricate carvings and a serene atmosphere that makes you feel transported back in time. The views from the hilltop are stunning, overlooking the Cai River, and the short hike up adds a nice touch of adventure. Learning about the Hindu influences and the goddess Ponagar was enlightening—definitely worth the entrance fee.

That said, it can get quite crowded with tour groups, especially in the mornings, which made it a bit noisy and hard to fully appreciate the tranquility. Some areas could use better signage in English for non-Vietnamese speakers, and the steps might be challenging for those with mobility issues.

I'd recommend it to anyone interested in Vietnam's diverse heritage, but try to go early or late to avoid the crowds!

And if you’re hungry, Thap Ba street (running alongside the river near the complex) is basically a “seafood street,” where you can eat tasty seafood dishes for a good price.

We visited Ponagar Temple on a weekend and it was quite busy, with a mix of tourists and locals. Many local families had set up picnics on the grounds, which added a lively and authentic atmosphere to the visit.

The temple complex itself is a fascinating glimpse into Cham architecture and history, with several well-preserved towers and ruins still standing strong after centuries. The main tower, dedicated to the goddess Po Nagar, is especially impressive up close with its intricate carvings and reddish stone structure.

There’s also a small museum onsite that gives a helpful overview of Cham culture and artifacts, including statues, ceramics, and some historical context for the temple.

You don’t need too long here — I’d recommend allowing about 45 minutes to an hour depending on how much time you spend taking photos or reading the displays. There are great views overlooking the river and sea from the upper levels of the site, so it’s worth climbing the short set of stairs for the panorama.

Note: Dress modestly as you will need to cover both shoulders and knees. Also, best to bring a hat or umbrella if visiting midday — there’s not a lot of shade around the temple grounds.

The Tháp Bà Ponagar complex is located just a few kilometers from Nha Trang city center, making it easily accessible by taxi or motorbike. It’s a popular stop for tourists who are exploring Nha Trang’s many cultural and natural attractions. The site is open daily, and tickets are reasonably priced, making it a budget-friendly option for visitors.

### 6. **Nearby Attractions**
After visiting the Ponagar Towers, many tourists explore other nearby attractions such as the **Nha Trang hot springs** or take a boat ride along the **Cai River**. These activities can easily be combined into a half-day or full-day excursion.

### 7. **Best Time to Visit**
The best time to visit is in the early morning or late afternoon when the sun isn’t too harsh, and you can fully enjoy the complex without the midday heat. This is also when the lighting is perfect for photography, especially with the golden sunlight casting shadows on the red brick towers.

### 8. **Overall Impression**
Tháp Bà Ponagar offers a mix of cultural, historical, and spiritual experiences. Whether you’re interested in ancient architecture, religious history, or just want a peaceful place to admire beautiful views, this temple complex has something to offer. The combination of its rich history, religious significance, and impressive architecture makes it one of Nha Trang’s most captivating attractions.

### **Conclusion**:
Tháp Bà Ponagar is a must-visit for anyone interested in Vietnam’s ancient civilizations and spiritual heritage. The beauty of the temple complex, its serene atmosphere, and the cultural richness it embodies make it a memorable stop on any tour of Nha Trang. Whether you are exploring the religious significance of the site or just appreciating the intricate architecture and the views, Tháp Bà Ponagar is a place where history and culture come to life.

Ponagar Tower is a Champa temple, considered a prominent architectural feature in the brilliant period of Hinduism.  Ponagar tower was built between the 7th and 13th centuries.  This was the flourishing period of Hinduism as well as the ancient kingdom of Champa.  Ponagar tower is also known as Yang Po Inu Nagar tower or Yang Po Ana Gar tower, the tower was built to worship the goddess Po Ina Nagar - the god who created the earth, created rice trees, protected the world.  protect people from rain, wind, storms and flood and bring a prosperous crop and a peaceful life.  Up to now, the Ponagar tower still plays an extremely important role in the spiritual and religious life of the Cham people.  This place is open daily with an affordable entrance fee, some non-alcoholic drinks are served inside... highly recommend you visit this place

Build between the 7th and 12th centuries, these impressive Cham towers are still actively used for worship by Cham, Chinese and Vietnamese Buddhists. Originally the complex had seven or eight towers, but only four remain, of which the 28m-high North Tower, with a terraced pyramidal roof, vaulted interior masonry and vestibule, is the most magnificent.
This site has a continuing religious significance, so be sure to remove your shoes before entering and wear respectful clothing.

The Po Nagar Cham Tower in Vietnam is a representative Hindu historical site of the ancient Champa Kingdom, located in Nha Trang.

This temple is one of the oldest ruins of the Champa Kingdom and is considered a significant site, particularly because it enshrines the goddess Po Nagar.

Po Nagar is said to be the wife of the Hindu god Shiva and the goddess of fertility and creation.

Built between the 8th and 13th centuries, it showcases the Hindu architectural style of the ancient Champa Kingdom.

Constructed of red bricks, it features a unique and sturdy construction technique of stacking bricks without adhesive.

It gives a similar impression to Angkor Wat in Cambodia.

Advantages
✅ High Historical & Cultural Value
A typical Cham Pa architectural structure that remains relatively intact, reflecting the pinnacle of bricklaying techniques, carving, and ancient Hindu beliefs.
The sacred space offers a sense of peace and tranquility to many visitors (both Vietnamese and international).

✅ Beautiful and Accessible Location
Located right in the city center, it's convenient to get there by taxi, motorbike, or tour.
From the hilltop, you can admire the Cai River, the sea, and Nha Trang city – a very photogenic spot.

✅ Unique Cultural Activities
If you visit during the festival season (March in the lunar calendar), you can watch Cham dances, shadow puppetry, and offerings – a worthwhile experience.

⚠️ Disadvantages
❌ Small Space, Crowded
During peak season or during religious ceremonies, the tower area is quite crowded with groups of visitors and can be noisy.

❌ Partially Commercialized
Some surrounding services (souvenir vendors, incense sellers) sometimes pester tourists.
Some areas are slightly degraded or conservation efforts are inconsistent.

It's best to arrive at opening time, 8 a.m., while it's still empty and cool. The interior is interesting, but the main attraction is the views of Nha Trang from the hill.

One of the landmarks of northern Nha Trang is the Cham Towers, or Po Nagar Towers. They sit atop Cu Lao Mountain, overlooking the entire city. They are a spiritual powerhouse where Buddhist rituals are performed. The towers are part of a temple built between the 7th and 12th centuries. Four of the 10 towers remain. Unfortunately, the temple itself has not survived.

The mountain itself is not so large. A short staircase leads to four red brick towers. The largest is dedicated to Queen Po Nagar, and a statue of her is located inside. Three smaller ones are dedicated to the gods Kri Khambhu, Sandhaka, and Ganesha. Each contains an altar with Buddhist deities and burning incense. You can enter, but please remove your shoes before entering. The towers differ from each other, as they were built at different times. Long ago, rituals and sacrifices to the ancient gods were performed here.

The complex offers a beautiful view of Nha Trang, looking north on one side and the city center on the other. Introduction to Nha Trang Po Nagar Temple (Po Nagar Cham Tower)

**Po Nagar Temple (Ponagar Cham Tower)** is a representative historical site of the ancient Champa Kingdom and an iconic tourist attraction of Nha Trang, located on a hill overlooking the Cai River in northern Nha Trang, Vietnam.

History and Significance

Construction Period: This temple complex was built between the 8th and 13th centuries, during the flourishing period of the Champa Kingdom.

Religious Background: Strongly influenced by Indian Hinduism, the temple enshrines Bhagavati (another form of Parvati, the wife of Lord Shiva), one of the major Hindu goddesses, and Mahasyasur Mardini (the goddess who defeats demons). Locally, this goddess is referred to as ‘Yang Po Inư Nagar’ or simply ‘Po Nagar,’ which means ‘Mother.’

**Introduction to Nha Trang Po Nagar Temple (Po Nagar Cham Tower)** Cultural Value: A highly important historical site offering a glimpse into the Hindu culture, architecture, and sculpture of the Cham people; the Cham ethnic minority still performs rituals here today.

Location and Visitor Information

Address: 61 Hai Thang Tu Street, Vinh Phuoc, Nha Trang, Khanh Hoa.

Operating Hours: 8:00 AM – 6:00 PM.

Admission Fee: 25,000 VND (as of 2024).

Architecture and Structure

Architectural Style: It features a unique Champa architectural technique of stacking red bricks without adhesive. The bodies of the towers are adorned with various sculptures and reliefs depicting mythological figures, animals, and goddesses.

Composition: Originally a three-tiered temple complex, only parts of it remain today. The largest tower houses a statue of a goddess with ten arms, symbolizing the goddess's power to defeat evil and bring peace. Tower Layout: Four main towers remain, the tallest of which is known as the ‘Po Nagar Tower.’

Inscriptions: Inscriptions in various languages, including Cham script, Chinese characters, and Vietnamese, remain within the temple, adding to its historical and cultural value.

Other Features

Natural Scenery: Situated on a hill, the temple boasts a beautiful view overlooking the Cai River and downtown Nha Trang.

Dress Code and Etiquette: As this is a religious site, neat attire is recommended. You may observe rituals being performed inside.

Summary

Po Nagar Temple is a Hindu historical site of the Champa Kingdom, which preserves the long history of the central and southern regions of Vietnam. It is a representative attraction of Nha Trang that combines unique architectural beauty, a mysterious atmosphere, and beautiful natural scenery. Still revered by locals and ethnic minorities, it serves as a special space for travelers to experience Vietnam's ancient culture and religion up close.

For travelers visiting Nha Trang, one of Vietnam's premier resort destinations, the Po Nagar archaeological site is not merely a tourist attraction, but a sacred place for experiencing the depths of history. Located on a small hill not far from the center of Nha Trang, its easy accessibility is one of its attractions.

This complex of ruins once functioned as an important ceremonial site for the Champa Kingdom, which flourished in south-central Vietnam, and its history is estimated to date back to the 8th or 9th century. Among the oldest surviving Champa ruins, its meticulously constructed brick shrines convey the essence of ancient Champa architecture and its unique cultural style. The site is compactly arranged, allowing visitors to experience the profound history of the Champa civilization even in a short time.

The exterior walls are adorned with intricate carvings, including the guardian deity Naga (serpent god), and the detailed design is truly breathtaking. The five existing shrines have been carefully restored and preserved, demonstrating how these historically valuable structures have been carefully protected through the ages.

The main sanctuary, situated at the heart of the ruins, is a sacred space dedicated to Po Nagar, the earth goddess and patron deity of the Champa people. The sanctuary is filled with the smoke of incense, and devout Vietnamese worshippers continuously offer their prayers. Even amidst the bustling tourist crowds, this place offers a glimpse into the sincere faith of the people, making you feel that it is not only a historical legacy but also a powerful object of worship that continues to thrive today.

To respect the sacred space, there is a dress code (avoiding skin exposure) for entering the main sanctuary, and jackets can be borrowed near the entrance. Beyond its historical value, this complex of ruins also functions as a viewpoint offering spectacular views overlooking the city of Nha Trang and the magnificent ocean, thanks to its location on a small hill.

The entrance fee is a reasonable 22,000 VND for adults, and transportation from the city center is easily accessible using ride-sharing services. Whether you're interested in historical architecture or simply want to reflect on the grand history of the Champa Kingdom and experience the profound faith of a different culture in the resort town of Nha Trang, this will be a truly valuable opportunity.

Po Nagar is beautiful, early in the morning a cup of Vietnamese coffee and a view of the water, boats and bridges, and on the other side a terracotta temple complex, I love this place.

I loved the unique experience, the beautiful and mysterious architecture, and the traditional dance performance. I remember the entrance fee being 10,000 won, and it took me just 30 minutes to see everything from the entrance, take photos, and come back down.

The Cham Tower, recently restored. Entrance fee: 30,000 VND per person. Visiting to appreciate the beauty of the Cham Tower is a good idea. There seemed to be some kind of performance in the late afternoon, but we only visited briefly before leaving.
From Porgar Pagoda, you can enjoy a panoramic view of Nha Trang City and a beautiful river view. Admission is 30,000 VND per person, and a brief folk show is also available.

This temple is built of stone. It's smaller than you might think, so I wouldn't recommend leaving the city just to see it. It's probably fine if you're on a tour. The scenery is beautiful, and it's definitely photogenic.

The architectural landmarks and temples of the Cham people, one of the famous tourist attractions in Nha Trang... The management board should invest in audio guides at the site for tourists.

The entrance fee is 30,000 VND per person. I took a Grab taxi from Ban Mi Pan to the temple and it didn't take long to see everything. I think it would take about 30 minutes to see everything. It's worth a visit. I recommend taking another Grab taxi from here to Dam Market.

Beautiful, majestic tower

Hindu temple in the city. It's very touristy, but worth a visit. Entrance is 35,000 dong, so it's not too bad.

The entrance fee is 30,000 VND. The scenery is quite beautiful. The grounds are well-maintained. It's a good place to visit when in Nha Trang. Even on weekdays, there are many tour groups, but you don't have to queue or wait long.

It was conveniently located near the city center. Perhaps because it was the weekend, there were a lot of people, but it wasn't overly crowded. The temple was so beautiful that no matter where I took a picture, it was a photo opportunity. If it hadn't been so hot, I think I would have explored more.

A compact park with temples. You can walk around and see it all in 20 minutes. I recommend coming on Sunday, as there are slightly fewer local tourists on this day. They won't let you in with a low-cut top. The main minus is a swarm of …

The tower is beautiful, and the walkway is now paved with nice, clean tiles. When I visited, it was a Saturday, and there were many foreign tourists. The entrance fee was 30,000 VND per adult, and children were free.
A typical tourist destination, small but photogenic. It appears to have been under Hindu influence. It's often crowded, so plan your visit wisely. Early mornings are the least crowded.

The place is truly impressive, right in the heart of bustling Nha Trang. We wish there had been guides available during our visit. It's a very popular spot, but it also offers a great view of the city's rooftops! An ancient dance performance took place at 4:30 pm during our visit.

There are a lot of group tourists, especially Chinese. Once they settle down at a photo spot, they don't think about leaving. Visit early in the morning. If you go late, you might get angry because of the heat and group tourists.

This Hindu temple is a tourist attraction in downtown Nha Trang. It offers numerous photo opportunities and is often crowded with tourists. If you're visiting Nha Trang, this is a must-see attraction.

If you're traveling to Nha Trang, Vietnam, it's worth taking some time to explore. While some people might not be interested in this kind of place, it's a popular tourist destination for local Vietnamese as well. Climb to the top of the …

You can admire the exotic temple. Since it's used by locals, there's a dress code when entering the pagoda. If you're wearing shorts or short sleeves, you can borrow them for free from the nearby clothing rack before entering the pagoda.

Small and old Hindu temple in the middle of a busy touristy city. This attracts a lot of people and most tour operators offers this visit. Tickets are cheap 22k and parking 4k. …

Beautiful and sacrificed place, the column is outstanding as well as a main temple
