A good walkaround. Guided by onetrip nha trang, had the history and significants of the site shared which made it more than just a "walk around an old temple". Tends to be crowdes by tourist but, everyone is in beachcity for a good time, so …

We visited the Po Nagar Cham Towers and found them to be an interesting historical site. The ancient brick towers are well preserved and give a good sense of Cham culture and architecture. The setting offers some nice views, and the visit is fairly quick and easy. It can get busy with visitors, but overall it’s a worthwhile stop when sightseeing in Nha Trang.

The Po Nagar Cham Tower in Nha Trang is an ancient Hindu temple in the kingdom of Champa (8th–13th centuries), dedicated to Po Nagar, fertility goddess and consort of Shiva. Built of red brick without mortar, its architecture vaguely resembles Angkor Wat.

I visited here as part of a tour from a cruise ship. It’s a popular venue for tourists. You can feel the history from the moment you step onto the grounds and climb the stairs to visit the towers and temple. Great views and gardens too.

A famous cultural relic in Nha Trang, bearing the unique ancient Champa architecture. The campus is spacious, clean, with lots of green trees and resting places. This is a very suitable place for those who love historical culture.

It offered a fascinating insight into Cham culture and a real opportunity to learn about a unique part of Vietnamese heritage. It’s not a huge place, but it was interesting to see something so ancient. Glad we went, but it's probably a one-and-done kind of visit for us.

Exploring the ancient Cham towers and learning about the culture was fascinating. The intricate brickwork is incredible, and the view over Nha Trang from the site is just spectacular. A very cool and unique stop!

Po Nagar Cham Towers These are Hindu Cham temples that are over 1000 years old and are still very impressive today and should not be missed in any visit to Nha Trang. Not only the sacred area gives the complex a grandiose picture, but also the surroundings. It is located on a gentle hill rising from the waters of a sea bay. The latter, for its part, stands against a remarkable backdrop of a massive mountain range. The result is a fantastic image that can be seen from the bridge leading to it. One sees a very harmonious symbiosis of nature and culture. Unfortunately, unfortunately, this impression disappears once you reach the temple complex: First of all, you stand in a queue of variable length. Overcome them, one then encounters a crowd that robs the place of any cultivating character with its clothing, behavior and behavior. It also goes slowly up a flight of stairs, as the countless people taking selfies naturally find time for the back-grounds they like. Once on this ‘mezzanine floor’, we stood almost shoulder to shoulder. There were of course also the photographers, but unfortunately also many tour groups, they gathered around their companions, who tried to inform their assembled about their surroundings with a loud voice. Another annoyance was that a not inconsiderable proportion of the visitors were not adequately dressed. The result: These people were turned away when visiting individual sacral places and there were discussions, traffic jams ... It was unbearable. The very core of this place – a religious reference – was completely destroyed here. In short: A visitor to this facility could only be satisfied if he only wanted to see the ‘hard ware’ of the place and he is not interested in transcendentals.

Interesting ancient towers in Nha Trang. Went on my own whilst my wife chilled by the pool. She may have made the right decision. You get some good photos but unless you have a guide it's pretty difficult to make sense of it. They do have q r codes which gives info in very bad English. Spend about half hour here. Steps are easy.
Beautiful historical place, but if you go without a guide, there's not much to do. But the photos will be beautiful, including a panorama of the city

We took a taxi to Ponagar temple reaching there at noon The entrance fee is 30.000 per person. This temple is a very miniature touched up version if compared to Angkor Wat. We had banh can tom for lunch opposite the temple. Time spent - 1 hour.
Excellent! Beautiful decorations and beautiful scenery. We are very satisfied! 👏👏👏👏👏👏👏👏👏👏👏👏👏👏👏👏👏👏👏👏👏👏👏👏👏👏

Temple complex, heritage of the powerful state of Champa (or Champa). There is an entrance fee, you can get there by taxi or bus, there are always a lot of tourists inside. IMHO this place is a must visit for all vacationers in Nha Trang.
When we arrived at 10:00 in the morning, the area was overly packed with visitors. It opens to visitors at 8:00 am, so if you want to escape the lines, I suggest arriving early.

Nice old temple from the 7th century, which has been restored. Spontaneous entertainment such as dancing from the ancient Cham people was much appreciated. There are coats to borrow to cover knees and shoulders if you want to enter the buildings. Good thing to think about is that the temple buildings are still in use, when people come in and pray.
Came here as part of a coach tour. Worth visiting if in the area. Very photogenic, popular place for people to take weddings photos.

It's very small compared to the My Son ruins in Da Nang, but it's well maintained, compact and easy to see. It's quite impressive. Apparently they have irregular times for the dance performances, so I happened to see them. You only have to climb a few stairs, but the view of Nha Trang from the top was nice. You'll be done in no time. You buy a ticket for 30,000 VND at the entrance. It took about 10 minutes by Grab from the center of Nha Trang, where there are many hotels. On the way back, I just walked home, but I didn't see anyone walking, and there were mostly buses carrying group tourists lined up along the river. It was nice to walk along the sea. There were no pushy taxi drivers or people trying to sell things. (There might have been some, but I went near the group tourists.)

This old Hindu temple is located on a hill just outside Nha Trang and is still in use. At the top you have a beautiful view of the city of Nha Trang. The remains are very well preserved. A number of objects, mainly statues, are also exhibited inside. Very worthwhile. Song and dance performances also take place outside in style.

Compared to Long San Pagoda and Nha Trang Cathedral, which we visited before on our tour of the city, this is the oldest building, and it is also a Hindu temple, a religion that we don't usually come into contact with, so it was a very interesting visit.
Ancient and spiritual relic site! Unique sightseeing place in Nha Trang! Learn about ancient Champa culture with your family

thap ba Ponagar Temple is a great place to stop by for a quick photo shoot on your way back from a motorcycle ride. There are many foreigners and locals, and motorcycle parking is free. You need to buy a ticket to enter, but the price is negligible. I saw a performance here and it was very good. The temple has a tremendous amount of energy. Of course, this is a personal opinion, but a trip to this magical and ancient place is definitely worth your time.
Nice stop over the bridge from the center. Entry cost a little over 1 USD. Arrived around noon which was peak time with large crowds but still an enjoyable break on the way north to other places.
A small site but captivating. There are a few small temples within the compound as well as a small exhibition room. Entry fee is cheap. Definitely a must do if you are staying in Nha Trang city.
It was not too bad for a short stop over.. only need less than an hour to finish the tour around.. the place is fully restore and some angle has better photo views

It was a grand, imposing tower that exuded a sense of history. I was lucky enough to catch a glimpse of a traditional dance performance. I bought a framed calligraphy piece as a souvenir at a shop on the grounds. It was quite lively as we encountered a tour group, probably from South Korea.

Beautiful place to visit, however a man who I initially thought was a tourist cornered my husband in one of the temples/ museums. Very smart, as there was no one else there so he was obviously watching our movements & waited to isolate us. He first excused himself very politely then started waffling on about being a teacher. Handed hubby a paper who gave it straight back with a " no thanks".  The man became quite angry , stormed off and swore in Vietnamese. What ever department runs these lovely sites should really watch out for these types of people. We're well travelled so can sniff them a mile off. We were also approached by "students" at Long Son Pagoda whom we totally ignored.  Definitely worth a visit, just be firm if anyone approaches and enjoy.
Really nice, accessible, and quick to do. They lend out outfits for visiting the temples. Don't hesitate if you're passing through Nha Trang.

Interesting and unusual temple for Vietnam, with good reconstruction. Very reasonable entrance fee (10k) and only 50k for an English-speaking guide (although her accent was not easy to decipher). There are also QR codes on each tower for English explanations.

A very pleasant place to touch the ancient history of Vietnam. These orange bricks hold the history of millennia! Very similar to ancient Indian temples or temples in Cambodia. You can take interesting photos and listen to folk music! Small fee - great view!
We visited Po Nagar today, and really enjoyed it. It only takes about an hour or two to see the whole grounds. Very neatly kept grounds
That is very beautiful place.It located in the center,so its easy to come.The ticket in arround just 1$
The place is pretty, although you'll quickly see everything; it's quite small. Entry is inexpensive (24,000 VND, I think).
This is the ancient architecture of the champa people.Its very beautiful and legendary.The entrance ticket is also very good value
The unique Cham culture is a highlight of Nha Trang. The Ponagar Tower, located next to the Bóng Bridge, is a historical landmark in Nha Trang.

These Champa ruins might not be as expansive as those in My Son (near Hoi An/Da Nang) but they are very impressive and beautiful. It is not expensive to go in and the money goes to upkeep. You should plan to dress appropriately but if you don't have suitable clothing, you can borrow a cover-up at the top at no charge. They seemed clean enough. Don't forget the small museum at the back that shows pictures of before and after restoration.

I finally made it to one of the iconic landmarks not only of Nha Trang but of all of Vietnam – the Po Nagar Towers! I honestly contributed 22,000 dong to preserving the country's history. I didn't feel the powerful energy that only a true Buddhist can sense. Apparently, I'm not a true Buddhist... Getting to the towers is easy. Take bus number 4, tell the conductor "Po Nagar," and he'll send you off at the right stop to learn some history. By the way, all conductors now ask when issuing tickets: "Where are you going?" And all the information can be found online. So, if you don't have a guide or are an introverted traveler, go on your own. It's beautiful and colorful. Sometimes you can catch a music and dance show. Another advantage of a solo walk is that you notice your own "gems"! For example, I saw a small replica of our Krasnoyarsk cliffs from the Krasnoyarsk Stolby National Park – Feather Rock. However, with a good guide (the key word is good!), you'll undoubtedly get more information.
Table View, South Africa
The tour guide was very enthusiastic, and the scenery was beautiful and picturesque, with the Cai River flowing alongside the Xom Bong bridge.
Lovely place to spend an hour or so. Hard to grasp how old these towers are considering most old things in Vietnam seem to be demolished in the name of progress. Gardens immaculate and views lovely.

I've visited many Cham towers in the central region, and this is my second time at Po Nagar Cham Towers. It's probably one of the few tourist attractions in the heart of Nha Trang that still retains its ancient architecture. Therefore, it's quite crowded with tourists. I had to wear a mask throughout my visit to protect my health.
Very quiet and not so many guests as we visited after the corona crisis. a small place to chill out but not so many services to add on.

The temple is a mystical place for prayer, reflection, and peace. If you are not dressed appropriately, it's not a problem, as the place provides clothing to enter respectfully and offer your prayers. It's worth a visit.
Great little 45 minute experience.  Very similar to the architecture in Angkor, Cambodia. Great value at one dollar entry. Nice pics to take.
Nothing out of the ordinary, you can come and walk around, there is an entrance fee, there is a parking lot for scooters, there is a good view of the city
In the busy city of Na Trang, this is a peaceful spot. Beautiful view of the city and the sea. A taxi here costs about 2 euros.

The Cham ruins are a beautiful site. The chilled coconut was delicious. You need to take off your shoes to visit the temple and wear the robes they provide. The only downside is that the site is a bit small. Taking photos takes about an hour.
Cheap to get in and easy to get to. Worth a visit as the site is so old and good value for money. Would recommend a tour as nó information is provided

A beautiful historical site that's very easy to reach from Nha Trang (about a half-hour walk from downtown Nha Trang, 10 minutes by taxi). The towers are in good condition. It's perfectly possible to visit without a guide, just read the information online. Robes are provided for entry into the towers within the temple complex, so any clothing is acceptable. The grounds offer a beautiful view of the pond with boats and the bridge leading to the southern part of Nha Trang. On a sunny day, they make for beautiful photos.
It is really nice place to see but I highly recommend taking a tour guide because there should many things and stories to tell
This place looks very picturesque. If the weather is clear, you can get great photos against the sky. And if a good guide tells its story, like we did, everything is just superb.

20,000 Dong entrance fee to this small temple. There are some nice flowers on the climb up the stairs to the temple. When at the top there is a main temple in the middle then 2 smaller ones to either side. It is well maintained and you get views down to the river. There are lots of places to sit in the shade and a stall selling ice cream and drinks. We spent about 20 minutes there.

It's interesting to stroll around and see the ancient buildings. Some of them have been restored. You can enter one of the buildings after changing into a gray robe (they're hung near the entrance), but there's not much to see and the smell of incense is very strong. Entrance to the complex is 22,000 dong. You can get there by bus number 4. A tour is definitely not necessary.
A nice place to stop by and experience some of the culture if you're in Nha Trang. It's small, but it has a unique atmosphere.
Historic place kept clean and free of rubbish not the place to spend the whole day but worth a visit
It enjoys a good location, and is characterized by the use of red brick, but the interior is modest except for the deities with black faces. Its elevated position affords a beautiful view.

A nice half-day trip to visit this place. Went inside the Towers and it leaves you speechless for a moment, truly beautiful. Afterwards, enjoyed the stunning view over Nha Trang, sitting comfortably on a rock and enjoying the view. Had a delicious Vietnamese iced coffee on the terrace; it's less than 1 euro, definitely worth the visit!!! Everything is well maintained, including the surrounding garden. I don't think this is very suitable for younger children.
Beautifully restored temple From the Champa period (similar to Khmer period) Worth a look in if you like ancient temples and culture...

A very beautiful site located near the city of Nah Trang. However, if you have already visited the enchanting My Son Sanctuary, you won't be as impressed by this temple. Note that for 20,000 dong you get a pleasant and entertaining visit lasting approximately 45 minutes. I recommend this place if you are in the area and don't plan to visit any other temples of this kind.

I don't consider it a must-see attraction in Vietnam, especially if you've already visited the My Son Sanctuary near Hoi An. However, for less than US$2, it might be worth the detour if you're in Nha Trang.
Small but valuable place to visit. Price of the ticket is affordable and you can use an official parking next to the temple for cheap price.
Magnificent man-made architecture... All the buildings were magnificent, but it was chaotic because there were so many visitors... It was a great place for taking photos, so I liked it.
I think Po Nagar reflects Vietnam culture better than other temples due to the multicultural influences. Despite many visitors it remains serene and brings you closer to God.

I can't say it's a must-see, but it's interesting. We rode a bike and parked across the street. There were almost no Russian faces, just a lot of Chinese. There was a short performance in the courtyard, with girls dancing in traditional costumes. It's a seven-minute drive from downtown Nha Trang, so 20 minutes was enough for the visit.

You really need a guide to tell you the history of the Cham people who built these towers and how it was damaged and reconstructed again. Quite interesting... and there was a small air-conditioned room (what a relief!) where they housed several relics and historial photos/info. Beautiful views of the River Cai and the 2 bridges - great photo opportunity!
There is not much big trees so it a lil bit hot and sunny. Interesting history about Cham people.Nice construction.
A lot of visitors, but this is worth the trip. Ancient blend of Hindu and Buddhist temple, well restored. Unlike any other temples we've seen in Vitnam before.

Not too far from hotel and a chance to understand a bit of history Dance band there playing authentic music and showroom fascinating showing pictures of temple now and hundreds of years ago Worth a visit
You can get there on foot, or just stroll along the seashore. There are plenty of places for beautiful photos, just like there are plenty of Chinese people.
The most exotic experience of my trip to Nha Trang! It reminded me of Angkor Wat, and the quiet, reverent atmosphere remains in my memory. However, do not expect too much! (That’s all)

An amazing place in Nha Trang! We got a glimpse into the ancient history of Vietnam. We saw all the towers, walked around the grounds, and had a wonderful time. We took a taxi from the European part of the island, but you can also take bus #4 for 9,000 dong. We arrived at 10 a.m. and there weren't many people there. Entrance is 20,000 dong.

Really beautiful structures. Super busy with tourists though, so it's quite difficult to get some nice photos. It's a place of worship so can't have shoulders/ chest, midriff of thighs exposed. However they offer a robe that you can wear if you would like to go inside. 20,000VND (1USD) per pax.

Four towers are compactly located. It has a sense of antiquity. It's located very close, making it incredibly popular. This is also a downside. There are huge crowds of tourists, making it difficult to take decent photos. And this is during the low season. It takes half an hour to explore.
This is a small place located in a very busy area of commercial activity. Thankfully it is set aside and preserved for historical purposes.
A beautiful temple with lots of bonsai-like plants and a view over the city of Nha Trang. There are several local restaurants nearby.

We went ourselves on bus number 4. Entry is 20,000 rubles. We arrived around 12 noon on a weekday. There were about 100 Chinese tourists per square meter. Taking beautiful photos was nearly impossible, although the place is beautiful. The temple has a special atmosphere. At the entrance to the large temple, you have to put on a robe for free (if your shoulders, stomach, or legs are exposed). You can stroll through the small park around the temples. It's okay for a one-time visit.

Because I love history, I would strongly recommend this place. Remember the more famous Ang Kor Wat in Cambodia??? This same Champa dynasty that built Ang Kor Wat also built numerous temples all over South and mid Indo China Peninsula. Do take time to appreciate the stones built with a mysterious "glue" which my guide told me that modern day people still cannot figure what exactly those great Champa people used. There is a small museum at the back of the temple grounds that exhibits precious artefacts found and they typify Hindu gods. Enjoy at least 1-2 hours and admire the city view from this hilltop temple. I saw a few brides takung their wedding shots on that sunny day I was at this temple. So I guess if the locals love this place then you might find some instgram-worthy shots too.

Upon arriving in Nha Trang in the morning, the first place we visited was Pho La Gar Temple. It is located right in front of the beach, and the architectural style is unique. It takes less than 30 minutes to look around, and the path to the temple is cool thanks to the shade of the trees. The entrance fee is 22,000 VND per person, and there are vendors selling fruit and souvenirs along the way.
I actually enjoy going to places like this because I like exploring historical landmarks with ancient architecture.
The entrance fee is 22 000 per person. 5 000vnd for bike parking. You could spend about 25-45minutes there. Preserved and maintained very well.

A religious site and open-air museum, with a well-maintained and manicured garden. Inside the site, there's a room with a dedicated photography exhibition and some furnishings; I'm not sure they're original because there are no descriptions. Recommended.
I flew to Nha Trang for the first time, and it's amazing that such ancient places exist. There are old bricks, as well as new ones.

If you are in Nha Trang this is definitely a place that you should visit. Really interesting site. It must have a lot of religous significance as there are a lot of Vietnamese people praying. Was crowded, but not a nuisance,

We were delighted with these attractions in Nha Trang. The views are very colorful, the ancient architecture is lovely to look at, and we were absolutely thrilled with the local performances. One of the places you definitely need to come and see everything.

The view of Champa Pagoda is very beautiful, took a few night views, there are also robes inside the pagoda for tourists to wear, always temple place, clothing must also be modest, so I also recommend tourists to visit this place.

We visited the sights ourselves. We weren't expecting anything special, but overall we enjoyed it. There weren't many people. If you're exploring the city yourself, it's best to choose a weekday; there aren't as many Chinese tourists. A little life hack in the heat: a coconut in front of the entrance is 30 dong; 30 metros later, toward the bus stop number 4, it's 10 dong.

It is a bit far from the city center, but it is worth getting a ticket and visiting. If you have too high expectations, you might be disappointed, but considering there aren't many sights or tourist attractions in the area, it is a great spot for photos.

This is a popular place to visit and is quite crowded with locals and tourists. There is a good view of the river from here and there are performances by musicians and dancers throughout the day. You can have a guided tour if you like, but you must be dresses appropriately and no shoes when entering the building.
The scenery is beautiful but a bit crowded. It's a famous landmark for religious people. We will definitely recommend it to our friends.

15,000 is fine. You can climb, you can see far from the top) I think you can see Phan Thiet and Mui Ne, there are some catacombs up there) There's not much to do there... for general development, and on a moped, it's fine

The legends about the deities are very beautiful, and the towers themselves are beautiful and architecturally interesting. Although it is a non-aggression religion, it prayed for the health and well-being of its loved ones. The photographs are striking.
Ancient architecture, rich in cultural characteristics. Suitable for traditional sightseeing and families.
Very picturesque view with crowds of tourists, tedious but bearable. Worth visiting. You can also find some local crafts here and a small market nearby.

Before the trip, we researched what we could see on our own. This beautiful place can be reached by city bus for 8 dong per person. And for about $1 per person, you can see this architectural monument. It's a very unusual structure with functioning temples. It's beautiful and worth a visit while staying in Nha Trang.

Most tourists probably come to Nha Trang specifically to see Po Nagar. The complex isn't as large as you might expect and is very crowded, especially when cruise ships are in port. Early morning or evening are the best times to visit. You should wear long trousers and clothing that covers your shoulders; otherwise, you'll need to wear a robe to enter. Since its origins date back to the 4th century, a visit is a must.
I find it an interesting historical sight. There’s a sense of calmness despite been crowded with many tourists.

I sat quietly on a chair at the back to relax in the extreme heat. The crowded area is a Hindu temple, featuring architectural structures not typically seen in Korea. The building is still used as a temple, so you can go inside, but it wasn't really worth it...

They are located in the city itself, it is not difficult to get there, the entrance is pennies. If you are interested in seeing Hindu and Buddhist temples, then this is a good place. At the entrance they give you special robes for free to go inside.
The place is clearly ancient, expertly restored. The crowds of Chinese ruin everything—there are SO MANY of them! But otherwise, it's very educational and a wonderful way to experience ancient times.

This place is a wonderful highlight in Nha Trang. Next to cai river in the middle of the northern part of the city. Very historic and still used as a temple by the local villages. This is very popular with tourist and can be busy in the mornings and late afternoon with tour buses!!
Very authentic, well renovated, and not too expensive. A really enjoyable visit! There are a few shows to see on site, and you can go inside the temples.
You can go there just for the sake of it. Just have a look and take some photos, but you won't get any real wow factor there. There are a lot of Chinese there, so be prepared for that.

It's quite an interesting place, but the crowds of tourists spoil the experience, especially for Chinese citizens. So if you want to enjoy it, you'll have to arrive at opening time. 30 minutes is plenty of time to see everything.
Good to visit. Beautiful temples! I rent a motorbike to go there. It is easy to find this location and you have an area to park !
A very popular spiritual site in Nha Trang, attracting many Chinese tourists, with a unique tower architecture. I would definitely visit again if I have the chance.

The castle stands majestically on a hillside overlooking the river that connects to the sea. It's easily accessible by taxi. The entrance fee is 22,000 dong, but I didn't like the fact that people were smoking; it should be designated as a smoking area.

Lovely to catch the sunset here. The spotlights in the main temple only lit up the lower part of the interior. If you look up above the altar, all you see is darkness. If you are wearing shorts, you need to wear a robe (loan free-of-charge) before you go into the temple. Don't miss the tiny 'museum' at the back of the complex. There are some interesting stone statues and old photographs of the Po Nagar Cham Towers.

Most of my visits to Nha Trang, involve a visit to this Ancient site.  This time , l had more time, or it must be the season, to relax and take in the culture.  I saw more this time than in all previous visits.

It's so hot today! There are so many people inside, especially Chinese people. You can't wear shorts, flip-flops, or off-the-shoulder clothing into the attraction, but you can borrow robes from the side. The temple is dedicated to Hindu gods, but I couldn't tell who was who. I only saw a painting of Shiva dancing. There were some indescribable sculptures.
A good place to visit when sightseeing in Nha Trang. Although not large in scale, there is plenty to see and it is a great spot for taking photos. You will need to wait in line to purchase tickets.

We took a Grab taxi from central Nha Trang for 65,000 rubles. A city tour was $15, but we didn't take one. I don't see the point; all the sights in the city are close to each other. You decide when and where to go, and how much time to spend. Entrance to the towers is 22,000 rubles. You wander around, take photos, ooh and aah. It's a beautiful place, but the people! Before you go, Google it, read up on what it is, what these buildings are for, and what their purpose is. Learn how to behave in a temple, what to wear. Beautiful ancient structures, all surrounded by greenery and flowers. Lots of tourists from China. A leisurely two-hour walk is more than enough.

The temple itself is small, so you can see it quickly. You can experience a temple style different from those in Korea. However, taking photos is difficult due to the hot weather and the large crowds.
Good place to visit and not expensive.  They provide you with overalls to allow you to enter the towers should you be under dressed.

Hot and sunny day to visit but good for photoshoot. Remember to bring umbrella, hat, sunglasses and lots of water. You may bring along food for picnic.  The temple area also gives peaceful aura. If you are lucky, you may also get to witness some cultural performance. One of the smaller towers had a few small exhibits explaining the history of Po Nagar Cham Towers.  Be sure to take ample photos for your memory.

A self-guided tour by bus number 4 costs 9,000. Entry is 22,000 VND. After 10:00 AM, there are crowds of tourists. Go early. There are plenty of natural photo spots, and a little further behind the towers, there are quiet alleys where you can sit and relax. A local café sells delicious ice cream by the glass. If you take bus number 4, the Rock Garden is three stops further along the route.
Beautiful place to visit, entrance fee reasonable. It is also clearly visible from outside the entrance gates, should you not wish to go inside.

The Po Nagar temples have been really well-restored. It was worth a visit and doesn't require a long time--maybe a morning. We wandered around, took some pictures, relaxed in the quiet areas. Ee found it quite interesting though we had to look the history up on Wikipedia as there wasn't much info in English around the grounds. It's still used as a religious site and women need to have their shoulders and knees covered to enter the temples...they do provide cover-ups for you there if you aren't worried about wearing something other people have.

This is the only temple worth visiting in Nha Trang. There is an entrance fee, but it is cheap. Once you go up, it is very simple; it feels like an unfinished miniature version of Angkor Wat. However, it is a must-visit in Nha Trang... there is nowhere else to go.

We visited as part of a sightseeing tour. It's worth a visit. Beautiful and clean. The only thing is, there are a lot of Chinese people in the morning. Lots of tour groups, buses crowded everywhere, and tourists with umbrellas making noise. It's beautiful inside. There's a small alley at the top where you can sit in the shade.
A little bit of Siem Reap in Nha Trang, Vietnam
There are so many Chinese people, you need to go early before they arrive. Impressive! Everything is well-kept and very beautiful, exotic.

The entrance fee is quite cheap. The ancient tower is beautiful, and there are Cham dances and musical instrument performances. This place is suitable for those who like to explore culture and history.

The only thing that surprised me about the Cham Towers was their steep staircase. This dilapidated structure would be of interest to those who appreciate culture and just want to check the box. And perhaps feminists and those whose religion includes the worship of Nagar and Shiva and their sons.

I have been there early in the morning, but it was so crowded. Anyhow it was interesting place. its needed about 1 and half hour to see all the site. There are some small shops from where I bought souvenirs for presents. Recommended!

It's very old and unique, however, 90% of visitors are Chinese. The location is relatively easy to find, but it's been overly urbanized, leaving very little surrounding space, and there's no parking available, with cars spilling onto the road, causing safety hazards and detracting from the urban landscape.

You can get there on foot from anywhere in the city. We went on a sightseeing tour with a group without having to wait in line. The line at the ticket office is less than 5 minutes long, based on the view. It's interesting that if there weren't so many Chinese people there, you could relax and take in the views. The sightseeing tour description says to wear protective clothing, but that's only if you want to go inside the towers themselves. It's not very interesting inside, just an altar and a black, smoky room. So dress lightly. They'll give you a robe if you want to go inside.

Nha Trang isn't exactly known for its sights, which is probably why the Cham Towers are so highly rated. It's a picturesque place, especially at 6 a.m., when there was absolutely no one there. I'm not a fan of crowds, but since I was going out for a run that morning anyway, I ran the 5 km to the towers and thoroughly enjoyed the walk. I can't say I'd recommend it, but if you're stuck for things to do, it's worth a visit.

Since Nha Trang is a resort town, there aren't many notable tourist attractions. The Cathedral, the Stone Buddhas, and the Po Nagal Temple are commonly mentioned. Although the weather was hot, I skipped the Cathedral because I had seen more magnificent ones in Europe, and the Stone Buddhas because I had seen them in Korea. I only visited the Po Nagal Temple, and it was an experience that is hard to find elsewhere. Although small in scale, it is worth visiting at least once if you go to Nha Trang.

Since there aren't many notable tourist attractions in Nha Trang, I am very grateful for this place. It has the atmosphere of a very small Angkor Wat, and the view of downtown Nha Trang from near the tower is beautiful.

The architecture of this tower is beautiful and unique. Don't worry if you'tr wearing shorts or sleeveless shirt because they provide a grey gown if you want to enter the praying area. Remember to bring water and umbrella/hat because it's too hot and crowded in there.

So beautiful doesn’t look like it should exist in real like. Unlike other temples where they kick you out for wearing shorts we got a very fetching grey robe to wear which circumvents the problem. Also had a very nice tradition Vietnamese music act playing. The only drawback was how packed it was, perhaps limit the number of people at the site during one time?

The Ponakha Pagoda is located in Nha Trang and is easily accessible by Grab car for both going and returning. The entrance fee is 22,000 VND (28-29 THB). Inside, there are several pagodas and an antiquities museum to visit.

There's really nothing much to do here. Just look at the towers. I went into one little temple. It was really stuffy. Their incense is strong. It's not our thing. We walked around the garden, and that's it. Chinese, Chinese, Chinese. And it's terribly hot. The only thing I can say is: don't worry about dressing, as they say, everything is closed in the temple. They give you robes. You put them on, go into the temple, come out, and hang them back up.
It's an interesting and beautiful place, but the whole impression is spoiled by the countless number of Chinese tourists who are simply everywhere.

This is a Cham tower in Nha Trang. I think the tower has a unique Cham architectural style. You can take many beautiful photos at this location and experience a novel feeling while visiting the tower. However, perhaps because it's one of the most famous landmarks in Nha Trang, it's very crowded and quite chaotic. There aren't any information signs in front of the towers to teach tourists more about the history or special features of this monument. Nevertheless, if you ever visit Nha Trang, you should still come and see it!

There are several chapels and a 12th-century tower on the grounds. Entry is barefoot and shoulders must be covered. We entered the chapel, which is small and has black walls. The energy is very heavy. Some kind of ritual was taking place in the largest tower, as it is still in use. The grounds are nice, with a fountain. Lots of Chinese people after 1:00 PM. A one-time visit is fine.

The towers are fascinating and a worthwhile place to visit in the Nha Trang area. According to some sources, the complex originally consisted of ten towers, each dedicated to a different deity. The towers' architecture and appearance varied greatly, as they were built at different times, sometimes centuries apart. Only four towers remain, all located next to each other. The interior of the towers is quite dark and cramped, with no daylight penetrating. Small altars have been erected, and the brick walls are almost blackened by incense smoke. A sense of antiquity and a connection with history never leaves the visitor. Within the temple complex, next to the North Tower, there is an exhibition hall displaying several sculptures of Hindu gods and photographs of the Po Nagar complex before restoration. The tour takes no more than an hour; admission is required. The Po Nagar temple complex is located in the north of Nha Trang, across the Cai River. You can reach them via either of the two bridges connecting the two parts of the city. The walking route from the center takes about 40 minutes. You can also take bus #4 (get off immediately after the bridge) or a taxi. You can also rent a bike or bicycle to reach the towers.
The location is beautiful, with great photo opportunities. It's small and very crowded with visitors. I haven't been inside the tower yet, so I can't comment on the interior.

This temple was belonged to the Cham peolpe, a good place with many trees around. It's so cool if you take a panorama view from here. There are 6 huge pillars made from brick, supposed to be a place for offering worship. I was really impressed with the statues and beliefs. It has a stone slab carved the legend of the tower.

We visited the towers. There were so many people there, the Chinese almost trampled us. We didn't go inside. The surrounding area is very beautiful. The trash in the Cai River delta was disappointing. Also, on the way to the towers, along the embankment, there was a foul smell. If all of the above doesn't scare you, then boldly go forth and experience ancient Vietnam.

A lot of reviews remark about this attraction being overcrowded with tour groups. We went at about 4:30 pm and there were no tour buses in sight and a light crowd. In addition the heat had gone out of the day and a cool breeze drifted across the site. Yes the towers have been rebuilt from ruins but they are no less interesting or spectacular for that. Many locals and tourists still pray here. There is one main tower with 4 smaller towers around it. Inside each are altars with deities and incense pots. Cover shoulders, knees and remove headwear and shoes before entering. You will also have good 360 degree elevated views of the town and ocean. 22,000 vnd per person to enter. Plenty of metered taxis to get you the 3 km back to town centre if it’s too hot or yr too tired to walk. Worth a visit late in the day. It closes at 6 pm

My first visit to the Hindu temple. It was so beautiful there. Try to visit it in the early evening during the sunset. The temple is lit up by the sky and sun as well as the artificial lights, which enhance its beauty. Overall I enjoyed my visit a lot.
What a beautiful place! The views are fantastic, perfect for a photoshoot. But it's very crowded!

One of the must visit place in Nha Trang. The temple is genuinely unique and am so glad that my wife talked me into coming to see it. Expect lots of other tourists too as the place was buzzing with people. Great views.
One of the must visit place in Nha Trang. The look of the temple is very unique similar to those in Siem Reap/Angkor Wat.

It's easy and quick to get there from the city center by public transport for 9 dong. The place is colorful, authentic, and steeped in history, offering plenty of great photo opportunities. The big downside is that it's always crowded, noisy, and tires you out quickly.

A beautiful Buddhist temple, of course, with lots of Chinese tourists there. There's plenty to see inside, including the culture, the statues, and the flora. The downside is that across from the temple is a river-cum-dump with, pardon me, floating toilets. It really smells like garbage because everything floats there.
We visited this city landmark. Yes, it's ancient. You can go inside the temple and watch the dancing. But the area is small. Lots of tourists during the day. It's best to go early in the morning.

I went with friends and my child. I deducted one star because it is difficult to use strollers or wheelchairs. It is beautiful and nice every time I visit. Nha Trang is convenient and nice because the tourist attractions are not too far from the city center.

Still active temple of the Cham, Chinese and Vietnamese Buddhists, and impressive in its own rights, though it is a complex of temples rather than just one. What I take to be native Cham dancers and musicians present a local dance for either ceremony or the tourists, which is a winner whatever the reason. Not sure what the times are and perhaps a check online could ensure you're there in time.

Ancient Hindu temple complex of the Cham people. Unfortunately, it's heavily marketed to tourists, so you'll have to share the site with crowds of people, especially Chinese, if you go at the wrong time. Nevertheless, it's worth seeing, a little gem. From here, you also have a beautiful view of the port facilities of Nakh Trang.

Not up to the magnificence of Angkor, of course, and the majority of the structures have been renovated, but give an impressive idea of what the site was originally like. No 'interpretation' there so just as well I always research history before visiting places such as this. Yes, be prepared for the hoardes of iritating people 'posing' for selfies, though not really knowing what they are looking at!
If you're bored and have nothing better to do, you can go and see them. The towers weren't exactly jaw-dropping, but for anyone interested in foreign cultures, a visit is a must.

Located about 10 minutes from downtown Nha Trang, this place is definitely worth a visit. However, I highly recommend visiting at 7:00 PM before it gets crowded with tourists. It will be less hot, and by 7:30 PM, the crowds are massive.
Small small angkor wat. Iice. Worth visiting i recommand because there is not msny things to see in Nah trang

If you're a seasoned tourist and have visited many different sights in Asia, you'll be very bored here. There's nothing much to see, crowds of tourists (especially the screaming Chinese), the heat... It's better to choose another destination for your excursion. Tourists visiting these towers are apparently attracted by the low entrance fee.

Po Nagar Cham towers are more than a thousand years old and is one of the major icons of Nha Trang and a must visit divine destination. It’s not a very big place and an hour should be enough time for the visit to go conveniently around the site. You will need to go up around 50 stairs up the hill and since it’s an outdoor attraction it is recommend to plan the visit at a time when you can avoid the sun, such as late afternoon. It consists of 3 towers as well as the temples and a showroom that displays some of the pieces from the history. Live music and dance shows can also be seen at the towers. You can also have a view of the city below on side and the sea and the bridges on the other side. There are a couple of souvenir shops at the top and few at the base. Since it is one of the main tourist attraction you can expect it to be pretty busy most of the time. Fresh coconut juice is available at the exit  WC available at the site

We came around 4pm and there were as much tourists entering the place as there were leaving.  Costs VND22,000/entry. Be prepared for crowd. I’d say visit if you have the time. At least see what the towers look like. They are really beautiful ancient sculptures that have been taken good care of.
Very nice and historic place. HIndu influence - place of worship to Shiva. High on the hill so it gave you some scenic views of Nha Trang. You can go inside the temple to pray. Highly recommended

The place itself is epic and really worth a visit! Just watch for the million or so Chinese tourists who seem hell bent on posing for a selfie or standard crap Instagram pose...also as with the beach there was a lot of rubbish...there certainly is a theme with rubbish here

A pretty interesting place to check out in Nha Trang.  The entrance fee is cheap but when we went (in the late afternoon) there were so many other tourists it slowed us down a little bit. I've seen other people commenting on the same thing, so I would suggest arriving first thing in the morning or possibly the last thing in the day. I would have thought 1 hour is more than enough time to check out the whole area.  The towers are quite well maintained and there are some good photograph opportunities (depending on the number of other visitors). If you're wearing shorts and want to step inside a sacred place - they have complimentary robes which can be borrowed meaning you won't miss out on seeing things.  The outside carpark is superbusy with tour buses and other cars. We used the Grab app to get there and back which was very convenient
Nice place to go and good to know about the history of Nha Trang before the Viet arrived there. Of course lot of tourists.

You should definitely visit Po Nagar Cham Towers. The towers are preserved very well and in good condition. It's not necessary join day tour to visit the towers as it is located in a convenient location. However, it is necessary to visit early to avoid the busy crowd as they create quite a nuisance around the towers, from smoking everywhere to spending hour taking pictures with no consideration of others. Therefore, if you want to take some quality photos, please make sure you go early as it gets busy after lunch till closing.

If you were expecting Cambodia's Angkor Wat, this isn't the place. However, it is perfect for experiencing the Champa Kingdom. It is a pretty and beautiful temple. Even though it is hot and there is no shade, it would be a shame to just pass by. You can fully appreciate the artistry of the skilled Vietnamese ancestors. I recommend stopping by and experiencing the charm of that red temple.
This place was too crowded, mainly Chinese tourists when we got there at 10 am. It opens for tourists at 8:00 am in the morning, so I recommend you getting there earlier to avoid the heaps.

Visit in the morning, before 11. After that, it's very hot. Plan on 1 hour. There's a restroom. You need to cover your shoulders and ankles. But you can borrow a robe for free before entering the temple itself. Read the wiki first.
An exotically beautiful place, the stones breathe time, the photographs turn out excellent, it is interesting and awakens the right thoughts and desires.

It's a beautiful ancient civilization, but it's very crowded. If you go on a weekend, there are tons of Chinese tour groups, probably thousands. The weather is quite hot, and it's very congested. There are Vietnamese street food stalls outside the area that are inexpensive and taste good.

We visited Cham towers on a Sunday afternoon, it was very very busy when we arrived, with all the bus tours we did enjoy it and there was traditional dancers on the day we was there, my best advice would be visit in the morning before it gets too busy.

About 1pound sterling for two people to get in, best to grab a taxi to get their rather than go on a hotel guided tour far cheaper. you have to wear long trousers or dress to get in the temple, but it still good to go round the outside of it

Nice place to visit on the way to Phan Thiet. Well maintained, lovely view from the top- worth walking all the way up. Quite isolated so would recommend having the taxi wait as might struggle to get another one. Our driver was excellent took us around all morning, waiting at each stop- worked out a lot cheaper than tours of Phan Thiet.

An interesting, historical place. An observation deck. Vietnamese girls in bright costumes were dancing. I didn't take the bus, but a Grab motorbike taxi. The price was 20,000 rubles from the center. Very convenient and inexpensive.

This is unlike other temples, churches, or other places of worship. There was some dance performance at the time of our visit. Some description of the structures would have been nice, but they wouldn't likely have provided them in English even if they did.

We visited the Cham Towers during a tour. It's a stunning temple complex. It's beautiful both inside and out. You need to bring a robe to go inside. The Vietnamese guide's explanations of male and female principles were amusing. And many people were seriously into it. On the platform between the towers, monks and women in traditional dress put on short performances. The enormous columns were also impressive. I highly recommend this place.

This temple is not as big and grand as My Son or Angkor Wat, in fact it is very tiny compared to those. But I still would say go for it because there isn't much other things to do in Nha Trang (in my opinion that is). The temple area was clean and still active as worshipping place so if to enter one of its temple, remove shoes, dress appropriate (although they provided free temple clothing to borrow if you need one) Overal 30 mins is enough to wander in this place. Avoid midday as it could gets really hot.

It is the only Hindu temple in Nha Trang, but it is a bit uncomfortable because there are so many Chinese people. There is a place where you can rent clothes for free. If you want to see the inside of the temple, you must rent and wear the clothes to enter.

This place would be nicer to visit if not for the number of people who were there. Much renovation needs to be done to fully preserve this amazing place. Difficult to get good photos with the crowds. The buildings are pretty amazing to view.
Saw temples as part of our visit to Nha Trang. Very old. Appealed to my daughter more than myself. But worth a visit.Small entrance fee.

We spent about an hour there – taking beautiful photos, briefly watching a performance of dancing girls to live music, and peeking into the temple itself – a very interesting narrow, dark entrance. Inside, the walls and ceiling were pitch-black. We didn't circle around the statue of the goddess, as only believers entered before us to bow and pray. So, after walking through the hallway to the entrance, we turned around and left. It was a bit uncomfortable having to take off our shoes – we had no socks, and after seeing the bare, dirty feet of those standing in front of us with their unhealthy skin, we weren't really keen to go. So if you're squeamish but want to peek inside the temple, it's best to wear socks. There were a lot of people, but it was still possible to take a photo without anyone else in the frame; we just spent more time there.
Easy to get to , forget the nha trang day tours . Just use a taxi or hire a bike. This is central . Best in the morning before the buses of Chinese overun the place.

We took bus #4 from the hotel ourselves. There's an entrance fee. It's a small space, so about 30 minutes is enough to explore. There are periodic national dance performances on the museum grounds, and we managed to catch part of one. Inside the towers are statues and candles; you can go inside to see them. They provide free clothing to cover your legs and shoulders. It's interesting, but we didn't have a guide; it's better to learn the history of this place first; that way, it'll be even more interesting.

So, we went there on a sightseeing tour. The only significant downside was the ton of Chinese people!!!! The upside was the beautiful view from the top. While walking up to the towers, there was a terrible smell from the rotten fruit (which they sell along the road). If it weren't for the sightseeing tour program, we wouldn't have gone there.
Been there twice but did not want to go in because it was too hot during the summer but we came on our third trip! The weather was nice and it was kind of interesting.
It was quite interesting, we learned a traditional method of weaving on a loom, heard Vietnamese music, and looked at costumes

We walked from our hotel to the Cham Towers. We were rather impressed of finding such a retreat in the middl eof the hustle and bustle of such a busy town. A lovely garden. Remember to wear long pants to enter the inner parts.

Quite a few people, because naturally there isn't much to see in Nha Trang. It's nicely renovated, clean, with beautiful gardens, restrooms, and drinks. I wasn't familiar with the Cham civilization, but I learned about it thanks to my guide's explanations. To those who compare it to Angkor Wat in Cambodia, you have to compare apples to apples! 😀

The site is very accessible. A few steps and you're already at the foot of the columns of this remarkably stylish temple. It's all surrounded by a lovely little shaded park. The views of the river are also worthwhile.

A Hindu temple that's centrally located in Nha Trang that's worth a visit. Not much climbing and the Temple offers a interesting view of the city plus old Cham architecture that's nicely restored for the tourist industry. Visitors going inside the temple need to be in pants or wear a cloak provided by the Temple.

As part of a Cunard tour, a necessary evil in some locations, the place was chock full of tour groups. Did not bother to climb up the numerous steps as one could get some good shots from below or around the towers. Good to see that interest is being taken on preserving the past, unless of course it is simply tourism economics ?
I don't know if it was the time of day but this sight was very crowded. However, it's a very interesting piece of the cultural landscape and the history of Vietnam so is worth a visit.

The perfect place to start exploring touristy Nha Trang. It's easy, beautiful, and inexpensive. You can avoid encounters with Chinese people in the morning. Getting there is easy—take a city bus. The stop is nearby, you can't miss it. Conductors on the bus will always ask where you're going and point you in the right direction.

We've been there twice: last year with a guide, and this time on our own, taking friends there. It's best to visit in the morning—it's not so hot and there aren't as many Chinese; they arrive in huge groups around lunchtime. And, of course, it's best to go with a guide—they'll tell you a lot of interesting things.

We visited the towers on our own. We took a regular bus that runs along Nha Trang's Line 3. You won't miss it. We wandered around for about an hour and a half and watched an impromptu folk performance in front of the temple.
A historic site, worth a one-time visit for general education. You won't be able to enjoy the peace and quiet there, as there are too many Chinese tourists.

I am writing this on behalf of the Cham dancers who worked very hard, for what seems to be little pay, for those who are ungrateful. I visited this place with my father in morning and upon our arrival noticed that there were hundreds of Chinese tourists. They were load, rude and dirty, leaving trash everywhere and having complete disregard for the staff. After a dance, these Cham dancers sat under a doorway for a break and to get some air. Some rude Chinese tourists decided it would be funny to take pictures with them despite their refusal. I told the tourist to go away since they were being rude. I ended up tipping each one of those girls 200,000 Dong each, in a respectful manner. The Chinese tourist there ruined the experience for me since they were disrespecting a sacred ground, but the Cham people there reminded me of my purpose for being there.
