A very interesting place, a tribute to a bygone people. Lots of Chinese tourists. Great photo opportunities. Inside the buildings are ritual altars. A special robe, covering your shoulders and knees, is available for free entry.

When Po Nagar is literally translated it means “The Mother of the Country” and the apparently the Po Nagar Cham Towers were built to honour the goddess Po Nagar. Made of square red bricks, the tallest tower is around 25 metres and originally there were 10 but now there are only 4 left. They are located high on a hill which gives you lovely views of Nha Trang and its’ busy waterways. We were there at 9.30am and it took us about 20 minutes to see them all. People still come here to worship which you need to respect. There is a dress code and they had free “dresses” available for services. Some of the towers are quite small and dark inside but all were in good condition

We liked the visit of the pagoda which is consists of a few reasonably well restored pagoda style temples nicely situated between shadowy trees and a little museum providing limited insight on the restoration efforts of the sight. Entrance fee is 22,000 VND, children were free.

The cham temple is in very good state and one must see it here in ponagar nha trang. Buy your entry directly from the gate on your left. There is free parking for bikes and cars. The temples are in good upkeep. The main building is actually an ancient शिवा temple with the Lingam placed in the inner chamber.. But the vietnamese have done the unthinkable and have mounted thier local goddess on top of the lingam using the lingam as a base!! Smaller buildings around are different deities. A good visit allow 2 hrs.

I was staying in Cam Ranh and visited Nah Trang for the day. Got a taxi from Nah Trang Centre , by meter was only 45.000VD (~$2), and 5min drive.Entrance was ~$1. Lots of tourist. No real literature on the temples there, so read up online before. Amazing ancient site. Surrounded by lovely gardens. Worth a visit if I’m Nah Trang. You don’t need any more than ~30mims at this place.
Pretty cool to see but overloaded with tourists there for pics just to post on social media. Great views of Nha Trang. Worth the trip.
The remains of the Cham era, the one that gave the world Angkor Watt, are nothing special for those who've been there. A very touristy place, best to arrive in the morning.
The place is mesmerizing, but the crowds of tourists ruin everything. Just crowds of Chinese people from whom you can't escape anywhere.

The Cham were very early settlers moving into Vietnam from India. They thrived mostly in the southern part of the country. Ruins are in museums in Hue and other areas.  This is a nice grouping of some of their best preserved temples. Other areas are not as well kept and the museums only have pieces of Cham architecture and art.  You will get more out of this collection with a guide. We did not have a guide and there was little in the way of signage on-site. The guide books have very general descriptions.  While we were here there were some dancers and musicians. Apparently on the weekends especially there are such demonstrations.  This is an area to visit if you have extra time. 1 - 2 hours will do it.
Really cool place in the heart of Nha Trang . A lot of history the Cham people are different then Vietnamese of course . In one of the temple in English explaining the culture Cool place
Easily accessible by motorcycle. It's best to arrive early, as it gets very crowded with tourists. Motorcycle parking is subject to a fee. The entrance fee for the temple complex is reasonable.

The towers representing the old culture of people that once populated that area are definitely a must visit place. However, before you go there, read as much as you need because at the spot you will not find any information about what, when, how, for what purpose etc. The structures are interesting in their architecture and the decorative aspects. Definitely worth the entrance fee...
In the city on the side of the river is this old tower its full of tourist but they are very nice and worth it.

The ancient Po Nagar towers are one of Nha Trang's most colorful landmarks, a must-see on any city tour, and a significant cultural site. A little history: The main temple complex, of which the Po Nagar towers are a part, was built between the 7th and 12th centuries, although the first tower was constructed in 784 during the reign of the Champa king. Religious ceremonies, rituals, traditional processions, and other events continue to take place here today. Of course, over the centuries, the towers have been subjected to all manner of adversity. They have withstood fire, pirate raids, revolutionary uprisings, and military invasions. The temple has been destroyed several times, but local residents, believing in the powerful energy and immense power of this place, have always restored Po Nagar. Initially, the temple complex consisted of 10 towers. Each tower had a distinctive architectural composition, as their construction dates were several centuries apart. Today, you can explore four of the towers up close. The Northern Tower, Thap Chinh, is an example of Cham architecture. Built in 817 by Harivarman I, it was sacked by Malay pirates. Today, it is famous for housing a statue of the ten-armed goddess Uma and a Shakti figure of Shiva. The largest tower was erected in honor of the goddess Ponagar, believed to have bestowed agricultural and craft skills upon humanity. The tower stands 28 meters tall. The central tower dates back to the 7th century and boasts few embellishments. However, it once housed many treasures. Mieu Dong Nam, or the Southern Tower, is dedicated to Ganasha. Richly decorated, it houses ancient statues, inscriptions, and, of course, an ambiance. The current Western Tower, founded by King Vikrantavarman, has not fully survived. The northwest tower is dedicated to Shiva (Sandhaka), the south tower to Ganesha, the god of wisdom and prosperity, and the central tower to Kri Kambhu, patron of fertility. A Buddhist festival is held annually in the temple complex at the end of March. Colorful rituals, ceremonies, and mass theatrical performances attract both tourists and locals. We really enjoyed it; the place is atmospheric, and the photos turned out beautiful. I recommend it to everyone. Dress code: Of course, visiting the Cham towers without going inside is a big mistake, but before entering the towers, you must be appropriately dressed. Proper attire includes covering your shoulders and knees, no headgear, and bare feet. If you arrive at the towers inappropriately, you can borrow special attire from the designated area. All robes provided to tourists are gray-blue. Generally, an hour is enough to visit the Po Nagar temple (both the towers and the grounds). Afterwards, you can wander through the souvenir shops and pick out a few cute gifts for loved ones or as a keepsake. They sell all kinds of figurines, magnets, bags and backpacks, jewelry, colorful scarves, and other themed trinkets. At one of the shops, a woman teaches tourists the art of weaving. Naturally, for a nominal fee. Entry to the Po Nagar Cham Towers is more than nominal—$1 (21,000 VND).
The towers aren't impressive in size, but the park is beautiful. It's worth spending half an hour there. Tickets are 22,000 dong. It's also worth seeing at night, as it's beautifully illuminated.

very popular tourist attraction over the bridge with great view of the city. The ruins are very interesting but require more english signs to explain the significance of the site. Its also nice and shady the walk up is not strenuous and there are no hawkers trying to get your business- there are some souvenirs and beverages.in the complex plus stalls next door.
It's very beautiful and the 1$ entrance fee is definitly worth it. You can visit the hole place in 30minutes.
Surprising to see an ancient Hindu temple in Nha Trang! Situated on a small hillock it has a good view of the surrounding river and bay.  Reasonably well preserved and active temple.
The Po Nahar is a Hindu temple with various temples and gods. Not large, but it does make a good impression.

We walked from the vitamin sea hostel in the centre, takes 20-30 minutes to get there. The temple is interesting and worth a visit, you may want to bring your own guide as there is little English information on the temple and it's long history. There is also very little to keep you occupied for long, 15 minutes tops.
There are many tourists, but it was beautiful. There were some nice temples, and a beautiful view. I think it cost 44,000 Dong to get in. Nice price, for a good experience

This is a very special temple that you can’t find in the other places. The admission fee is very reasonable (USD 1). The place is not very large but worth visiting. 10 mins drive from tourist city center.

The historical site was nice. However, it was too crowded and noisy, so I left in a hurry! It seems like this place is part of a Chinese group tour course. Almost everyone there was Chinese. It is worth visiting at least once.

It is amazing what believes can accomplish. This magnificent site as a lot to offers to any visitors. Built over a thousand years ago 4 out of the 10 original towers remains intact.  You can pray, wish, discover a page of history or simply relax in this beautiful environment which also give stuning view of the city day or night.  If you plan a visit, remember that it closes at 6pm. Several original ortifacts are still displayed.

The towers stand on a hill just north of central Nha Trang, so offer picturesque views across the Cai River. Originally the complex had seven or eight towers, but only four remain. The North Tower at 28m high with a terraced pyramidal roof, vaulted interior masonry and vestibule, is the most magnificent. There are a few stairs to climb, but nothing too difficult.  We would recommend to visit early in the morning as the site gets very busy. We arrived around 11am after a morning of sight-seeing and it was packed! Managed to get a few photos not overwhelmed with people, but husband didn't enjoy the crowds. There are a few small gardens around the edges of the area though where we found a few moments of peace and quiet. Spend upto 1 hour here. Open 6am to 6pm. Tickets are 22,000VND (70p) each. There are clean toilets which are free to use. A taxi from Long Son Pagoda cost us 80,000VND.

The Cham towers of Po Nagar are located approximately 2 km north of central Nha Trang. The towers of the Cham Kingdom were built approximately 13 centuries ago on Cu Lao Mountain, on the banks of the Cai River. Only four towers, varying in size and architecture, built at different times over five centuries, remain today. Each was dedicated to the worship of a specific deity. The interior of the towers is very cramped and filled with the scent of incense and fruit. The tallest tower is approximately 23 meters. For the Vietnamese, this is a spiritual site where not only Buddhist ceremonies but also ancestor worship are held. It is very popular with tourists, who are brought here as part of "sightseeing tours." Admission is 20,000 dong. Parking is available for a fee.

The Po Nagar Towers are the only monuments to the Khmer presence in Nha Trang. A visit won't be a memorable experience, but in terms of price and quality, this is one of the best excursions. Tip: Visit independently using public transportation.
It's worth visiting and seeing, but it's best to do it early in the morning as there are always a lot of tourists, especially Chinese ones.
Nice place to soak up some culture. We took the opportunity when the weather was a little worse. You got to go in and check out a lot of temples. Cost 22,000 vnd per person.
You can visit this place When you want to see something peaceful and by visiting this place You can go to the street where you can eat seafood in cheap price they have entrance tickets of 22000vnd.

It was a rainy day but we decided to go anyway - and were glad we did! The ancient site is one of very few cultural things to do in Nha Trang so it’s worth the trip and small admission fee. There is definitely climbing involved - not strenuous, but difficult as far as varied size steps, uneven pavements, etc. so care needs to be taken. Even on a rainy, gray day, there were quite a few people (girls) there to take photos and they tend to block the entrances and viewing spots. This IS a bit annoying. In case you’re hungry afterwards, there’s a little, hidden, gem of a restaurant located nearby - right on the river. Be sure to check out Sunshine Bar!

It's relatively close to Nha Trang city center and can be reached by motorbike. The entrance fee is very cheap, only 20k VND. Some tour groups come to visit; there's a dress code for entering the building, but you can borrow a free long gown at the entrance.

The historical site in Nha Trang was originally a Hindu temple, later converted for Buddhist use. The site is small, with a small museum providing information and some replica sculptures. However, it shouldn't be compared to Angkor Wat, as the two are vastly different in scale and historical and cultural significance.

Worth a visit in my opinion just for the hussle and bussle of tourists.  Ruins were in a pure state even though some were restored and contrary to previous post the restoration is not perfect it has its own charms.  We two kids 1 and 3 year no buggy and it was fine.  Nice market across the road to buy fruits and snacks.  Very tourist heavy on weekends so avoid if that is a concern. But was genuinely not an issue for us and added to the experience.

Username: f i q a m i r
Comment: Po Nagar is a must visit spot for those who appreciate architecture of the ancient monument. the faćade and the architecture is compelling and impressive. Could be crowded with tourist all the time but worth visiting for a photography and appreciation

When we arrived in Nha Trang at dawn on the first day, full of enthusiasm, we decided to go sightseeing! So, we left our luggage at the hotel, had breakfast, visited the cathedral, and came over. We bought tickets right in front of the building. My husband said the ocean view from the top was great, so we enthusiastically climbed up, but the water was muddy. I guess we timed our visit wrong, going during low tide... Most of the visitors were Chinese tourists, and there was a separate area for prayer. There were also rest areas to sit and enjoy the breeze, and the restrooms were spacious, though not as clean as those in Korea. After walking around, we noticed a traditional performance taking place. We watched for a bit and then walked down along the path that resembled a walking trail.
The site is very small, but the entrance fee is low. However, it's difficult to take a photo without someone in the frame.

I visited it during a city tour with a tour operator. It was the only cultural stop on the itinerary. It's located on a hill, a good vantage point for panoramic photos. The grounds are pleasant and well-maintained. Free restrooms are available. There are souvenir shops. They provide free robes for entering the temple.
It's almost the only attraction worth seeing. Inside is a traditional Buddhist temple.
The place is very beautiful, we took a tour around it, everyone was naked, we didn't really have time to look around. Don't bring clothes, they give you robes, there's no point in bringing sweaters.

These towers were built between the 8th and 11th century so it is a historical site. If you can arrange for a tour guide, probably you will enjoy it more. There is not much to see but worth your time as you can take some neat pictures.
It is a decent place for a quick look around. However, I recommend visiting in the morning, as it can be difficult to take photos in the afternoon due to group tourists and the heat.

It seems well-located if it were a hotel... easy to access, surrounded by urban development, very crowded (did you see my selfie...?). It's still very beautiful, but we'd prefer Po Klong Garai if we can (100 km south).

This temple is about 10 minutes drive from town and its worth visiting if u happen to be in town. You can call 'grab' and its about vnd 50k for the ride from city to po nagar. Its not huge so it's just a place for you to take nice pictures. You won't take more than an hour.
With its long history and unique architecture, this place has become one of the most beloved destinations in Nha Trang.
We got there on our own, by bus. It was beautiful and atmospheric. Panoramic views of the Kai River and the bay. Lots of Chinese people.

The staircase, preserved in its original state and off-limits to tourists, is impressive. The descent is very steep. If your shoulders and knees are exposed, you can wear gray robes, tailored to your size. Entrance to the temple is barefoot and hatless. The temples are cramped and stuffy, but the scent of fragrant incense fills the air. You can stop by for an hour to see...

Beyond the bridge over the Cai River, the Po Nagar Towers, built by the Cham people, come into view. Of the four remaining brick towers, the North Tower, dating back to 817, is the tallest at 23 meters. The Central Tower, from the 7th century, is the oldest. King Sri Mara founded an independent state that stretched across southern and central Vietnam for 1400 years. The Viet ruled in the north, and the Khmer in the west. Wars raged between the Khmer and the Cham in the 12th and 13th centuries, culminating in the destruction of Angkor. From the 15th century onward, the Viet gradually gained the upper hand over the Cham territories. You can find more information about Nha Trang on my travel website, toeightycountries.com.
A historic site of the Vietnamese people! The interior is surprisingly small, and the visit is short.

Once you get into a trap of artificial destination called Nha Trang you should visit this place as it is only historical site from near to far worth visiting. Entrance fee was 22 000 VND. It´s situated on a little hill which allows to see 360 around if you walk around a couple of old reconstructed Cham towers. Don´t hesitate to enter them, there are a little sanctuaries with old atmosphere. Nicely maintained gardens with little paths all around, benches. Group of dancing woman with live music next to main tower.

This is truly a must-see! These ancient Hindu structures will leave you speechless. I recommend visiting early in the morning, rather than on a sightseeing tour. This way, you can explore the towers without the crowds and the scorching sun.

I paid the entrance fee to go in. It wasn't as big as I expected, but rather cozy. It was unique because it is a Hindu temple that you can't see in Korea. There were a lot of people, but not so many that I couldn't take pictures. However, since it's all outdoors, it's hot lol.
The towers are very beautiful, but you only have 20-30 minutes to visit them because there's nothing else to see. However, this spot offers a great view of the city. There are a lot of tourists.

There are many tour groups within the scenic area, where you can join guided tours and learn about Cham culture. The architectural style of the complex is very similar to Angkor Wat, exuding a strong sense of history. The ticket price is reasonable and definitely worth the money. There are viewing platforms with great views, and small exhibition halls within the scenic area where you can learn about Vietnamese history.
Nice place - if lucky and not crowded good for nice temple pics. Also has a nice view if the city and bay. Easy to get there by taxi.

Perhaps I should have given this place 5stars but, honestly, the sheer number of mainly Chinese tourists and the generally unsmiling staff cost it the 5th star The temple complex itself is quite beautiful and plenty of relatively quiet spots in the surrounding gardens to relax in. Easy enough to get to from anywhere in Nha Trang and pretty good views when you do  Insider tip...if you really want some peace and quiet, actually go inside one of the temples, everyone else is far too busy taking the ultimate selfie to have time to go in Failing that, it was much quieter just after 12.30 when the morning tour buses have left and the afternoon ones have yet to arrive

This place is close to Nha Trang city center, and it's very convenient and quick to get there by taxi. Although the park itself isn't large, the entrance fee is inexpensive, and it's unique and great for taking photos. I personally think it's worth a visit.
Fantastic historical site. Lots of beautiful sites and views. Worth a visit. Try to avoid tourist groups. Can catch taxi for about 60,000dong from Nha Trang beach
A national landmark. Not a must-see, of course, but definitely worth a visit. We took a free tour with a tour operator, so we didn't regret it. It offers a beautiful view of the city's surroundings.

Po Nagar Cham Towers definitely worth a look. It is a quick taxi ride or a longish walk along the beach front from central Nha Trang. We enjoyed the walk. (Me 63, wife very fit 70). It is located on a small hill with great views. There is a walkway under the main road to the entrance. Nearby restaurants serve great food at cheap prices.

There's a fee to visit these "towers," though it's not particularly expensive. We went around 12 o'clock, and apparently some kind of ritual was taking place there. It was quite fascinating to watch. You can see this attraction quickly, in about 20 minutes. It's worth visiting for those interested in architecture and historical and cultural treasures. But frankly, there's not much to see there.

We took a taxi from our resort to the temple and enjoyed it. There weren't lot of people visiting so it was great. We did some research before our visit so kind of knew what we were looking at but more information on the site would be helpful.

We visited this on a private tour on a very hot day. There were heaps of people and our guide tried to give us lots of information during. Wikipedia was a bit better to get an understanding. The ruins are well restored. Would gave been good to have more historical information available on site.

This was one of the few attractions to visit on our list whilst staying in Nha Trang. It is quite a distance outside the main city centre, we walked along the beach and over the river (took us about 50mins from our hostel) but we’d recommend getting a grab or taxi. The towers themselves stand on a granite knoll right on the banks of the river Cai. They are pretty impressive, admission costs 22,000d.
splendid location, full of charm at sunset, to be avoided possibly on weekends and at peak times due to overcrowding
Nice architecture of this tower, need to wear assign dress code when enter into temple for pray. Evening time, cultural dance and music performance is available.

Impressive Hindu Temple right by the Cai River. Po Nagar Cham temple complex was built between 8th and 11th centuries by the Cham people who once ruled the central plains of Vietnam. You will need to dress modestly and take your shoes off to enter the main Temple (one of four) and smaller Shrines but they provide smocks to borrow. Well worth a visit if you are in Nha Trang - best with a guide to explain the Temple and its history.

Cheap entry but no English guides available. Signs not in English so I couldn't fully understand the history. Interesting brick work and good to go inside where people were praying and offering goods. Very congested inside the temple
Astounded by the architectural design and mere size of the temple. Great view of the river and ocean from a distance.
It's a nice area, with some scenic views, but all the benches are occupied by Chinese people. There's nothing much to do there except take photos with the sights in the background.

We took a taxi at lunchtime. The ticket was 22,000 rubles per person. There were four towers, one with a small temple inside, and a garden around it on the banks of the Kai River. Lots of Chinese people!
A beautiful spot with a great viewpoint over the bridges. Interesting plants make for beautiful photos.

It was nice to experience the quiet atmosphere of the temple, unlike the bustling outside. Although you cannot enter the temple wearing short clothes, they provide free clothing rentals, so you can simply wear those to go inside. There is also a seafood street near Po Nagar Temple, making it a good place to have a meal.
Beautiful towers, with a beautiful view. The towers are well preserved and well looked after. Watch the parking scam outside, the parking is free!
A look into the history of the area. The area is clean and nicely preserved. Nice views of the Cai basin. Worth a visit.

If you find yourself in Nha Trang, like any city, there are must-see tourist spots. The Po Nagar Cham Towers are just such a place. It's hot and crowded. The area isn't large, but it's quite pleasant. I can't say I was thrilled, but it's an interesting place. There are pretty good views of the river. We were there on our second-to-last day before leaving. It was incredibly crowded and hot, and of course, you can't get around the Chinese. Let's just say that if you have some extra time and don't know what to do, you can visit this place. And of course, don't take a tour.

I went before the i-Resort tour, and even though I went early in the morning, it was hot. There isn't exactly a lot to see, but it's worth a quick look around. It's not huge, so it's not overwhelming.

It was a must-visit place I recommended. Po Nagar towers was Cham temple. The car was forbidden to park near the towers so I crossed a small road near the market for tourists, went under the bridge then straight to the towers. [ Honestly, at some point, there was strange bad smell ]. The rumor was the towers were made for worship of Shiva's wife. But then there was another rumor that Shiva was a woman so the towers was made for the worship of Shiva. No one knew the real story behind the wall of history. To go inside the temple, there was a place to borrow a long coat with long sleeve. Everybody must cover full body and take off their shoes to go inside. The statues were cover in some colorful clothes like statues in normal Vietnamese pagoda. It felt like something were wrong because the real and traditional Cham statues were not cover in any clothes. Anyway, Po Nagar towers was a really beautiful, special and historical places.
The reviews on the Cham Towers vary. Yes there has been much restoration and it is a popular tourist attraction. It is still worth a visit.

Children are free, and tickets are very cheap, just a few dollars. However, it's extremely popular with Chinese tourists. If you have time, you can check it out. But it's quite small; if you've already seen larger ones, I wouldn't recommend coming here.

Enjoy our little trip here. Beautiful temples, there was super busy when we got there. The security guards should learn how to treat visitors. Their attitude was terrible towards some tourist in front of us. What a shame! Don’t they know tourist pay for their salaries??? Probably not...

A beautiful Cham temple, a must-see in Nah Trang, especially since there aren't that many other things to see in this city... Well-maintained and used by locals who come to pray. There's a small "museum" area where a bit of the temple's history is explained. Many groups of tourists, mostly Asian, at least judging by the number of photos taken every minute without any discretion... (cliché? :-)

The Po Nagar Towers are definitely a famous landmark in Nha Trang! The entrance fee is only about 1,000 won, so it's worth taking a look around. I heard that the current buildings are what remain after parts of the original were demolished, so the original structures must have been much bigger and more magnificent.

It is a place worth visiting without high expectations. Besides the temple, if you climb the stairs and look down, you can see the beautiful scenery of Nha Trang. The temple is also photogenic, but there are so many people that you should give up on taking pictures of yourself alone.

Po Nagar is a smaller site than Po Klong Garai but it is just off the centre of Nha Trang and can be easily accessed on foot. It is quite a nice walk from the beach to the site across the fishing port.
A beautiful place I visited in 1991. Well-maintained and restored, but the only downside was the large number of Russians and Chinese tourists. Aside from that, given the heat, the place is pleasant.

This is one of the few historical sites worth visiting in Nha Trang. It is a Hindu Champa ruin site. Although it is hot, the view looking down from above is excellent. It would be even better if you went with some knowledge of its historical significance and value.

Po Nagar is a temple complex of the medieval Champa kingdom, which disappeared in the 10th century. Po Nagar is the goddess of fertility and agriculture. We got there by taxi from the Lotus for 45 dong (135 rubles), and entrance is 22 dong (65 rubles) per person. It's a picturesque place, but only 50 m by 100 m in area, so don't linger long. Inside, 2 m by 3 m statues stand, as everywhere else. There's nothing pompous or unusual about the temple, just interesting writings on the walls and views. Opening hours are from 6:00 a.m. to 5:30 p.m. It's best to visit on your own without a tour, as it's not worth the extra cost. You can read Wikipedia for yourself. From here, a taxi for 50,000 dong (150 rubles) takes you straight to the cathedral built by the French. Or you can walk all this if you are not afraid of the heat, distances (5 km) and street traffic.... Here is an overview for you... a garden or a cape of stones is probably not interesting to anyone... it's like taking someone to Lenin Street in any Russian town... Enjoy your stay.

You can feel a distinct atmosphere and energy that is quite different from temples of other religions. Although it is not very large, it seems like a suitable place to visit within Nha Trang in about an hour. Aside from the pagoda, there are no special amenities.
Located near the city center, in the Western Quarter of Nha Trang. The space is beautiful, but unfortunately lacks greenery. Families with young children should avoid visiting during hot weather.

It was very hot. It takes about 10 minutes by taxi from downtown Nha Trang, so I recommend renting a car. The scenery is nice, and you can enter the prayer area inside the temple wearing something like a monk's robe. Many people were praying. I deducted a point because the beverage vendors were incredibly unfriendly.
The Po Nagar Temple felt like a temple that could symbolize Southeast Asia. The photos turned out beautifully, and since it wasn't too big, I was able to look around without getting tired.

A must-visit place when visiting Nha Trang. There isn't anything particularly special to see, but you can see that the architectural style of the Hindu temple is truly unique. Be mindful of the scorching sun, and be careful as Chinese people smoke inside the temple.

It's quite close to the city center of Nha Trang. I can't believe there are ruins in such a place. It seems to have been built around 780 AD. The restoration work isn't great, but it has an impressive and dignified presence. There are souvenir shops and it's become a tourist spot. It's convenient to come and go by taxi. You can easily catch a taxi back as well. I think it's worth going. I personally love ruins, so I might be biased. It's on top of a hill, so you can see the city of Nha Trang.

Was nice to visit however it was crowded and filled with Mainland tourists, making it difficult to truly enjoy and visit the temples peacefully. Felt that it was quite disrespectful as these temples are still in use for worship. They provide a cover for people who are not wearing clothes that cover their shoulders and knees as respect to enter the temple.
In Nha Trang, this is one of the must-see places. An ancient structure with modern additions. A place of worship. Views of the city and the river.

It's a wonderful place, but the ubiquitous Chinese don't allow you to properly explore it. And the dancers were already tired, with sad faces. We were there at around 11 a.m. You should probably come either earlier or later in the evening.
Those who don't want to trudge through the sightseeing tours, especially since they all cost money these days, can easily visit the sights on their own. Bus #4 costs 8,000 dong.

It is a meaningful place where you can get a glimpse of ancient Vietnamese history and culture. You can see just how great the ancient Champa Kingdom was. One or two hours fly by as you look around. It is a highly recommended tourist destination.

I went here because it is in the city tour, I think the view is beautiful, overlooking the river, city, .. The tempers are vintage, close to nature,

Very interesting architecture, a stunning place. We took many amazing photos here. We highly recommend visiting. But be prepared for crowds of tourists, especially Chinese ones. They create a lot of noise and commotion everywhere.

Many informal places accept US$ but the exchange rate is poor. TOP TIP - Cyclo/Pedicab Taxis are great fun, especially when they take you the wrong way along one way streets. However, there is a health warning – make sure that you absolutely agree the price AND the currency before you start. On two separate occasions our price went from US$2 each for a 200m journey to US$50 for a 500m journey – once in, you can’t get out. If you get dropped off out of sight of your destination (and associated security services) you know that you are going to get fleeced. Our solution was to offer the original fare (with a decent tip), leave it on the pavement and walk briskly away. Didn’t fail for us.

Real crowded and hot in the afternoons so make sure you get there early in the morning. If you are staying in midtown a number three or four bus will get you there for less than two bucks. Best also to read up on this site's importance as nothing appears to be in English.

Although it’s small size, but it looks nicer than picture u can find in google. However we were forced to pay to a little counter there (it was stated free on the internet). Good place for photos, but nothing much else u can do there. Worth visiting as it is not far from the city area.

you can get there by taxi or bus. just a short distance out of town. this is a small temple but historic values. it is built centure's ago. need to pay a small entrance fee but whole the time if you like old architecture.

Very Popular tourist attraction. Interesting set of mini temples, some accessible, mysterious appearance, easy to get to.

We decided to go to the Po Nagar towers) We went on our own, took bus 4, near the Galina hotel, there were a lot of us, Russians) The fare is 8,000 dong, we showed the conductor on the map where we wanted to get off, she called out when we were getting off... Entrance is 22,000 dong (about 58 rubles) children are free. We were lucky with the weather, it was not scorching, we went at 2 p.m., there are a lot of Chinese, probably a million! We went on a tour; you can buy and try durian under the bridge near these towers. The towers themselves are very beautiful, but go to Vietnam and listen to the tour guide; he'll tell you everything in detail.

A beautiful place, with stunning views of Nha Trang and pleasantly maintained grounds. And everything would be fine if not for the hordes of Chinese tourists. It's a veritable plague of locusts. The Cham Towers are located on a small hill, and there are a lot of people taking photos there. If you want to take good photos, remember to avoid going during peak tourist season.

The local religious buildings are quite old and unique. There's a dress code for worship; shorts, short sleeves, and short skirts are not allowed. We visited the Cham Towers as part of a sightseeing tour, our first in many years of traveling (a free bonus from the tour operator). And we didn't regret it at all.
