It's quite small, with architecture characteristic of both Bangkok and Cambodia. I came here because I missed the architectural styles of Bangkok and Ayutthaya so much. I'd say it's alright, I guess. I took some really nice photos.

This is the shrine of Queen Po Ina Nagar (also known as Yang Po Inư Nagar or Yang Pô Ana Gar). The overall architecture of Po Nagar consists of three levels, from bottom to top. -Lower level: At ground level is the gate tower, which no longer exists. From there, stone steps lead up to the middle level. -Middle level: Here, only two rows of main octagonal brick columns remain, each with 5 columns more than 1 meter in diameter and more than 3 meters high. On either side of the large columns are 12 smaller and shorter columns, all resting on a brick platform more than 1 meter high. Based on this structure, it is believed that this was originally a large building with a tiled roof, a place for pilgrims to rest and prepare offerings before going to the shrines above. From this middle level, a steeper series of brick steps leads to the top level. -Top level: This is where the towers were built, directly in front of the main tower. These steps... It has long been out of use. The laterite stone steps currently seen south of the larger Po Nagar Tower were built in the 1960s due to increased tourism demand. The main tower in the front row is quite large and about 23 meters high, the Po Nagar Tower, commonly known as the Lady Tower. Originally, it was a tower dedicated to the goddess Parvati, Shiva's wife. The main tower is dedicated to the goddess Po Nagar (Umar), Shiva's wife. The Lady Tower has four levels, each with doors, statues of gods and animal figures made of stone, and four smaller towers at the four corners. Inside is a statue of the goddess (2.6 m high) carved from black granite (previously made of sandalwood, and even further back, gold) sitting on a majestic lotus-shaped stone pedestal, her back resting against a large Bodhi leaf-shaped stone slab. This is a masterpiece of Cham sculpture, a harmonious combination of round sculpture and relief carving techniques. The other towers are dedicated to: Shiva (one of the three supreme gods of Hinduism), Sanhaka, and other deities. Ganeca (according to legend, the son of the god Shiva). Next to the main tower, about 20 meters to the south, is another smaller tower, less ornate and about 12 meters high, possibly dedicated to the god Shiva. The Cham tower complex was built and restored over several periods from the 7th to the 12th centuries. The Po Nagar Cham Towers may have been built by King Harivarman I of the Champa Kingdom around 813-817. Over time, the tower suffered damage. During the French colonial period, the French School of Far Eastern Studies organized repairs: rebuilding many parts with bricks and adding some statues to the tower's structure. During wartime, many artifacts were stolen.

Known as "Little Angkor Wat," this ancient site is worth a visit and is a great spot for taking photos. An entrance fee of VDN20,000 per person is required. It's quite popular with tourists, but a short walk uphill offers expansive views. I arrived around 2 PM; the sun was very strong and hot. I stayed for about half an hour to take some photos before heading to i-Resort. There are plenty of taxis outside; the ride takes only about ten minutes and is very convenient.
You can visit independently by taking bus #4. Throughout the day, there are performances of traditional dances accompanied by music.

Before visiting attractions, we always read reviews and recommendations online. This time, too, we reread all the information we could find and were a little disappointed, as nothing was really written anywhere, and the reliability of the information was rather questionable. We went on our own, which we deeply regretted. The temple, although small, looks fascinating and mysterious, but without a historical guide, visiting it turns into jostling with other visitors and trying to take photos. Don't listen to anyone; take a guide, even an English-speaking one. While we were in the temple, we joined other groups to listen!

It's surprising to see such a piece of history in a hyped-up tourist town. These towers are overly hyped, only of interest to amateurs, and they're not worth disrupting your beach vacation for. Similar structures abound in Cambodia, but here they're more of a sham, for tourists. There's no need to waste time on them.
Nice place to spend an hour, with excellent views across the river. Don't worry about going wearing shorts etc as they provide free coverups for you to borrow

The place is beautiful, and like any monument, its significance and beauty cannot be measured by stars alone; it's a whole story in itself. The star was removed due to the crowds, which, of course, are mostly Chinese tourist groups.
Inexpensive to get in and outstanding structures. They also have robes for free so you can enter the temples!
We visited in the morning. It's a beautiful place, definitely worth a visit. The only downside is the looooooot of Chinese people.

We visited with friends on the last day of our holiday in Nha Trang. We went in the morning. Before visiting, we specifically learned about the history and purpose of this religious complex. We really enjoyed it. We loved the atmosphere, the architecture, the interior decoration of the towers, and the surrounding garden. Again, there were too many Chinese people, preventing us from fully enjoying the view and taking even a single decent photo as a souvenir. We highly recommend visiting.
The sight is worth seeing. Beautiful towers. Admission is barefoot, with knees and shoulders covered. Very crowded.
Nice places to relax with view of Nha Trang city with it's ancient temple right in the city... relaxing walk around.

We arrived at the towers, and there were so many Chinese at the entrance it was creepy... If you ignore them, the place is very interesting. The ancient ruins look so majestic, it's hard to believe that all this has survived to this day. A must-see for lovers of all things ancient.

Hello everyone! We visited the towers on a tour with a wonderful guide (a Russian man who's lived there for six years). It was quite interesting, he showed us a vibrant place and told us a lot of history, but as always, it all immediately slipped my mind! But if you have time, it's worth a visit!

If you're in Nha Trang, get away from the beach for a visit to this historic site. The Cham towers have a similar appearance to the Khmer relics in Angkor, but at a more human scale. They're still used by the Cham community in the area -- our visit included a performance of Cham music and dance. The towers are up on a hill which is beautifully landscaped. Go early, though. I think the site gets more and more crowded as the day goes on.
Small but different where statues have both Hinduism and Buddhism as it evolves through time. The architecture and man made bricks are truly of workmanship. Costs 22k per person
It is a must visit if you are heading to Nha Trang. Although it is no match to the famous Angkor Wat, it presents a historic building in similar architecture style.

My husband and I traveled there independently, taking a city bus (by the way, public transportation is quite convenient; the fare is 8,000 dong, and the buses are air-conditioned). The entrance fee was 22,000 dong per person. Since we're not big fans of visiting ancient architectural structures, we weren't expecting much from this trip. Nevertheless, it was interesting to wander around the grounds and took some nice photos. Our "tour" took about 15-20 minutes, and combined with the bus ride through the city streets, it was quite interesting. It's best to arrive early to avoid large groups of tourists brought in on tours.
There's not much to see, though. You can take a couple of great photos. Parking and tickets are separately charged. You can hang out here instead of in the park. It offers a nice view of the city.
A Hindu temple overlooking the Kai River. You can wear long robes, which are provided on the temple grounds, and enter the towers. You can sit in the shade of the trees. Entrance is 22,000 dong.
Lots of tourists at the temple complex! Definitely worth seeing! The view is beautiful. Don't wear shorts! If necessary, you can borrow a robe or wrap!

We also walked to the towers, having spent a decent amount of time on our own. Tickets are very inexpensive and sold at the entrance. There's no point in buying them anywhere else. But the first thing you'll see is the Chinese. No, not really... Crowds of Chinese. They're brought here in big tour buses. They don't really care about history, architecture, or beauty; they've come here to take selfies. So if you want a nice photo, you'll have to wait in line :)) The towers themselves are beautiful; we've been inside them all. At the entrance, you leave your shoes and put on a robe that covers your knees and shoulders. Everything is hanging nearby; you don't need to bring anything. We also saw a sudden dance of girls in national dresses; it's truly beautiful. And the view of the city and the river is simply stunning. From above, the difference between the Vietnamese neighborhoods and the tourist area, where construction never stops, is even more striking. And all this in one of the most historical places in Nha Trang.

We walked from our apartment in Central Nha Trang over the bridge on the north side of the Cai River which took us about one hour. There are four towers that remain from the Cham ruins. Worth a visit.

A pleasant surprise for my trip to Nha Trang, which was ruined by rain and winds which rendered us unable to enjoy the beach nor the pool at our hotel. Only a short taxi ride away from the Nha Trang main beach.

The most prominent central tower is said to be dedicated to Shiva, one of the three principal Hindu gods and the god of destruction. It is considerably smaller and less conspicuous than the tower dedicated to the goddess Po Nagar, but it has a beautiful brick-colored exterior. Further inside, there are statues and long, cylindrical lingams. There is a theory that the goddess Po Nagar is Shiva's wife, Parvati, but this is not well understood.

The Po Nagar Cham Towers certainly aren't comparable to the famous temples of Asia; this complex has its own atmosphere. If you're vacationing in Nha Trang, you're definitely going to visit it; it's very easy to get to. And if you love history and architecture, you'll have a great time here. We visited with our family, including an eight-year-old child. She found the grounds fascinating: the slightly mystical incense mist inside the small temples, the girls dancing in traditional costumes, the beautiful views of the city, the cute fountains, and, of course, the souvenirs. Incidentally, the prices were cheaper than near our hotel.

We went to the Cham Towers as part of a Nha Trang sightseeing tour. So many tourists! Beautiful! I can't say more. You can only enter the towers themselves if you wear clothing that covers your legs and shoulders; free "robes" are available for those who need them. We walked around, looked around, took photos, and "asked for what we needed in the towers." According to legend or tradition, you must visit all the towers and, in each one, think or utter a secret thought that you can only share with the higher powers. Maybe you should ask for something, maybe you should make a wish—it's up to you to decide.

This is an old temple made by stone constructed by the Cham people. There are similar temples in Cambodja and Thailand. It is still an active Buddhist temple. You have to a pay a minor entrance fee to get in. It is situated on a hill so have a rather good view from there.It is not a big and impressive temple but there are many visitors. It can be wortt a visit but is not a "must".
A beautiful vestige of the Cham kingdom, but as in many places in Vietnam, there are too many tourists, to such an extent that it can spoil the experience of visiting, go outside of peak hours.
It's an interesting place. They periodically host various performances there. We saw girls dancing with jugs. There are plenty of great spots for photos. It's truly a fascinating place!

My wife and I recently visited this historical site while in Nha Trang. Built between the 7th and 12th centuries, this sight is still actively used by Vietnamese, Chinese, and Cham Buddhists. Architecture is unique for the area. A great tour guide really makes this sight interesting.  Well worth a visit when in Nha Trang
I recommend visiting these towers, they are within walking distance from the European quarter, the entrance ticket is 1 dollar, but it is very beautiful there

Worth a visit, and the entry cost is only a few dollars. Beware, it can get very busy, especially with Chinese tourists, all with cameras. They provide smocks to allow you to enter the main temples. Make sure you remove your shoes.

This is a must if you're in Nha Trang.  The towers are beautiful, the view is great and it's interesting to see the different cultural and religious traditions.  The toilets are clean, and they provide you with a free robe to wear if you want to enter any of the towers.  I would have appreciated more information about what we were seeing.

We visited during a sightseeing tour. It was so crowded, it was impossible to take photos. There was a musical performance, with girls dancing in traditional dress with jugs on their heads. It was interesting to see. It was very stuffy inside, and a lot of incense was burning.

Great attraction. The towers are located on a high point of Nha Trang so you can see part of the city from there. It can become very crowded when the tour buses arrive, so try to get there early in the morning. This place reminds me of our visit of Ankor Wat in Cambodia, but at a smaller scale. A must see attraction for anyone who visits Nha Trang.
Po Nagar is amazing in that they built the structure without mortar. It's cheap to visit but one tends to get pestered by touts selling souvenirs.
There isn't a great deal to see in Nah Trang. It's primarily a holiday town with a fantastic beach. The towers are a highlight. Admission is €0.50.

for those alrdy visit angkor wat , you will feel po nagar is so small and nothing to be surprise . however , it is good activity at nha trang ,especially for those think vinpearl theme park ticket is so expensive
Very old and interesting architecture. Unfortunately, we visited on our own, without a tour. There's very little space inside the towers. But it's still very interesting and beautiful.

Really beautiful place to visit with lovely views over the Cai River. Was busy with lots of people. Was great to buy some coconuts and water to drink while there as it was a super hot day. We also saw dance performance while there which was fun.

This temple is different in style as its for a hindu goddess but with a local interpretation that was developed many centuries ago and so its different to the indian style for this goddess. The building has been restored so a lot of it is not original. Only 22,000 dong per adult entry and quite interesting. You can also sit on the terrace near the cafe which can catch the breeze which is nice with views of nha trang, islands, the fishing boat harbor and the river.
Old buildings with new bricks. Captivating performances. It's hard to believe these buildings are over 1,000 years old.

The towers, grounds and sights from the frounds are beautiful. Unfortunately for us we visited during a rain storm. The crowds were fairly dense making it difficult to manipulate the small space and enjoy the peaceful ambience of the place - I guess that's the price you pay for visiting a popular destiantion during Tet.

We were here on a sightseeing tour after three in the afternoon, and it was packed! Photos were impossible, and even walking was difficult—there were crowds everywhere. The towers themselves are beautiful, but I wouldn't make a special trip here.

You can take a bus or a bike, there's plenty of room for great photos, and admission is free during the day. I haven't tried it in the evening, but I think you can sneak in at your own risk. There's no fence or gate on the other side of the official entrance. The show is fun, and there's bike parking. You don't need to make a special trip there; an hour's visit, just passing through, is plenty.

A very worth visiting place must go for nha trang tourists, ticket needed but the price is reasonable only 22000 vnd, but be aware a lot of Chinese tourists here not obey no smoking rules and security seems no function on second hand smoke control.

The towers are located just beyond the bridge. A taxi ride from the center of the European district costs $3. There are many tourists. Entrance to the grounds is $1. To enter the central tower, you must have clothing that covers your shoulders and knees. If you don't have one, you can wear a robe for free. They are hanging on the right side, 10 meters from the entrance. Inside the towers, there's very little space—only room for a small altar. The towers were built between the 7th and 12th centuries and are currently undergoing gradual restoration. They're definitely worth a visit. It's close, inexpensive, and interesting.

What a peaceful feeling place. Once one climbed up the steps and saw what it was you could not help but be impressed that it has survived for so long. The bonsai trees were fabulous and one got the feeling of peace while there, in spite of the crowds. Good toilet facilities and a gift shop as well. Well worth going to.
I decided to see the Cham towers of Ponagar, I got there by bus at about 11 am, there weren't many people, but I wasn't thrilled.

This place is good and clean. From the place ,you can view the nha trang over the river. There is a small room to introduce its history. In the back the towers, dancing and music plays will shown on fixed time, please check the schedule if you want to see the shows.
I live here and my wife is Buddhist so we go there quite often. I can see it from my house. It is not as big as one thinks it is.
It's very close to the tourist area, the entrance is inexpensive, and the grounds are well-maintained. And it's a cultural heritage site, after all. Overall, it's a must-see for tourists.
A small green area, a quiet environment, located on a hill, with a view of the city of Nha Trang and the river.

It was very crowded during the Chinese New Year holiday. The venue isn't very big, but it was very congested just walking around. The ticket price was reasonable and not expensive. It's a good place to take beautiful photos and create lasting memories.

We have traveled extensively so in the scale of things to visit in the world its not particularly special but for Nha Trang definitely worth a look. It is still a place of worship so make sure you cover up - there are free robes if required. Various towers to visit and a small cafe. Can get very busy.
It's worth a visit; it's captivatingly ancient, and the energy of this place is incredible. Everyone prays and asks...
Try to visit in an early morning. So many Chinese tourists are there and very nosy in the afternoon. Really worth to visit.

The place is truly beautiful, with a stunning view of the city and ancient temples. We'd been to Nha Trang before, but we'd missed the towers, which turned out to be a shame. This year, we went with the Russian Information Center for a short sightseeing tour. We learned that you could take the No. 4 city bus yourself, but we wanted to hear about the city and see other sights.

It was interesting, there is no information in English to explain what it is. It was also crazy busy!! I would try going early in the morning to see if you can avoid the crowds as afternoon many tours visit this area. It would of been amazing if there had been less people.

The location offers a nice view, and the ruins themselves are interesting, but unfortunately, the restoration work seemed rather haphazard, even to an untrained eye. However, the surrounding fruit stalls created a pleasant, distinctly Asian atmosphere.

Although it was built a very long time ago, it is well-preserved. The view from the temple is also magnificent. You are not allowed to enter the temple wearing sleeveless tops or shorts, but I was able to because someone nearby offered to let me wear a robe for free.
Interesting to visit Fishing people is close linked to it for safety and good luck Close buy on other side of street An unique experience to try out A food délicatesse

Personally, I think it's the most unique attraction in Nha Trang. It's not very big, the ticket price is very cheap, and there are also ethnic performances interspersed throughout. Although they are all very mini, they have the charm. You can finish visiting it in 30 minutes. It's a miniature version of Angkor Wat.

We visited the towers as part of a city tour, so we took in everything quickly and didn't linger anywhere, but such a place requires a leisurely visit. There were also crowds of people. Covering clothing is required, but if you're wearing open clothing, you can borrow special robes. There's a small museum on the grounds, and during our visit, there was a folk dance performance. The temples themselves are small, but magical; you can immediately sense the sanctity of this ancient place. We'd love to return.

It's interesting to see it as part of a fun excursion on the way to Long Weather or to the Thap Ba Spa. It's more interesting from the outside than the inside. We got there by taxi from the embankment for $2.50, but you can also take bus #4.

Definitely worth a visit to witness the history and culture of the fascinating Champa kingdom. The site is rather small but very interesting to compare to the ruins at My Son. There are also nice views of the surrounding area from the top of the hill. I don't know how crowded the site gets at other times of year, but I visited close to the Chinese New Year and the site was packed with throngs of tourists all taking lengthy posed photos in front of every corner of every building.

It's very small; you can see everything in fifteen minutes. You can easily find information about its cultural background on Google and Baidu. It's dedicated to a deity who protects seafarers, similar to Mazu. You can see locals worshipping there. Shorts are allowed inside, but you need to change into a robe provided to enter the main area.

These amazing buildings sit on a hill overlooking Nha Trang. The details on the buildings are beautiful as are the views from the platforms. As the towers are one of Nha Trang most popular attractions you will encounter crowds.

It was conveniently located downtown, and while there were many traces of history, it was a shame that the tour ended so quickly since there were only one or two buildings. I recommend visiting at least one temple when traveling to a Buddhist country.

We visited this on a day tour with one of the locals. A very cultural signifcant place as it is over 1000 years old and called the small Angkor Wat. Plenty of history and you can go inside the temples as well as get a great view of the city from around the grounds. There also various areas set up to relax or meditate. You can also grab lunch there. Some amazing photo opportunities however it is extremely busy so you have to be quick

At first I was a bit pissed by the rude ticket seller at the booth but maybe there were too many tourists during my visit. Hence the surroundings are crowded and kind of noisy. But as soon as I reached the top, the scent of the incense sticks and the surrounding views manage to calm me. Within the towers were the Hindu deities where devotees went in to pray while the other tourists busily squeeze in and out to take a glimpse or take a photo of the deities. There was a small museum on site that gives you a little info on the temple. But be warned, it might not be a physically friendly destination as it's only reachable by climbing the stairs. To fully experience the site, kindly check with the locals for the best time to visit so it won't be as crowded and you may spend more times on this must see site without people pushing you around.

It has a strong historical feel, the place is beautiful, and it's a place where you can linger for a while. The ruins have a strong traditional Vietnamese vibe, and the local Vietnamese people are very friendly.

Interesting place, a “must see” place. But Nha Trang has became a mainland tourist outlet, loud with kids running around. Not only kids climb on structurebut parents as well. But it’s a must see so come beware. And have fun.
TourVietnam organized this tour perfectly, and the price was very competitive! We loved it.

I was drawn there on a Sunday—there were so many Chinese people there. It was hard to take a photo without them. I'd have loved to have gotten a tour of the place, but I didn't get a chance. I visited on my own and watched the girls dancing with pots.
This ancient Cham site is more than 1,000 years old. It was once burned by fire. Statues from more than 1,000 years ago can be seen inside, and there are dance performances inside.

The Po Nagar Cham Towers are accessible by bus and are quite crowded depending on the time of day and the weather. We were there around 1 p.m. and it was absolutely packed. The entrance fee is 22,000 VND (less than €1). You can see the towers in under 30 minutes. Located on a slight elevation, they offer a nice view over Nha Trang. Taking a taxi from there to the Long Son Pagoda is worthwhile and costs a maximum of 50,000–60,000 VND (approximately €2).

If it weren't for these towers, I'd say there's nothing to see in Nha Trang. The towers are very interesting, though unusual for a Russian, in terms of architecture. They're somewhat reminiscent of Cambodian temples. Overall, it's worth a visit. I don't think there's much to do with a tour. You can explore on your own.

The local historical sites are about a 20-minute drive from the city center. The areas aren't large; free robes are provided for people wearing tank tops and shorts. An hour is usually enough to spend there. Unfortunately, there are so many Chinese tourists, making up about 90% of the visitors, that the whole place is quite noisy.
It's a really small site. The entrance fee isn't excessive, but you can see everything very quickly.

We visited the Cham Towers on our own. We arrived at lunchtime. There were so many Chinese people there, it was impossible to take nice photos. Friends were there in the late afternoon, and it was quieter. It's an interesting place; we went into the towers themselves, but you'll need to either wear clothing that covers your shoulders and knees or bring a robe, which is free. The view from the towers is stunning.
I came here twice, nothing special but it has very nice view from the top. You can see the whole city from there.

A visit to the Cham Towers was part of my walk around Nha Trang. It didn't make much of an impression on me, perhaps due to the crowds of noisy Chinese. Taking a photo without people in the frame is practically impossible. However, after walking around the complex, you can see a large stone and take beautiful photos with the towers behind you. The complex also offers beautiful views of the sea and the city. If you return to the city on foot, you can also take beautiful photos of the complex from the bridge.
Cool place to visit. It felt that is this he only historic place in Nha Trang. The rest are relatively new.
Really well kept site with easy access and cheap entrance fee. There is very little information on display at the site, which could be improved.
This is a nice walk with views. You only need an hour or so but bring water can get hot. Beautiful Towers with load os history.

You won't be able to stroll around in solitude, or take photos, as there are so many tourists. But overall, everything is fine. You can enter each tower and explore. You can borrow a free robe for visiting the temples on the grounds—for those wearing revealing clothing, as it's forbidden to enter the temple with bare shoulders and knees.

This was the last stop on our tour, and it was very interesting to see the various influences on this area. We could have skipped this because it is lit up at light night, but I don't if you can visit it at night.

For once the site is well maintained but not free. It is not very expansive (22k per person) and enables to keep the monument in a good shape. We had the chance to see a short show with Vietnamese traditional dance.

There are a huge number of Chinese tourists, and Chinese package tour groups constantly flood in. Perhaps due to a lack of respect for cultural heritage, you can witness the uncivilized behavior of climbing the towers and taking photos in all sorts of poses at the Po Nagar Towers. Aside from that, the temple has its own charm. It features a architectural style not seen in Korea, allows you to go inside and pray, and offers a beautiful view of Nha Trang from the towers.
Came here more out of curiosity then having planned visit. The tower gardens are very peaceful abs the archetecture amazing. Had good views of Nha trang city but can get very busy.

Inexpensive way to spend and hour. They are located in the city. The grounds are small the the towers themselves are really impressive. The will give you an outfit to put on if you want to go into the temples. Worth seeing if you in Nha Trang.

On the last day before our flight, with the sea and pool cold, my mom and I decided to explore the sights of Nha Trang. One of the stops was the Cham Towers. If you go on a tour, you'll get a Vietnamese guide, but for reasons only they understand, only locals are allowed to talk about the shrine there. If you're going on your own, don't expect any historical information. The complex itself is small. I'd say small. Taking photos, exploring, and buying some trinkets (there are souvenir shops on the grounds, as always. By the way, the souvenirs are beautiful, there's plenty to buy, and the prices are quite reasonable) will take about 40 minutes. You can visit each of the temples in the complex. If you're not dressed appropriately, there's a locker with free monk's cloaks (they look like a long gray robe); just throw them on and go everywhere. Don't worry about looking like you're wearing shorts near the shrines. A smart approach, I'd say. The complex, as I've already said, is small. But you can see it and it's probably worth visiting once. If it's your first time in Nha Trang, you can see dancing Vietnamese women, hear folk singing, or sit next to artisans (not always, but you can usually catch them in the late afternoon). I'd give it a 4 because without historical knowledge, it'll quickly become "boring." Russian guides know, but they're not allowed to tell you anything (some local laws). If you're not lazy and read up on where you're going in advance, it'll be really interesting. Once you know, you've been. The towers' location is also worth noting. The grounds offer views of the city, bridge, and islands. A picturesque panorama is guaranteed. If you approach the question from the point of view of "is it worth visiting or not?" Then it's mostly "Yes")))
Walking around these towers makes you wonder just how clever these people were back then. Ever brick placed strategically to create these towers, only 4 remain standing today.
Very nice place. The ticket is 24000/ person. Inside you can put on special uniform which is in a cloak room next to the entrance to walk inside the temple. Nice place.
We were only two of many hundreds of people. I am certainly glad we saw this. Grounds were tidy and clean.

Po Nagar Cham Towers is located at Tha Pa which is located at the north of Cam River. The bus#4 is passby here. the bus fare from Hung Voung to Tower is VND 7000.  The Entrance ticket for the attraction is VND 22,000. We reach here about 10.30am. There is a lot tourist there mostly from China.  The Cham Tower is located at a hill. Thus, you can have a nice view towards the Nha Trang City Center.  Accoss the road from the main entrance, you can found several local food stall such as Ban Xeu.

If you never been to Angkor Wat, you should visit Po Nagar Cham Towers. You may find it interesting, historical and worth a visit.  Po Nagar Cham Towers are just a small-scale temple. The view there is great. We enjoyed looking at Nha Trang city and the sea.  If you want to enter the main temple, there are robes for people wear shorts or sleeveless clothes. They are free of charge.

Very beautiful, but very touristy. Lots of Russians and Chinese who don't respect the rule about removing their shoes before entering the temples... And normally, women are supposed to cover their shoulders... They don't care... Not very respectful, I think. But otherwise, knowing that it dates back to 949 and is still there is amazing!

Just like a mini version of Cambodia with less crowds. If you have never been to Cambodia, you can come and see this kind of architectural feature. Other than that, I felt nothing special myself. But you can really take beautiful photos

An interesting place, the remains of ancient temples, a view of the city and... crowds of Chinese people taking selfies, group photos, etc. Because of this, you lose sight of what you actually came for... I hope to visit there on an unexpectedly deserted day.

We took the Vespa 1 day tour to visit Nha Trang and all the iconic places. The Po Nagar Cham Towers were the 1st stop followed by visit to a local manufacturer, the Pagode, the Church, lunch at a nice location in the old Nha Trang city. The towers are restored but inside you can see the original pictures how the place was found and what was restored afterwards.

Po Nagar is a Cham temple tower dedicated to Yan Po Nagar, the ten-armed goddess of the land. The Po Nagar complex is located on La Lao Mountain. It consists of three levels, the highest of which spans two rows of towers. The main tower is approximately 25 meters tall. The Cham-Ponagar temple complex is located on a hill. The complex contains four towers, the remaining of eight, built between the seventh and twelfth centuries CE. The towers are dedicated to different gods.

There's a small ruin on top of a hill. It seems to have been restored, as it's quite well-preserved and surprisingly enjoyable. Further inside, there's a small museum-like structure with stone statues, some of which are beautifully designed, so be sure to take a look.

It is a history building of 'cham' people as they called. It is a tourist attraction to know more about their history. It is impressive to know the process of building up the cham tower. The view from the place to the city and sea is eye catching.

The place isn't bad. They usually take you on a sightseeing tour, but we went on our own on the first day of our arrival because the weather wasn't perfect for the beach. So, we decided against it. Overall, the place is interesting and atmospheric, with a beautiful view of the river and well-maintained grounds. However, the tower itself is difficult to navigate and smells strongly of incense.
It's very beautiful inside. I was impressed by the spiritual mystery. The time allotted by the guide was short. Maybe because it's a free tour.

If you've been to Hindu temples before, this place won't surprise you. There's an entrance fee of 22,000 dong. There are tons of Chinese people blocking your photos. It's a place to visit after you've seen everything else.

There were many tourists. It had just started raining when we arrived, and people had to rush to shelter from the rain to have a chance to take photos. The place isn't big; half an hour is enough, but the scenery is quite nice.

We visited this Hindu temple after visiting Nha Trang Catholics church and Buddhist Long Son temple.  We enjoyed the view while sitting next to the kioski. Warm wind blowing to our faces was great feeling; and watching tourists passing by.  Suggest to go early, there are many cruise groups marked with different numbers who we also met in Long Son temple.  Suppose it's a must go tourist attraction in Nha Trang. Ticket costs 22,000VND.

The temple complex is small, but located on a hill. It offers excellent views in all directions. There are numerous paths around the temple, dotted with sacred statues. Locals pray, and tourists are welcome. It's a popular place, so it's best to arrive early.

We took a sightseeing tour bus because it had been raining since morning and we didn't want to walk around in the rain. There are three towers on a raised platform. The inside is small. You have to take off your shoes before entering, and there's nothing much to see inside, just standing around. We went into one, and just peeked through the doors of the other two. It's only worth visiting if you have a lot of time to spare.

From the photos online, it seems like a large area with many temples. But in reality, there are three towers. The area isn't very large, but it's worth a visit. We took a bus (4) for 7,000 dong; the entrance ticket is 22,000 dong. Your tour operator will give you a map, or you can pick one up from a street tour operator; it lists all the buses, routes, and attractions. Whatever's best for you and your wallet. Have a wonderful vacation!

A little bit outside of the city, this is a very well preserved hindu temple. More than 1200 years old. Again lots of people. This is probably the biggest attraction in town. Not to many stairs to get on top. You have to pay 20000 dong about one US$. There is also some small shops and food. Interesting history.

You can get to the towers by city bus #3, which leaves from the Galina Hotel. The fare is 7 dong, which is about 15-17 rubles one way. The ride from the European Quarter is about 15 minutes. As soon as you enter the bridge, you will immediately see them. It is impossible to miss them. Don't be afraid of getting lost, there are always a lot of people there, including Russians. The area is small, but don't rush to leave. You need to be there, sit, and relax. There is some kind of grace there. Go into the towers, look at Shiva, take a closer look at the people. Let this not be a fleeting vision, feel the atmosphere of this place, and you will surely remember it for a long time. You need to enter the towers with covered shoulders and feet, barefoot. But even if you accidentally passed this place on the way back from Zoklet, for example, it's okay, there are special light blue capes-robes in which you can Take a stroll around the towers and explore them from the inside. If you're lucky, you'll see Vietnamese dancing traditional dances on the grounds. There's a small souvenir shop, but you can buy the same things in town, so the choice is yours. Guides offer tours of the towers, but tourists are significantly overcharged for them. Don't be fooled; you can do this on your own. Research the towers' history online beforehand. Have a great holiday!

i reached there around 3pm, hence there was so many tourist in group, very difficult to take picture and when we have set up timer, they are still passing and walking in front of you. An early trip might help you to avoid the crowd.

When we reached at 4pm, this place was just a mess... busses with tours were just coming and it was hardly possible to enter the little temples. As the other reviews suggest, visit preferably in the morning
It's worth a visit, inexpensive, and exudes a sense of antiquity and religion. Everything is interesting, but the site is small. We got there by bus...

My husband and I had previously visited temple complexes in Thailand and Cambodia, which require at least two or three days, and this one was only a small, albeit cozy, part of what I'd already seen. Nothing special, nothing particularly emotional. It's a quiet place where you can come to take a good photo or read a book and admire the beautiful views, but nothing more.
Having visited large temple complexes in Cambodia before, these places don't impress me. The grounds are very small and restored. It's worth exploring.

We visited the place with a local tour guide who highly recommended it. Although it was very hot and you have to climb a lot of stairs, although the stairs are comfortable, it was worth it. The place is undoubtedly a beautiful place that is worth visiting. You have to go up and visit patiently and see the gardens and the place and the beautiful view from it. It is also recommended for families.

Beautiful temple with some nice views. I would recommend going in the morning, there is hardly anyone there before 9am, but by 10/11am it is over run with tourists. There is a woman there that sells sand art, which was fascinating! If you do go inside the temple, please be respectful of the Buddhist dress code displayed outside. So many tourists do no follow the conservative dress code, which is very unfortunate and disrespectful, don't be that person!

We went ourselves, by taxi; you can easily get there by bus. If you're in Nha Trang for the first time, be sure to stop by for 30 minutes. It's beautiful and interesting. I don't see the point in booking a tour here or to other places you can visit yourself.

There are only four of the original eight towers left, but these are impressive none the less. A lot of very old brickwork, with very dark & dingy interiors (still actively used as shrines). Cheap to get in, lots of steps to climb. Very photogenic. Good views of local bridge and the bay. Busy.
The respect for religion is impressive, and it's a place that transmits peace and balance. You really have to go there to see for yourself.

Plenty of tourist ! Lots of gift shops ! Over priced go to the local market for local products ! Plenty of steps to climb so wear good runners ! Local people offerings every where to their god ! Can borrow a grey coat if you want to go inside ! Check out the room with lots of photos & beautiful dresses! We spent time wondering around taking in the local culture ! Small fee to enter !

Just a short drive from the popular Trang Pu street by taxi. Entrance fee is 22,000 vnd.  The temple sits on top of a small hill with views of the surrounding area and city.  You need to wear a light robe to enter the temples as a mark of respect.  Good for a short visit for some photos.

There are so many people inside the towers that it's impossible to breathe. You can get there on your own; tickets are cheap. They provide free clothing for visiting the towers; shoulders and knees must be covered.

After a cheap taxi to the temple its nice and cool inside away from the sun, very old and historical. Youll get some good pics, souvenir lady was friendly with cheap goods. Im sure a tour would be more informative than just walking around. Cost 22,000 dong which is around $1.20 AUD.

The route to i-resort passes by the ruins, so it's worth a visit if you're traveling in a group. There's an entrance fee, I think it's around 2,000 to 20,000 VND! About 30 minutes should be enough for a visit. The location is easy to find, and the scenery outside is quite nice; definitely worth a look.

It seems like one of the few remaining historic sites in Vietnam. It's a shame the explanations aren't very clear, but I think it's worth visiting at least once. Ethnic minorities perform there, which is fun to watch. However, there isn't a single patch of shade, so be sure to bring a parasol, hat, or sunglasses. The view of Nha Trang from Po Nagar was also amazing!

This is a beautiful and spiritual place great view of surrounding Nha Trang. Cost is $22,000 dong, the ticket office only accepts cash in Dong currency no USD or AUD. You certainly don't necessarily need to do a tour at this site very easy to do it yourself. You are free to wander around the grounds on your own and taking pictures etc. To enter the actually temple you need your knees and shoulders covers up. It was very hot when we were here so wearing a singlet and long flowy plants was OK and just a scarf to cover shoulders when entering the temple itself.  We took a cab here from the resort we were staying in, we only used either VINASUN (red, green & white) or the QUOC TE (blue & white) taxi's while in Nha Trang.
This landmark is located practically in the center of Nha Trang and can be visited on a guided tour or independently.

We got to the towers by bus; everything was clear and close. The entrance fee was 22,000 rubles per person. There were so many tourists that we didn't experience any cleansing or energizing effects. There were tons of Chinese people, everyone was trying to climb everywhere and take photos, no peace. The towers themselves are fairly well preserved, the grounds are clean, there are plenty of benches, and the area is beautiful and interesting.
A worthwhile place to visit to learn about ancient Cham culture. Watching the Cham dances is fascinating and unique.

Stunning buildings and gardens with beautiful views – well worth seeing. The infrastructure is in place and the entrance fee is very reasonable. The walk back across the islands and through the large market to the waterfront promenade offers a glimpse into the lives of the local population away from the tourist areas of Vietnamese cities.
A temple of the indigenous Cham people, now almost completely exterminated by the Vietnamese. Similar to Ankor Wat in Cambodia.
I bought a little tower souvenir for my sister and she loves it. It's only 15000 Vietnam Dong. Very cute.
An interesting place! But you need a decent guide to tell the true story of the Kingdom of Champa. The main drawback is the crowds of Chinese comrades!

The Ponangar Towers are included in any sightseeing bus tour, but you can easily get there yourself on city bus number 4. It was our departure point, but you can check when it is. Thirty minutes before dinner is enough time; the site is open until 6 p.m., so you can explore it fully, take photos, and sit on the benches in the beautiful spots.

Very close to the center of Nha Trang and a short walk from the beach. It's worth taking a stroll along the beach and crossing the river to get there. The towers themselves aren't anything special if you've visited similar temples in Laos or Myanmar, but if it's your first time, they're fine. These temples are frequented by worshippers, so it's best to be respectful. To enter the towers, you have to remove your shoes, and robes are available to cover your shoulders or knees if they're exposed. The best thing about the towers, without a doubt, is that at the exit you'll find numerous restaurants serving local cuisine and seafood, cheaper than those on Nha Trang beach, and offering excellent quality. This alone makes it worth a visit.
A very interesting place to visit. Steeped in history so best with a tourist guide to get full benefit. Towers themselves are quite amazing but grounds pleasant to walk round
This was first built by settlers from Malaysia who were Hindus. The buildings are magnificent. You must go inside the temples and walk around the gardens.

A nice little temple to visit in the middle of town. The entrance fee is 20,000 VND, which is less than €1. The temple is small but very charming, in my opinion. I recommend visiting, but not in a group; go alone. It's very simple; a taxi costs 50,000 VND each way.

The Po Nagar Towers are a landmark of Nha Trang. They are located in the northern part of the city, just beyond the bridge. You can get there by bus or taxi, which are inexpensive. There's an entrance fee; there's a kiosk to the right of the entrance where you can buy an entrance ticket. The small area contains quite a few interesting objects, and you can take photos of everything, except for the ubiquitous Chinese who don't care about others. Robes are provided for visiting the private areas where the venerated saints reside, as shoulders and knees must be covered. I also recommend walking along the sea along the second street from the towers. There are no Russians there, but the seafood is very inexpensive and no less delicious than in restaurants.
It's an interesting place, with plenty to see and photograph, not just on the grounds but also around the area.
If you are interested in sight seeing and history of things then make sure you stop to have a wonder around here. Hard to explain. Best to see yourself.

This is a really pretty place and seems a little out of place in Nha Trang! You couldn't spend hours here but it's worth a visit if you are in the area anyway. Wish I'd seen more information around the site about who built these and why.  Amazing to think how long they have been here. Really close to the beach and 20,000VND for entry.  The day we went it was so hot it just wasn't comfortable to walk around, and it was so crowded. I imagine it's always pretty busy.

A fantastic place, great towers, temples, really interesting. If you want to go inside you must put on a robe and take your shoes off. There are 3 temples to see, plus lovely gardens, food and drink stalls and great views over Nha Trang. There were also dancers and musicians performing. Worth spending an hour or so.
Interesting place to visit and a critical part of the vietnames history dating back to the 7th century, recommend getting a good guide and visiting this if history is your thing.

You can get here on a sightseeing tour or by bus. The entrance fee is 21,000 dong. I confess we weren't prepared and didn't read up on the history of this place. You won't find any explanations on site. So, let's walk around and enjoy the ancient ruins.
You can visit them once, no more. The dirt in front of them is horrendous, just like everywhere else we've been in Nha Trang and Phu Yen.

A beautiful ancient architecture in the scenic spot, this attraction is worth to visit and learn about Cham culture and history. Interesting culture show is performed routine to entertain visitors. If you don't visit this attraction as tour scheduled the i would recommend a late afternoon visit in combining with walking back along the beach front street back to the downtown. Really good experience.
Although it is small in scale and cannot compare with those in Cambodia, it still worth to visit as it is with the same architecture style of Angkor Wat.
A historical site of Vietnamese cultural revival. The very architecture of the towers exudes a profound antiquity that you can feel.

Visited yesterday around 1pm. (Apparently best to visit early in the morning.) It was swarmimg with tourists but we still managed to find a couple of quiet spots and even got a few photos with none or very few of them in 😊 It's an easy place to visit and not to many "hagglers" around. Local taxi cost us 65000d from central beachfront. 22000d entrance fee. Definitely worth a visit - spend approx 1hour max there.

The Vietnamese version of Angkor Wat, a smaller scale, unfortunately suffers from very basic restoration, lacking the original historical atmosphere and resembling more of a park. There are musical and dance performances, but they are lackluster and feel perfunctory.

A beautiful spot to go and see if you have a spare hour. Doesn't take too long and will only cost you about 22 000 dong. Extremely easy to find as it's pretty much in the middle of town. Dancers with pots on their heads and Vietnamese music played while we were there as a small show. Not to sure how often they do that but we were only there for an hour and were lucky enough to catch a glimpse of it. If you stop in Nha Trang definitely check this spot out.

It's a bit small, but still worth a visit! A complete tour takes only half an hour. Nha Trang doesn't have many attractions, so it's worth taking the time to visit. If you still have time, you can browse the shops further inside; they sell souvenirs and unique items at reasonable prices, no bargaining required.
Since there isn't much else to see in Nha Trang this is a cool place to visit. Beautiful culture, nice premises to have a stroll on.

It's worth a visit. It's not far from the city center. We arrived by bus in less than 10 minutes. The sights will take you a maximum of 30 minutes. There's nothing to do there for longer. The red brick structures are interesting, and there are structures higher up (like small temples). You can take photos, and it's best to combine the trip with other nearby attractions—Long Son Pagoda, Cho Dam Market, or Thap Ba Muds.
Suitable for history buff and worshiper. Robes are provided if wanted to enter the temple for praying.Great view of the nha trang from the tower.
Characteristic temples atop a promontory overlooking the city... the entrance fee is 22,000 VND, or less than a euro.

Although the park is a bit small, the entrance fee is reasonable and it has unique features, so I think it's worth a visit. It's great for taking photos, has shade so it's not too hot, and there are also live performances to watch.

There isn't much culture to see in Nha Trang so this is worth visiting if you are there. Nice temple with interesting areas for pray. A little bit of history given but could have more about its origins and role over the years.

Walk here today. Arrived just when all the tour groups came(enuff said).

Spent half a day absorbing the whole sprintual aspect of the religious site and …

Like most of temples... seen one, seen them all. The neon LED screws it up a bit though.
Opposite is a circleK good to get an ice-cold Tiger, sit on the wall and watch the bridge. Kind of fun.

tourist trap.

if you've seen the temples in cambodia, this one looks like a miniature replica. …

Really fascinating site! Beautifully preserved.  There are lots of vendors, which kind of detract from the sacredness of the site.  Tourists mob the place with nothing to really direct people in any particular direction.  There are no signs …
1€ ticket is a fair price but there’s not all that much to see. It’s a pretty temple but not really worth the effort of visiting.
Have a relaxing time swimming at Nha Trang Beach in the morning, and visit Poganar Temple here after 3 p.m. to enjoy the sunset.

Well living in Thailand it's kinda like "if you've seen one temple you've seen 'em all." It's a nice hour long diversion from the day. There's a nice view of the city and they put on a cultural dance presentation which was interesting.

The temple is beautiful but there isn't really much to do here. The showcases inside were average and it took about 29 minutes to see everything. I wouldn't visit again but isn't bad to visit, just don't expect too much when you go here.

We traveled to this temple by motobike,it quiet far from  hotel. It is very well deserved Temple, it very similar temples  building style in Chiang Mai, Thailand and in Siem Reap,Combodia, made from yellow bricks. This temple is located in the top of mountain, so can see the city view around.
Not a huge place but it's good to see if you're in Nha Trang. With so many tourists touching everything, it has lost its uniqueness. …

One site in the city with some historical context, yet mostly attracting big tour groups and therefore super busy in the day (but most came to take a few pics and leave). Interesting restored column structures not seen in other Khmer …

It’s not a negative review. But how do you rate an actual place of worship? It was not built with nosey tourists in mind. It is fascinating to look at the buildings considering how old they are, however the craftsmanship isn’t as impressive …

The tour of this heritage takes about 30 minutes.
Anyone over 100cm tall will be charged an entrance fee of 30,000 VND per person.

Not a must see as the visit is very fast (35 min maximum by taking time). 4 towers, atypical for european eyes though the place is crowded with korean and chinese more interested in selfies or model photos 😆 …

It's beautiful collections of shrines and historic monuments. Had lots of beautiful photos moments. But at one of the site with Lord Shiva. The very ill mannered lady staff shouted at us and forbid us take photos outside the temple, where …

I just took pictures. Haha …

Walk here today. Arrived just when all the tour groups came(enuff said).

Worth the 30k vdn entrance for the visit to enlighten ones soul and for some great photos you can take with your selfie stick or tripod.( Best you take snaps on the stairs as people climb them it's more rewarding).

tourist trap.

Although it is not a vast site, the ruins of an ancient kingdom in Nha Trang are well-preserved, making it a decent place to stop by for photos or sightseeing.

The Po Nagar Cham Towers are situated on a high hill beside a river, in a beautiful location with many green trees and a cool breeze from the river. Entrance requires a ticket; children under 1.2m are free. The ticket is a card that you simply insert into the gate to pass through, eliminating the need for paper tickets, which is convenient, economical, and reduces litter. Right at the entrance are brick pillars – these were originally gate towers, but due to age, only a few remain. Further up are the towers, named according to their orientation. Behind the towers, a group of Cham Brahmin people performed traditional dances. They spoke to each other in their own language. They seemed shy in their communication; I tried to strike up a conversation to learn more about Cham culture and history, but it was difficult. There is no parking here, as it's right next to the road. If you want to park, you have to go across the road, under the bridge, where there's a pedestrian walkway. There's a separate entrance and exit gate. Within the grounds, there are resting areas for visitors with plenty of seating. Please dress modestly when visiting; if you happen to be wearing short clothing, you can change into long clothes on the right side of the tower, free of charge.

It doesn't feel like a place you'd just rush to.

Be careful of taxi touts in front of the temple. Grab is the most convenient option.

It is a temple with a unique architectural style.

However, the temple was poorly maintained, so there was a smell of urine throughout the temple.

It is one of the few historical sites worth visiting in downtown Nha Trang. It is smaller than expected, consisting mainly of four towers, structures, and a memorial hall. You can see everything and take photos in about 30 minutes. Don't have high expectations.

It's not as spacious as I expected. I recommend checking it out if you happen to be in the area. It's not far from Dam Market.

It's not a must-visit.
The place isn't big; you can walk through it in under 30 minutes.

You shouldn't expect things like Angkor Wat or Prom Banan. Since there isn't much to see in Nha Trang anyway, it's worth visiting at least once. It's also affordable.

Opposite the Po Nagar Cham Towers, there are several decent snack stalls.

The Po Nagar temple complex, dedicated to the goddess Po Nagar, is an interesting historical site. However, despite its significance, information about its founding is scant. Wikipedia states that the complex was destroyed, but there is no clear information about its construction period.

The complex is not particularly large.
Overall, this site may be of interest to those interested in history and architecture, but more information and materials are needed for a complete tour experience.

Apart from beach, food and foot massages there’s not that much to do in Nha Trang. So sightseeing the ancient Cham temple ruins is worth interrupting your beach routine. The bonus is that they’re only 5-10 mins taxi ride away or a 50min walk from the beach. Taxi cost is approximately 65,000VND with not much traffic. The entry fee is 22,000 VND. There are two main temples and the rest is ruins but you get a feel for what it would have been like on the banks of the river some 1400 years ago. The temples on the site can be visited but the cover up the body rule applies (so shoulders and below the knee - there are robes for hire if you didn’t bring you additional clothing). Some great pics can be taken of the temples and looking back to the beach. Overall, allow yourself 60-90 minutes to something different in Nha Trang.
Good to visit but there are just TOO MANY tours around the area. Maybe I would go next time if there is less people.
At least this venue has some real history and is not reproduced purely for tourism’s sake. There are again many steps to climb which can be slippery when wet.

Opens @6am - find it best time to visit: not crowded with tourists, not too noisy in town yet, so may enjoy beautiful sunrise view and calmness... And pray (if applicable)  Entrance fee VND 22k, parking ia available (paid VND 4k for apprx 20-30 mins).  Early morning was only about 5 people (including us) visiting the place, so 20-30 mins was sufficient.

very interesting archaeological site. do "NOT" take a tour there completely unnecessary. if you would like to walk around freely and take photos without an infinite amount of chinnese tourists, take the advice as i did from other reviews. get there early 8am. you will not be disappointed. short walk from Nha Trang centre(15mins). I recommend don't buy any food on that side of the bridge as prices are jacked for tourists.(but still cheap compared to other countries)

The Cham Towers are listed at the top of every guidebook when it comes to visiting Nha Trang. However, they unfortunately don't live up to this reputation. Certainly, the architecture and building techniques of the Cham people are impressive and interesting. However, this site has degenerated into a mere hotspot for photo tourists. Asian tourists, in particular, swarm around the structures with selfie sticks, snapping photos. Information panels about the history of the towers are nowhere to be found. The "showroom" selling replica statues completes the picture of a purely tourist attraction. This is a real shame, as the history of the towers (according to Wikipedia) seems genuinely fascinating. Unfortunately, this is a common problem in Vietnam: it's geared more towards photo tourism than a museum.

The towers are beautiful and impressive; we really enjoyed them. The place is beautiful, and the buildings are mesmerizing. I was disappointed that when we bought the ticket (even though they say it's free), we were cheated out of a large amount. Be careful.
It was interesting. Lots and lots of Chinese. Long lines everywhere. On the Kai River, next to the towers, there's a terrible garbage dump.

For the solo travelers, better to have taxi wherever you go in Nha Trang. Many motorbike drivers lie in terms of the price, they ask more than the agreed price.. they suck like a hungry leech!! Be careful guys!

The place is overcrowded so it's not easy to get a decent photo. Part of the complex are original but some parts were rebuilt using modern technology. Charges an entrance fee but no too pricey. If you have been to Bagan in Myanmar, then this place is not so spectacular anymore.

It's lovely. Even though the area is small, we wandered around for about an hour and even took some good photos. It turns out it wasn't just for show. Right behind the towers is a stall selling various souvenirs, but the prices there, as is usual in such places, are steep. My partner wasn't allowed into one of the towers, apparently because she was wearing shorts!

It's a surprisingly small place, you can view the towers on the outside and on the inside in 45 minutes tops. There are also nice little sculptures in the yard and a few images on display showing this place before renovation. It's worth going inside of the main tower, for me that's the only place in this whole complex where you can really feel the history and mistery of this old place of worship. It's dark, mysterious and quiet in there. BUT it's noisy and crowded everywhere else in the complex. Lots of tourists, mainly from Asia, are posing for pictures, taking pictures, shouting and pushing. When there was a little dance performance by the towers, these tourists were cutting in front of performers, getting in their space and taking selfies like there was no tomorrow. Forget about Western untold sightseeing rules: getting in line, waiting your turn and treating each other with consideration. Noone behaves this way. If you can, try to visit this place when there are less people.

Basically a temple for visiting to learn the culture around the place. Not advertised but big notice should be put out beforehand to be wearing pants if you wish to go in. Can always request to wear a monk cloth if not to be able to go in he temple to pay your respect. Cultural spot and that’s it.

The place is beautiful. A bit small, so there's not much to see. When we were there, it was incredibly crowded. You could barely get into the temples. The Chinese tourists are really rude about respecting the monuments (climbing on walls for photos...).
The place is beautiful. The view from the top is amazing. However, the place is crowded with tourists. Every corner is packed with people,difficult to get a decent picture.

It's about a 70 VND taxi ride from the InterContinental. It's very close. It seems to be related to a past dynasty, but there's no particular explanation. The entrance fee is 22 VND. There's a prayer space inside the temple, and you need to take off your shoes and hat to enter. I wouldn't say it's a place worth going out of your way for, but if you're tired of the beach and the pool, why not check it out?

It's a beautiful place, there's an entrance fee, lots of people, and a very beautiful view of the city. Sightseeing tours take you there, so it's packed with people, including Chinese. We got there on our own, as I don't see the point in taking a sightseeing tour and wasting half a day on them. Some of the towers have already been restored, but there are many where you can get a taste of "ancient times."
If it weren't for the bunch of Chinese, everything would be great, but where would this country be without them? And overall, you'll take a couple of photos!

From the door to enter the circle about fifteen to twenty minutes to finish the walk, because everyone in Nha Trang city photo spots are very close so by the way you can go to see the ruins of people's land, finally I went to the mud bath did not take pictures 😅

This is one of the few tourist attractions in Nha Trang. We arrived around 9:30 AM, but it was packed with tour groups from mainland China. Buses kept arriving one after another without interruption, and entry was restricted. I recommend visiting early in the morning. You can enter from 8:00 AM. It's located on a small hill, offering a great view of the city and the nearby fishing port. Beautiful flowers were in full bloom, and I really wanted to see them in peace! The ruins have been restored and are in beautiful condition.
One of the few tourist spots in the city that is worth visiting. A complex of old Buddhist temples on the edge of the city, classic with history to follow.

Spend a few minutes here, take a taxi to the pagoda close by, then take another taxi to the church. That's most of your religions covered. It's an interesting tourist area, but far too small to linger. Tons of tourists with their children running around too, so it's difficult to stay.
The temple isn't very big, so it's cozy and nice to walk around. However, it's crowded, so it's a bit difficult to take photos, but I think it's still worth seeing.

It’s a bit hard to reach, isn’t it? Anyway, since it is a historic site of significant importance in Vietnam, I think it would be good to visit at least once. I wore long pants out of courtesy, but they lend out robes for free at the site, so you can go in shorts.

Around 4-5 pm, the setting sun shines from behind onto the red soil of the ruins, contrasting beautifully with the blue sky. The choice of deity to worship depends on personal belief, but the place is incredibly crowded.
Very packed full of Chinese tourists but was lovely and cheap at only 22000vnd. Worth a visit and not too much walking involved so good for all abilities

When you think of Nha Trang, you think of Po Nagar. We arrived by taxi shortly after breakfast. It was Sunday morning, so it was crowded; the ticket office was packed. Climbing the slightly undulating stairs, we reached the famous tower, where many people were taking pictures with their smartphones. We were able to take some nice pictures with the trees with beautiful white and pink flowers in the background. As everyone's reports said, the ruins had been restored. The uneven coloring was noticeable, but I don't know if they did that to avoid obscuring the fact that it had been destroyed, but it was quite impressive. Descending the stairs, we saw a lot of people walking towards what seemed to be a fruit market under the large bridge near the entrance, so we followed them part of the way, but the strong smell got the better of us, and we turned around and got back into a taxi.

We were really looking forward to visiting this site but it was positively overrun with tourists - I’m well aware I’m one too - but it kind of spoilt the visit with SO many people milling about noisily. It’s beautiful and lovely to experience this type of historic site, but perhaps earlier in the day would be quieter.

We had fun viewing the buildings and seeing the gardens next to the river with a beautiful view. We also got to see some locals dancing but it only took us 30 min to do all. I would reccomend going to see it for the photos are pretty but nothing wildly amazing.

Full of Chinese tourists mainly. Was a live traditional dance which was quite nice. And some nice views. But other than that, given how crowded it was it is hard to enjoy this place. Would probably skip if I knew better

This is a Hinduism stone brick tower site which consist probably 1 main and 2 sub towers. Tower is not very big and tall, but it's story can back to few hundred years back to Champa era dominating center Vietnam. You need to queue up to enter the main tower which worshipping god Siva, whereas the 2 sub towers usually don't queue but the space is very narrow within the tower. This place is crowded with the local and tourist but on this hill you might be able the bridge crossing to Nha Trang.

Don't expect any exciting events; this is a solemn place. Photography is allowed outside, but shoes must be removed before entering the building, and clothing should not be revealing. Robes are available for rent free of charge.

At the top of Cu Lao Mountain, overlooking all of Nha Trang, stand the Po Nagar Towers. It's hard to believe these Hindu and Buddhist temples are over 1,000 years old; they're so well preserved for their age. The Po Nagar Towers were part of a temple complex built between the 7th and 12th centuries CE, during the Champa Kingdom (or Champa), which is why they're also known in literature as the Cham Towers. Of the ten towers, only four remain. Built at different times, they vary significantly in architectural design and size. Each Po Nagar tower is dedicated to a specific deity. Upon entering the tower-temples, you're immediately greeted by the pungent aroma of incense, which fills the entire space with a mystical haze. It's quite cramped, and even altars housing Hindu deities barely fit. Address: Nha Trang, 2 Thang, 4. Opening hours: Monday through Sunday, 6:00 AM to 6:00 PM. If you plan to admire the towers from the inside, you'll need to think about appropriate clothing in advance. In active temples, shoulders and knees are expected to be covered, and hats are not permitted. Shoes are traditionally left at the entrance. If your attire doesn't meet the requirements, special robes are available at the entrance. There's no dress code required for the tour. A tour of the towers themselves takes about half an hour. We went with the tour operator Anex, and honestly, the guide didn't provide any useful information. He simply drove us there and told us to meet at such-and-such a time. However, we went on a paid sightseeing tour, which cost us $16 per person. If anyone reads the review, it seems to me that we have many beautiful columns in Russia, including the Kolomensky National Park and Reserve, Bulgar near Kazan, and many others.
You can't miss this attraction if you go on a sightseeing tour. The impression will be spoiled by crowds of noisy Chinese.
We got here on our own, I don't think there's any point in taking a tour here. It's nothing more than an object for broadening one's horizons.

There are so many, so many people. It's impossible to squeeze through. The easiest way to get there is by bus No. 4, but probably early in the morning or late in the afternoon. The site isn't large, but during rush hour you feel like a sardine in a can. And the site administration could do with imposing a noise limit—it is a temple complex, after all—and the Chinese tourists are being quite unruly.
For about 20 minutes, no more. Just to look around and at the towers themselves, that's all. We got there by bus from the center.

Hello everyone! We went to these towers ourselves. We picked up a map from a travel agency (with a Russian information center) with Nha Trang's landmarks and directions for getting there, so we decided to give it a try. After lunch, we took a city bus (I think it was the #4) and got there in half an hour. We bought a ticket right at the ticket office, but I don't remember the exact price. It cost 22,000 rubles per person, I think. The whole hike takes about half an hour, so there's no point in overpaying the travel agency, even for locals. It's a couple of buildings, lots of tourists, and some beautiful camera-friendly views of Nha Trang from a different perspective. Then we walked across the bridge and into the city—it's still interesting to see the Vietnamese flavor. When we got tired, we got back on the bus and went to the European quarter. That's the whole trip!

Nha Trang has very few attractions, so the Cham Towers (their other name, for some reason more popular than the Cham Towers) are the number one must-see. We spent a maximum of 15 minutes there; you have to wear a funny gray robe to enter the temples, and the grounds themselves are very small. Yes, the towers are impressive, but not enough to give them an "excellent" rating. Entry is 22,000 VND, and you can arrive by motorbike, which is what we did.

A fairly ancient structure. Everyone is brought here. There are a sea of ​​people. We've been here twice already, but the third time we didn't want to crawl up the steep hill in the heat. The structure itself is more reminiscent of an Indian temple. Once you reach the top, you'll find the temple itself and nearby (as usual) various souvenir shops. Sometimes, a folk ensemble even performs local dances, or even a strange man with a staff in his hands and a turban on his head crawls out. Upon entering the temple, you have to take off your shoes and cover your shoulders and knees. It's dimly lit inside, and there's not much to see—blackened bricks? No frescoes or images of any kind. Around the temple itself, there are several picturesque spots on the hillside. From here, there's a magnificent view of the city itself, and there are several (I hope) ancient pedestals with images of maidens, similar to the apsaras at the Cambodian temple of Angkor Wat.
Lots of Chinese people. Old brick buildings. It's an acquired taste, unless you're on a sightseeing tour. It's not worth wasting your time or money.
Visited the attraction during new year, packed with people, better to go during off peak seasons as mentioned.

There aren't many places worth visiting in Nha Trang, but this is close and worth seeing. However, there are too many Chinese tourists. The view of the beach from under the bridge is also fantastic! If you are wearing short clothes, there is a place nearby where you can rent them for free.

I don't know why, but I wasn't impressed by this place. Overall, it's not bad, it's beautiful, but it didn't really wow me. It's probably an acquired taste. The area is very small, so half an hour is plenty to get around and see everything. It all depends on what interests you. There are a lot of people there, and the place is inviting. I'm not saying it's not worth visiting, but it's best to explore before you go and decide whether it's something you're interested in.

Note that you must wear long pants to enter the tower, but you can also tour the outside. If you have time, it's worth a visit as it's relatively small and you can walk through it in just a few minutes. A taxi online will cost approximately 15 RMB.

The red-hued architecture of the temple was exotic and impressive, but it is difficult to find it interesting unless you have some background knowledge or are accompanied by a guide. I tried to take a decent photo, but I felt very annoyed because I was jostled by all kinds of tourists everywhere. I think there is no need to go unless you visit early in the morning, right after it opens.
Located about 5 km from the center, so it is possible to walk, costs 22,000 Dong. Nice little area, with beautiful views. You can use approx. 30 min in the attraction. Can be touristy.

Recommended as a must-visit spot in Nha Trang, but in my personal opinion, I didn't find it very special, especially with the extremely large number of Chinese tourists and the overall chaotic atmosphere.
Nothing. We went to the sightseeing tour and regretted it; we wasted a ton of valuable time. You only need to go if you want to buy something.

A bunch of Chinese people, and it was 10 a.m. There are several structures in a small area that don't exactly inspire aesthetic, spiritual, or architectural admiration, but that's just my opinion. It's worth stopping by for 10 minutes and taking a look. The performance there was quite good.

We were on a tour and it was our first time here. Aside from the hot weather, it was pretty average. The scenery wasn't as beautiful as we'd imagined, and the architecture was the same. The only downside was the sheer number of people, mostly Chinese. Before leaving, we went to the restroom, but we went into the wrong men's stall – so embarrassing!
Local Buddhist temple that’s extremely busy. If you prefer to beat the heat and the crowds ten visit in the morning. Drinks and food is available.

Although I go to Nha Trang for a relaxing vacation, it's a great place to take a quick break and stop by for the kids to feel like a field trip. The red brick buildings are really pretty. It's easily accessible from the city center. About 15 minutes by taxi?
It's worth a one-time viewing, but only if it's a free overview. Nothing special, just a small Buddhist temple.
A very small area with a crowd of Chinese. It probably took longer to get there than to be there. I'd never been to a Buddhist temple before, but it was interesting to see.

We stopped by the Towers during the Bylina tour. There were a lot of Chinese people there, but the super guide, Dima, gave a very interesting explanation. Otherwise, without a guide, it's hard to understand anything.

A temple, very crowded, entrance fee 22,000 Dong. I planned to wear trousers, but I saw they lend out a kind of smock to cover up. You go in without shoes; it's very stuffy and small. A little disappointed.
Place of worship, five small pagodas. Dress appropriately. Entrance fee: 22,000 dong, approximately 8.5 euros, plus 4,000 dong for parking.

It's very small... about 4 towers? I went because prices were so cheap and I didn't have much time for sightseeing, but I don't think it's worth going out of your way to find. There are tons of Chinese tourists and it's noisy. If you try to go inside the tower, you'll be in trouble.
