An interesting place. But I didn't understand it! The Chinese are settling these pillars with interest. Many of the pillars have been restored.
Small temple but visited by many nice people. Perhaps the restoration was done a little poorly, but it is worth a visit. The entrance fee is 22,000 dong.

I stayed at the Champa Resort and visited the nearby Po Nagar Temple on the morning of the last day of my trip. I learned that this was the region where the Hindu Champa Kingdom was located. There were many Chinese group tourists, and although it seemed like places they shouldn't climb, many of them were climbing up to take photos. I find meaning in the fact that I saw a Hindu temple in person. It is a shame that it didn't particularly resonate with me because I viewed it without hearing an explanation of the region's history.

Surprisingly out of context Hindu architecture in Na Thrang. Disappointed to see Hindu statues replaced by Buddha statues. Evidence yet again of culture being superseded, erased. Visit in the early morning to avoid huge tourist crowds.
The place is small, just wander around. The entrance fee is inexpensive, and there's very little information about its history.

If you're in the area, it's worth a visit. After visiting neighboring Thailand, these types of places don't impress, but they do have a unique atmosphere, with a lot going on. There are dance performances. A 30-minute reservation is all you need.
Just normal. Do have so much things to see. The view are blocked by the big building in the front of the bridge.

We were nearby by chance, and ended up going inside...and then walking right out. Everything more or less beautiful about this art object is visible from afar... By the way, if you want to see an interesting place...there's a Sunshine bar on the left along the path around...just don't expect much. And then you'll discover it.

There isn't much to do if you just take pictures and look around for about 20 to 30 minutes. It isn't very spacious. It's worth a quick look around in the morning before visiting other places, rather than in the middle of the day.
Nice but touristic and many people. Only cultural place in nah tran a place where there is nothing authentic to see.
If you enjoy mingling with lots of other tourists and queuing for a picture in front of a tower, have fun! Unfortunately, nothing special...
It was a noisy and chaotic place swarming with tourists. Not only was it impossible to enjoy it calmly, but touching the historical sites with my hands was also unpleasant.

Overall, it's not bad. Many Chinese tourists have been to Angkor Wat before, and it is indeed very similar; it's a memory for many. However, the area is not large, and the visit time is relatively short, as it's a tourist attraction close to the city.

It's quite possible to get there on your own. Take a bus from the European Quarter. It's a 3-minute walk from the stop. There's an entrance fee. There's not much to see. It took me 20 minutes. The view from the top of the towers is good, but the towers themselves are nothing special. You can go inside, but you'll need special clothing, like a hoodie. They provide it, but putting it on after so many people is a challenge. There are souvenir stalls and benches. If there aren't any Chinese people, there won't be many people. If you have nothing better to do, you can visit. But it's best to do it in the afternoon.
Not that much to see - dilapidated brick structures. It’s something else to see in Nha Trang other than the beach.

It's worth a visit. The view is beautiful, both from the entrance and from near the towers. The towers are majestic. You can light candles; lighters are located next to the candles. The restrooms are on the right as you exit.

But there are sooooo many people, especially Chinese tourists, so it's best to visit it yourself, outside of a package tour, if possible. The place is truly tangible and sacred, and you can feel the energy there, but the crowds are truly distracting...

A lovely place, a vestige of the Cham civilization in Nha Trang. Worth seeing. Take bus number 4. Very easy to get to. The small port isn't very clean, not very charming. But hey, if it's gray in Nha Trang, it's worth the detour.

The Po Nagar Cham Towers has the potential to be a very nice site in Nha Trang, however, it just misses the mark.  It's a popular tourist spot so it is overrun with people. You will find yourself battling with hordes of Chinese tourists to find ideal locations for photos and to experience this cultural site. The actual towers are rebuilt and could be very interesting if more of an explanation was provided. Try to go early in the day to avoid the tourist buses.  I've been here twice and the experience was the same.  You can easily reach the towers by bus (#4 from the city center), taxi, or motorcycle.
The place is probably much more fascinating if you are accompanied by a good guide. Slightly overrated place I'd say. Most probably one wouldn't linger around for more than 15 minutes or so

Easily accessible by public transport (bus #4, 8000). Entrance fee 22,000. It is a tourist trap, but definitely worth it. Beautiful view over the river and the tall buildings of the tourist center behind it. The temple is also beautiful.

Went here today to see the buildings, they were lovely and got some great photos. It does get busy, biggest disappointment was its right in the city, for some reason i was imagining this would be located in a rural setting. Worth a visit if in the area.

An interesting place. My wife and I visited it while vacationing in Vietnam. It's easily accessible from the tourist area by regular bus. The towers are captivating in their antiquity. The observation deck offers stunning views of the fishing harbor, offering a wonderful glimpse into the local character.
We got there on a city bus, number 4, as if we were on our own. We crossed a bridge. The entrance fee was 22,000 dong. We took some photos. It was terribly hot.
My family and I went on a sightseeing tour; the area is small and there's not much to see. If you're interested, you can take a short bus ride yourself.

There are so many tourist lurking at same spot for ages (photo perfection) so was hard to get a quite spot. Otherwise the buidlings are nice too see. Wish they could include more story about history and uses if it.

Nha Trang doesn't have many tourist attractions, so it was incredibly crowded with tour groups. Currently, five shrines remain from the Champa Kingdom era. They've been restored, so they were in remarkably good condition. Perhaps because I'd already visited the My Son Sanctuary, the scale was smaller and the restoration was so meticulous that it didn't quite impress me. It's also a viewpoint overlooking Nha Trang Port.
It's a beautiful, historic place, but the crowds of rude Chinese people everywhere ruin it! I recommend visiting, but be patient beforehand.
Both Chinese and Koreans become nuisances here. Please visit as quickly as possible when there are no people around. Since the weather is hot, I recommend bringing some drinks.

We went here by bus without a guide. We didn't understand anything except that there were TONS of people here. For some reason, they were eating on the ground. They were walking around in Kula. The park was small. There was basically just one temple of some kind on the grounds. We went inside and liked it. But there wasn't much else here. Especially not worth paying 22,000 dong for.
You can visit it just for the sake of it; there's nothing surprising about this place... There are a lot of Chinese tourists, and the only people there were Europeans...

Very disappointing! Efforts to restore these ancient towers were inferior compared to numerous other such ancient temples built elsewhere in Vietnam and Cambodia Use of regular bricks and cement statues did not impress The place has been basically neglected for too long! Bollywood music jarred whatever tranquility was present to attract the multitude of site visitors until the dancers appeared to the traditional strains of local orchestra which was nice! Views of Nha Trang & ocean were nice and best part was that the toilets were exceptionally maintained totally clean & fragrant! We were pleased our tour fir the day only involved a half hour visit as I don’t know how anyone could have spent 2-3hrs as suggested in another tour!

The site is beautifully actually. Temple is great to see. However, too many Chinese tourists and Russian. We are the only Americans group here. Nha Trang now is like a little China. And, you can not do anything. Just go with flow! Choose your best time is early in the morning to buy your luck.

Would not take my shoes off so i viewed it all from outside. Still quite interestning. The guide on our bike tour explained some of the history. We were there on a really hot day. Now i know why they use umbrellas in the sun!

We came here to see it as I was interested. I dot think I will be rushing back. It was very busy and not as interesting as I hoped it would be. Go if you have time, but don’t make time especially for it.

The ticket price per person is 20,000 rubles (or 60 rubles). There is parking (to the right of the entrance). Inside, there is a small area - a mini-park and the towers themselves. We were there around 4 pm - there were a lot of people (it looks like everyone came here after the beach and lunch. Lots of Chinese people. They are all taking photos in front of the towers, and they do it very slowly). The towers are interesting and beautiful. Photos with them in the background are beautiful, but it is impossible to take a good photo of the towers themselves due to the large number of people. There is a small souvenir shop and a performance by a local dance troupe. If you want to go inside the towers, make sure that your knees, stomach area, and elbows are covered - if necessary, there are robes hanging there that you can wear. There are small statues inside the towers (one in each tower - three towers in total). Overall, you can stop by for about 20 minutes, as there is not much to do there. But the towers are beautiful.
If you visit Nha Trang, you should go there. Price for one person 20,000VND. It's in the city. It's easy to get there by a taxi
Pick a time when it's not so busy, if you want to see it and take your time. We had to sort of que up , as there were o lot of visitors there. Still not to be missed whilst one is there.

Overall, it's interesting. Lots of Chinese people. There's an entrance fee. Free robes are provided for those who want to go inside. It's more interesting with a guide. But 15 minutes is enough for a quick tour without any historical background.

The map address on the website is incorrect!! We walked 6 kilometers from the city center according to the map, only to find we were at a bus station!! What a wasted trip! After returning to the hotel, we searched again on Google Maps and found the ruins were located much closer to the city center!!

Where Nha Trang stands today used to be part of the Cham kingdom, which was once more powerful and larger than Vietnam. These ruins are Hindu - the main religion of the Cham’s, although some were Buddhist and Muslim too. The fact that the three main attractions in Nha Trang are an operational Catholic cathedral, an operational Buddhist pagoda, and the ruins of an Hindu temple is interesting - maybe a sign of the openness of the Vietnamese people?
It's unclear why this place is so popular. It's interesting to visit once, but you expect more. For me, it wasn't particularly interesting.

This ancient temple, with an entrance fee of 22,000 đi, is very charming. There are traditional performances on site. Dress is required to enter the temple (shorts and skirts are not suitable), but free costumes can be borrowed nearby. The temple area is not large, and it gets very crowded with mainland Chinese tourists, making it difficult to take individual photos. However, it's a must-see attraction for anyone exploring the city.

If you're not spoiled by Asian temple complexes, it's definitely worth a visit. Otherwise, it's not impressive at all. The area is small, with several small ancient temples. You can spend about 40 minutes here at most. It's very crowded in the evenings. Entrance and bike parking are subject to a fee.

I went to the towers on my own, on bus number 4. It's worth seeing at least once. I think it's best to go with a guide who can tell you all about this place. The incredible number of Chinese tourists ruined the whole experience—it was impossible to see or photograph anything.

During my trip to Nha Trang, I had the opportunity to visit the Ponaga Tower, a truly excellent attraction for those wishing to explore Cham culture. However, there were too many noisy Chinese tourists, so we left early. These ancient cultural values ​​need to be preserved and promoted so that future tourists can admire this site.
Not the best Hindu temple I have seen, and as others state, full of groups of chinese tourists just getting in the way for their Instagrammable photo shoot!

A Hindu site with a real history... but it's impossible to appreciate its charm with the noisy crowds and the heat. Furthermore, without a guide, you can't distinguish the ancient structure from the rebuilt parts. In addition, the surrounding area is dirty and crowded with vendors and vehicles. It smells of seafood and grilled squid. Well, it's a quick visit.
A very small place and need to purchase ticket to enter it.  Not much happening but it is a good and nice place for some photo taking too.

The towers will not keep you busy for long, although they are definitely worth checking out if you are in the area.  There are obviously better examples of similar architecture in Asia, such as Bagan in Malaysia or the Angkor Wat complex in Cambodia, although this serves to show you what can expected of those locations on a smaller scale.

The entrance fee to the Ponagar Tower is 22,000 VND. You can admire the beauty of the ancient Cham towers. Within the area, there's also a small exhibition hall displaying images of the towers over time. You can take photos and enjoy seafood right on the street next to the towers.

The tour guide gave us one hour for one stop on the itinerary. I thought we could finish the visit quickly, but there were still quite a lot of people. I didn't go inside to visit each exhibit; I just looked around from the outside and then found a cooler spot to stay.

Overall, it's an interesting historical place, but like all tourist spots these days, it's lost its unique spirit. Don't expect anything spectacular from this place, but if you've never seen anything like it, you should definitely visit.

Go early in the morning as soon as it opens. My partner and I went about 9.30 and there were people everywhere and not the kind that are considerate and will kindly move if you wanted to try take a picture. Beautiful sure and towers but for sure go first thing.

There aren't many attractions in the city, and this is just one of the places to visit. The entrance fee isn't expensive, and a walk around the ruins takes about 15 to 30 minutes. There's no historical information provided (maybe I just didn't see it), it's just about seeing the historical buildings. It's very crowded, a typical tourist spot. From the hotel area near the beach, a taxi costs about 60,000 VND, and on the way out, another taxi to i-Resort costs about 70,000 VND.
Average site , to overgrouded but as there is little to see in Nha Trang it’s worth a visit 22000 enywtrance fee

Nice place. Its not that big. I recommend getting a taxi from the hotel area abou 60k. There is an entrace cost of 22k. We went and it was so busy with chinese tourist. U can spend about 1hr there. Enjoy a cold drink. It does get hard on the kids.

I went there on a whim in the afternoon, and it was packed with tourists. It's basically a place for taking photos and checking in on social media, but the hard part is finding a spot without anyone in the background. When I went, there was a nice dance performance, and there were also girls selling things who were weaving on an old-fashioned machine – their craftsmanship was amazing.

Wife and I visited Po Nagar. It’s a beautiful setting and the buildings temple is a must see. The thing which let it down MAJORLY were the amount of people allowed in at any one time. It was crazy, especially the Chinese tourists who arrived by the bus load. The majority were we’ll behaved, but many of them are incredibly loud, disrespectful and rude - going into the temple with short shorts and singlets and literally taking 15+ mins trying to get the “perfect” photo in a spot where other visitors might also like to take a photo. It really let the whole thing down. You’re not missing anything if you skip this!

Its what you expect.. Old history being upkept with the money that is paid for by the entrance fee . Usual hawkers and tat on sale.. great if you want photos to show how you are discovering Vietnam... i am sure that the temple itself could be far better restored and protected if a fraction of the money it earns was spent on preservation .. also . Make a proper drop off point for taxis and bus..

It took approximately 5 mins to walk around the towers and there were sooooo many people that you found it hard to get any nice photos... if you have a short holiday planned then avoid nha trang and the po nagar cham towers and spend your time more wisely in da nang and visit my son sanctuary why you are there!

I love the history and story surrounding the Cham Towers. It was a small site and only took 15 - 30 minutes to see the entire thing. It was not expensive but just know that there isn't a lot to the site. However, the site is amazing!
It was nice to go and visit but of course this is a major tourist hub so very busy. A lot of people.
A very popular and interesting place in the northern part of the city, be prepared for a crowd of Chinese tourists.
Went by taxi, quite near to town area, convenient. Very hot weather, nice view from top. Small area, finished walking by 20 minutes.

Actually, this area is mainly by the sea. Unless you are a big fan of history, it is quite small and the visit ends quickly. There is nothing else to visit or do nearby, and you will feel quite empty afterward. Overall, unless you plan to spend many days and have plenty of time, I think you can skip this place.

Since we were in the area, we decided to check it out. It's a relatively small complex consisting of about three monuments (it reminded us of the Prambanan temple complex in Indonesia). It's alright, but quite small and manageable. You can go inside the individual towers, but you have to dress appropriately (long dresses are available there, possibly for an extra charge). Of course, you have to pay for the entrance fee (around 20,000 per person) and the moped parking (around 5,000).

Worth seeing its a $4 can ride from middle of town. It's about $1.30 entry fee. Interesting site but nothing nothing mi d blowing. Things to know there is a set.of steps up not really steep just a few probably.not great for people not able bodied. Lots of tourists. Not in the middle of the day. If you haven't travelled much you will probably love it.

The crowds of excited and noisy Chinese tourists make you forget the religious aspect of the place. The site consists of towers: temples of varying importance where you can pray. You must remove your shoes before entering a temple. Large tents set up between the towers offer shade to visitors but greatly limit the possibilities for taking overall photos. A symbolic entrance fee of 22,000 rubles is charged. There are restrooms on the site.

It's a temple where locals pray for peace, but it's very small and under construction, so the lighting is poor. Inside, the scent of sandalwood is very strong, making the experience unpleasant. There's a dress code; you can't show your upper arms or thighs. However, the architecture is very rough and ordinary, and the scenery is extremely small—you can see everything in five minutes; there's not much to see.

Our ship was initially to dock in Ho Chi Min City (Saigon) but because of the number of very large container ships in port at the time and no docks available that could accommodate our ship, the itinerary was change to Nha Trang. The excursions made available seemed to be haphazardly assemble. This site was VERY crowded. We arrived mid day and there were already thirty or more busloads of tourists. Additionally the mid day heat was intense making the effort to climb just twenty or thirty narrow steep stairs a choir. There is also a small straw market at the bottom of the site and the vendors will chase you all the way back to your bus trying to get you to buy something. This excursion was not worth the price paid to visit. The one positive thing is the street vendors took US and Australian dollars and RMB.

This place has great views surrounding the 4 towers but in actuality the towers themselves aren’t so exciting. They are quite crammed and hot inside and from a tourist point of view you feel a little intrusive to those who have come to pray. The place feels a little like a human zoo and I’m guessing most people complete to tick off the box. I would be brave and give this place a miss if you are not praying.

The visit is short, the ticket is cheap, and the architecture is interesting, but it's too crowded and the place isn't very big! If you have time, you can go check it out! If you don't have time, just skip it! Mmm...
This was interesting, but packed with tour busses. So many people take away from the visit. It's a pretty small complex. They have free drinking water!

It feels stifling because there are so many people in a small space. There is no consideration even when trying to take a single photo. The architecture is worth seeing; it is fascinatingly intricate. Since a trip to Nha Trang is truly about relaxation, it is just a good place to stop by briefly and take a picture.
VERY overcrowded busy place as it’s in Nha Trang. Try & go early to beat the bus loads & get interesting lighting.
We visited this place out of curiosity. It's not a place I would highly recommend. If you have the time visit. If not you won't really be missing anything!

We discovered this place with a guide who explained what it was. The monument is very beautiful, but when you go alone, there's a lack of explanations or information to avoid making mistakes, as it is, after all, a religious site.

You can visit for a change. The whole process will take no more than 30 minutes. I wasn't very impressed (It's not worth wasting time on a separate trip; you can stop there after the rock garden. It's nearby).

I was here for the first time in November 2016. We had a great walk. There weren't many tourists. Now it's completely jam-packed. There are Chinese everywhere. Everywhere you look. It's very noisy. I practically ran away from there.

I'm probably being pretty kind with the rating here. I went, as the title suggests, just passing by. I had planned a visit, but when we got there it felt like they had carved out a thousandth of Angkor Wat, Cambodia, to have something to show off. Surely more interesting for those who haven't been to Angkor.

Old temple of the Cham people. Overcrowded with tourists and lots of trinket vendors around. People carried trays of food (dead chickens), fruit and other things and offered it to someone in the temple.
It would be a lovely place if it weren't for the billion Chinese "friends" who yell and act like pigs all the time.
There's basically nothing to do there. It wasn't impressive, but the crowds were wild. In March 2018, the entrance fee was 22,000.

A truly beautiful temple, but unfortunately, the inconsiderate behavior of tourists (mainly Chinese) spoils the visit. While the building is immense, don't expect to visit it like you would a cathedral or church. Instead, remove your shoes before entering, join your hands, bow three times, walk around the altar, and exit (feel free to leave a small donation in the collection box for the upkeep of the temple). Photography is not permitted inside. Outside, the gardens are stunning, and you'll have a lovely view of the harbor. The entrance fee is 22,000 dong (less than €1).

If you're visiting this place as a sightseeing tour, don't expect anything special. It's quite interesting for cultural enlightenment. But I wouldn't recommend it for a solo visit. There's not much of interest.

Not bad, but nothing more. It's easier to take a taxi from the center along the route Cham Towers, Catholic Cathedral, and Long Son Pagoda. A taxi from the European Quarter will cost about 150 dong. Then you can take bus #2 to Lotte Market or Big Sea, or take it back to the European Quarter. Be sure to wave the bus vigorously, otherwise it won't stop.

Unfortunately, we arrived at this place during the peak tourist season of the Chinese New Year, so we weren't able to fully appreciate its cultural value. The complex is located on a hill with beautiful views of the nearby river and surrounding area. The complex itself consists of several towers, a souvenir shop, and a small exhibition hall with photos documenting the complex's history. It's easily accessible on foot, by bus, or by taxi if you're staying within Nha Trang.
As far as the attraction is concerned, it's a great place, a super mini version of Angkor Wat. But there are just so, so, so, so many Chinese tourists, and you know what happens.

It's a very small attraction, worth a quick visit. The entrance fee isn't expensive, and children are free. Half an hour is enough; taxis are readily available. Personally, I don't think it's necessary to come with a tour group. Just take some photos. There are performances inside, which are interesting but not particularly high quality. It's said to be a mini-Angkor Wat, but I've never been to Cambodia, and I'm not impressed... Don't have too high expectations...
It's a sacred temple, just one more like others out there; if you come here exclusively, it has no merit. My recommendation is to come if you have time and are bored.
An impressive structure, a five-minute drive away. There are benches for resting and a very expensive market nearby.
We were there in the morning. There were a lot of Chinese and Russians, of course. To make the walk more interesting, it's best to take a guide, but we didn't, so there wasn't much to do there.
It looks like the Angkor Wat ruins, but the restoration work is completely inadequate. I wish I could teach them the meaning of the word "restoration."

It's a picturesque location, with the entire city spread out before my eyes. Every time I visit this amazing country, I always return. I'm simply drawn here! The marvelous ancient architecture is captivating. There are countless souvenirs to remember my visit by that can only be bought here. I first visited Vietnam in 2013—I come back every year to celebrate my birthday. I've become obsessed with this country. Maybe I'll move here when I retire. The French really know what they're talking about!
It's a comfortable place, and you can buy Vietnamese and Thai oils there as well. However, the Chinese people are off-putting. Just look, don't eat. I suffered a bit after eating the ice cream.
It's worth a visit if you have nothing to do and no plans. It's a nice place, but nothing special. You can go inside the towers.

We took a 10-minute taxi ride from our hotel to the towers for 75,000 dong (about 200 rubles), and then decided to walk back. We walked slowly, strolling, and taking photos. Entrance to the towers themselves is 22,000 dong. We spent 15 minutes looking at the towers, but then sat and admired the views of the city and took photos. The views are beautiful!

The Cham ruins are quite far and remote. However, it's a major tourist attraction in Nha Trang, so it's worth a visit. While there's not much to see—just historical sites and not much entertainment—it might be a bit boring. But if you enjoy photography, it's a great place to take lots of selfies. I took many myself. Despite its remote location, it's definitely worth the trip.
The buildings themselves were exciting to see even though it barely took more than an hour. Minus for all the busloads of tourists, but that's not the building's fault. ;)
Russian guides are not allowed there, there are no Vietnamese ones, there is a clear lack of normal guides to tell about the Cham

Just a couple of AUD$ will gain you entry into this small site. We were there on a miserable rainy day and weren't really feeling it, but perhaps better weather would have improved our mood and our experience. Also, knowing more about the history of the place would have made it more interesting.  FYI there are toilets inside the grounds.

If you are in Nha Trang and want to occupy your time besides beach relaxing, check out this place, right in the city. Small temple and Ok to visit in about 30 - 45 minutes. Nothing much to see but anyway go there if you don't want to miss any parts of Nha Trang
Old temple that is okay to go to if you want to look at something old. Many tourists everywhere, especially mainland Chinese and Russian.

The area is small; you can walk through it in about 10 minutes. Be aware of tour groups; they're always scrambling to take photos and get a good spot, which is annoying. The view towards the sea is nice, but remember you need to buy a ticket.
A small ancient temple on a low hill. One of Nha Trangs’s iconic must sees, tranquil but not very spectacular to temples in other counties. Nice photos can be taken of the river Cai from above.

There aren't many places to visit in Nha Trang, and this attraction is one of the few historical sites besides the beach. It's also a popular spot for tour groups and middle-aged women to take photos; half an hour is enough.

Personally, I think since you're already here, although it's a bit small, a quick stroll will suffice. Those who are fishermen or enjoy historical sites can visit to pray. Generally, it's more popular with group tours. The entrance fee is even cheaper than the price of rice noodles. If you're traveling independently and have time, you can take bus number 4 from the bus stop 50 meters opposite the Sinh Cafe (7000 DV per person). After crossing the second bridge, it's on your left.
It's worth a visit, the only ancient landmark in Nha Trang. It's a bit pricey, 22,000 dong for a tour that takes about 10 minutes.
Nice temple. Smal enterancefee. Very busy place. Somethimes they show traditional dances.about 2-3 km from the citycentrum.
It's a beautiful site but there was no information to tell us what we were looking at! Suggest googling before you go

I went there because it was near our accommodation, but the kids didn't really like it. Personally, I found it fascinating and cool. It was chaotic because there were so many people, so I took photos from the car while looking after the kids. It looked pretty cool blending in with the scenery.

Not that impressive compared to the rest of the temples that you could see around Asia - in both the level of complexity and style of the architecture, as well as the scale of the site. Extremely crowded with tourists. Worth a visit if you're nearby just to check it off but you won't be missing much if you've already seen the bigger sites in Thailand or Bali.

Nice little temple. You will have seen everything in 20 minutes. Place offers a nice view on Nha Trang. Entrance fee is 22'000 VND. If you have only limited time at your disposal, I would recommend to visit Long Son Pagoda instead.

I received directions from the accommodation and took a local bus from a nearby stop. It is also a 30-minute walk from Thapba Onsen, so it is worth visiting both together. I am not usually a fan of temples, but it was interesting because it was a type of temple I had never encountered before.

Beautiful buildings with nice views of Nha Trang and the river. Very cheap entry and many nice opportunities to take pictures. A lot of tourists though, so you have to fight your way through the crowds for the best angles.

You don't have to visit. We were passing by on Sunday and wanted to check it out, but due to the large number of Chinese tourists, we decided to come on Monday. We went on Monday and didn't see anything special. It was beautiful, or maybe we weren't so inspired, it was hot. 🙈 We took some photos and left. There were Chinese people, but not many.

You are free to enter the stupa, but if you are wearing shorts and a short-sleeved shirt, you will need to borrow and wear a cover-up garment. The entrance fee is 30,000 VND for adults, and the parking fee for motorbikes is 7,000 VND per vehicle per visit.

If you're not a fan of ancient relics, you're unlikely to find these towers appealing. A bus ride from the city center costs 7,000 dong—it's inexpensive, plus about 20 dong for the entrance fee. Inside, the traditional dances (if you can get there) and hand-made pottery are interesting. The towers themselves are unimpressive. The only good thing is they're close and inexpensive.

There are always a lot of people. The towers are located on a hill. They are very recognizable – two of them, but completely different. Ancient. Behind the towers, girls dance to local music with pots and coconut shells. You can't enter the towers with bare shoulders and knees. Here, you can put on a robe and cover everything at once. It's not a pareo, but quite adequate clothing. You can't enter with shoes. The tower itself is 3 by 3 meters from the inside. I can't even remember what was in the middle. I was captivated by the ceiling. Or rather, the lack of one. The roof of the tower is pyramid-shaped. It's about 10 meters high. I peered at the ceiling for a minute and didn't find either a flat surface or a recess corresponding to the roof – just blackness, emptiness, and abyss. The second tower is even smaller from the inside. Since the towers are on a hill, there is a view of the city. But again, that's not what caught my attention. It was the landscape of boats on the river we were passing through.

I heard how pretty Po Nagar Cham Towers are and I was excited to go see it but it was so crowded when I arrived, I almost turned around and left. But I stayed the course and waited out the hoard of Chinese tourists and once the crowd thinned I was able to enjoy the towers and temples and take some gorgeous photographs. It was funny watching the hoard though, they were a little scandalous, saw flashing legs and panties, yikes! In the adjacent shore of the canal there's a lot of garbage so you get a city view and the view of the garbage and you can smell the garbage too so that dampens the experiences a bit. Overall if you go expect crowds but they hopefully should thin out.
Good short trip. Worth a look on a sunny day. Depends what you are into (not really my thing) but my mrs enhoys the historic sites.

This is a must-see attraction for tour groups, and the entrance fee is very reasonable. We arrived around 11:00 AM, and the entire site was packed with tourists; it was so crowded that taking photos was difficult. Also, the explanations provided were too limited; you had to consult a wiki online to understand the history.
There are a lot of Chinese people there, and Russians find it quite boring, but the stone towers are worth a look. We went in and out, stones, stones, stones...
There's not much left, just a lot of Chinese people who won't let you take proper photos. They brazenly climb into your background.

A popular photo spot, it's crowded with tourists. Although there are historical buildings, there's no clear explanation of their historical background. Maybe I just missed it? But if you're in Nha Trang, besides the beach, check this out!
The towers here are a lot bigger than they look in pictures, and supposedly they’re very old. It’s worth taking a look and getting some pictures, but you won’t spend much time at this attraction

It's a very small area, at most a small park with a historical relic, the kind you see everywhere in China; you might find it somewhat informative by looking at photos and reading the guide, but it's not worth making a special trip for.
You can take a look if you have nothing better to do! That's about it! To finish off the symbols, I'll say that if I didn't have the bike, I wouldn't go there for the money!

It was my first time visiting, and it was very convenient. There weren't too many people. I wandered between each site, trying to understand the beliefs of the people there, how they practiced their religious activities, and the whole process was quite intriguing.

Not impressed. There's really nothing to see. On the other hand, the price is very low (22,000 dong). You can take some nice photos. But you have to go early in the morning, as the Chinese arrive in huge numbers for lunch.
This is the most attractive place to go if compare with other attractions. Extrance fee is 22,000v. We spent 20minutes then went to tha pa spa, just around there.

There were quite a few visitors. It's rather unique, built of red bricks. It's relatively small; you can see everything in no more than half an hour. It's definitely a local must-see attraction. The sugarcane juice at the entrance is worth trying; it's quite good. I think it's around 7000 won a cup, but I forget the exact price.

An interesting place to experience the Cham religion. The grounds include three small tower-temples, which you can enter to see the altar. The only downside is the sheer number of tourists. The towers themselves are crowded, and the grounds are crowded and noisy.

It was a historical site with lot of tourist. I stayed there for 30 mins and walked around, there is a photo exhibition to showed out the past and present site. However, this is a must see item for the tourist group and made this place a bit noisy and crowd.

The Chamskie Bashki were included in our sightseeing tour, but you can get there on your own. Don't expect anything spectacular; you'll be surrounded by the ubiquitous Chinese, and you won't get a single decent photo. For a general introduction to the country's cultural heritage, if you're tired of lounging on the beach, you can explore it.
We covered this site in about 45 minutes. Not worth an excursion but worth popping in to see. The temple is amazing in its design and you'll vet some great pictures.

The Po Nagar Cham Towers are just a spot to see and very small in scale. We spent only 20 minutes only to walk around and take pictures. However, it is convenient to walk from here to Thap Ba to have a seafood meal.

Visit this beautiful and interesting place in the city. Nearby is a river that feeds fishermen, and there are many interesting and exotic sights. It's always exciting to discover new countries, new cities, new monuments, and new landmarks!

I visited this place during my morning run. It's a religious site built by the Champa people, who are of Cambodian descent. It's a large building made of red brick, and its atmosphere is reminiscent of Angkor Wat. As it is a religious site, if you are wearing revealing clothing, you will need to borrow a cloth to wrap around your waist when visiting.

The towers are probably an interesting architectural landmark in themselves. They're also a functioning religious complex. Within them, Hindu gods with mysterious faces peer thoughtfully down at the worshippers from the darkness. Around the towers is a compact garden with varying levels of platforms and boulders used as staircases. The grounds are small, and you can see everything in about half an hour. However, be prepared for countless Chinese people there, making it difficult to take decent photos. And of course, there's the usual Chinese "ooh" in the background. From certain vantage points, you can easily see the city itself and its "great tourist construction site," but if the sun is shining on the spot, the Chinese won't be there, and you'll be able to enjoy the peace and quiet.

Although it's a must-see attraction in Nha Trang, I was quite disappointed after entering. The entrance fee of 22,000 VND isn't expensive, but once inside, there was practically nothing to see. Vietnam, as a former French colony, has very little of its own culture. It's barely worth a visit.
It's a remarkable structure, but nothing more. It may be of great historical value and worth seeing, but it's not exactly remarkable.

Beautifully renovated temples after the war. Quite interesting to see how it looked before it was renovated. They had pictures of it.  It was a full-blooded tourist trap! Way too many people for a very small complex. If you've been traveling just a little in Southeast Asia, it's honestly not worth seeing. It's very average.
We went and it was very crowded to get in, you have to pay 20,000, it didn't seem like much to us since we were in Myanmar and saw many pagodas but overall it's okay.
They are really crowded. Not to much to see. Inside it's nice, lots of smoke and people who are donating.

This temple reflects the religion and local customs of the past, making it perfect for those interested in learning more about these aspects! Besides visiting and worshipping at various temples, you can also try on special costumes for beautiful photos with the temple in the background. However, transportation here is somewhat inconvenient; it's recommended to check the local bus schedule!
We visited this place on a sightseeing tour. The guide rushed everyone and didn't give us time to see everything. In short, it was a hop, skip, and jump across Europe.
We went there ourselves, by bike. It's not a particularly interesting place, and we weren't impressed, but if you've been everywhere and seen everything, it's worth a visit.
Interesting design though the territory is quite small. When we reached there, it was full of tourist and the weather was too hot, so we just took some photo and left.

It's a nice place. I went in the morning and there were tons of tourists. It must be part of the classic tour because buses were arriving packed full. The temples themselves are beautiful. But if you're planning a trip to Cambodia or Thailand, I'd recommend spending the time on this excursion going to the beach for a while. The downside is that there aren't any signs explaining anything. You go, see bricks, and go back to your hotel without knowing what you saw. There's a lack of information. Lots of tourist shops.

I took a taxi from I-Resort. I don't know the name of the taxi I used, but it was white with light blue lettering. When I showed them the metered fare, they gave me a discount. It was an evening visit, but it was crowded with tour groups.

Worth a visit and since the cost is so low, 22,000 vnd each, it really doesn't make you feel as though this is a rip off. The ancient buildings are fascinating to admire and the shrines are wonderfully colourful. Some fabulous photo opportunities. The site is small, and as mentioned by other reviews half an hour to an hour is ample time. It can be busy with coach loads of tourists but just sit down, relax, take in the sights and they soon come and go. Toilets are on site and a small cafe serving simple drinks etc. After the visit we ventured around the local town. Not too much 'tourist' stuff to see, but we enjoyed strolling down the side streets and looking at the local houses and stalls. We even tried a small stall selling a mix of simple local food and we got invited inside to sit at one of the two small plastic table and chairs. The owner even ran down the road to fetch us a few beers. Needless to say whatever we ate was delicious and extremely inexpensive. Owner was so friendly and happy for us to point at the food we wanted, which his wife prepared and cooked for us. Such a fabulous experience....although our teenage daughter seemed less impressed😁
The site is small and crowded, with many Asian tourists who seem to enjoy close quarters and noise. The towers are nice, but nothing breathtaking.

Entrance fee 20k VND per person. Something like a park, nice for a walk, the monument itself is nothing special, a spiritual place. You shouldn't take pictures inside and your body should be covered, which everyone violates anyway. There are robes worn by monks, available to borrow free of charge. The biggest problem is the huge number of people, so it's hard to take pictures, but there are places where you can capture the monument from a nice angle and be on it alone (climb the rocks).

We visited the Cham towers around lunchtime and it was pretty busy with tourists but not as packed as i was expecting. The site is small but the towers were nice to look at and one of only a couple of places in Nha Trang that offers a bit of culture!

We arrived round about 3pm on a Thursday. It was packed with tourists, it's located on the road side so wasn't that impressive from the outside. Once you pay an entrance fee, you head up to the temple towers where you can go in and have a look at (shorts, skirts and shoes not allowed) but don't worry, gowns were available for free. The place isn't very big but it's worth a visit since it's not far from town and the hotel area. We were lucky enough to have witnessed the local cultural music performance and dance. Motorcycle parking is available on site at 4000 viet dong.
The towers itself are something you must see in Nha Trang but the fact that it is so filled with tourists that you can barely walk, loses all the attractiveness. 99% filled with Chinese tourists.
It is not very big. You can see the whole place in half an hour. A good stop on the way to other places.

This is like a very miniature Angkor Wat, with only four towers. They're okay individually, but because they're so small, they're not really impressive. It's quite far from Nha Trang city center, so it's not worth making a special trip here unless it's on your way.

In the heart of Nha Trang, this site is nice to visit, with beautiful towers housing prayer temples. You can even enter wearing a gray blouse provided free of charge by the attendants. Scooter parking costs 5000 dong, which isn't expensive.

The view to the left from this spot is quite nice, but the precious relics aren't very well preserved. There are some cafes and decent seafood restaurants near the site, but they're not cheap; the owners cater to different customers and charge much more than locals.

We went ourselves, by bus, bought tickets at the ticket office and went to see... We arrived at 11 am, but there were already a decent number of people, a lot of Chinese, of course, but the territory is not large, there is nothing special to see... yes, there are towers, yes, small temples, someone is praying, bringing offerings... in general, it is certainly worth seeing, but don't expect anything grandiose...
It's a boring place, not worth a visit. They take tourists there because there's not much to see in the city. The visit took 10 minutes.

Nothing special, we went on a city tour, which was more like shopping, and they kept telling us the prices were the lowest just for us. At the end of the tour, which lasted about five hours, they took us to the towers to see them. There were tons of people and it was raining ☔️, so the bottom line is: you have to go on your own without a tour.

Easily accessible, near the city on a hill with a beautiful view of the city and the river. There is an entrance fee. Climbing the steps to the plaza, you'll find charming stalls selling typical Vietnamese souvenirs and dance performances. The gods in the temples are still worshipped, and you're expected to enter barefoot and with your shoulders covered. The main temple features a very beautiful depiction of the goddess. In a house behind the temple, you can see her richly embroidered robe and ceremonial objects. Definitely worth seeing!

The group came; the weather was cloudy and not too sunny. The place isn't very big, so we finished looking around quickly. There aren't many towers inside. You need to change into free robes and take off your shoes before entering. Generally speaking, there's not much to experience; just a brief overview is enough.

We went on a package tour, which the tour operator gave us as a complimentary gift for the tours we purchased. Vietnam, in my opinion, doesn't have a huge selection of attractions, especially the Phan Thiet/Mui Ne region. On a small hill stand the remains of two large and one small Cham tribal towers. They are partially under reconstruction, which is obvious from the brickwork and color of the bricks. This is a one-time tour.
It's fun to stroll around and take photos near them. You can make a wish by going inside each one. You have to wear protective clothing to enter, but if you don't, don't worry, you can take one.
You can reach this local attraction independently by city bus #4. It's worth setting aside 1-2 hours of free time.

Amazing place and it is on one block. Lots of steps to climb but I would recommend you wears runners. Temples were interesting and watched cultural dance. Good stalls to look at. Had no problem to get taxi home
There's plenty to see and do, and plenty to photograph. They even provide free robes for entering the temple. For those who appreciate antiquity.

We visited the towers on a free sightseeing tour from a tour operator. There's nothing to do there for a fee, as there are crowds of tourists (mostly Chinese), it's hot, and without a guide, it's uninteresting. Just take a few photos and run to the bus...

Visited about 13:00 on a Saturday afternoon. Entrance fee 22,000 dong.  Remarkable structures but completely over crowded and a surprising lack of information. It took Wikipedia to tell me that these towers were created in the 7th century - extraordinary! Don't understand why they don't provide any of the historical info.  Overall a worthwhile visit just thought it could have been better.
Trying to take in the Cham history with the towers and temple. Interesting views from the top of the city. Interesting shrines inside but not much room inside.

There aren't many tourist attractions in the area, and this is one of them. It's very crowded with Chinese tourists and tour groups. The area isn't very big; walking around it takes about fifteen minutes. It's a good place to take photos and kill some time.

It was very crowded, apparently a must-see for tour groups. The place inside is quite small, and there are no information boards. You should do your research beforehand. It's a half-hour or a few minutes' taxi ride from the city center. The taxi fare is more expensive than the entrance fee (VND 22,000).
An interesting place. You can get there by public transport (bus #4). I don't recommend buying a tour from a tour operator.

Nice place, unfortunately quite a lot of tourists, probably all the time. So it's hard to take a nice photo. Peace of mind is also missing :-) nevertheless I recommend a visit because it's something different than the ubiquitous pagodas. We walked from the center of NHA TRANG and it can be done in an hour at a slow pace, with a good view of the river with boats along the way.

Pretty, not very expensive ($1). But it's a very small site, you can see everything very quickly! It's best to do it in conjunction with other visits. On the other hand, there's a lovely view of the bridges and boats.
It's not an interesting place, either externally or internally... The grounds are dirty and poorly maintained. There is, however, a place to wash your hands and a toilet.

Hi everyone! The towers are located in the northern part of the city, near the Kai River. Basically, you can see them once, nothing special. Firstly, there's no architectural wonder there; yes, it's ancient, but nothing special. Secondly, there are a lot of Chinese people, they're noisy and uncultured—well, you'll understand when you get there. Thirdly, the tower complex is located next to a rather dirty river, the streets around the complex are quite dirty, and there are lots of scooters. Basically, there's a lot of traffic. It's noisy. It's very hot during the day, so you want to see everything and run to the bus or leave the place as quickly as possible. But for those who want to immerse themselves in the atmosphere of real Asia, I recommend it.
It's a cultural experience worth seeing; it's always crowded. They also sell a huge variety of local coconut souvenirs.

A visit to this place was included in the sightseeing tour, and we were given 40 minutes. More was not necessary. Just visit the three towers (wear clothing that covers your shoulders and knees, and take off your shoes before entering. If you're naked, blue robes are provided free of charge), walk around the circle, and then exit.

We went along early in the morning (8.00am) to try to miss the crowds.....FAIL!  There was a religious holiday and throngs of locals were already there to present their offerings. We didn't stay long but I think it would be more interesting if not so busy. Anyway, I took some nice pics and will attach them.  Entrance is about 20,000 VND for foreigners so not going to break the bank 😀

If you like temples I would recommend visiting here it was very sweet with a lot of old building work which was nice! We went quite early so there wasn't much happening and was very quiet which some people love! After reading reviews we expected it to be quite lively with lots of dancers but none of that was there but I can only assume it's because we went early morning! It's a beautiful place but didn't take us long to get round and see everything

We visited it on the way back from the rock garden just for the sake of it, as there really wasn't much to see there. We took a family photo with the towers in the background, framed in the form of parchment.

I cycled all the way here for sightseeing, but it was really just okay. The entrance fee wasn't expensive, but the interior is quite small and has its own unique features. Remember to change into a long robe when you go in to worship.

If you're on a sightseeing tour, they'll take you there. If you get a good guide, they'll give you a little history, tell you about the Cham people, and it'll at least be something like that. If you go on your own, you won't even know! There's not much to see there, really.
Very crowded, entrance fee 22,000 dong. Nice view of the city, we got there by bike, it was awesome. Oh yeah, the towers themselves are great.

It was entirely different to others sharing about Po Nagar Cham Towers. I found that, it in my latest trip there, was just for Chinese tourists. Noisy and crowded! I Couldn't enjoy the temple beauty. Also, it looks sacred enough for me to discover it without rude behavior!
It's definitely worth a visit, but it's best to take a free sightseeing tour. You can take some great photos.

One of the few cultural attractions worth visiting in Nha Trang. The visit is quick. Ideal when it's still too hot, while waiting for a swim at the beach. There are also small, free dance performances on site.
You can go and see them, just for the sake of it. When you read or looked at them on the Internet, you thought one thing, but in reality it turned out differently.
Just for show! Although, maybe I didn't appreciate it because we didn't go on a tour! City bus number 4 goes there! It runs until about 6:40 p.m.

It's one of the few attractions in Nha Trang that's worth a visit, and you can walk there. We walked from the Lotus Tower in about an hour, exploring the less touristy part of the city along the way. There are a huge number of tourists, and the towers themselves aren't that many, so visiting them doesn't take much time.

It was smaller than I expected, but worth a visit. In fact, there are many much larger historical sites elsewhere, so it isn't a must-see, but it was quite fascinating to find a ruin like this right in the middle of the city. Although Nha Trang is centered more on resorts and entertainment than historical sites, it is accessible enough to make a quick stop while out and about.

Honestly, I wasn't impressed 🙄 You can come here, but only if you have absolutely nothing else to do. You can see everything in 15 minutes. It looks more interesting in photos than in reality. Besides... the Chinese ruin everything... they bring them in several buses from morning until evening. It's impossible to relax, neither walk nor take a photo... 😬 They're constantly fussing, shouting, running... The 22,000 entrance fee isn't expensive, but it's unclear what it costs? I liked the Long Son Pagoda much more. And there are far fewer people there!

My wife and I visited here after we had been to Long Son Pagoda, on reflection this wasn't the best way to do this.  Although the Po Nagar Cham Towers are impressive, the sense of spirituality we experienced at Long Son Pagoda left us slightly underwhelmed at this site.  It doesn't take long to go around the site, its good to see the old pictures of how it used to be and how after its restoration it now looks. We went around the site twice and there were certain things we picked out that we missed on the first look around the site.  On the whole it is well worth a visit, but bear in mind you can do a few of these sites and attractions all in the same day, we saw the same people at the three sites we visited on this day, so that tells you something.

Cham Towers - entrance fee 22,000 VND, accessible from central Nha Trang by buses 3 and 4. The grounds are beautiful and well-maintained, with stunning views of the city. The towers themselves resemble a Cambodian temple. You can take plenty of photos, put on cool robes, go inside and take some photos, or buy a beer and sit in the shade of the trees overlooking the river.

It's a completely ordinary, run-of-the-mill attraction. You can see it if it's on a tour or if you're nearby. It's difficult to see as a standalone attraction. The buildings aren't particularly intricate, there are crowds of tourists, and there are queues. You can stop by, but I wouldn't make a special trip. Especially if you're well-traveled and haven't visited historical sites for the first time.

The site looks those pagoda in Myanmar. They have put in effort to restore the site. The volume and towers are back to initial grand. The Cham tribes will be remembered for many years on term of their culture and living.

The ruins of the ancient Cham kingdom in Vietnam are one of the few worthwhile attractions in Nha Trang. However, if you're planning a trip there or have already visited the My Son Sanctuary Cham ruins in Hoi An, you can skip this one. The architecture is somewhat similar to Angkor Wat, but you can only learn about it at My Son Sanctuary. In Nha Trang, unless you have a guide, you won't understand anything; there are no explanatory signs or brochures. The ruins were used for ancient religious ceremonies, and there are strict dress codes and shoes must be removed before entry. The interior is small and can be seen from the entrance, so there's no need to go in. If you really want to go in, there are costumes available for rent. The site isn't large; to use a Taiwanese analogy, it's roughly the same size as Chihkan Tower in Tainan, without all the explanations or signs. Climbing the hill and walking around it takes about 20 minutes at most. A few quick photos are all you need to do.
It's an interesting, beautiful place, but it's absolutely teeming with people. It's best to visit it yourself, early in the morning. It's easy to get to; we'll include it in our city tour.
You can get there by bus; it's a 10-minute ride from the city center. Be prepared for crowds of tourists.
I enjoyed going to see this temple but at the same time we walked to this location about 2 miles or so round trip. It was kind of crowded and you can walk through it pretty fast.
Easy to get to. Entrance is 22,000 VND. When entering the towers, if you are wearing revealing clothing, you must wear special kimonos (hanging to the right of the entrance).

If you have nothing particular to do in Nha Trang, it is worth a quick stop on your way to the I Resort. It is a temple that mixes Hinduism and Buddhism. You pay respects at a Buddhist altar at the entrance, and if you go up the stairs, you will find the Hindu temple. It reminded me of the atmosphere I had when I visited Yogyakarta, Indonesia.

I walked there, and aside from a river that looked nice, I'd recommend taking a car. There were tons of tour groups and many non-Vietnamese tourists. The entrance fee wasn't expensive, around 22,000 VND. Like many temples, photography wasn't allowed inside the halls; you had to wear flip-flops, long pants, and tank tops. Free robes were available to borrow. The architecture was somewhat unique, but it was just incredibly crowded.

My first excursion, I was expecting the exoticism of Southeast Asia, its uniqueness. The Cham Towers, a tour project, yes: beautiful, ancient buildings, paraphernalia aimed at tourists. But when you learn the country's history, you realize that preserving this site required an incredible amount of effort.

Well, we stopped by and had a look. It's spotlessly clean, there are lots of tourists, and you can take some great photos. To enter the temple, you need to wear a special robe. They provide them for free. However, some tourists just go in... in shorts. The complex is operational, and people pray there. The whole thing takes about 30 minutes; there's nothing else to do. Oh, and by the way, you can get there by public transport, route 4, for 7,000 VND.

We came here around lunchtime after checking out of the hotel. Fatal mistake, unfortunately we hit the peak tourist season with masses of tourists. I find all the souvenir shops right next door very out of place for something so beautiful. It's definitely worth seeing, it's easily accessible by taxi! But not at lunchtime.

To walk the entire thing only takes about 15 mins. It is pretty and offers a great view of Nha Trang but in regards to the temples, I wasn't impressed. It's only 22,000, however, so I recommend it as a casual touristy attraction
I decided to visit this place, but in the end, I couldn’t see anything against the backdrop of the Chinese, and I could see everything in 5 minutes.

Probably the top attraction in town which means it's packed with Chinese tourists. I took a cab there which costs 60k VND - meter price is 55k VND but you are better off haggling for a fixed price since most meters are rigged anyway. I walked back along the beach which took 45mins.  The place consists of a few small temples. You will need to cover your knees and shoulders to get in. Free light Grey coats are available at the side for tourists. The temples are really small so a 2 sec glance covers everything. A small museum at the back shows you the contrasting state of the site 80years ago before the restoration started. I must say the Viets have done very well restoring it despite the war.  One thing to add though - I don't see a plate explaining the significance of this place which is a shame.

We spent about an hour here, 22,000vnd each so not expensive. If you want to go into the temples which are only small and very dark you can borrow a cover up piece of clothing at no charge. It was very busy when we went at around 1pm a lot of tourist buses were there, you can catch a bus number 4 from Naha trang but we got a taxi on a meter which was 60000, there are places across the road to get food and drink and also souvenir shops both outside the temple and inside.

We were there during the day, so we found ourselves surrounded by endless groups of tourists. The place itself is beautiful and worth a visit, as it also offers a beautiful view of the city. If you visit during the day, be patient and tolerant, as the Chinese are everywhere, and all you can hear is their conversations; it's a constant bustle.

These structures are very interesting to see, but unfortunately one is unable to actually enjoy and see much of the structures as Chinese people flood these attractions and are very inconsiderate towards other tourists. You can't even take proper pictures as they stand in your view taking selfies with their selfie sticks the whole time. And another thing that I found most disgusting is they spit everywhere.
A very sleek tourist spot. As a park, it lacks the atmosphere of antiquity and sacredness, like the Cham Towers in Phan Rang.

The entrance fee is 22,000 VND. You can see everything in about 20 minutes. There's not much information about the attractions; you just know they're Hindu artifacts from 1000 years ago. It's quite boring. But Nha Trang really doesn't have many famous historical sites.
We were here prior to cruise along the Cai River, it was located in a busy street and the Temple itself was not impressive.
I wasn't impressed with this attraction, I have seen more impressive temples. It was quite busy with tourist and the ticket cost 22000D.

I looked. The ride takes 20 minutes. Entry is 22,000 VNG. It's best to go in the morning when there are fewer tourists. I didn't want to go, but I had to visit for my report. Besides the towers, there's a beautiful view of the city.
There's nothing to see there, well, maybe a view of the city ;-) but it's not worth it. It's not worth a special trip there.
It's like an ancient temple, entrance fee is cheap, building is special, located near city, and can get here by bus. You would expect quite a lot of tourists here.

I'd say it's nothing special. Entrance to the grounds is 22,000 dong per person, and it's more expensive with a guided tour. The views of the city are picturesque, and the grounds are lush with greenery, trees, and plants, but the place is overrun by Chinese people; they're everywhere.

We didn't intend on going, but after aimlessly walking around Nha Trang we popped our heads in for a sticky beak. If you want to see plenty of tourists attempting to take candid photos of themselves, in front of water fountains, or resting against a tree or gently caressing a fern, then this is the place for you my friend! The temple itself is quite interesting, not much in the way of English descriptions, but from an architectural standpoint, it was impressive.  Not a must do in Nha Trang (still trying to work out what that is) but if you've got time to kill, like to laugh at the oddities of tourists and have a cheeky 22k burning a hole in your pocket then add this baby to your list.

If you're interested in this culture, it's not bad; otherwise, it's just something novel to see. The entrance fee is 22,000 Vietnamese Dong. It's good for taking photos and enjoying the city views. There are many tour groups, especially Chinese tourists. Please be civilized and don't overdo it with your photos!

The Po Nagar Temple, also known as the Cham Towers, is, as I understand it, Nha Trang's most important landmark. There's an entrance fee, and visitors are required to cover their knees and shoulders. It's located near the city center, so you can take a bus or visit the temple yourself.
You can visit it on the way to the beach, but I wasn't impressed. There's a lot to do, and after Angkor Wat, it lacks scale.

It's not worth a special trip; you can just stop by and see it. Admission is inexpensive. During our stay, it rained and the sea was rough, so we saw everything we could. If the sun had been shining and there had been no waves, the Ponagar Towers would have been a waste of time.

Unfortunately, the restoration of historical sites with new materials is not beneficial to them. The new bricks look very poor. After Cambodia, no towers or temples in Asia can impress. And after the Royal Palace in Bangkok, the architecture of Vietnam is unforgettable. In short, if you've been to Thailand and Cambodia, don't waste your time visiting temples and other things in Vietnam. Vietnam has much more interesting things.
We visited on a cloudy day. We took some photos. Entrance tickets are 20,000 dong. People are eating and selling inside. That's all.

Having been to Angkor, I found this to be far inferior. For those who don't want to make a special trip to Angkor, it's a quick look at the Angkor style and quite okay. There are performances throughout, but overall it's decent.

We only ride our scooter to the towers so for us it was only a visit to view the historic architecture of the temples. I'm sure if you took a guide they would be able to explain each temple and give u in depth explanations about each of them. Nice for a photo opportunity overlooking the water of gardens. Only costed us 5 dollars all up. Worth a visit to view the craftsmanship of the Hindu temples.
The pyramids are beautiful, but nothing spectacular. We walked there from the hotel on the beach. It's about 5 km. If you have time, go there; otherwise, you won't miss anything.

It's an 8th century heritage Hindu temple. Peace and calm with small souvenirs shops. The temple as well the view of city is photogenic. The architecture seemed similar to angkhor temple @cambodia. They also have small prayer cum dance performance evening everyday.
There were a lot of tourists that day but to me it's an overhyped place. It's actually is a boring place with only a few building to see.
Overall, it's not bad. You can get to the Cham Towers by bus No. 4 for 7 dong, and the entrance fee is 20 dong. There's not much to see, but it's worth a visit for general education.

If you enjoy visiting historical buildings or have religious reasons, you might consider a visit, but the area is small, and fifteen minutes is enough to see everything. Afterwards, you can eat at a nearby seafood stall, but the hygiene felt questionable and the seating was uncomfortable, so we ended up taking a bus back to the tourist area to eat.
Nice temple but still needs better care, especially for the facilities, toilet nearby so tourists may use.

Good place for a visit for the history. Need to wear clothes to cover shoulders if you want to go into the altar. They are robes you can borrow if needed. Lots of walking and climbing. Great view the city.
The towers with its temples inside were quite nice to see, but to be honest, nothing really special.

Lots of steps in the heat but its an interesting place especially if you take time to research the history. My biggest tip for you is to be sure to wear socks if you're going to go inside the temple as shoes are not allowed. Also, you must don a grey robe which is available on the side entrance.
I wasn't particularly impressed by this landmark, considering that almost everything there has been rebuilt and restored. It's worth a visit for the sake of it.
There isn't much to see—it is somewhat worth visiting if you know the history—the Champa Kingdom, the Vietnam War, and the Angkor Wat ruins.

The grounds are beautiful, the towers are not impressive from the inside, the dome of the middle tower has been horribly renovated; there are tons of Chinese; 22,000 dong, it's worth a one-time visit.

We were there on a free tour from Asia Travel. The legend is quite interesting, you can catch some nice views of the city panorama, and you can make a wish according to the legend. Otherwise, everything is pretty standard and unremarkable. It's simply one of the must-see places in Nha Trang.

After being in Bagan and Angkor Wat, we wanted to see the temple that everyone were talking about. There is nothing special, we payed about 22000 VND entrance fee. The view from the top is nice, you see parts of the town and also some view of the sea. This is ok not more..
Nha Trang has limited offer of historic relics and this is one. It's a fair deal as a quick checkpoint and a nice photo taking background, considering the entrance fee is just less than 1USD.

We went on a sightseeing tour the other day. It's worth going once for general knowledge. The guide started the tour on the bus. Recently, a Russian guide got into a fight with a Chinese guide. Unfortunately, I don't know who beat whom, but I think ours won. After that fight, the guide isn't allowed to lead tours, so we go and see for ourselves. There are steep stairs to climb, but not high—maybe three floors or less. You can take photos on the grounds, and they also let you into the temple, but I didn't see anything interesting there. I went into the temple and almost died. The heat, the smoke, and the heat were terrifying—it was impossible to breathe. They gave me some kind of robe to cover my shoulders and knees, but I can't go into the temple dressed like that. Inside the temple, there's a two-by-two room and it's crowded... My wife liked it, but I didn't get the joke. Well, that's about it.

We went on a sightseeing tour with Anex Tour. It was crowded with Chinese people, the history of the towers was just 5 minutes long, it was nice, modest, unclear, far away, and smelled of fish and durian nearby. It was a one-time trip.
We visited this place as part of a sightseeing tour! It's good for general education, but nothing special. You can skip it.

The towers are located in a beautiful central spot. Easy to reach. It is a beautiful site; some towers and parts have been renovated. The towers look very beautiful. They are old and have seen a lot. Some guests indicate that they are boring inside, but do not forget that it is not a tourist attraction. It is an old temple. A religious site. Unfortunately, for many people it is a tourist attraction, which means it can be very crowded and not everyone knows how to respect the place or each other. Therefore, go early in the morning or in bad weather, as it might be less crowded then. Do check if it is open, and remember to wear covering clothing.
If you want to explore Vietnamese cultural beauty, lets check this place such as the original Champa ethnical people.

We took a free tour from Anex, and the last stop was these towers. They're certainly accessible by public transport, but we took the free tour. We started by visiting various factories (crocodile, coffee, jewelry store), and then finally the towers. We arrived after the Chinese had already arrived, and they filled the entire area around the towers. We didn't see much, but it was interesting. These 12th-century buildings are worth a visit.

Po nagar Cham towers is reachable from the nha trang city limits , we visited as a couple on a rainy day ( entrance was 22 k vnd / person ) . Good place for its architecture yet could have been maintained well and it was crowded with tourists .An ok place to visit if you admire architecture and feel connected with a divine place .
We took a free Anex tour. It was a great way to kill time during the rainy season. It was beautiful and interesting, and only lasted half an hour.
It's worth a visit if you've run out of places to go. The area is small and you can walk around it in 15 minutes. Take bus number 4 from the city center. Once you see the towers, get off.

We visited this temple on a sightseeing tour. It's a very interesting place, with a touch of history. The towers are constantly being restored, so some of them look like new. We were given a tour by our tour operator, but we didn't like the story. But the photos are very beautiful and mysterious.
A nice place... have a good view! Nice coffee with milk!! 22k to get in and plus 5 to park the bike!
Very interesting location, but totally overcrowded. It is not really necessary to book an expensive tour. Depending where you stay, you can walk or take a taxi or scooter. Entrance fee is about 1US $

If you have spare time you can visit this place. Although the architecture of the place and the ancient history is attractive but the place is very crowded and you hardly get a place to stand and admire the beauty. We did not had a good experience.

In the tour guide photos, PNCT looks very interesting, but in reality it was a bit of a snooze. Luckily, this sight is only a mile away from my hotel and not much of an investment. At 22,000 VND I'd say it was worth it to look at the old structure which has been reconstructed, but only if you have time. There are plenty of souvenir shops on the premises.

This is Nha Trang's most notable landmark. You can get there on your own, as we did; entrance costs around 7,000 dong, and it's definitely worth a visit. But if you've been to India or Indonesia, you won't be impressed.

After eating most of our way through Vietnam, we were interested in taking in some local ancient history and learn about their Cham ancestors.  The towers sit on a small hill and date back to 7th C. Most of the complex has been restored. In terms of size, it is relatively small area compared to other temples of that time (Angkor). But it's still worth a quick look if you wanted to take a break from Nha Trang beach to see the carvings, get a hilltop view and see some ancient artifacts.  Note: there are a number of stairs to reach the top level.
The place is too small to be majestic. Please wear full coverage cloths in order to gain entry into the tower to pray. If not just stay outside the towers and take pictures.

It doesn't have any particular charm, but it holds great significance in Vietnamese history. You can make a wish for the birth of a child in one of the towers. If you're visiting Nhi Trang, it's a must-see!
Well, if you visited Cambodia, no reason to come to Po Nagar Cham at all. Just very small temple. Very small! Nothing special Entrance is not free.
An interesting attraction for history buffs. I liked the option of going inside the towers. I didn't really like the Vietnamese dancers in the courtyard performing the "two claps, three claps" dance.

A beautiful temple complex, worth a 5-minute visit! There's no point in wasting time there! Not impressive at all! Better to go to Dalat! Unless you're just going for a sightseeing tour, maybe in bad weather, and you could waste time there! In short! It's beautiful, but not worth it!!
Interesting building but doesn't have information in our language then Vietnam. The garden is very quiet.
The place is wonderful and memorable, you can visit it on your own or with a tour, even my 12-year-old daughter liked it.
The complex is small. It might be a spiritual place for the Vietnamese, but not for me. Fifteen minutes is enough.

I wouldn't come to the city specifically for the temples, but if you do, it would be a mistake to miss it. Beautiful temples, symbolic entrance as a heritage site, and the place is well-kept and pleasant.
Get a little history and culture minutes from the city

Well, considering the history of southern Vietnam and Cambodia it won't surprise some people to see almost identical carvings to those found in Cambodia itself here at the Po Nagar Cham Towers. I went there on the back of a motorbike that my girlfriend had hired, otherwise I would never have known of their existence. Some of the stonework has been heavily weathered probably by coastal storms over hundreds of years unlike some of the temples in Cambodia but with a keen eye one can see what used to be elephant monuments which my girlfriend was completely unaware of despite her visiting this spot over the years many times. The towers are worth finding and they give a good elevated view of Nha Trang from outside of the city.
It's a small temple as you can see a bit more in the north around Da Nang and/or Cambodia and Laos. It's more a tourist trap, but the view on Nha Trang is also nice from the highest point.
We visited the towers on our own, not as part of a tour. We walked around and looked around; it was nice, but nothing special. Perhaps it was because we're not big fans of temples and pagodas.
There's not much to see. Just standing next to the towers, just for show. Good thing we brought a drone!!))
