Parking is prohibited all day around the ruins, so please note that taxis will not wait for you. The entrance fee is 22,000 VND, and if the timing is right, you can see a traditional dance (by the Champa people) inside the ruins.

If you've been to Angkor Wat, you might be disappointed. It's relatively small, just three towers, the entrance fee is low, it's easily accessible by crossing a bridge, and it's close to the mud baths. Locals don't allow outsiders to provide guided tours, probably to protect local guides. This attraction is considered a Nha Trang specialty.

These ancient towers are quite typical for Asia. But since they're located within the city limits, and on a hill, they're a must-see. From the top, there's a nice view of the city, spoiled by the hordes of Chinese people scurrying back and forth, ruining photos.
The atmosphere is reminiscent of Angkor Wat in Cambodia. Inside, people are constantly coming and going, praying earnestly.
Ok to view if you read about it before hand very limited signs in English and too few english speaking guides

The entrance fee was 220,000 Vietnamese Dong, which wasn't very expensive. However, it was the first tourist attraction in Vietnam that charged an entrance fee. The place wasn't very big, and you could walk through it in just a few minutes. Since I didn't know much about Cham culture, I felt a bit disoriented while walking around, so I just took some photos and left.

This red brick building requires an entrance fee and is a temple where local fishermen worship their goddess. Vietnam itself doesn't have many tourist attractions, so it's just a casual visit. It's not very big, but you can take some photos.
The place is pretty mediocre compared to what you can see in Asia. But it's a beach town, so it's worth a visit for some downtime. If you're lucky, you might even see folk dances.

We were doing a full day tour of varrious attractions around Nha trang and we asked if we could see a temple. We were taken here, it was really quick to get to from our hotel by Nha Trang beach so not much effort to get to.  The temple is very nice and there were some traditional Vietnamese dancers which was a nice extra attraction. It was very busy when we visited though.  Women have to be covered to go into the temple but there are free cover ups available there.

If you're looking for a bit of culture and religion in Nha Trang, check it out. But for us, having seen lots of pagodas and temples in China/Japan, this was pretty tiny, smelt very dank and nothing too special. View of Nha Trang was nice though.

The towers are on a small hill overlooking the river / bay: the trees offer a welcome relief from the hot weather. As far as the towers are concerned, if you have seen Borobudur, they probably don't get you terribly excited. At any rate, worth a visit.
There's nothing special to see there, and if it's a tour, it's a far-fetched one... but you can go... on the plus side: there's a market nearby where they sell cheap fruit...
I went with the fam two years ago. Never heard of this place. Took a little walk around to enjoy the scenery.

A wonderful opportunity not only to lounge on the beach but also to experience a bit of Vietnamese culture and history. The Cham Towers are just 10 minutes from the hotel. It doesn't take long to explore, and then you can dine at one of the many seafood restaurants.

A beautiful view of the city opens from the territory of these towers. Almost all the church towers are remake, reconstructed. Ponagar Towers is a place of pilgrimage. Let's respect the culture of another country - comply with the dress code. Shoulders should be covered, knees too. Men are allowed to enter in shorts and a T-shirt. Entrance fee in to the Tower is 20,000 dong per person, parking space for one motorbike - 5,000 dong. A beautiful panorama of the city opens from the territory of these towers. Almost all the tower temples are remake, reconstructed. This is a place of pilgrimage for the Cham. Let's respect the culture of another country - observe the dress code. Shoulders should be covered, knees too. Men are allowed to enter in shorts and a T-shirt. Entrance to the towers is 20,000 dong, parking for one motorbike space - 5,000 dong.
We visited during a free sightseeing tour of Nha Trang. Beautiful and mysterious. Inexpensive souvenirs!

Not really impressive but since you don't pay so much and since the temple is not far away from the city Centre, they are with a visit. They are well preserved and quite old - the oldest temple that still has the original bricks dates from the XII century.

The place worth a visit, but don't expect to much if you've been to other similar temples: it's not even close to the temples of Angkor, there are too many tourists and the place does not feel holy at all. However the view from the top of the hill is quite nice, and there are no other cultural points of interest around, so, if you have some time, pay this place a visit.  The best time to come is in the evening, I think .
We visited them as part of a city tour. The towers are just towers, nothing else to see. The guide saved us from boredom – thanks for that.

This is the only building in Nha Trang that has a real sense of history. I think the entrance fee was around 200 yen. Tour buses were constantly arriving. There are no explanatory signs or plaques, so I recommend reading a guidebook beforehand. If you're going independently and not on a tour, you can take a taxi, and there are plenty of taxis parked around, so you can easily catch one on your way back. Just tell them your hotel name or show them your hotel key card and they'll know where you're going right away.

Yes, I understand it's a historical site and all that, but for us personally, the Cham towers didn't evoke any surprise or excitement. Of course, one can indulge one's imagination and imagine how, centuries ago, on a full moon, priests with torches walked among these columns, ascending the steps to the towers. But it's all pretty ordinary... People probably come here to take photos; there's no point in going inside the towers because the smell is so strong.

These are very old, recently restored temples. In my opinion, they're more of an Indian tradition. They're active shrines, so there's a strict dress code for women. Luckily, if you're wearing shorts and a tank top, you'll be given a special mouse-colored robe and can enter without any problem. One of the corners of the towers offers a pleasant view of the sea, fishing boats, and the bridge, making it perfect for some picturesque photos. They also sell interesting souvenirs (funny stuffed animals, ceramics), which I haven't seen anywhere else in Nha Trang.

It looks reasonably impressive from a distance, when you're up close you realise most is rebuilt, inside is very basic. But at 22k vnd it doesn't cost much. Worth viewing if you're going to the nearby spa
It was interesting to see but nothing more. If you want to go in and look inside, you have to put on a special costume that they provide for free.

We went on a sightseeing tour of Nha Trang and visited the Cham Towers. It would have been interesting to learn more about their history, but the guides gave us very little information, and we had little time to explore them. My advice: do your own research online beforehand.

It's a good place to kill time, but there's an entrance fee, about 100 yen. There's not much to see, so it only takes a short time, but it might be a hassle to go out of your way to visit. There were lots of tour buses and taxis there.

In such a beautiful beach city, this is just an activity you ca take if you want to discovery around. Nothing special, a lots of tour groups make the place become not as traditional as it supposed to be. Too noisy and crowed

There are so many Chinese! It's impossible to see anything properly, they're in huge crowds, all with selfie sticks! To enter the temple, you have to put on special robes that are hung for everyone to wear. Imagine how many sweaty people put them on, and you won't want to go!

The Po Nagar temple complex is nice and well maintained, it is however fairly small and, certainly when we were there, very crowded. If you are without a guide read-up on the background first as signs and what I assume to be info panels are in Vietnamese only.
Beautiful, however the Vietnamese who come to pray at this temple are detestable! I still recommend it though.

We were there at 8:30 in the morning. It was very crowded. Entry was 22,000 dong. It was interesting to see, but the knowledge that everything had been restored was a bit of a hindrance. There's a small museum, which is free. It offers a beautiful view of the city. A must-see; it takes no more than 30 minutes.

We arrived here around late afternoon. It is incredibly crowded, and all you see are Chinese people pushing you aside to take 'selfies'. They were beautiful towers, but otherwise not very special, unfortunately. You can also see the towers from the bridge in Nha Trang. Avoid going there when it is crowded, because it doesn't get any more enjoyable...
A beautiful building from the outside, but not very special inside. It was also very busy during the period we were there, in August.

It didn't interest us much, perhaps because neither Buddhism nor Hinduism appeals to us. However, the observation deck offers a magnificent view of the city and the river. Overall, like all religious buildings, it's more intended for the faithful than the curious.

The Cham ruins aren't very big, and honestly, without a guide beforehand, there's not much to see. Local regulations prohibit foreign guides from providing explanations, and local Vietnamese guides don't offer explanations either. It's quite difficult to understand anything, speechless! For visiting the Buddhist temples, one must dress solemnly, and the management provides free local clothing for tourists, which is commendable.
So excited to come here at first. But disappointed of my expectations when I was inside. Just almost were temples!

I'm not buddhist or hindu, but I like this place anyway. Not so good to understand all the things that people made, but I understand that they prayed for strength and gave their thanks for the help.....

Those who have been to Angkor Wat will likely be disappointed here, but it is still a historically valuable site in Vietnam, so it's worth a visit. It's accessible via a main road and a bridge, so transportation is fairly convenient.

This place was filled with hordes of tourists. It was a very small complex. Entrance fee is 22,000 dong. It has a nice view of the city from the top, and the buildings are nice but it's hot and crowded. Don't wear shorts if you want to enter the buildings.

Nothing spectacular here. Its nice to visit something cultural amidst such a beachy town. The architecture is beautiful and the views are nice. Downside is the troves of other tourists arriving by the bus load. Makes it a little difficult to take in your surroundings.
If you have been to angkowat or similar size temple then i would suggest you can give this place a miss. Quite a small temple and nothing spectacular to be honest.

Since I was traveling alone, I visited using a motorbike. If you have plenty of time for your trip, it is a place worth visiting at least once. The temple is small, but the view from the top overlooking downtown Nha Trang is nice. I do not recommend it when the weather is cloudy or raining. The entrance fee is 22,000 VND, which is about $1, so I think it is reasonable.

Given the lack of historical sites in Nha Trang, the Cham Towers are certainly worth a visit, as they are, arguably, the only example of ancient Vietnamese architecture. We took a taxi near the hotel and covered almost all of Nha Trang's interesting sites in 3.5-4 hours. It's a small place, just a couple of large brick buildings and several smaller ones surrounding them. There are certainly signs of restoration, but, again, it's worth seeing what religious sites looked like in Vietnam 150-200 years ago. A visit probably takes 20-30 minutes. By the way, the taxi cost about 400 rubles (our local currency), and that was for 3.5 hours. So, it's practically free.

I'm sharing my experience, accompanied by photos I personally took, about my disappointment at the Thap Ba Tower, but TripAdvisor wouldn't let me post it, saying it was invalid, so I'll write it again! To be honest, I visited the Thap Ba Tower when I was a child and found it mysterious, ancient, and captivating. But now, arriving at the entrance, I'm overwhelmed by the sheer number of cars, buses, and tourist vehicles, and the overly modern buildings and surroundings that detract from my appreciation of the ancient beauty of the tower. Inside, there are many added structures to serve tourism, as well as shops and stalls, which only makes the Thap Ba Tower look more chaotic. In short, I just strolled around, took a few pictures, and left without leaving much of an impression.

We took this tour independently on bus number 4. We paid an entrance fee, I think it was 20,000 rubles per person. You can walk around the grounds with bare shoulders and knees, but if you want to go inside the towers, you'll need to cover up. You can borrow free robes inside the towers for this purpose.
Had a look at the towers, OK to visit but I wouldn't say it's a must see. Very crowded, and we went early, so photo taking was hard. Highlight was watching the monkeys swing through the trees.

If you've seen temples in any other Asian country, it's not worth wasting your time. In fact, everything is tiny, and there are so many Chinese people there that it's difficult to even take a photo. There are no other attractions nearby.
Entrance fee priced at 22k VND and there are more tourist retail area then the ancient relics to see... good height to view northen nha trang

It's definitely worth a visit – it's fascinating to see the ancient buildings from the outside and to explore the interior. A guide would be best, as there's no information in Russian about the place, so you can just wander around and explore. The grounds are beautiful, with views of the city and river.

Impressive old stone temples. Good to see something other than the usual beaches, bars and cafes. It was very busy as we went mid morning. Hoards of tourists clutching cameras, hard to get photos without a photo bomb. We didn't stick around a long time because of the heat and crowds. There are stalls selling drinks and snacks, we didn't bother. Souviners on sale too. Interesting views of the fishing boats and another side of Nha trang. It's not a must see activity but it was a nice change .

A popular tourist spot, but to get photos without other tourists, you need to be prepared for the right time – it's too sunny and crowded before 11 am, so I recommend going in the afternoon. Tickets are very cheap, and the cafe in the Metrang across the street is good; you can rest and have a drink there to cool off after you've finished exploring.

I visited alone about two months ago and came back with my family. It looks like the sculptures have been restored, but I was a bit disappointed because they used colors that didn't match at all and perhaps rocks made of a different material.
This place has an original architecture where you can find something about spirit from ancient human. Staffs are friendly, but I smelt from dead fishes of people live around here. It very terrible!

The complex is very small, 30-40 minutes is enough to see everything. They try to make up for it with performances by "musicians" and terrible-sounding drums. You can get there by bus. Entrance is 22,000 dong.

It has that same feel to it. If you haven't been to Angkor, you should definitely experience it. If you have been to Angkor, it doesn't seem very interesting to you, and you can skip this attraction altogether.

We visited the Cham towers during a free sightseeing tour with Anex Tour. It was interesting to hear about the Vietnamese gods. Interesting! At each tower, you can ask for something good, like prosperity and health, and another tower has symbols of male and female. The guide said that couples and women usually ask these symbols for children, some for a boy, some for a girl.
A classic spot for sightseeing tours. You can even get there by bus. Join any group to listen and watch.

On my second visit to the Po Nagar Cham Towers, I noticed that the area around the main gate hadn't changed much, and the ground wasn't clean. Besides being a beach destination, Po Nagar is also a tourist spot, so I expect there will be changes to create a lasting impression on visitors, encouraging them to return or recommend the site to their friends. For example: organizing interactive performances, giving visitors stamps as proof of their visit, and creating a photo booth to certify their visit to the Cham Towers (similar to the Angkor temples).

The towers weren't very far from the hotel, so we visited them. It was modest, especially after visiting Angkor Wat; it didn't leave much of an impression. If you're looking for something authentic, go ahead.

Since Nha Trang doesn't have much variety in terms of attractions, this place is worth a visit. Unfortunately, there wasn't a wow factor. Entry is 20,000 dong, which is about 70 rubles in our money; we took a taxi. We didn't go inside the tower, and frankly, we didn't bother taking off our shoes; it was just too dirty. We saw a tourist room, and the girls danced beautifully. There's a small museum with a lovely view of the city. The whole thing takes no more than 40 minutes.
It's unbelievable that people hundreds of years ago were able to build structures like these. They're so beautiful.

Located 2 kilometers north of Nha Trang, on the left bank of the Song Cai River, the Po Nagar Cham Towers have an architectural style somewhat reminiscent of Angkor Wat, but are much smaller in scale and less intricately carved. If you've already visited Angkor Wat, a quick visit here is sufficient; it's a very small place. Po Nagar is a transliteration, also sometimes translated as the Temple of the Goddess Thien Y. Due to its relatively high elevation, climbing to the top offers panoramic views of Nha Trang's harbor.

During the visit, many sheds were erected around the ancient site, making it difficult to take good photos. Shoes must be removed before entering; please wear shoes that are easy to put on and take off. There is a small interpretive room inside with precious photos of the site before restoration—don't miss it!

The towers are just towers. You can visit them just for show. Yes, overall, it's beautiful. But the complex is small. I wandered around and left. The entrance is inexpensive. Lots of tourists. It's a place for the obligatory tourist photos, so you're unlikely to gasp.

When we went, it was very crowded, making it difficult to take photos. If you want to go inside, there are free robes available to borrow outside! It's a good place if you just want to walk around and look at the sights; the view from there is beautiful, but the area isn't very big, so you can't stay for long!

A place with its own unique, rustic charm. The entrance fee is 22,000 VND per person. Among the ruins in Nha Trang, this is the one that is worth the money. Actually, I liked the view of downtown Nha Trang from here more than the pagoda itself, as it had a charming, quaint atmosphere.
We were there on the weekend and it was very crowded. Overall, it's fine if you get there without people. You can go around to the right and climb up for free :)

We stopped by this architectural monument as part of a sightseeing tour. The visit would be more appealing to unmarried women and girls. It's sad to see the sour expressions of the men accompanying us during the "I Want to Get Married" ceremony at one of the temple's statues and the kissing of stones to fulfill non-material desires. The temple is still active and partially restored. If you've been to Thailand, there's nothing to be surprised or impressed by.

There is an admission fee. It is a very small historic site that takes only about 20 minutes to look around. It takes about 10 to 15 minutes by taxi from Nha Trang Beach, and it is easy to get there by motorbike. Visiting is prohibited during the midday heat. I recommend visiting before 10 AM or after 4 PM. Since you are traveling far from home, it seems like a local attraction worth seeing at least once.

One of the attractions in the city, but honestly, having lived in Sichuan and seen plenty of temples, it felt pretty average. There were some shops nearby selling local clothing, which was alright. I bought a dress for 50,000 Vietnamese dong, it was awesome!

It's about a 10-minute walk from the city center. It was incredibly crowded with Vietnamese and Chinese people. It's on a hill and offers a view of the city, but a huge hotel is under construction at just the right angle, obscuring the beautiful beach.

Compared to Angkor Wat, this place is indeed too small to linger in. However, it's precisely this small town that offers a glimpse into the everyday life of Nha Trang by the sea: the bustling fishing boats along the shore, the noise of motorcycles on the streets, and the local Russians and residents near the tower. There aren't any great sunset viewing platforms, so it's all about finding unexpected surprises and moments of tranquility. The statue of Nha Trang isn't perfectly preserved, and much of the restoration is rough. I recommend visiting to get a panoramic view of the town and the sunset.
The architecture of the Cham towers follows the Cham style, like most other Cham towers. Anyone visiting this place can conveniently stop by for a mud bath as well. Very convenient.
One of the remaining Cham structures, with beautiful architecture; however, the restored areas are not very impressive.
Nice piece of architecture, worth visiting in Nha Trang. What was interesting is that you could see national dances on the territiry of the temple.

After visiting Hampi in India, these towers don't leave any impression. I took bus 4. A girl on the beach handed out a map—thanks to her. There's a rock garden not far from the towers. I've attached a photo. I liked the rock garden better.

It's a small temple. If you were in Angkor Wat it won't be interesting for you. It's better to visit it after 4 PM. Locals going there and you can watch out how they praying. It's gonna be interesting if you got interest in religions. Entrance fee 22,000 VND, it's around $1. However beware of the thief's around the temple.

What can you say about the cities that emerged with the advent of tourism? They lack historical and cultural treasures, lacking a legacy of generations. In this meager landscape, the Cham Towers occupy a proud first and only place. Come, see, enjoy, and leave in about 15 minutes—just enough time to explore the complex! And yes, it's best to be there in the evening—at least you can admire the harbor at sunset.

First, it's incomprehensible without knowledge of the history and culture. Second, the site is too small; you'll be out in ten minutes because you won't understand much. Third, due to its age, some areas were severely damaged and subsequently renovated, affecting the overall experience. It's worth a visit if you have the time.

This temple is quite small, very crowded, and not as well preserved as many other temples in SE Asia, but it is a favorite place for locals to worship, which is very interesting to see and is not so prevalent in other places tourists go. And we were lucky enough to enjoy local musicians and dancers - and lots of cute kids with their families. Just be prepared to climb up the hill and to take off your shoes to go inside any shrine. Don't miss the view of town and the fishing fleet!
An amazing structure at such a height. Steps that people used to have to climb to atone for their sins...

Kind of a beautiful place but highly overrated. I can't understand why this is the number #1 on Tripadvisor. It wasn't THAT good, really. IF and ONLY IF you have the time you should go here, do not have it as priority one.
You can see it; admission costs a dollar. Beautiful photos are taken within the complex. We didn't find a Russian-speaking guide. We read the information online.

I might have mentioned this attraction because Nha Trang is so boring. I've never been particularly interested in historical sites, but the view of the river, fishing boats, bridges, and traffic from the top was quite beautiful, which was an unexpected bonus.
Very nice and inexpensive, although you have to pay again to see the chapels inside. You should arrive early or it will be too crowded.

Since Vietnam isn't as well-stocked with attractions as Thailand or China, for example, you can visit this place. The area is small, so you won't get tired of walking around this complex. It's a good choice for a change of pace in touristy Nha Trang.
It's a quick cab ride there from the center and a 15-20 minute photo opportunity. Quite a few tourists in a rather small area, but it's worth it.

It's not difficult to get there; you can take bus number 4. However, there's not much to see; it's similar to Ayutthaya mixed with Preah Vihear in Thailand. There are a lot of Chinese tour groups. It's near some cheap seafood restaurants.

If you want to do something else in Nha Trang than be on the beach, a trip to this temple is OK. It is easy to get to and you can walk there from the central hotels. The temple is a nice example of Khmer buildings, but there is not much else. There are lots of people who mostly seem to be taking selfies, and that is basically it. However, if you walk from the temple and down towards the train station (away from the hotel area) you can enjoy local markets and areas that have a more authentic Vietnamese feel. We enjoyed that more.

You can visit the Cham Towers without guides or tour companies; the entrance fee is a mere pittance. If you live in the south, below the bridge, you'll need to take a bus or taxi across the bridge. The towers will be on the left, and on the right is Fish Street. I recommend eating there; seafood is the cheapest here. There's no service, but it's very cheap. And remember: when buying seafood, you need to bargain.

The Vietnamese have tried to restore the towers to their original appearance, which, in my opinion, has greatly spoiled the view. There are several towers where locals still pray. It's best to arrive before a tour group if you're traveling independently (there's a bus from the center). The price is appropriate for the attraction. To make their stay more enjoyable, tourists are treated to dance and music performances. There are souvenir shops, but everything is overpriced. A visit to the towers takes 20 minutes.

The cham towers are really old, yet when you go there you cannot expect to receive much information about them. Our guide book said it was a buddhist place, yet the deities were hindu.  There are some gift shops and a place to buy drinks, a beautiful park and a small house containing pictures of the towers prior to their restauration.  You should wear pants to this location as you are not allowed to wear shorts, skirts and other revealing clothing in the temple towers.

We took bus #4 from the city center. We saw the landmark (without much enthusiasm). It had a beautiful view of the city from above. If you're vacationing in Nha Trang, it's an added attraction, but otherwise...we wouldn't make a special trip. There are many temples like this or similar in Southeast Asia, and there are more beautiful places. It's convenient that it's nearby and inexpensive.
A smallish complex worth the small admission charge. Don't expect Angkor Wat and go by taxi as you will see it all in 10 minutes.

It's not an exceptional site, but you can take some nice photos there... Just go before 9:30 or 10:00 AM, after that busloads of Asian tourists arrive and you won't be able to get a single square centimeter of stone in a photo without a Korean taking a selfie...

Nice and good rebuilt tempel complex with a lot of shops around and a play back singer. A group of young ladies in dresses which should fit to the religious surrounding, were dancing to modern music, a bit strange.
The towers are just towers, nothing special. We came and looked at them, took photos. As in all Asian countries, funerary towers
Nothing to add, but for €1 you can take some beautiful photos of a place steeped in history. Note: no shorts allowed in the towers.

We came here simply to pass the time in the overcast weather. After previously seeing pagodas in Burma or Thailand, these towers held no interest. After taking a couple of photos, which took no more than 10 minutes, we moved on.

I finished browsing in ten minutes! Personally, I think it's worth a look since you're already there; it's especially beautiful when the lights come on at night! I recommend the seafood and rice noodle stalls across the street!

Loads of tourist buses and a bit too crowded. Maybe go early in the morning. We rented a motorbike and getting there was easy enough if you can manage the traffic. Would've loved more information panels so better off hiring a guide.

Whilst you're in Nha Trang it is definitely worth visiting. We were situated about 3 miles from the site and decided to walk along the coast to get there. If that's an option for you I'd definitely recommend walking as it's a good way to turn a short tourist visit in to a longer experience. The towers themselves are worth seeing, a nice different and cultural experience from the usual beach experience of Nha Trang. Don't expect too much but considering the cheap entry fee and the simple nature of the journey there I'd advice going there.
You must visit the impressive Cham towers of Po Nagar. Cham is a religious style with the Hindu god Shiva at its center. We were lucky enough to see dancing.
We walked here from the center of Nha Trang. It's certainly a colorful place, but nothing special, compared to Ankor in Cambodia, it's a mediocre place.

This a religious sites, a hindu tample. Some people come to this place, have a visit inside the temple and got them selves blessed by the monk. You need to dress properly to visit the temple inside, in means no shorts or tank, or they will lend you a special dress to wear. This place is small, you will be able to cover everything in this place for few minutes. No guide unless you are willing to pay for 50,000 VND (may equal to 5$)  Nice for photos or just spend the day.
Beautiful, ancient, built by undoubtedly great masters, but for those who have been to Cambodia and seen Angkor Wat it will not be interesting.

It's a temple/some ruins. If you've never been to champa ruins before it's good to go, but super busy and you might get bothered by people trying to sell you postcards. They are REALLY aggressive.  The view from the top is actually the best part.
We visited independently. Everything was as usual. We liked the panorama from the top platform. We were surprised by the entrance fee. It's quite a distance away. You can get there by taxi or bus.

Small Cham site, still in use as a religious place for the local population. If you have never seen Cham towers, this is a nice thing to do in Nha Trang. If you have seen Cham towers before, for example on the border of Thailand and Laos, then this is a bit of a pale imitation.

Interesting and informative. But it mostly depends on the guide. If they're not amorphous, it'll be interesting. If they're like us... you just walk around, looking, and weaving through the numerous Chinese.
You don't have to waste time and go there... I'm not even talking about the fact that we went there on bikes, and someone else will still have to pay for the excursion...
A pretty spot with its little towers overlooking the seafront and the river that flows into it, but not much to see...
We went with a tour guide from ANEX. The guide was snappy and rude. She didn't really explain anything, which is probably why we didn't like it.
Beautiful buildings that are interesting both in terms of how they are constructed and their history. Beautifully situated on a hill with a view.
go there, take your 10mins to take a photo and go. there isnt much to do but it is worth your 10mins to look at the old building

The ruins of the Champa Kingdom. We went to Po Nagar Towers!! These towers, which were supposedly built without using any adhesive, are still a mystery. The altars and stone steps are very picturesque, but climbing them is quite a challenge. However, the view of Nha Trang city from the top of the tower is beautiful. Inside the tower, there was a Cham dance performance, which was very enjoyable. It really makes you feel the history.
It's a small site consisting of four towers in fairly good condition. The place is well-maintained. You can see the city from the top.
Theres really not much to see here. Just ok. Its a shame that they now take money for entry. Theres not much to see up there

The site is absolutely incredible but completely ruined by tourists arriving from all directions, taking photos indiscriminately and littering everywhere, preventing those seeking genuine discovery from immersing themselves in Cham culture... Otherwise, the organization is good and inexpensive for parking (2000 VND for 1 bicycle) and for purchasing entrance tickets (22000 VND).

It was interesting to see. Stylistically, it's reminiscent of Angkor Wat. Perhaps the design was similar, or the developer was the same. Especially since they were built around the same time. It's still in use, so you can pray there.

The Cham Towers was built to worship the Mother of Nature by Champa Kingdom from 12th century. The Tower is easy to get by car or by motorbike because it is right in the city of Nha Trang. Entrance fee is cheap and you also do not have to climb up too high. Easy to access for old people.
Yes, if it's cloudy and the weather is a bit worse, you can go here and take a look. I didn't think it was anything special but still fun to see! 22,000VND per person

If you enjoy taking photos, this is definitely a highly recommended spot. It's not a large area, but it makes for a fantastic backdrop! The effect is even better on a sunny day! You absolutely must come!
Its good for a change in Nha Trang, definitly not worth coming to Nha Trang just for this. Maybe i am spoiled with the other great temples in Asia ;-)

Not a bad visit but was expecting it to be bigger and better considering the other reviews and rating of the attraction. They are pretty and the gardens surrounding are nice too but i have seen better in other parts of the world. A good view of the bridge and the city from the sight its a good photo opportunity as well. Cost 20,000 VD to enter and you're free to roam about for as long as you please. Located in a busy area so good to have a walk around after. You can also enter inside the actual temples free of charge to wearing a robe provided, unfortunately no cameras allowed inside.
We visited in the evening and wandered around the garden. It's a great spot for a panoramic photo overlooking the river and fishing boats. The Towers themselves are nothing special.

It gives you a purpose for the day, and even though it's fairly well preserved, there isn't much to see. However, it's overrun with Chinese tourists ready to lean against any pillar to take a picture in a pretty dress...
An interesting insight into the Cham era, it seems that every tour pays a visit to the towers, this in turn tends to make the sight very crowded.

It's not much, and you pay here, unlike other places of worship, which are free. 1 euro per person, to be precise, 22,000 vietnamese baht, plus 5,000 for parking your motorbike. There's not much inside beyond the four turrets visible from the outside. You could easily miss this visit.
A mystical place. Interesting, but small. It takes 45 minutes to get around. If you're in Nha Trang, go there; it's worth your time.

Old towers dedicated to a monastery. If you love history, it's worth a visit. It can be reached by taxi or scooter, and is located just across the bridge. It's nice to see how architecture and nature intertwine, but it's nothing special and not very well maintained.

I visited at the end of 2014. It was a rainy day, so I rode a motorbike there. The entrance fee was 20,000 VND per person, and parking was 0.4 VND. I wasn't particularly interested in this attraction and just glanced at it. If you're not very interested, you can skip it. The farmers' market next door is worth a visit.
Little bit of Viet history, cost about a US dollar to go in....that's about all it's worth. It really is only an hours visit.

The towers are interesting, but there's nothing unusual about them. They're worth a visit, if only because Nha Trang doesn't have many attractions. There's a small park around the towers where you can stroll and take photos with the bridges in the background.

the cham towers are definitely not a must see in nha trang, however considering the fact that there are limited options for tourist attractions in nha trang, it could still be worth doing to fill in time.
The Towers are quite impressive and offer a nice view over Nha Trang. If you want to spend an hour off Beach this is a nice spot to go.

Was interesting when you thought about the age of the building. Not too much to see once you have spent lots of time in Asia and seen many temples and places of worship. Good for an afternoons walk if you have free time.

Oh wow, that's it! It looks pretty amazing, but when I got closer, it was just okay. Probably because I don't have much knowledge about it. The people who came with a guide were getting lots of explanations, so I think they were enjoying it more than I was. The entrance fee was 22,000 dong, and the taxi from the city center was 69,000 dong. I walked back, but it wasn't too difficult. It was about 3.5 km.

To be honest, I was very underwhelmed by this site. It's small, well restored and kept, but it's also just really nice to view from far away as it sits on top of the hill, almost hidden among the green trees. You can go inside and pray. Also, there's a restroom, just in case.

Obviously, after visiting Thailand and Cambodia for their historical temples and archaeological sites, this one seems rather underwhelming. Still worth a visit, though, especially since there isn't much else to do here. Easy to find, walk there along the long beach, then return by taxi. Plenty to see along the way, a very pleasant walk. The visit is quick, but be mindful of the sanctuaries' attire... Remove your shoes and respect those who are praying. The sanctuaries are quite narrow. The air inside is a bit stuffy. Too many shops around, which is a shame! Nearby, there are picturesque and authentic alleyways. You can get a taxi at the exit for the return trip, which is a bit long. Make sure to check that the meter has reset to zero.

Nothing too special, but an interesting site to visit. It won't take you much more than half an hour to have a good look at the site and take some nice photos. Has some really nice views of Nha Trang over the water.

There are only three main buildings, right next to each other. It's about an hour's walk from the city center. There are song and dance performances at fixed times every day. It's not really worth coming all the way here; it's fine if you're just strolling around like me.
Nothing special, just a 20-minute diversion. You can get there yourself by taxi. If you want to see something interesting, fly to Cambodia.

I appreciate the history of the site, though I thought more information should have been made available to visiting tourists. Very cool that the place is still used as a site of worship today, but very weird to see Buddhists praying while other people take pictures of them. I enjoyed the gardens around the back and also the culturally appropriate song and dance performance.

Worth checking out but only downside for this nice and historic attraction is lack of historical information on site. Don't wear shorts or skirts as you cannot enter the structures. Same for taking photos inside.

This is a temple you must visit at least once when you go to Nha Trang. Although it is not large in scale, if you look down from the temple, you can admire the city of Nha Trang and the sea. You can also enjoy folk dances.

It's a very small attraction, quite local in style. It's not exactly a huge fun attraction, but it's great for taking photos. You can go inside the pagoda to worship, but you need to take off your shoes and can't wear sleeveless tops or shorts. If you are wearing those, there are places nearby where you can borrow robe-like garments to wear. The entrance fee is 22,000 VND. Taking a taxi outside is quite convenient.

This is Nha Trang's oldest landmark, so a visit is essential for a true sense of history. The towers themselves are architecturally reminiscent of Cambodian temples, but there's not much else to see, so I recommend taking a bus instead of a tour.

Arrived at 9.30 was peaceful, interesting history behind it but seems a little lost now. Was there for only 30 mins which is enough. Luckily we had a guide with us who took us there and explained its history.

After Angkor Watt, I wasn't impressed. The grounds are small, and there's almost nothing to see. On the plus side, it's easy to walk and doesn't cost you money on tours. They also give you free robes; as long as your shoulders and knees are exposed, you can go inside.

For general development, you can, in principle, visit this Nha Trang attraction. Some will be impressed, while others will be bored. I find it difficult to recommend or not recommend visiting this attraction.

There are only a few Hindu stupas, and the site is quite small. English-speaking guides are available, but their level of expertise is unclear. If you've been to Angkor or are planning to go, skip this; if you haven't been, just have a casual, visit-it-and-see attitude and don't have too high expectations.
I liked it, but I wouldn't be interested in going again. There are lots of places to see. The visibility is decent in some places. Everything is well-kept and beautiful.

I thought there was more to see, but in the end, we had walked around three times in half an hour. There are three small buildings with altars with Buddhas inside. Behind them, a room with statues and photos. A small garden.
Go closer to sunset, there are fewer people, and the sun illuminates the towers to advantage, you can take great photos.

The place is beautiful, very small, but still beautiful. I spent about 1,5 hours there. For sight seeing 30 minutes is enough. There's a scheduled amateur traditional dance also. But i spent an hour just to relax. There's plenty of benches. The view is also nice, because this place is located at a top of a hill so you can see the sea quite nicely. So just sit, relax, read, or buy some drinks. Enjoy!
We visited the Po Nagar Towers, but had expected more. We much preferred Tam Ky. However, there is an excellent view from there across the Cai River to Nha Trang City.
Its nice and small place its worth a visit if you are aruond. Its very cheap cost only 22 dong. 30 minute its Enough
The Po Nagar Cham Towers is Ancient Temple architecture of Khmer. For the traveler and student who love the history.

The site is a relic of an indigenous Vietnamese religion, but it has been renovated and is still in use as a temple. Inside, you can see worshippers praying. It is similar in style to many pagodas in Southeast Asia, but this religion is likely heavily influenced by Chinese culture, as the totems inside are dragons and phoenixes.

The towers are located away from the European quarter, but you can easily reach them on your own. The complex consists of, I believe, four towers. The entrance fee is 22,000 dong. You can borrow long clothing for free to enter and explore. There's free Wi-Fi throughout the grounds, so you can learn about the history right there. We had a group with a Russian-speaking guide from Pegasus. The tour wasn't informative at all. All the jokes were below the belt, but the tourists laughed. It's much more interesting to get there yourself and explore everything calmly on your own.

We drove to the towers ourselves. We walked around and then left. We didn't see anything special. It's worth a visit for a broader perspective. But buying a tour that includes them and some fishing village is a waste of time and money, in my humble opinion.
A must-see... Not bad views of the Kaya River... Folklore dance show with live music... Souvenir shops...
Nice spot for photos and a bit of culture, not breathtaking but nice for a quick visit 😊 We got there by motorbike in 10-15 minutes from the beach 😊

Po Nagam is beloved by the Vietnamese people. It was clear that it's a sacred place. The view from above was beautiful. Our guide told us stories about the Vietnam War. The bullet holes were heartbreaking.
There aren't many impressions about this iconic historical site, there are a lot of people in such a small area, mostly our groups with tourists

Honestly, there's nothing special about it. The complex is small, crowded, and crowded, making it difficult to take photos. The souvenirs are expensive. The only thing is that you can wear robes and go inside the temples, but photography is prohibited. You can also see folk dances (similar to the Indians singing in parks in large cities like St. Petersburg and Kazan).

Nha Trang doesn't seem to have many historical buildings, but this tower appears to have been built around 1200 years ago. It looks more like a temple than a tower, and there were indeed Buddha statues or something similar displayed inside. It seems to be made of brick, and it has a very historical feel to it; it's amazing that it's still standing. It's not a particularly large place, so you can see everything in about 30 minutes, but the entrance fee isn't expensive, and I think it's worth seeing.
Curious... A different culture, a different religion, a different architecture... Everything is different... It didn't really grab me.

It likes a small copy of one of the tower in angkor wat. Tour guide here are very friendly, dressing ao dai extremly beautiful. A bit sad that they can not keep the original tower, the towers were fixed and mixed, feeling not... normal anymore.

First place we visited on arrival to Nha Trang, its beautiful in the architecture but don't expect too much. The whole thing can be seen in 15 minutes. We didn't go inside the temple, note need to cover up, though full cover dress is provided.  Nice to see on the way to somewhere else..

So museums and historical things are not really my cup of tea. I maybe a little off with this review, but I can't see how this is number one! This is built on top of a hill that over looks the ocean and a river. The entrance fee is $1.00 USD and at 3 pm there were no crowds. It really is a pretty temple, but really after 15 mins of walking around the place, we were ready to go. I did not think there was that much to see either. There were no guides or people offering to tell us the history behind the temple either. The number one restaurant and the number one spa I agree with, think both are fantastic.... Just think this was a slight let down. ( at the time of writing this the number one restaurant was Mix and the number one Spa was Sen Spa, we did both) If you come to Nha Trang, I think you should definitely do both of these.... If you missed this one you did not miss that much. But if you have a spare buck and an extra 15 minutes you can knock this off your bucket list!

As you drive through town in a taxi, everyone will point and explain things to you. It's not very big, so you can visit it in just a short amount of time. Take a look around Nha Trang and see the town for yourself.

There's not much to do in Nha Trang anyway. Visiting this temple is a good way to kill time. Actually, the building's vibrant colors and the different shades cast by the sunlight are quite beautiful, though it's very small. However, being situated on a small hill, it's a good place to cool off.

I guess we should have paid a little more for a guide. But wikipedia tells me that this temple is very very old!  We saw the priest(?) and followers giving their offerings and people selling souvenirs are not that pushy. Which is good.

It's very convenient and easy to get to, and you can combine a visit to the hot springs with a trip. You can also observe and experience another aspect of Khmer culture. It's worth visiting at least once to see for yourself.

Nice towers but 15-20 minutes are enough to visit it. Must pay 22000 vnd /person as entrance fee. For the scooter the parking fee is 4000 vnd. Nice see and cityview but it is a bit far from the centre, so rent a scooter to go there or take a taxi.

I didn't initially intend to stop by, but I ended up visiting as I happened to be passing through. It is quite large. I was unable to take photos inside. It is a popular tourist spot where you can see the entire city at a glance. It is a good place to visit due to its easy accessibility.
Not that impressive and heavily restored in a modern fashion. But the view is great. Worth a brief visit.
I didnt find them exciting ......though they are pretty much on top of must see list everywhere ....skip if you are short on time !
I liked the figures on the towers and the performances of local dancers on the grounds, but unfortunately, you can't go inside. I also didn't like the huge number of visitors.
The towers reminded me of Angkor Wat in Cambodia, only in miniature. I was pleasantly surprised by the girls dancing in front of the temple.

From the top, you can see Nha Trang city and the sea at a glance, and there are also folk dance performances. Additionally, there is a Buddha statue enshrined inside the pagoda and a small exhibition space, so it is a good place to take a quick look around.

I went with high expectations because it was recommended as a must-visit tourist attraction, but I felt there wasn't as much to see as I had anticipated. I think it would be more enjoyable to visit if you looked up its historical significance beforehand.

When I got there, it wasn't as big as I expected. However, it's one of Vietnam's oldest ruins and has a certain charm. It's only about a 10-minute taxi ride from Nha Trang, and there are plenty of taxis waiting when you return, so it's not a problem.

A place you can easily explore on your own without any tours. It's interesting and unusual. But it's not worth traveling all the way from the other end of Vietnam. There are far more interesting places in Vietnam.

We took a cab from Galliot Hotel (fare about 60k VND) and strolled leisurely about Po Nagar. Spent close to an hour poking about each of the towers (as they say in Vietnam - same same but different) and the mini-museum, with about a quarter of an hour watching the half-hearted traditional band and dancers. Most amazed at how they integrated electricity *into* the towers; also a great view of the area. Leaving, turn left on the main road and you'll come upon a bus stop for #6 and #7 buses heading back into town.
Our bus stopped there on the way to Dalat. Nothing exceptional, but it was nice to see. A bit of culture near Dalat.

The age of the structure is impressive; independent tourists need a guide; it's impossible to understand everything on your own, as there are no explanatory signs in any language other than Vietnamese.
I went here during a brief stay in Nha trang as it was the number 1 attractions according to trip advisor. It was interesting but after an hour I had seen it all and was ready to go.

It's very hot at noon so you should avoid that time. You should also wear proper serious clothes (no shorts or dresses or skirts or any other inappropriate clothing, though you could borrow a uniform there for free as well). There is a tour and a little museum there as well. You could also buy souvenirs and get free drinking water. The towers were nice.

The Champa Towers were interesting as a reminder of an ancient Hindu kingdom that ruled pockets of middle and southern Vietnam from 2nd century till 19th, and engaged in wars with the Chinese and Viet Kinh people. Descendants are still around today, performing on their drums and dancing to entertain tourists like what you would see in Malaysia and India. The towers are still being used by devotees, going by the lit candles and flowers. But the repairs done to the towers look too new and modern. The tiny onsite museum has some ancient coins and statues of Hindu deities but surely more found from archaeological sites could have been added. It appears that the authorities are only interested in its tourist value, not its full history.
If you want a cultural experience, Nha Trang is perhaps the best. It's very touristy and crowded...

The Po Nagar Towers are certainly worth a visit, if only because they're located practically in the center of Nha Trang, and it doesn't take long. They're a fine architectural complex, which, unfortunately, was heavily damaged by time and has largely been restored. I'm not a fan of modern architectural monuments, and for that reason, I only give them an average rating. The park behind the towers offers a nice view of the river. Overall, go, but don't expect too much.
Nothing special to see unless some old kovil. Ticket was 20000VND and not worth it. Good to take some pics
Not particularly wonderful, but ok … cheap admission fee > Probably over-rated, but worth a look. You can take some pretty good snaps/photos though ...
It was quite average, but the only highlight was that they had maintained the place very well, we found a nice corner overlooking the river and the boats , sat their for some time enjoying the view.
The towers themselves are magnificent. Being near them, you sense a thread of time. Moreover, they are active places of worship. People come and offer their prayers to the Almighty.
Not far from the center, about 30 minutes' walk away. The outside of the tower is more interesting than the inside. There are too many noisy Chinese people around.

Me and my girlfriend decided that we could go there for a while, since we were in the area anyway, the entrance fee is 22,000 VND, which is very cheap.. The "temples" are beautiful and you can see they have done a good job maintaining them, so it is worth looking at. If you go inside you can feel the atmosphere and see that they are still used to worship the gods, however, you should remember a long-sleeved shirt and a pair of long pants or shorts if you want to go inside the glories, otherwise there is the possibility of borrowing a "dress" so you can cover yourself and remember to take off your shoes before you enter the temple. (If you don't want to walk all the way to the city, you can get a taxi for about 50,000 VND, we drove to mojzo inn)
There's nothing exceptional about this site; it's small and you can see it very quickly. But it's one of the few places worth visiting in Nha Trang.

An old Hindu temple which has undergone restoration. Consists of complex of small temples. Tidy and clean but don't feel the peace one goes to temples in search of.30 minutes is enough to go around the place

The area where the towers are located is small, and you can enter the towers wearing special robes, which you can borrow at the entrance. The monument itself isn't particularly captivating, but the taxi drivers parked near the towers are rude. Always have change and the exact amount agreed upon, otherwise you won't get change and the meter will be set to make it look like you're coming from the airport.
Very interesting architecture, remnant of the Cham Kingdom. However, there is very little information around these historic monuments.

I found the site rather unspectacular, however, I could imagine that people with a greater interest in history and old buildings might find it worthwhile. At least there's a nice view from up there, and I managed to take a few photos in front of the old stones. A qualified recommendation.
I went because I lived nearby. You can admire the ancient brickwork. Otherwise, it's a typical Southeast Asian place.
there's not so many english translated instructions there. If you would like to know the history, probably google it before you go.

I was there today. It's impossible to take a good photo because there are so many awnings around the towers that they spoil the view. Inside the main tower is a very small, smoky room, about 4x4 meters, with an altar in the middle. It's a functioning temple. We saw two monks praying there. They're very small, and they're probably handpicked. Don't expect much, but it's worth a look, as Nha Trang doesn't have many attractions.
It's nice, but there's little to see. Only go if you're really bored and have nothing else to do. The towers have been restored, but there's no compelling history attached to them.

As mentioned in previous reviews, if you've been to Angkor Wat or Wat Arun in Thailand, this place might seem ordinary. However, if you haven't seen this type of architecture before, you can pay VN22,000 to go inside. A leisurely visit will take about 30 minutes. You can sit under a tree, enjoy the breeze, overlook Nha Trang, and experience the slow pace of Vietnamese life.

I have been to many historical sites, and this one was surrounded by too many modern structures. And some of the bricks were replaced with modern ones, which make it look...fake? In my pov, they should have preserve in a more serene, natural way.
Was an interesting place to see however became pretty crowded which spoilt it a bit and meant trying to move around was not as easy plus was difficult to get good photos!
This tourist attraction is a bit of a tourist trap but the venue and temples are presented and preserved well. There are good views to be had back across the Xom Bong Bridge and Cai River.
in the north part of Nha Trang it is a landmark - on eof the few really old buildings in the area. you have to pay entrance fee

I was there in October 2014. You can get there by bus from the center; check the map; there are plenty of them everywhere. You buy a ticket at the entrance and climb a small hill. The temple looks like it's made of brick, only very old, but it's very artistically beautiful. There are benches nearby overlooking the bay. I wasn't particularly impressed, to be honest. There's also some kind of mini-temple, a "chapel," where only men are allowed... I didn't go. You can try fresh seafood nearby; it's like stepping out of the towers, right across the street, and about 200 meters away. Good luck.

The Po Nagar Cham Towers are one of the surviving testaments to the powerful Cham Empire that dominated Vietnam from the 7th to 17th centuries. The towers are located on Mount Culao within the city of Nha Trang and were built between the 8th and 13th centuries. Only four towers remain today. The complex is located on a small area, so exploring this historical and cultural landmark takes very little time. You can explore not only from the outside but also go inside, observing simple rules: clothing must cover your shoulders and knees, and hats must be removed. However, if you're unprepared, you can rent a robe for free! Speaking of cost, restoration and maintenance of the towers requires money, so there is an entrance fee, but it's not expensive – 20,000 dong (US$1 or 60 rubles). You can take some nice souvenir photos.

On our first visit to Nha Trang, we visited the towers on our own. You can get there by bus #4, cross the bridge, and there they are—the towers. There's an entrance fee, and beyond that are several smaller structures with their own history. You can read all the information online without spending money on tours. A thorough inspection will take you about an hour. I recommend covering your shoulders and knees, out of respect for other faiths.
It's a long drive, the towers are small, the grounds are bare, and it's hot during the day. I don't think it's worth wasting your time or money on it.

The title says it all! If this is your first time in Asia, you'll be more impressed, of course, but if not (and especially if you've seen Ankor Wat), it'll be a bit of a letdown! There are four towers, all well-preserved, and the grounds are quite small. The much-vaunted view of the city—well, I don't know, you could find a better viewpoint! But overall, it's quite nice, and, again, its prime location right in the city makes it worth a visit.
The view of the city and harbor is good the climb isn't too bad and there are benches in the shade for resting.
After seeing Ankor Wat in Cambodia last year, well Po Nagar Towers was ok and we mean no disrespect to anyone but lacks a bit of info in English re the history of the structure so did not stay long.

The towers are located in the northern part of the city. You can stroll along the embankment past the Pasteur Institute after lunch, cross the bridge, and you'll see the towers themselves on the left. The distance isn't very great, so we found the walk quite comfortable. There's an entrance fee, and you can hire a private guide, but in my opinion, it's not worth it. The area itself is small, surrounded by greenery and flowers, with restrooms, cafes, and shops. For general information, you can read about these towers online and visit one of them to see for yourself. Bare shoulders and knees are prohibited, but you can rent protective clothing at the entrance for free. There's nothing to see inside.
Been to loads of temples around Asia. Worth a look. Ours was part of a tour so not sure regarding entrance fee. There was local native dancers there which made it interesting

They are not spectacular monuments but worth a visit if you wish to understand the Hindu influence in this part of the country. If you are lucky, there might be a cultural performance at the back of the towers, and the Hindu influence is visibly noticeable. What is clear is that construction was highly sophisticated more than a thousand years ago in this part of the world .

Po Nagar Cham Towers is an ancient hindu temple that dates back to hundreds of years of history. There isn't much to see at the site, except that the shrines are made of bricks and stones. It is uncommon to see Hindu temple built in this manner, so I'd recommend photo taking. The tour guide(50,000VND) is actually quite redundant as you can read the story behind Po Naga Cham Towers in the information centre. The area also overlooks the ocean as well as the harbor area. Please go to the ticketing booth to get the tickets at 22,000VND and not get the tickets elsewhere.
If you haven't been to Angkor Wat, you can take a look. If you have, you can skip this, as foreigners need to buy an entrance fee of 2000 VND. From the top, you can see the scenery of Nha Trang Bay.

Nice place to visit, it would hardly take 30 to 40 minutes. I was surprised to see idols of Indian gods here and even the ticket vendor greeting us in Hindi language. If your plan has a buffer of 30 to 40 minutes would recommend it.
It wasn't particularly impressive. The architecture lacks spirit. But since the choice of attractions is limited, it's worth a visit.
If you haven't been to Siem Reap have a look It's close to town so easy cab ride we did it as part of the day town tour

We visited the towers ourselves, simply taking bus number 4. Taking a tour is pointless; it'll be much more expensive, and a guidebook will provide at least as much information about the place. The towers themselves are quite beautiful, surrounded by greenery, flowers, and figurines of gods. Two of the towers are quite small, with only room for one or two people inside the sanctuary; the other two towers are much larger and much more ornate. You must enter the towers barefoot and with your shoulders covered. There are several tables with red incense candles in the park; they look especially beautiful at sunset. The hill where the towers are located also offers a good view of the river and Vietnamese boats, making for some lovely photos.

This place is alright but its pretty small and to be honest there are fair better ruins, temples to visit in vietnam but I suppose its worth a visit while in nha trang. Be warned bus loads of tourists seem to pour in here so it can get rather busy.

Since we're resourceful and, one might say, "experienced" tourists to the towers from Nha Trang, we took the bus ourselves—route 4! I don't know how much this pleasure costs at the agency, but it cost us 54,000 local dong per person. (The round-trip bus and entrance fee were 14,000, or 40,000, if I remember correctly.) We arrived late in the afternoon. There are plenty of excursions (you can catch a glimpse), or bring a guidebook and read about everything yourself. We explored everything ourselves. We really enjoyed it, and it only took 30 minutes. There's nothing longer to do there.

It might be a relic for the locals, but for us Russians, having seen enough of the dilapidated churches, it wasn't particularly impressive. But while the tour was underway, my wife and I didn't bother climbing the stairs, instead wandering into the souvenir shop. It turned out there was an artist there, a young guy. While everyone else was wandering around looking at the strange ruins, we managed to snag a cool portrait, and for quite a cheap price (I can't say exactly how much, but it was cheaper than going out to eat...)
Po Nagar is worth a visit if you are in Nha Trang. As we happened to make a stop in Nha Trang we went. The site can be seen in half an hour.

All in all about a $9 trip for two people including taxi fare from the beach ($7 round trip) and the entrance fee ($1 USD per person). Enough to see for about half an hour. Nice gardens, interesting to see the shrines set up inside the temples.

As we were in Nha Trang for a 1/2 day (from Mia Resort outside town), we decided to get a taxi from the centre of town to check out this very old Temple. I am happy that we did take a look as it was interesting (and also to see all those Russian tourists on package bus tours) and there were some local dancers as well. We spent less than an hour there.

Not much to do and see. One hour or less is enough for pictures and tour. Nothing special. For the girls, try to cover up or else you can't enter some of the praying area. Although they do have some cloth for you to use. Worth a visit.
The old, dilapidated buildings might be worth visiting to broaden your knowledge of the country's history, but they don't leave much of an impression. You can kill time exploring the towers.
There are only two towers in a small area. Despite their antiquity, they weren't very impressive. But they're worth a look and a good time.

I passed by here and being no history buff, I could find some appreciation for the architecture of the place... Definitely a marvel of their times... Ancient and beautiful.  That said, a glance or two from various angles was all I needed, I probably spent at most 15 minutes here browsing the grounds until I covered everything.  So all in all, worth a visit if you are in the area but you may want to reconsider if you want to make a journey up to the northern part of the tourist area. If you have seen other such structures already, you can safely skip this place to unless you want to learn about its cultural significance.

We didn't see anything particularly remarkable. Ancient towers, peeling on the outside, blackened with soot on the inside. Each tower contains a small altar. There's really nothing special to see in Nha Trang, and that's why even such a dull landmark attracts crowds of tourists.
I liked the colors of the building. I liked the setting. I liked how it is clean. if you have a zoom take a picture of it from a roof top. Very nice.

A must-see for broadening your horizons. It's included in a PAID sightseeing tour (at least with Pegasus). Skip the paid option! Entrance is 20,000 dong (per person), and a taxi is 160,000 dong roundtrip (from the European Quarter). There's no time limit, so enjoy the antiquities to your heart's content. It's best to arrive around 5:00 PM: there are no tour operators offering tours, but you'll see dancing and a great view of Nha Trang at sunset.
Place is realy Beautiful, but for me is to many people there,...or exact phrase to many Russian, like all other places in Nha Thrang. Chua Sui Do temple is 100% better choice to see.
We went there as our hotel was very close to it. Is interesting and worth a visit. Ticket entry is 22,000 vdg
Nothing special, there are some annoying beggars, but it's a free sightseeing tour so it's worth a visit.

This is a nice place to visit if you are in Nha Trang. Whilst the temple was fairly impressive I wouldn't say it was any better than many of the other temples and pagodas across Asia, hence the 'average' rating. However, the views of the city were great and it was a nice spot to sit and watch the world go by with an ice-cream in hand. Visit if you are in Nha Trang but dont expect wonders!

As I have read myself, it is nice to have seen. However, it could use a bit of maintenance in terms of restoration. The cost of the park is 22,000 dong per person. I was able to take a number of nice photos there. It is not big, so don't expect too much from it.
We visited on our own, on a moped. You can get there by public bus. We didn't have a guide.

Another beautiful site of southeast asia. This "temple" (not sure if that's the proper term for this site) is set atop a hill overlooking the Cai River with the city skyscrapers across the way. It was a nice place for photos. The temple itself is a beautifully constructed site itself. If you want to go inside you must have knees and shoulders covered. There are garments for you to borrow if needed. You can see people still leave offerings. There are very narrow steps that used to be used for bringing the offering to which was neat to see. We were not bombarded by beggers or salesman at all which was nice. I give it an "average" rating because many temples are beautiful and similar in southeast asia, so while this was a nice stop it wasn't hugely different from other sites we have seen.

We got to the Cham Towers on a tour operator's tour, so we ran through them very quickly, and I didn't have time to enjoy them. These are ancient structures, heavily restored, and the towers didn't impress me, probably because we were there for such a short time. Entry is permitted for both men and women, as long as their knees and shoulders are covered. If you're wearing shorts, don't worry; free robes are provided upon entering the towers, which will cover everything.
#NAME?

The site is clean, and the entrance fee is really low. The architecture is lovely, and the site is quite small. The best part is arriving by scooter and then continuing to explore the surrounding area! Eat at the street food stalls, stroll along the coast, etc... renting a scooter for €8 a day will quickly pay for itself!
It's nothing spectacular, but I think it's worth seeing. But go without high expectations. The ruins are relatively small and few in number. The view from up there is beautiful.

A genuine relic from the past the remaining towers are small but restored. Excellent view back over the road bridge to the city. There is an entrance cost & the visit is about one hour. Respect dress customs (no shorts) & you can enter one of the towers. An essential place to see when in Nha Trang. Peaceful but can be crowded with tourist buses.

We walked here all the way across the bridge, in broad daylight. The ruined towers creates an amazing view overlooking Nha Trang. We came without a guide,thus are unsure of the history, but looking at the red bricks building, one can imagine the ancient society once lived on this hill. Any1 is allowed to enter the temples to pray, however those not cover in long pants/sleeve can borrow a 'jubah" to wear

The view from the top is nice, but the brick buildings are a dubious landmark? The area is very small, surrounded by stench and piles of garbage. On one side are squalid fishing houses, and in the bay is a dump. Yes, the restoration from 1938 was probably difficult, but considering the devastation in Russia after the war... I guess I should have hired a guide to fully appreciate it. 😀
The building is old, but the restoration isn't quite right. It's a shame they closed the stairs—you can't get there. The decorations on the towers are covered in concrete.
Beware of hawkers selling overpriced gear. The temple is certainly an amazing structure and entry fee is cheap enough.
a place to see to find out about the cultures .boring for kids.but for me and my wife it was somethink interesting very quite place .

Located on a hilltop, the site contained three fairly compact tomb ruins. Local people were inside offering prayers, so I didn't look around closely out of consideration for their presence. However, the view from the hilltop had a very authentic Vietnamese feel to it, which was wonderful.
Worth a quick look. There are a small number of towers which have been rebuilt. Nice view. Not much else to add. There is a small museum with photos but you need a translator as there is no English.
Not really interesting but if you wanna try a city tour and try to find out more about Cambodia's culture. That would be great.
As far as Cham towers go this was pretty underwhelming. The pair in Quy Nhon city were higher.  Nicish view of town. Only 22000d to get in.
Overall, it's an interesting temple complex. I didn't like the fact that it was restored in a too modern way; it lost some of the spirit of antiquity.
Nice temple, but if you have been to others anywhere in SE Asia this would be considered a very (very) small one. Still nice, but 10 minutes and you can be finished with this one.

I say that because I think it was not so great but worth the little visit just to get you away from the beach and wanting to included in a small tour. We have rented a driver/car. we did a little tour of all there is to visit. fun to get away from the beach.

Remnant of the Cham dynasty, now these towers are being used by Buddhist people. Entrance was about 40.000 VND pp if I remember well. Beware to pay repect to the belief by not wearing too short shorts or sleeveless tops, you are not allowed to enter the towers this way. Somehow I expected more of it. The inside of every tower looked almost the same to me. Lots of "linga's" and "yoni's" and also the Godess Uma with her yellow robe and people praying and worshipping. The view up there (a view of the fishing boats on the Cai river), it has to be said, is nice! There is a little exposition with pictures of (I think) the discovery of the towers and damage made by the war bombings. Interesting to see, but my travel guide book had put the expectations too high ("a must see!" ...)

Just before leaving Nha Trang I decided to check out the towers that I saw on my way to the mud-baths. I payed 3000 VND for parking my motorbike and 20,000 for entering. I looked around and felt (as in Angkor Wat) that I wanted somebody to explain and talk to about the things I saw. I went back and got a local guide for an additional 50,000 VND.  The guide was a student that didn't talk english that good but I loved her entusiasm. We ended up going to the two biggest temples and she telling me the best she could about the gods and culture of the Cham-people. It was interesting but I couldn't help to compare it with Angkor Wat and the temples of Siem Reap. I'm happy I visited though, the place was beautiful and just walking around in that ancient place of spirituality made me feel calm and relaxed.  Don't forget to put on the long dresses and take of your shoes before entering the temples!
Lots of people. It's not historically relevant to us. Just curious. It's not worth a special trip, only with a tour. At least they'll tell you something.
- I was lucky to see traditional dance there. - The entrance ticket fee was cheap. Parking fee just 1000 VND (I used bicycle) - It just small temple, nothing much to see.
For local visitors, it is not much to see. We can find similar tower in some other coastal cities in Vietnam.

It's some kind of architecture similar to Cambodian cave temples! I don't really understand it! Unless you find a local to explain it to you, you really wouldn't know what gods they're worshipping! Locals can enter for free, but they start charging entrance fees as soon as they see foreigners!

We didn't take the sightseeing tour; we went to see the pagoda and towers ourselves. Everything was clean, and there was an entrance fee of 12,000 dong. They provide free clothing (elbows and knees must be covered). There were lots of souvenirs made from colored sand; I couldn't resist buying one for myself.

I didn't know what to expect here...but I wasn't expecting something this Lara Croft/Indy Jones worthy! Not only is a group of beautiful buildings but love all the great sculptures of the Goddesses...plus great river views. It's very close to Nha Trang...do not miss this

If you're starting to get templed out in Vietnam, the Po Nagar towers are still worth seeing just because they're so different to just about every other temple you'll see. Its a very formal place - and even offers up robes to anyone who hasn't come dressed 'appropriately' free of charge. However, there's nothing of obvious interest outside of the unique architecture and the views from the top - interesting to see, but not a must do.

You don't necessarily need a tour to get to this place; you can drive from your hotel and wander around. The buildings are quite dilapidated, but there's something interesting about them. We saw local Vietnamese women playing drums and wearing traditional costumes.

One of the rare local attractions, it's worthy of your attention. That's assuming, of course, you're in awe of a pile of old, unremarkable stones. I'm not an expert on Cham culture, so forgive me if I didn't fully grasp the spirit of this ancient structure.
I think it has lost a bit of its charm as there are many stalls and it is clear that it is now a tourist magnet. It is almost a must to visit, it is incredibly beautiful.

These towers are great for those who haven't seen any cham towers or similar before. For myself, having seen both the My Son temples and Ankor Wat Temples in Cambodia, these were simple and uninspiring. Great views of the river and the city from the top and a lovely place to relax with a drink and watch the world go by.

You can visit if you're interested, but I wasn't impressed. The views are beautiful and they make sand paintings, that's all I liked. It's possible that these towers are 7th-century, but when I was there, the builders were sitting there and throwing concrete at them, so I'm not sure there's anything else from the 7th century there. There are tons of tourists, but they don't offer fans, which was a relief. Go there yourself; don't take the sightseeing tour.

Po Nagar is a little distance from Nha Trang city, so we took a taxi from our hotel. It's located on a hill, so the view is great, and we could see the Sombon Bridge leading towards the city. The breeze was refreshing. The inside of the temple was dimly lit, and when we took off our shoes and went further inside, smoke filled the air. In front of the statues, some local middle school-aged girls were praying devoutly. Outside, many locals were lined up in an orderly fashion, praying, and I felt the depth of their faith.
