In a plaza-like area at the back, four or five girls performed a dance to the accompaniment of traditional musical instruments. Their shy expressions were cute and memorable.

If your entire vacation is spent in Nha Trang, you should visit this place, if only to add some variety to your beach routine. The architectural structures resemble carefully preserved Cham buildings, modest in size and form. A nearby fishing village is a short stroll away, so the trip won't be wasted.
Nicely restored and quite busy with tourists. Easy walk around. Lots of fishing boats nearby make great photos.
Beautiful monuments. Very crowded with visitors, as people on a sea cruise are dropped off here, as you can expect from a monument like this.

These Cham towers, too, suffered during the war. I have seen better restorations in Vietnam; here, the difference between new and old is terribly large. The Cham towers were built with interlocking stones without the use of mortar, so there are no joints; naturally, it is then very difficult to restore this properly. Still, it could have been done better.

Was it nice to visit the temple while I was in Nha Trang? Yes absolutely. The view from the temple over the river and the city alone is worth it and at night the temple is beautifully lit. The temple itself was also very nice but the inside was actually very small. It's good to see they restored it to it's former glory to preserve this piece of the heritage and culture of the Cham in the Nha Trang region and the location is very handy located in Nha Trang for a quick visit but if you are going to travel around in Vietnam make sure you'll also visit the My Son temples near Hoi An.
This Cham temple is certainly worth seeing, as it is somewhat different from the other pagodas and temples of more recent times, but those who have seen Angkor Wat can skip it.

We visited for the Vietnamese Lunar New Year. Admission was free for everyone. On weekdays, admission is $1. There's a beautiful temple at the entrance, perfect for unique photos. Behind the temple is a museum with exhibits of male genitalia (phalluses and testicles). Nearby is a very inexpensive area where you can try a variety of seafood for very little money. Getting there is easy, take city bus #4 to the final stop.

If you feel like leaving your comfortable beach hammock for a while and visiting some cultural sights , on the outskirts of Nha Trang you will find a nice complex of well preserved and reconstructed Cham towers. The so called Po Nagar towers offer the visitor a unique opportunity to see the remains of the ancient kingdom of Champa.  Several tourist visit the area but not in large groups so it is certainly a nice half day excursion which will be even more enjoyable with the explanations and company of a good tourist guide.  www.theleisurediaries.blogspot.com

We went there on our own bikes. We explored everything ourselves (although the guides offered to go only with them for $50 each). We joined a tour and learned everything. There's nothing to see inside. Overall, it's a place for amateurs and a few photo enthusiasts.

Cham Towers. They honor the people who lived in these lands. They were a separate state but later united with the Vietnamese. Currently, the Cham make up only about 1.5% of Vietnam's population. This is a functioning temple, with services held on holidays and weekends. Admission to the prayer rooms is subject to dress code (as in any temple). The rest of the time, it serves as a tourist attraction. Admission is inexpensive (around $1).

It would be great if it weren't for the Vietnamese restoration (they've really updated it with fresh bricks and cement). But if you're really interested in seeing the monuments of the ancient Cham civilization, I recommend visiting My Son in Da Nang (it's more interesting there).

We were taken here by our guide. Having been to a number of Temples in Vietnam, Cambodia we were a little underwhelmed by this one. Having said that, it IS culturally significant to Nha Trang. If you have seen many temples before I dont think you would mind missing this one too much.

It's a historical site with a temple in the middle of the city. A must-see, not too far from the beach. It's a 7-10 minute scooter ride away. You can see fishing boats on the mountain. Worth a quick visit!
the view is breathtaking. We were there after a drizzle so the weather was just perfect. I would say one of the best vantage points to get a view of Nha Trang

Hmmm, was I at the same location was the other reviewers? Granted the elevated site has nice views but there isn't a whole lot to see here. Absolutely worth a look if in Nha Trang but I cant imagine even a thorough visit lasting more then 15 minutes.
We cycled from Nha Trang and the journey was good. The views down the river were worth it but the towers themselves werent.

It's quite interesting, worth a visit. Several well-preserved towers stand on a hill on a wide bend in the river that flows into the sea; and this, in my opinion, is the most interesting sight, worth the trip. Plus, the island on which the towers stand is surrounded by a completely non-touristy area with its own vibrant life, and that's interesting. All the information about the towers is on Lonely Planet, and I doubt anyone will tell you more about this place :).
A very pretty temple, in the same style as Angkor. You can quickly see all the temples. It's a shame the incense smells too strong inside. Easily accessible by public transport and on foot.

There aren't many sights in Nha Trang, so visiting the towers was just one of our activities. We even took a taxi there ourselves. It's not far from the hotel, and we were staying at the Sheraton. We took a few photos, went into the temple, lit some incense, and placed it there. That was it.

Although the Po Nagar Cham Tower founded sometime before 781. But, it definitely disappointing us as nearly 80% of the tower structure restored in the modern day. The remaining tower very small and nothing much you can see. If you been to others world heritage area such as Angkor Wat, you will not amazed by this tower. Not many sculpture on each tower. However, is still a good place to visit to see the similarity and differentiation of the champ tower with others Hindi architecture and art around world.  Most of the tourist just spent about 30-40 minutes.The tower still visited by most of the local people's for their prayer.  On the others side of the tower is the river, you will see the fishing boat/ bridge busy with local transport and village along the river. On other side, you will have stunning views of mountain and sea. Besides the tower, there is a small museum about the Champ tower history.  Note: 1. You will hardly take the photo due to the space limitation and tower surrounded by big tree. Crowded with people. However, you can take the photo on the whole tower near the tower garden. You may have a look at the photo stall nearby the tower to get some tips for best photoshot.  2. If you wish to enter the prayer tower, tourist with short pant and sleeves are advise the rent the traditional garment with no cost that will cover you.  3.Hiring a tour guide specially for the tower is expensive. Suggest take the one day tour with guide from The Sinh Tourist. It will save your cost thus the tour is conduct in small group (only 6 people during our tour) and cover most of the city attraction is Nha Trang

It's located on a hill overlooking a river that flows north through Nha Trang city. It certainly has the structure of an ancient ruin. The view towards the city is fairly good, partly due to the river in the foreground. There were many tourists, including tour groups arriving by bus. Be careful when crossing the road in front (which is a small roundabout) as traffic is heavy.
Worth to visit with culture and structure in this place. View from the tower to city and beach is beautiful.... and good place for taking photo as well.

The towers, beautifully located overlooking a river in Nha Trang, are some of the few remaining evidences of the Cham empire which flourished over a thousand years ago. Amazing that they're in such good state, the brickwork is stunning. Very popular with Buddhists as it is still an active worship site. Is it worth an overnight in Nha Trang where most of the tourist signs are in Russian? Probably not.

The towers were nice and well maintained for their age but I know that there are far more majestic temples than this in Asia. Since it was included on my tour -- I enjoyed it -- had I gone out of my way to see it -- I would have been disappointed!

It's OK as far as temples go. If you've visited Angkor Wat and surrounding temples in Cambodia I wouldn't bother coming here. No impressive carvings, nor does it quite have the same majestic atmosphere, still nicely laid out and looks nice amongst the trees from the road.

I was taken to Nha Trang Cathedral, Hon Chong Cape, and other places by a local. It's the ruins of a temple that was burned down, but five buildings remain, and it seems that many people still come to pray there, as the smoke from the incense they offer was billowing up.

A beautiful viewpoint and a religious site where worshippers come to make offerings. Not far from the center of Nha Trang, you can get there by bike or on foot (just past the bridge). A lovely walk to take in the morning.
Honestly, I wasn't particularly impressed... But the view from there is beautiful. It's worth stopping by for 15 minutes while driving by...
The views are nice, the temples are nice, it's nice that they give you free robes to wear to cover up, nice gardens...it was all nice!

Gorgeous location high on the hill overlooking Nha Trang. It's an easy walk up to the towers and is quite shady all the way, so it's quite cool even when the sun is hot! We got great photos of Nha Trang & the river from up here. Our son loved looking down on all the boats. Very reasonable entry fee too.

Just a few blocks from Sherraton hotel. You can walk (20 minutes) or take taxi. As the other reviews mentioned, please don't pay attention to the sellers and straight to the temple. Women need to wear clothes cover shoulder and knees.

The towers is a nice place to spend no more than a couple of hours. The towers them selves are well kept and the view is stunning. Remember to bring something to cover shoulders and knees if you want to go inside :)  The view is pretty cool as well.

This is a neat attraction and the temples are still in use by the Cham people of the area. However, if you don't have any back knowledge it will very likely be a lackluster attraction for you.  There are 4 small towers, each built for a different deity. Some date back to before 781 A. D. Knowing a bit of history definitely makes the trip more meaningful.  It's a bit over a dollar for admission and there is parking out back for a nominal fee. Do arrive before the 6pm closing time to avoid disappointment.

We visited the temple when it was a stormy day and we couldn't get on the beach. I don't know if we missed a trick here, but I simply wasn't seeing why this is one of the top rated things to do in Nha Trang....it was just OK.

There are three brick towers, I think they were rebuilt recently. Inside each one is the usual altar with a Buddha. It's nothing special. Perfect if you're in the Nha Trang area and want to experience some culture.

It's a nice, interesting place. There's an entrance fee, but it's inexpensive. It's just not clear what the point is without a guide. We walked around and looked around for about 30-40 minutes, but that's all we needed. The towers offer a nice panoramic view of Nha Trang. There was a short performance of dancing girls in traditional dress near the structures. If it's a cloudy day, it's worth visiting.

Po Ngar Cham Towers - just another tourist attraction. It's at the far end of the beach, after crossing the bridge, The entrance fee is about 21,000VND, but not much to see. Don't compare it to Angkor Wat, it's absolutely 2 different things. It's said you can see the sunset there. Unfortunately, I couldn't see it on the day I visited there due to bad weather. Very few tourist visited there on that day. There're some seafood restaurants nearby, but quite expensive.
We went because someone recommended it. We are just interested in other things. Certainly others may have very different experiences.

I completely agree with the previous review. It's a one-time, short-term visit. If you don't have proper clothing (elbows and knees must be covered), they provide robes. There's a dance performance by Vietnamese girls near the temple.

It was smaller than I thought and only few towers in there. Nothing much to enjoy. But there're not many attractions to visit in Nha Trang, so if you're going there for more than a week, it's also worth a visit, the fruit juice in street vender was great.
A decent example of these religious temples. It's not too far from the centre so worth a visit although I wouldn't travel far to see them.

Amazing ruins but I think it is not presented well - no signs, not v organised, you don't understand the meaning of the ruins unless you can find a guide or have read extensively about it. Lovely motorbike ride out there though from Hoi An.
the restoration is not done well, it lost the ancient feel if u look at it closely... from far, it is still nice historical building...

We went to Nha Trang mainly to dive, but since we were there, we took the opportunity to see these temples. It's very easy to get there; just take bus number 4, and it drops you off almost at the entrance. Taxi and motorbike drivers will tell you that you can't go by bus, but that's a lie. The temples are nice, but we didn't find them spectacular. We liked the views from there much more.
Temples are about the most interesting thing in town, but are small. If you've seen anything like this in India/Cambodia you'll be disappointed.

The price of a ticket is 16,000 dong. There are a few steps to climb up but not many. The place was very very busy when we went - mostly Vietnamese tourists. There are 3 towers of varying size.  It is possible to enter the towers - they each have a shrine inside - but there are signs requesting ladies displaying a lot of 'leg' or 'bosom' to refrain from entering the towers so you may want to consider what you're going to wear in regards to this. You need to remove footwear before entering.  While we were there, there was some kind of religious ceremony being held by a lot of older ladies who were chanting and banging a wood block. This was on one side of the largest tower while on the other side there was some dancing and pop song performance going on.  I don't think the view is quite as good as so many reviewers have made it out to be I thought it fairly mediocre.  However, it's cheap to get in. I wouldn't go a second time, but I'm glad I've been.  We went here while on the way to Nui Hon Chong - another attraction in Nha Trang. They are fairly close to one another - only 5 minutes by taxi.

Not what we expected. It's small complex with only a few towers. No instructions or explanation in English of all the old tower.... But is has a tremendous view over the water towards the fishing village with all of the brightly coloured boats moored in the harbour area. Worth a visit but not very special.
It's quite nice but I would not be too sad if I missed it! There was a dancing performance when I was there which was very nice.

Worth having a look on the way to the mud baths but nothing special as a stand-alone visit. The towers themselves are quite interesting but the site is small. They are on the way to the mud baths so easy to visit an a nice view of the fishing bots in the harbour.

The towers are somewhat interesting, but I would not put them on a must-see list by any means. You have to appreciate the towers for their historical significance and beauty, but there is nothing overly speical about them. The complex is small and does not provide a "wow" factor. You can pretty much look at pictures, and you have been there. It does provide a nice view, but I would not go totally out of my way for this 15 minute experience.

The towers have a good view over the harbor and city. They are interesting but if you have been to any Cambodian temples you could give it a pass and you wouldn't be missing too much. The bike ride from the tourist center is interesting especially if you do a loop on the beach one direction and through the town on the way back.
it looks marvelous with its red bricks, it is a well made piece of art, also you could enjoy the view at the top restaurant there, prices are a little over-priced
Don't expect some big and spacey towers or temples in here. There are only several towers here but I love the view of Nha Thrang from Po Nagar.

I went to visit the Cham towers of Po Ngar one morning, and they were interesting, although to be honest, after having been to Cambodia and Myanmar, they weren't exactly magnificent. I walked back from there along the beach—a perfect stroll.
Great place to visit to take photos....!!!! with beautiful views.... !!!
If your looking for something a little different and want to get away from the city check it out

When you are in Nha Trang you have to visit these towers. But don’t expect too much. It’s a small complex with only a few towers. The Towers are built on a small hill overlooking the river. From the top you have a beautiful view on Nha Trang and the river.  They did a lot of restoration work to restore the towers but this could have been done better. Some parts of the towers just look like they were built a few years ago. We also visited the Po Klong Garai Cham temples and the looked a lot more original (and less crowded).

This attraction is within the city so a short taxi ride takes you there. It is on a hill overlooking the river and very nice views of the river, inlet, Nha Trang, and ocean. This site is small, only 4 thaps and one great hall. The site is reconstructed so the statues are all new. It was basically made to bring in tourists.
It was ok, however a lot of the towers have been rebuilt so doesnt feel very authentic to me
There were armies of souvenir shops around this ruin however it is practically a single renewed old tower with some nice new religous statues inside.
Worth a visit if you're bored from lying on the beach.
Interesting Cham ruins, walkable from just about anywhere in nha trang
Only a few towers on top of a hill. Good for about 30 minutes of photo ops.
Rebuilt partially, only a couple little towers. I you won't get to see Angor Wat, go for it, but if you've seen it it is a bit disappointing, although from a different civilization (Champ)
It's interesting temple and a we can see the river at the top, but the place is too small, you only need less than 1 hour to explore all of the place

This is a pretty old Hindu Temple with Hindu + Buddhist mix, set on a hillock. The view from here to the bridge, river and sea is pretty good, lots of photo ops here. The place is located near the bridge, if you ask for the bridge and Cham Towers to the taxi guy or walk it you'd not miss it, there's an entrance of 2USD and a small climb up to the place. The towers have styling of Indian temples and pictures in the small gallery prove it. Would be OK in the evening if you can walk around, soak up the place and sit quietly on the bench or landscaped place or watch an artist at work nearby painting. We too could watch a religious ceremony in progress, took about 30 minutes, was a different experience to listen to the Buddhist chanting going on. Well worth investing 2 quiet hours.
Gorgeous view of the river and fishing village and the temple is interesting too.
There are just several towers in this historical place. Nothing special, but it's OK to check out just to know more about the history of the city.

I will admit the beauty and wonder the towers gave. They were something pretty great to lay eyes on, but only for a given amount of time. Once you're done looking and taking pictures, there's not any "activities" (for a lack of a better word) to do at the site. When I visited, it was very crowded, almost over-packed, which made the experience less enjoyable. The view though, from both sides of the hill, are stunning.

This is a beautiful look into the past, but with little to no details provided about it on site it requires either a really good internet connection or a tour guide.

There was a QE code to a flashy 360 style website with great views but the internet wasn’t strong enough for it to work properly. A great idea on paper, let down by details.

The local performance at the venue was a welcome addition to the atmosphere

Worth seeing to say you’ve seen something cultural but not overly impressive.

I think if its your first temple you see in Asia you will like it. Its very small and full of tourists.

You are meant to be respectful in these places,  however a terrible show called 'The Only Way Is Essex' were filming in the temple. Security guards blocking people from viewing the temple and just simply being aggressive. Telling people to be quiet eventhough the 'Z-list celebrities' were talking loudly and on there phones. What a shame. If you've never heard of the show. Trust me, never watch it. Its pure English trash worsening culture in England.

For the $30,000 VND ticket price required for entry, you do not get much. All the buildings, structures, and decorative elements are poorly maintained, if at all. Not much to see, and very busy when we went. Save your money, time, energy, and get a nice bowl of pho or banh canh with the $30k instead.

I guess, I've visited this place during some kind of religious holiday, too many local ppl, there was some performances, it was very loud, I felt myself not safe there, I spent maybe 20 minutes n right away left by taxi

The building is quite nice. The staff are very rude and heard you around by pointing and yelling at you. It is very crowded. Overall quite a disappointing experience that I would not recommend to others.
One member of staff was particularly bad tempered, I attached a photo of him (in blue) in the hope that someone who works there can instruct him to be more friendly and helpful.
It was nice inside but it actually says free entry in Vietnamese here. So maybe because we are from foreign countries, we have to pay 22000 vdn.
Chicken and extra avocado.Can you throw  on some extra chilies there ?
extremely crowded with people sitting and staring everywhere… looked like a market, eating on the ground…. could not see and enjoy the temple or experience its atmosphere….  very disappointed 😞

We were there during „rush hour“, so it was very crowded. Perhaps it‘s great to be there in the morning. But the only thing I could see was tourists shouting and taking selfies from all possible angles, which kind of ruined the atmosphere of this place completely.
Long trip, doesn’t worse it
Looks better on the photos than it does in person. There are far better temples and pagodas to be seen. If I had to liken it to something, I'd say.......Lego.

Hell of a tourist trap. You'll have to pay 120.000 Dong to see the remains, together with thousands of tourists. It's just one of 2 cultural things you can see in Nha Trang, so everyone will be here, be sure. Smartphone  zombies everywhere, almost no sign of real religion. Can't recommend at all.
Super busy, nothing special
It's nice but crowded af.

No need to spare time to visit here if you have been to the other ancinet hindu temples.
Only worth for whom have never been to the others.
Small and old temple
Nice ruins but very very busy!! Reasonable price at only 22,000 VND
Too crowded. Full of hundreds of Chinese. Ancient buildings are fine.
With the lack if attractions in Nha Trang, this place earns itself a 2 star rating.
Wasn't the most impressive temple and not that informative.
heavily damaged by the restoration. Always full of chinese tourists.
Very crowded.
there is too many people. vert hot
Business place, now is a budhist Tempel

Tourist magnet
Same old temple thing
Crowded by brutal Chinese people
Not much to see really.
Nothing much to explore
So unique here
Too crowded
Temple
Needs improvement
Rude staff
I visited Po Nagar, the Buddhist temple, but I wasn't very interested. I don't have much interest in Buddhism.

Unless you have a tremendous interest in history or some insatiable, inexplicable passion for the Champa Dynasty, it’s actually just so-so.

The entrance fee tripled in 2023 to 30,000 VND per person.

Aside from the three towers visible as soon as you climb up, there isn't anything particularly spectacular.

There are so many mosquitoes, so be sure to bring mosquito repellent.

Even if you urgently need to use the restroom, please hold it until you get back to your hotel.

Unless you want to be covered in mosquitoes from head to toe...
I wouldn't recommend it as a must-visit place for tourists visiting South Korea just because it is a mosque, but if you have the time, it is worth seeing at least once.

Honestly, I wouldn't recommend it; the one in Nha Trang is much more beautiful and detailed. The rudeness and lack of empathy toward tourists on arrival and at the ticket office made the visit worse than expected.

The most overrated place in the area. It's always crowded with Chinese people, as tourist buses arrive one after another. It's a 10-minute walk and costs 22 dong (plus 4 dong for parking your bike).

An easy visit to do on your own (30,000 VND/person), perfect for those seeking cultural experiences in Nha Trang. The site is of medium size but very touristy; arrive before 8:30 am (it opens at 7 am, ideal for those arriving in Nha Trang at 6 am on the overnight train).

There are several stone pagodas made of red clay bricks, which you can see if you walk up the stairs. It's a shame I couldn't look at them for long because it was too hot. There are so many damn Chinese bastards acting up, so I acted like a jerk. They were walking around filthy and sweating profusely; I thought they were Chinese.

We took bus 05-2 from the city center. We got off at the stop just beyond the bridge. It's another 15-minute walk to the site. We walked along the street, not the Cai River embankment. The street is where real life, the kind you wouldn't expect from tourists, flourishes. Although there are jewelry stores there, too. The towers stand next to a busy road, and pedestrians, of course, are not allowed through. We immediately noticed a lot of tourists, but didn't expect so many. We arrived at the ticket office around 10 a.m. The ticket costs 30,000 VND. Entry is through a turnstile. There are restrooms on the grounds, and plenty of souvenir shops and other odds and ends. And there are a ton of Chinese tourists, brought in by bus in large groups. With so many people, it's impossible to appreciate the architecture, the greenery, or the space. The Vietnamese are actively making a killing, turning a temple into a beehive for money. Everyone is yelling, bustling, and taking photos. A ton of sloping steps (without handrails, of course) lead up to the towers. Those with limited mobility will have a hard time getting up and down. Independent tourists are rare. I couldn't find a cafe with vegetarian food. We ran away after 10 minutes. You should probably come during lunchtime, when the buses with Chinese tourists are taking you to the restaurant. I've never seen such a crowd in such a small, complex, and sacred area. Once again, I conclude that the place of power lies within. Don't run around touristy shrines. Strengthen yourself within.
Entrance fee is 30k VND for both children and adults...children should only be charged HALF the adult price, that would be more reasonable...The tower is beautiful and clean.

The Ponagar Tower is a cultural heritage site originating from the Cham people. Later, people from the South adopted this belief. There are Cham dances and golden tray dances to thank the Goddess. Why allow such inappropriate dancing, imitating the spirit medium rituals of the North and the Tiger God of the West? It's out of place and nonsensical. It's both appropriating Cham culture and falsely claiming to be the Mother Goddess religion to do wrong.

This Vietnamese cultural site reveals the influence of Chinese culture and Hinduism on Vietnam, with its overall religious spirit showing traces of Chinese, Thai, and Indian influences. However, the site fails to fully explain the migration of these cultures. Furthermore, the modern construction methods used in the restoration have completely destroyed the site's value. Unless specifically requested, this area can be skipped.

On the day we visited, there were many foreign tourists there. Due to cultural differences, they ignored the staff's advice and touched and climbed the ancient sites. It was very upsetting to see. I hope that people who have the opportunity to visit the restaurant there will also respect the local ancient culture and ruins.
From an architectural point of view, it is not of particular interest. Otherwise, the place is of cult importance. It is neat and tidy.

We took the bus. We thought we'd get to the towers for free, but admission is 22,000 dong per person, and there's absolutely nothing to see. Everyone has a different idea of ​​beauty, but we didn't see anything eye-catching—just old bricks, one tower, and that's it. Inside, there's nothing special either; I didn't notice any culture or historical significance. Maybe a tour would explain something, but there's no point in going there on your own.
There were a lot of people. You have to wear a special robe; they're hanging in the closet, free. The towers didn't impress me or my child at all.
It's a so-so place. We were expecting something more. If you're bored, go ahead. Otherwise, don't waste your time. By the way, there's an entrance fee.

Sorry i have to say this site is nothing to compare to Angkor Wat. It's a very small site with a few tiny "temples". We read there would be tourist guide available on some websites but apparently there was none on the day of our visit. So we only got a very brief introduction leaflet from the counter. No further explanations could be found for each little "temple" or whatsoever. A small info center to show some photos of before and after the discovery and renovation. To be honest, some bricks of the structures look a bit too modern (or should i say fake) to build on top of the old ones. If there's nothing much you want to visit in N.T, this place is good to spend your time for about an hour max.
There are so many people, it's impossible to take a decent photo, there's nothing to see there - three buildings in a row, inside it's like a crypt - flowers, lamps, figurines
If you looking for the similar feelings as Cambodia one, this is definitely not the place to visit. The number of tourist exceed the capacity of the sight. Consider other sight instead this one.

Not recommended. It's mostly Chinese tourists, the place is tiny, and wandering around under the blazing sun is incredibly tiring. Most importantly, there's not much to see; the scenery is very, very average, and the so-called "overlooking Nha Trang" view isn't impressive at all.
It's just a very small Buddhism temple converted from a Hindu temple. If you have time and have nothing to do can still go to have a look.

We visited during the day, which may be why there were so many people there. It's billed as a sacred site, but there are more tourist stalls than towers. It's worth a visit, but not with a tour group, and should be explored independently.
A very small attraction and sooooo crowded! Probably because Nha Trang isn't exactly known for its attractions.

I bought a ticket, went up, took a quick look around, and came right back down. It was really pointless. You really can skip it. Nha Trang doesn't really have many tourist attractions. The whole trip only took about half an hour.

If you enjoy guided tours and prefer to visit significant sites in their company, it's worth a visit. I think they'll be able to entertain you with stories about a given landmark for at least an hour. We're not fans of following the herd; we always choose the places we want to visit ourselves and find all the information we need online. We've often encountered discrepancies between the guides' stories and the actual information. Apparently, guides get carried away and lose track of reality, while the poor listeners take everything at face value, listening with their mouths agape at the incredible "facts." So, we headed to the towers on a cloudy day to while away the time and avoid sitting in our room or cafe. We walked there, getting a glimpse of how the Vietnamese live. You can also get there by bus #4 (7,000 VND/person). The entrance fee is 22,000 VND per person. 30 minutes was enough to walk around the entire grounds, see the inside of the towers (you can enter with your shoulders and knees covered, so free floor-length robes are available at the entrance for those who need them, but keep in mind you won't be the only one wearing them), watch a short performance, and take photos. Unfortunately, the Cham Towers didn't live up to expectations and ended up as a "check-the-box" attraction, offering nothing particularly interesting, but they are definitely worth seeing, as they are one of the recommended places to visit. So, if you have a lot of plans, check these towers out the window of a bus as you pass by. Trust me, you won't miss anything.

They brought us a tour, 45 minutes. You can take bus #4 yourself, but there's nothing to do there. The towers are only accessible barefoot (???), there are tons of Chinese people with dirty feet... The souvenirs are crap. The "Pokemon" ones get in the way of getting the right angle. Anyway...3-

Nha Trang has nothing to offer besides beaches and swimming. It's an anonymous, gray metropolis completely dominated by Russians. There are countless Russian shops, restaurants, and travel agencies. One of the few attractions is the Cham Towers. Well, thousands of tourists are there, mostly taking pictures of themselves. It's okay to see them, but not particularly worth seeing. The same goes for the city itself.
The site offers a pleasant walk along the beach to reach it. Once there, it costs 22,000 dong and takes 15 minutes to admire the brick "temples." The real highlight is the dances and music offered.

Decided to head here mainly to kill time whist it was raining. Cost about 30,000 vnd in a taxi from the centre and 22,000 for entry to the site.  It was very busy with lots of tourists taking their selfies and posing for photos. I enjoyed taking photos with my new camera but aside from that was a little bored.  The view from the top of the hill isn't very intersting, an industrial river and traffic slowing moving over a bridge. (nothing to write home about)  Not really sure about the culture / history that didn't really appeal after visiting a number of temples in and around Thailand and Vietnam.
Nothing special. Just another temple with local shops... Not wanting to offend anyone, but the truth is that the mysticism of the place has lost its meaning with so many tourists.

In my opinion, this is as much of a landmark as a Lenin monument in any Russian city. Three towers on a low hill... Well, we took a photo for the collection... Something like that... You can just look at photos from other travelers!
Beautiful. Everything is quite beautiful, the scenery is amazing. I think Nha Trang is a perfect tourist destination. Everything is okay.

There must have been old churches there a long time ago, but now they're showing off the newer ones. There's almost nothing ancient there; instead of a wagon tour, it's better to spend an extra day lounging on the beach!

It is nice in itself, but not very special. The view is decent, the temples are mostly very old but not very beautiful, and everything is quite small. If you are in the area, it is nice to visit briefly, but there is little to do there.

We hired a private guide right there on the spot. The tour was in English. It was informative, and I learned a lot about Ganesha, Shiva, and so on. I wouldn't say it was particularly interesting, but it was worth a visit.

The site is quite small, with few buildings; most of it is reconstruction. A few Vietnamese girls were dancing in the open space. Then there were souvenir shops, just like in China—quite commercialized. It would be better if there was a guided tour.

We were very disappointed with this visit. The entrance fee is 22,000 dong, which allows you to see these pretty temples up close, but nothing else. If you want to go inside (it's already very small), it's only if you're going to pray. Paying 22,000 dong for a 5-minute visit is something I would have preferred to avoid.
If you're in the area, why not! Entry is 22,000 VND. The visit took 10 minutes! It was incredibly crowded, and the music was very loud!!
Nothing really to see. No tours to explain what the temple is there for or when it was built. Only 15k per person to get in but nothing really to see.
Literally just ok. Nice view. It is a religious site, but with all the tour buses going through, one would never know it. Perhaps we went at the wrong time of day. Too bad.
We went there while on a sightseeing tour. We spent no more than 20 minutes there, so it felt like we were driving longer than we were there. We made a wish and that was it. So-so.

I think the experience of visiting this wonderful temple would have been enhanced had there not been so many people there. The number of buses out the front was over the top, as was the crowd inside. I have visited many such temples around the world. There was a total absence of the calm and spirituality that you can glean from such places. Not sure how you could time a visit to avoid the crowds. But if they are there (check the bus count) go elsewhere and give it a big miss!!

Most importantly, this place was a zoo. If you're interested in seeing Champa-era designs, you should visit the My Son temples near Hoi An. These towers were crawling with tourists more interested in selfies than learning anything about the history of this site. There is no information posted, in English or any other language. You'd be best to visit early in the mornings or just before closing to avoid the tourist buses and read up on the significance before you go as you won't learn anything there.
Pretty boring to be honest, but only 22,000 dong for a ticket so maybe worth it if you are at a loose end and feel like being surrounded by Korean tourists taking pictures of nothing much.

This is supposed to be the #2 site at Nha Trang (beach is #1) just don't get your hopes up. There is not much to see. The idea that this structure is almost or over 1000 years old is cool, but that's about it..

After Akor wot, i dont think it's necessary to visit there.........it's very small and crowd there, 3 minutes can take a round of this temple already. Took a picture, and can leave..... Sorry maybe I don't know the history and story there, but really nothing to see.

The temple is pretty nice but there is not a lot to see. I'm impressed that it was built in the 1300s but it looks like a lot of renovations were done including adding power to the main temple spot. I would not recommend this activity unless you were already in the area its not worth a long drivr.
Old towers and nothing more. There's nothing special inside either. To visit the inside of the towers, you need to rent a complimentary robe.
Pretty boring to be honest, wouldn't recommend it. We couldn't find the exit and ended up finding some guy urinating on the fence in there.
Well, towers are just towers, if you can even call them towers. They don't seem that old. The locals pray, and tourists practically climb on their heads.

After seeing Borobudur, Prambanan and Angkor temples, this was not exactly exciting. But if you got to see a temple in Nha Trang, it is worth making it. There are a few temples to peek into or even go in, if you have the right attire. Most of the temple complex is renovated and looks just too new.

A couple of obscure ruins, absolutely not worth a separate visit. We take bus number 4, cross two bridges, and immediately get off. The towers are in all their glory, and next to them is a market for the locals. Charming! But it's worth going in the morning. In the evening, there are tons of seafood restaurants on the embankment. The prices are decent, significantly lower than in the hotel area.
The history behind it must of been interesting. I guess for something that is 1000 yrs old. But not much for me. It was not worth a trip here and definitely could have been missed.
Avoid it if you've seen Angkor in Cambodia and Champasak in Laos! But it's one of the few things to see in Nha Trang.

It was nice to see history, but we experienced such horrible and rude entrance guards. They saw us coming with small children and tried to direct us in one by one. It was told that children are free and do not need their own ticket at the ticket office. The result was that the children stood outside the gate. We tried to tell them in vietnamese that they are children and do not have their own ticket. They were angry, rude as hell and started to harass us. It was not a pleasant visit
Nothing but business. There are a lot of tourists who take photos and a lot of sellers but no Hindu monks.

The temples and gardens are beautiful and worth a visit if you are in Nha Trang.  30 k entrance.  However we did not have a good experience. Firstly the young guy serving in the gardens cafe was
utterly rude and practically rolled his eyes when we asked for coffees. He was playing on his phone and that was more important to him than actually doing his job. Secondly, the music group were incredibly bad. All sat smoking and eating then gave a very disinterested lacklustre performance. One guy was very rude, shouting at us as we sat in the seats they had put out to get our feet off the manky old mat that was in front of the seats. ! !!.... Sadly visitors are disrespectful here too. It is a temple, a place of worship, not a place to throw down your plastic rubbish. Cups strewn everywhere. Sad to see.

Visited 02/03/23.. Entrance fee 30k. Nice to see the towers but overall very disappointing experience. The staff are very rude and lacked interest in their jobs. Given this is a tourist site the staff need to be more engaged with the visitors & not playing on their mobile phone, sleeping & smoking. Cafe guy was extremely rude and ignorant. My advise is look from the outside and keep your money in your pocket. 👎

If you like to be among massive crowd of Chinese tourists, including children and grandmothers shooting loudly, this is the place to be. The towers are just renewed, nothing spiritual anymore, but anyway, it's a good walk to go there from downtown and the view worth it. Good fish restaurants just near and not to many tourists in there.

The shop vendors inside the temple are very rude and shout abuse at you if you do not buy anything. Also they try to charge you ridiculous prices which drop massively once you decline. The temple is very beautiful but the roads around the area are very dangerous. There are many large  seafood restaurants along the river roads  at night time
Horrible tourists crowded spot. It has more of a Disney Park than a temple, not my cup of tea at all.
they didn't let me in because of my open shoulder, but the other two girls with open shoulders let me in
Quite a disappointing place. Most of it has been completely rebuilt so it has lost its old charm. The are plenty of nice old original temples in Lao and Cambodia so go there instead
No wheelchair access, very disappointing as states there is wheelchair access on page.
Always very crowded. The monument was revamped so much that it seems completely fake. Complete waste of time.
Nothing much to see. Just hot and round trip grab. Not even for killing time.

If you don't want to see rude Chinese, don't go.
They smoke, spit, and they're really crazy.

Place is nice, the staff is the rudest I’ve met in the whole country.
0/10
Overrun place by tour groups. Cramped place
Why go here? Nothing to see
It recommend. Too crowded.
Very rude staff
overrun with chinese

For tourists and locals alike, the Ponaga Tower is a beautiful place with reasonable entrance fees. However, there's a staff member named Thuy selling Cham artifacts who has a very unprofessional attitude towards visitors. Visitors need guidance, but Ms. Thuy is rude and aggressive, even yelling at them. She's extremely impolite and uncouth. I hope the Ponaga Tower Tourism Company will warn her to avoid losing customers and maintain the civilized atmosphere of this place. Thank you sincerely.
Unfortunately, it was completely overrun with tourists. There are definitely better temples that are also free. =)

The female staff member at the ticket office is really ill-mannered. She has a bad habit of tampering with her hands. Make sure to check your change carefully. She didn't give me the correct change herself, got even angrier, and told me to leave. We couldn't communicate and there were a lot of people, so I had no choice but to leave, but it was a really unpleasant experience.

It is a good tourist spot for taking photos, but it isn't that big, so you can see everything in less than an hour.

Everyone, be careful. The cashier is playing games with the money.

It was 60,000 VND for two people, so I paid 200,000 VND, but lol, they gave me 40,000 VND in change. I think there might be people who don't know and just put the money in, so make sure you check the denomination lol.

I didn't just try to shove the money in and checked the denomination, lol. Then they immediately asked for it back, lol. But they gave it back to me as if nothing happened. It was seriously ridiculous. I didn't want to ruin my trip, so I just left, but anyway, be careful.

A dull place, nothing much to see. It's a sprawling, unkempt space.

There are few photo spots, and there's a long line for the good seats.

The only plus is the group dancing there.

The temple itself was nice.

However, after you finish touring, absolutely do not take the yellow taxis at the entrance. There are many yellow and green taxi drivers waiting (and they solicit customers aggressively). I negotiated the fare before getting in, but once you get in the car, they overcharge you and don't speak a word of English.

In Nha Trang, you absolutely must just use Grab. Payment is processed immediately upon request, so I didn't have to pay any extra fees (at least in my case).

It wasn't much money in Korean Won, but it really made me angry.

If you don't take photos, you can walk through the temple in 5 minutes. Everything has been renovated. The ticket price has increased to 300,000. It's just three small temples, but there are a bunch of rules: no shorts or short sleeves, or you won't be allowed in; no photos inside the temples; and the worst thing was that nun who threw my shoes far away when we were both off in the same spot. Even if she kneels before Buddha every day, she won't have any good karma with such a malicious heart. Stop pretending to be a deity.

The entrance fee was 30,000 VND, but since I didn't have change, I paid 500,000 VND and received 370,000 VND in change.

When I pointed out that I was 100,000 VND short, the employee checked the balance and said that the amount was correct and they could not give me the money.

I was angry, so I asked for a refund, and I was able to get 100,000 VND back.

When I bought the tickets, I accidentally gave them three 500,000 VND bills, but they only gave me change for one and denied paying for the rest. I realized it too late, but they never give change automatically.

The entrance fee is 30,000 VND. Please make sure to give me the exact amount.

The place is very nice, with history and views, but I give it one star because of the staff. My friends and I came here for a walk and wanted to take a robe for a photo and to go inside. A Vietnamese woman in a mask and hat started yelling at us in a mixture of Vietnamese and English. They didn’t give us robes, although everyone else got them without any problems. This spoiled our impression of the place.

There isn't much to see... It's really small, so honestly, you can see everything within 10 minutes, including time to take photos.

I came expecting it to be grand, so I had really high expectations.

It has historical significance as the Champa Kingdom, but the tour is over in 10 minutes!

The admission fee is expensive considering there is nothing to see.

Ticket seller at Thap Ba Tower, September 18, 2025

Extremely rude. Just 3 seconds after I turned away, they threw away my printed ticket.

When I returned, the employee said, "If you don't want it, throw it away."

Then they added, "If you want, print another one."

A place that welcomes international visitors, yet such a rude attitude.

I then gave the employee a good scolding. This kind of attitude doesn't deserve a ticket sales position; they deserve to be out in the sun picking up trash on the beach.

They don't accept card payments.
21st century, a tourist spot is also a landmark! Bring cash. We arrived early in the morning to see this beautiful place, but they didn't let us in and didn't accept card payments.

I went during the 2023 Lunar New Year. It was very crowded, very commercialized, and the area was small with nothing worth seeing. It was definitely not worth the trip; we left after only five minutes. Tickets were 30,000 RMB per person during the Lunar New Year.
I'm afraid I'll get ripped off if I recommend it as a place worth visiting. The entrance fee is a waste of money... I think it's better to just look from the outside and be done with it.

The comparison with Angkor Wat doesn't hold up.
We arrived in Nha Trang early in the morning at the Basse slipway at the bus station. We asked the taxi driver to just take a quick look, as the spot opens at 6 AM and it was still a long way to the hotel check-in. We decided to check it out right away to avoid driving halfway across the city. At 6 AM, there was no one around. 10 minutes was more than enough time to explore. If you've seen something similar before, don't waste your time.

A temple that clearly shows Nha Trang is a resort destination.

You pay the entrance fee, but there is nothing to see inside.

Most of the visitors are Korean. There are more Koreans than locals and foreigners combined.

Visited once. I passed by it a few times while riding my motorcycle around for five days.

Tour buses... unbelievable.

Anyway, I don't understand why people say this place is good. It lacks that Instagram vibe, either.
The security guard wouldn't let me park, reserving space for taxis. What a disappointment for tourists! I traveled almost 500km to get here.

It is great for taking photos, but I wish they would put more effort into preserving the artifacts. It is unpleasant to see Chinese tourists smoking at another country's historical sites. They would make a huge fuss if a foreigner smoked in their own Forbidden City... As travelers, I wish they would learn some etiquette regarding other cultures.
Currently, the restoration work is very shoddy and lacks foresight, detracting from the aesthetic value of the historical site.
A place you don't need to spend money on transportation to go under the hot sun.
It's not a place for tourists; it's connected to rickshaws and taxis that guide and drive customers, it's too expensive, and it's not for domestic tourists. The place is cramped, hot, and stuffy.

The Thap Ba Tower is okay. Sorry for the 1-star review, however, the souvenir seller at the shop on the left from the entrance is a scammer. She sells souvenirs like colored stone spheres, round spheres with "Nha Trang Thap Ba" printed on them.

The prices aren't up for debate, but when I showed her the large stone for 150k, she quickly wrapped it in newspaper and sealed it with tape, intentionally preventing me from seeing it. When I opened it, I found a smaller 80k stone that was old and scratched.

I'm giving her a 1-star review so the Thap Ba Tower management can address this issue with her!
Not worth a visit. Too crowded. Price: 30,000 rubles. There are a lot of tents on the grounds that spoil the authentic look of the ancient buildings!

A very wonderful temple.

However, the ticket seller who threw the ticket was very disappointing.

She snatched the money and threw the ticket at me, and didn't even apologize. An unpleasant experience.

We visited with a family of four, including two elementary school students. Initially, Employee 1 told us the price as "3+1 free," but since we didn't have cash, we exchanged money and returned. However, Employee 2 told us to pay the full price for all four people, which led to a brief argument. Even the currency exchange staff had informed us that it was "3+1," so the person handling the money told us something different.
It's hard to even take photos because of the Chinese group tourists, and the price has doubled compared to before—300,000 VND.

Make sure to check your change when buying tickets. I was supposed to receive 200,000 VND in change, but they gave me 100,000 VND. I thought it was strange, so I mentioned it, and they gave me the 200,000 VND bill back as if it were no big deal. Then they told me to move aside because they needed to serve the next customer... Since I had multiple bills, I could have been in big trouble if I had just accepted them and walked past without thinking.

The young female employee at the ticket office was rude and bothersome. I didn't explicitly tell her to leave, but the situation was extremely unpleasant. Since I was a foreigner, she deducted 100,000 VND from my change. She only returned the money after I was counting it right in front of her, without even apologizing. The Po Nagar Temples were definitely worth visiting, but I felt upset even before entering.

I think the tickets were strictly cash, 30,000 VND per person.

There wasn't really anything to see.

I parked my motorbike and went to the ticket office, but there were so many people that I decided to come back another time. I just went back to retrieve it, but even though it didn't take more than five minutes, they charged me the full day's parking fee.

The grumpy-looking lady giving instructions was absolutely merciless. It's only about 200 won, but Vietnam is so unfriendly.

Short sleeves and shorts are not allowed inside the temple.

I don't know why anyone goes here either.

I don't know what criteria they use to decide who gets in. Girls walked past me in miniskirts and off-the-shoulder tops, and I was wearing a knee-length, strappy dress (I know it's not appropriate), but you can see photos on Google of people who don't follow the dress code, which is why we weren't prepared. It's a shame.
Another place taken over by Chinese people. If you want to fit in with the local middle-aged women, I recommend coming here.

There isn't as much to see as I expected, but it's worth visiting since you're in Nha Trang anyway.

However, I paid 200,000 VND at the ticket counter, but they tried to swap the money for me, giving me 10,000 VND instead of 10,000.

I hope you check your money carefully when paying. ㅠㅠㅠ

The entrance fee is too expensive. It is 30k.

Once inside, there are only two temples. You can see locals praying.

I don't understand why this place is a tourist attraction that charges an entrance fee.

There is a market near the temples. You can walk a short distance to look around.
The staff wouldn't let me leave my belongings. I don't understand how they work.

There's nothing to see, and it's too hot.

I'd rather just stay at the beach (the entrance fee is a waste).

Having seen the towers from the bridge, we decided to walk up to them.
Busloads of tourists are brought to the towers.
We were there between 5 and 6 PM.
I wanted to take a few photos. You have to pay the entrance fee before going inside. And the place is absolutely swarming with Chinese people. The area is small, and the towers are guarded and fenced. This whole process, just for the sake of taking a photo with hundreds of Chinese people, is highly questionable.
For good photos, arrive as early as possible.

Unless you are on a package tour, do not go. It is crowded, traffic is dangerous, the staff are unfriendly, and taking photos is difficult. Above all, it is dangerous if you are traveling with children.

Dangerous vehicles ignoring traffic signals nearby. Always use Grab when getting around the area, as taxi scams are common.
It's beautiful, but parking is difficult. The organizers should consider improving the parking situation; that would be even better.
I lost money on a fast transfer and after a week I still haven't received confirmation from Viettel that the item has been delivered and received. The service experience was quite poor.
You can enter for free from the beach. Entry from the street is 100k.

I can't believe they don't accept cards at a national historic site lol. They should at least put an ATM nearby. The surroundings are dirty and hot too lol. Still, it's a tourist spot, so it's a shame to see such underdeveloped infrastructure.
Be careful from the moment you enter, as they give you less change than the ticket price. Even if there is a long line, you must check your change before making way.

This tourist spot, which has been photographed countless times, is pretty much a must-see in Nha Trang.

Because we were on a tour, the bus stop led to a side road leading to the attraction. And wow, all those vendors along the way... every time I see this kind of chaotic local scene, I have absolutely no desire to buy anything.

Once you get to the attraction, it's basically just a few buildings. Most people are just posing for photos, and even finding a toilet inside is a bit of a hassle. Also, this place really feels like a lawless zone. Some shady "masters" were putting on airs, and in the bus parking lot, there were even locals holding up signs for Falun Gong practitioners. The tour guide said they were just doing it for money and told everyone not to accept anything.

The whole experience was quite unpleasant. All the shops and vendors along the way had Chinese signs, clearly making a lot of money off Chinese tourists, and then they put up all this gibberish to disgust people. I definitely won't go back to Vietnam to contribute anything to these bastards. One trip to Vietnam is enough; of course, if you haven't been, it's even better if you don't come at all.
Extremely unfriendly staff. The facility is relatively small. A waste of money. There are better places.

Beware of Scams!!!!!!

We had a great time visiting the Hindu temple, but we got scammed by a taxi driver in front of it. He kept saying he would take us wherever we wanted to go, showed us the Grab app, and matched the fare. Then, as soon as we started driving, he acted like the money calculation was wrong. Thinking the denominations were different, we took out our wallets to check the currency. Just as we were about to take the money, he took a large bill, turned the meter off and on again, and pretended to return it, but actually took the money. Beware!! Always call a Grab.
What you see is all there is. I don't really understand why there is an admission fee.

The female ticket booth attendant at the entrance has a terrible temper. She kept giving me a disgusted look because I was confused about the currency unit and kept looking for money!! Don't ever come to Korea.
Aside from the fact that it is somewhat unique compared to Korean culture and architecture, there wasn't much to see, so it wasn't really a place I would recommend!
Actually, a historical site you don't really need to visit. What you see is all there is.
This place has sacred scenery and picturesque mountains and rivers.

Too much change
I don't like anything
It’s not really a course worth paying to visit, just a package deal.

If you have been to Myanmar or Angkor in Cambodia, please skip this.

It's worse than the restrooms at Angkor.

It's hot, so you don't need to wear long pants; there's no rule against it.

All new buildings lack the feel of historical sites.

You can probably just look at the photos. I had nowhere else to go so I paid to go in, but it was a waste of money and a waste of the Grab fare... Just go buy some durian within a 10-minute walk from downtown for half the price... Go buy some durian.
The ticket seller was unprofessional. Low personality. Needs retraining. You're wearing glasses and look fat.

It's not worth going in without a tour guide because there are no signs explaining the meaning of the symbols.
It's just annoying going in full Chinese traffic.

It's a score where even one star is too generous. When I asked about the tickets, they gave me only three because there were two adults and two children. When I asked about it, they threw the tickets at me and got angry, saying, "Look, there are three right here!" I paid 200,000 VND, but they didn't give me 100,000 VND in change, so I raised my voice in response. I turned the camera around and got my money back, but I was extremely upset. There was nothing to see, just a bad mood.
Vietnamese people wanting to learn about Vietnamese culture spend 22,000 VND plus 4,000 VND for parking. But there's nothing to learn.

Have you ever felt lost in your own country? You'll feel that way here. Everywhere you look, there are Chinese characters, crowds of Chinese people, noisy and jostling. I'd heard about it before coming to Nha Trang, but standing there, I truly felt the profound sadness.

The parking attendant acted like he owned the place... He yelled at customers who weren't sure where to park...
He spoke as if we were parking our car at his house.

THE PARKING LOT IS POOR, AND THE DRIVER WITH A 79 LICENSE PLATE IS A PROFESSIONAL PERSON WHO TRICKS AND EXTORTS OTHER DRIVERS TO MAKE MONEY, EVEN IF THEY HAVEN'T COLLISED ON YOUR CAR. IF YOU STEAK UP, HE'LL STOP; IF YOU ARE SOFT, YOU'LL LOSE YOUR MONEY!
Great
Not impressed! You can walk through it in 5 minutes. It's not worth paying for a sightseeing tour. You can visit it yourself. Entry is $1.

The store is nice, but the staff selling ice cream and drinks at the front are completely unfriendly. When I gave them change, they refused to give it to me and told me to leave. They were so rude, saying they didn't want to handle the change calculation. Owner, please take action.

My husband and I visited the towers. Entrance to the towers is now paid, so we decided to buy a ticket as usual. At the ticket office, we were charged 1,000,000 for two, but they gave us change for 500,000. Be careful and check your change. It was very difficult to prove the truth from the video; the ticket office staff were very quick to show it and falsify the facts. We had a very negative impression of the ticket office, but we did enjoy the Ponagar Towers themselves and the views of the city!
Sure, it's an ancient place, but there's nothing much to see except crowds of Chinese people taking selfies. So I didn't like it at all.
If you're a die-hard architecture fan and absolutely love crowds of Chinese people, you should definitely visit this place. If any of these points don't apply to you, there's nothing to do there.

There's no information for tourists. Many might not even realize it's a Hindu temple. It's completely overrun with people, and there isn't much to see. I felt sorry for the worshippers praying and crying amidst all the tourists. The view from the hill is okay, but nothing special. All in all, I think tourists are out of place here.

I gladly took a taxi here, but the traffic is congested, there are tons of Chinese people, and there is absolutely nothing to see. It was a waste of money. There is an entrance fee, too. I don't understand why it's famous.
The huge number of Chinese people, the unbearable heat, and the entrance fee ruined the experience, and the photos turned out to be worthless.

This historic relic is supposed to be the landmark of Nha Trang. However, I was quite disappointed to visit there. I was sweating like hell under the scorching heat. It was totally crowded with tourists and the scenery was not that impressive. I do not think it is worth spending time to visit this place.

There's nothing to see at all—three or four towers, each with soot from candles lit by the Vietnamese. There's practically no historical information in English; everything has been heavily restored, unlike the 1938 photo. It's worth stopping by to take a photo, nothing more.

A horrific desecration of an architectural monument. No respect for culture, they're just shops. The toilets are practically in the tower. The guide only took us to the entrance, then said a few words that were familiar to everyone who opened the tour program.

Went there with big expectation, but pretty disappointed for the site. Too many Chinese tourists were there, it almost look like 15mins photographing place~  Moreover, the tower and many parts were artificially restored with red bricks. Couldn't feel authentic remains greatness

We arrived there in the midst of celebrations in honor of the goddess Po Nagar. We couldn't enter the towers, but there were crowds of revelers milling around, with offerings and food laid out everywhere. It's a good thing we got there on a free tour. It's not worth going there on your own.

It's hot, there are a bunch of Chinese brothers, multi-armed deities, unkempt monks, and just plain dirt. There's a "fragrant river" nearby. Only go in the morning or evening, and only if you're interested in antiquities and Eastern religions. And there are traders everywhere!! Thousands of them...)))

The ruins suddenly appear on a small hill in the city. The area is quite small, and even if you take your time to look around, it probably only takes about 30-40 minutes. The abundance of fruit vendors near the entrance gives a lively, vibrant Vietnamese feel, which I think is quite interesting.
There isn't much to see... just three buildings... it isn't even perfectly restored... it feels like a waste of the 22,000 VND entrance fee. You should definitely go to the mud bath nearby instead.
Nothing special. When we came there was some kind of food sacrifising ceremony which wasn't interesting. View is way better from the distance so just drive by.

As a temple i thought it would be a peaceful experience. However when we arrived at approx 3pm in the afternoon there were about 4 bus loads of tour groups at the temple.  There is one part where you can go inside one of the building but it was over crowded. There were too many people in there and it was not worth going in because you could not appreciate it. There is an admission charge - a very small amount - around $30000 VND. It'll take you about 15 minutes to see everything.  I advise to go very early in the morning to avoid the crowds.

If you walk around the entire area, you can finish in 2-3 minutes. If you're going to visit Hoi An, you don't need to come here. A word of advice: wear brightly colored clothing for better photos. I wore stripes and black pants, which weren't very eye-catching and didn't look good in the pictures. Don't worry about not being able to get inside, as the site provides jackets that you can wear over your clothes.

It remains a mystery to me why this place is number one on Trip Advisor's list of things to do, even ahead of Vinpearl. Well, maybe for someone who's never tasted anything sweeter than a radish and hasn't been to, say, well-trodden Thailand, it's interesting. But we personally regretted wasting our time on this attraction. It's too touristy in the worst sense of the word. Temples, incense, Buddhas, "put on a robe, go inside," etc. Overall, not recommended.
The excursion time was chosen incorrectly, it was uninteresting and tiring. Overall it was good, but it would have been better to lie on the beach.

If you want to be touted with rubbish this will be heaven for you, otherwise it's the opposite. Apart from that it's an interesting pile of bricks that attracts a lot of tourists for some reason or other.

The towers aren't exactly ancient anymore. They're a modern remnant, constructed of new bricks. All that remains of the old towers is the foundation. They themselves are of no interest at all. The guidebook describes them so beautifully that you wonder if these are the same towers. There was a small concert on the grounds: two guys in strange turbans were beating drums. It was funny at first, but then they got really, really hooked. Then, girls with pots on their heads danced out of sync, as if they were doing it for the first time. The grounds are very small. The coolest thing is that inside the towers there are sculptures imitating phalluses, and people go inside these turrets and worship them. All the towers have this kind of allusion. Despite all this, the photos of the towers turned out beautiful.
